threat_vector: Stored XSS in Vehicle Service Management System 1.0 Category List Section
exploitation_method: Attacker injects malicious JavaScript payload into the Category List field during login panel interaction. The payload is stored in the database and executed in the browsers of all users who view the category list, allowing credential theft, session hijacking, or malware distribution.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unusual HTML/JavaScript characters in category input logs (script tags, event handlers like onerror, onload); (2) Database queries containing encoded payloads (%3Cscript%3E, <script>); (3) Abnormal DOM manipulation in client-side logs; (4) CSP violation reports; (5) Unexpected content-type mismatches in category list API responses; (6) Multiple users reporting simultaneous unauthorized actions correlating with category list access
mitigation_strategy: Implement context-aware output encoding (HTML entity encoding for HTML context, JavaScript encoding for JS context). Apply Content Security Policy (CSP) with script-src 'self' directive. Validate and sanitize all inputs server-side using allowlist-based validation. Implement parameterized queries/prepared statements to prevent injection. Add HTTPOnly and Secure flags to session cookies. Deploy Web Application Firewall (WAF) rules to block common XSS patterns. Conduct regular security code reviews and perform input validation testing on all user-facing fields.

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1564.008 - Email Hiding Rules
exploitation_method: Attacker creates or modifies Outlook/Gmail forwarding rules, filters, or client-side rules to automatically hide, delete, or redirect incoming emails (particularly credentials, MFA codes, or sensitive communications) to hidden folders or external accounts, concealing evidence of compromise and maintaining persistent access
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unusual mail rule creation events in Exchange/Office 365 audit logs (New-InboxRule), suspicious forwarding addresses in rule configurations, rules targeting security alerts or admin notifications, PowerShell commands modifying MailboxRules, abnormal rule conditions (Hide from address book), client-side rule files (*.rules) with unexpected modifications, gaps in expected email delivery patterns, rules created by compromised service accounts outside business hours
mitigation_strategy: Implement mail rule policies restricting automatic forwarding; enable advanced mail rule auditing and alerting in Exchange Online; deploy MCAS/Defender for Cloud Apps to detect suspicious mail rules; enforce Conditional Access policies requiring MFA for rule modifications; regularly audit existing mail rules organization-wide; restrict PowerShell access to MailboxRule cmdlets; use DLP policies to prevent sensitive data exfiltration via rules; monitor for unexpected external forwarding addresses; implement DKIM/DMARC to prevent spoofed rule-based impersonation

threat_vector: Spring Framework Log Injection via Malicious User Input (CVE-2021-22911)
exploitation_method: Attacker injects newline characters (\n, \r) and log-like patterns into user-controlled input fields that are logged by Spring Framework without proper sanitization, causing arbitrary log entries to be written that may include false error messages, credential-like strings, or misleading information to obfuscate actual security events.
detection_pattern: Monitor application logs for: (1) Unexpected newline characters (literal \n or \r sequences) in logged user input fields, (2) Log entries with misaligned formatting suggesting injected content, (3) Multiple consecutive log entries with identical timestamps from the same input source, (4) Log entries containing credentials, error codes, or system messages appearing immediately after user input logging, (5) Abnormal log volume spikes correlated with specific form submissions or API parameters
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Spring Framework to versions 5.3.14+, 5.2.19+, or 6.0+; implement input validation to reject or sanitize newline characters and control characters in user input before logging; configure logging framework to escape special characters; use structured logging (JSON format) with separate fields for user input; implement log monitoring and alerting for suspicious log patterns; apply principle of least privilege for log file access; conduct log analysis for injected entries to identify attack attempts

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1036.005 - Match Legitimate Name or Location
exploitation_method: Attacker creates malicious files, processes, or directories using names identical to legitimate system resources (e.g., svchost.exe, System32 folder, legitimate DLLs) placed in unexpected locations or with subtle naming variations to evade detection and gain execution or persistence.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Process execution from non-standard directories (/tmp, /home, %AppData%, %Temp%); (2) File hash mismatches for known legitimate executables; (3) Multiple processes with identical names from different parent processes or locations; (4) Unsigned or invalid signatures on system-named executables; (5) DLL loading from unexpected paths; (6) Command-line arguments anomalous for the purported legitimate process
mitigation_strategy: Implement: (1) Application whitelisting with full path validation; (2) Code signing enforcement for critical system processes; (3) File integrity monitoring (FIM) on System32 and other critical directories; (4) Restrict DLL search paths and enforce DLL load order; (5) Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) with behavioral analysis; (6) Regular hash verification of legitimate binaries; (7) Restrict write permissions to system directories; (8) Disable loading of unsigned binaries via Windows policies (Secure Boot, Code Integrity)

threat_vector: Pointer Dereference Vulnerability in GPAC 1.0.1 _fseeko Function
exploitation_method: Attacker provides malformed input (crafted MP4 or other media file) that triggers unsafe pointer operations in the _fseeko function, causing null or invalid pointer dereference leading to application crash or memory access violation
detection_pattern: Monitor for: GPAC process crashes with segmentation fault; abnormal file seek operations with invalid file handles; core dumps referencing _fseeko stack frame; application termination immediately after processing specific file types; repeated failed file I/O operations in logs with error codes indicating invalid pointers
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade GPAC to version 1.0.2 or later with pointer validation patches; implement input validation for media files before processing; use memory protection mechanisms (ASLR, DEP); run GPAC in sandboxed environment with resource limits; add file integrity checks before parsing; implement watchdog mechanisms to detect and restart crashed processes

threat_vector: follow-redirects vulnerable to Exposure of Private Personal Information to an Unauthorized Actor
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious redirect chains that cause the follow-redirects library to leak sensitive headers (Authorization, Cookies, Session tokens) across domain boundaries when following HTTP redirects to attacker-controlled or compromised domains
detection_pattern: Monitor for: 1) Outbound requests with Authorization/Cookie headers to unexpected domains, 2) follow-redirects library versions <1.14.1 in dependency audits, 3) HTTP redirect responses (301/302/307/308) to different domains followed by credential-bearing requests, 4) Unusual cross-domain redirect chains in HTTP logs, 5) POST/PUT requests redirecting to unrelated domains
mitigation_strategy: 1) Update follow-redirects to version 1.14.1 or later, 2) Implement strict redirect validation: whitelist allowed redirect domains, 3) Remove sensitive headers before following redirects to different domains, 4) Use allowlist-based redirect policies in application code, 5) Monitor and restrict redirect depths (max 5), 6) Disable automatic credential following via secure cookie flags (Secure, HttpOnly, SameSite=Strict), 7) Implement Content Security Policy with redirect restrictions

threat_vector: libbpf 0.6.0 and 0.6.1 heap-based buffer overflow in __bpf_object__open
exploitation_method: Attacker supplies a crafted BPF program binary to bpf_object__open_mem() that triggers a 4-byte heap buffer overflow during object parsing in __bpf_object__open, potentially enabling code execution or heap corruption
detection_pattern: Monitor for: AddressSanitizer or Valgrind heap overflow reports; libbpf version detection showing 0.6.0 or 0.6.1; BPF program loading failures with memory corruption signatures; crash logs referencing __bpf_object__open with heap-related stack traces; unexpected process termination when loading BPF programs
mitigation_strategy: Immediately upgrade libbpf to version 0.6.2 or later; implement input validation for BPF program binaries before passing to bpf_object__open_mem(); restrict BPF program loading to trusted sources only; run BPF-loading processes with minimal privileges and memory sandboxing; deploy memory safety tools (ASAN, Valgrind) in development/testing environments

threat_vector: @replit/crosis library versions < 7.3.1 - sensitive information exposure in container protocol communication
exploitation_method: Attacker intercepts or logs unencrypted communications between client and Replit container, or exploits improper sanitization of error messages/debug output that may contain authentication tokens, API keys, or environment variables transmitted through the protocol
detection_pattern: Monitor for: cleartext transmission of authentication credentials in network traffic, sensitive data appearing in application logs or error messages, unusual token usage patterns, authentication tokens found in git history or code repositories, environment variable leakage in debugging output or container logs
mitigation_strategy: Immediately upgrade @replit/crosis to version 7.3.1 or later. Implement network segmentation and TLS encryption for all client-container communications. Rotate all exposed credentials and API keys. Audit container logs and git history for leaked secrets. Implement secret management best practices using environment variable isolation and secret vaults. Enable audit logging for all authentication events and container access.

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Component Object Model (COM) Hijacking
exploitation_method: Attacker modifies COM registry entries (HKCU\Software\Classes\CLSID or HKLM\Software\Classes\CLSID) to redirect legitimate COM object instantiation to malicious DLLs. When applications request COM objects, Windows loads the attacker's DLL instead, achieving code execution with the same privileges as the requesting application.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Registry modifications under HKCU\Software\Classes\CLSID and HKLM\Software\Classes\CLSID targeting InprocServer32, LocalServer32, or ProgID values; (2) Unexpected DLL loads from unusual paths by COM-consuming applications; (3) Process creation with parent processes known to instantiate COM objects; (4) Sysmon Event ID 13 (Registry Set) with suspicious CLSID modifications; (5) Autoruns/WMI event subscriptions pointing to non-standard COM DLLs.
mitigation_strategy: Implement COM object registration monitoring via Sysmon/ETW; restrict write permissions on HKLM\Software\Classes\CLSID to administrators only; enforce application whitelisting for DLL loading paths; monitor HKCU COM modifications for non-standard paths; use Code Integrity/Device Guard to enforce signed DLL loading; audit and baseline legitimate COM registrations; disable unnecessary COM object activation via Group Policy; implement registry integrity monitoring for critical CLSID entries.

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Hidden Window
exploitation_method: Attacker uses Windows API calls (ShowWindow with SW_HIDE parameter, SetWindowPos with negative coordinates, or WS_EX_TOOLWINDOW style) to hide malicious application windows from user view, enabling silent execution of unauthorized activities like credential harvesting, screen capture, or command execution
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Process creation with hidden window flags in API call sequences; (2) Legitimate processes unexpectedly calling ShowWindow(SW_HIDE) or SetWindowLong with WS_EX_TOOLWINDOW; (3) Child processes spawned by non-typical parent processes immediately followed by window hiding API calls; (4) Discrepancy between process enumeration tools (Get-Process vs TaskManager) showing processes others don't; (5) High volume of SetWindowPos calls with unusual coordinates; (6) EDR/SIEM alerts for WinEventHook or low-level window manipulation without user interaction
mitigation_strategy: Implement: (1) Application whitelisting to restrict which processes can execute window manipulation APIs; (2) API hooking/kernel monitoring to log all ShowWindow/SetWindowPos calls with parent process context; (3) User Access Control enforcement to restrict privilege escalation required for persistent hidden windows; (4) Regular auditing of running processes using multiple enumeration methods and comparing results; (5) Disable unnecessary APIs via AppLocker or Device Guard policies; (6) Train users to investigate unexpected process behavior and restrict non-admin accounts from launching suspicious applications

threat_vector: PublishPress Capabilities plugin (versions before 2.3.1) lacks authorization and CSRF protection on settings update endpoints
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious webpage or email containing hidden form submissions targeting the plugin's settings update functionality. When an authenticated admin visits the page, the request executes with their credentials, bypassing authorization checks. The absence of CSRF tokens allows cross-origin requests to modify plugin configurations without proper validation.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) POST requests to PublishPress settings endpoints lacking nonce verification in logs; (2) settings updates from referrer origins outside your domain; (3) rapid or unusual settings modifications from low-privilege accounts; (4) wp-admin access logs showing settings changes from external sources; (5) audit logs revealing unexpected capability or permission changes
mitigation_strategy: Immediately update PublishPress Capabilities plugin to version 2.3.1 or later. Verify all installations via wp-cli or admin dashboard. Implement strict CSRF token validation using WordPress nonces on all settings forms. Enforce role-based access control (capability checks) before processing settings updates. Add audit logging for all plugin configuration changes. Consider using security headers (SameSite cookies) to limit cross-site request execution. Review recent settings audit logs for unauthorized modifications and remediate any changes made by the vulnerability.

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1548.004 - Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism: Windows Permissions
exploitation_method: Attacker modifies Windows file/registry permissions or exploits misconfigured NTFS ACLs to grant unauthorized access to sensitive system files, services, or registry keys. Common techniques include taking ownership of protected files, modifying DACLs, or leveraging Windows API calls (SetSecurityInfo, RegSetKeySecurity) to escalate privileges.
detection_pattern: Monitor Event ID 4670 (permissions on object changed), 4657 (registry value modified), 4656 (object access), and 4720 (user account created with elevated rights). Look for unusual ownership changes via takeown.exe, icacls.exe modifications, or API calls to SetSecurityInfo/RegSetKeySecurity in user context. Track processes spawning from user sessions attempting to modify system file ACLs.
mitigation_strategy: Enforce principle of least privilege via Group Policy (restrict users from modifying permissions); disable unnecessary privilege escalation tools; implement AppLocker/Windows Defender Application Control blocking takeown.exe and icacls.exe; enable and audit object access permissions; use Privileged Access Workstations (PAWs) for admin activities; regularly audit NTFS/registry permissions for drift; deploy endpoint detection and response (EDR) to alert on suspicious ACL modifications.

threat_vector: Apache James IMAP infinite loop via crafted APPEND/STATUS commands
exploitation_method: Attacker sends malformed IMAP APPEND or STATUS commands with specially crafted parameters that trigger parsing logic to enter infinite loops in the command processing stack, consuming CPU resources and causing denial of service
detection_pattern: Monitor for: sustained high CPU usage correlating with IMAP session activity; IMAP commands that timeout or don't complete within expected timeframe; repeated identical or similar APPEND/STATUS commands from single client; abnormal parsing state transitions in IMAP logs; thread dumps showing threads stuck in parsing loops with stack traces in IMAP command handlers
mitigation_strategy: Apply Apache James security patches addressing IMAP parsing logic; implement command parsing timeouts with forced termination; add input validation and length limits on APPEND/STATUS command parameters before processing; deploy rate limiting per IMAP client connection; implement watchdog timers for long-running IMAP operations; use fuzzing-based regression testing in CI/CD pipeline; consider WAF/proxy rules to block malformed IMAP syntax patterns

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1588.004: Digital Certificates
exploitation_method: Attacker obtains legitimate or forged digital certificates to impersonate trusted entities, enabling man-in-the-middle attacks, malware signing, or credential harvesting through phishing sites with valid HTTPS encryption.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: newly registered domains with SSL certificates issued within hours, certificate transparency logs showing unexpected cert issuance for known domains, mismatched certificate subject names vs. actual domain usage, certificates from unexpected Certificate Authorities, revoked certificates still in active use, anomalous certificate chain validation failures, and bulk certificate acquisition patterns from threat intelligence feeds.
mitigation_strategy: Implement certificate pinning for critical applications, deploy HSTS headers to prevent certificate substitution, establish certificate transparency log monitoring and alerting, enforce strong certificate validation in applications, maintain updated root CA certificate stores, conduct regular certificate audits across infrastructure, use multi-factor certificate issuance requirements, implement DNS CAA records to restrict certificate issuers, and subscribe to threat feeds monitoring suspicious certificate issuance patterns.

threat_vector: Pipenv requirements file parsing vulnerability (CVE-2022-24765) - versions 2018.10.9 through 2022.1.7 allow injection via malformed requirements entries
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts requirements.txt or Pipfile with specially formatted entries containing shell metacharacters or path traversal sequences that bypass sanitization during parsing, leading to arbitrary code execution when pipenv install/sync is executed
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unusual characters in requirements files (backticks, $(), semicolons, pipes in package names/versions), (2) Pipenv processes spawning unexpected child processes, (3) File access outside expected directories during pipenv operations, (4) Audit logs showing pipenv version < 2022.1.8, (5) Network connections initiated during dependency resolution phase
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade pipenv to version 2022.1.8 or later; implement file integrity monitoring on Pipfile/requirements.txt; restrict pipenv execution to isolated environments with limited process spawning capabilities; validate and sanitize all dependency specifications before executing pipenv commands; use dependency pinning with hash verification; run pipenv in containers with minimal privileges

threat_vector: Z-Wave devices with Silicon Labs 500 series chipsets using CRC-16 encapsulation vulnerable to frame manipulation attacks
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malformed Z-Wave frames with invalid CRC-16 checksums that pass validation due to implementation flaws in Silicon Labs 500 series firmware, allowing injection of arbitrary commands or state changes without proper authentication verification
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Z-Wave frames with CRC validation errors followed by successful command execution, (2) unexpected device state changes without corresponding legitimate controller commands, (3) repeated CRC calculation mismatches in device logs, (4) Z-Wave traffic patterns showing frames with identical payloads but different CRC values, (5) device firmware logs showing frame acceptance despite checksum failures
mitigation_strategy: Update affected devices to latest patched firmware versions; implement network-level Z-Wave traffic validation using independent CRC-16 verification; segment Z-Wave networks from critical systems; enforce Z-Wave security S2 framework with CBKE authentication; monitor controller logs for unauthorized frame acceptance; disable legacy S0 security if S2 available; implement Z-Wave network IDS monitoring for frame anomalies

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Digital Certificates (T1587.003, T1588.004)
exploitation_method: Attacker obtains or creates fraudulent digital certificates to impersonate legitimate services, sign malware, or conduct man-in-the-middle attacks. Methods include compromising CAs, exploiting certificate issuance processes, or using self-signed certificates with social engineering.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: certificate transparency (CT) log anomalies for unexpected domain certificates, SSL/TLS handshake failures with certificate mismatches, self-signed certificates from unexpected sources, certificate validation errors in logs, unusual certificate issuance patterns, revoked certificates still in use, certificates with unusual validity periods, and deviations from known organizational certificate inventory.
mitigation_strategy: Implement certificate pinning for critical applications, enforce strict CA validation and use CT log monitoring services, maintain certificate inventory management, deploy endpoint DLP to prevent unauthorized certificate installation, require code signing with organizational certificates only, enable OCSP stapling and CRL checking, implement mutual TLS authentication, conduct regular certificate audits, use hardware security modules (HSMs) for private key protection, and educate users to verify certificate chains.

threat_vector: Malicious Browser Extensions (ATT&CK T1176)
exploitation_method: Attacker distributes malicious extension via official app stores, phishing, or bundled with legitimate software. Once installed, extension gains access to browsing data, credentials, cookies, and can inject content into web pages or intercept network traffic.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unexpected extension installations in browser profiles, extensions with recently created accounts or suspicious permissions (tabs, webRequest, storage), network traffic from extensions to C2 domains, modifications to extension manifests, elevated privileges granted without user interaction, sudden changes in installed extension count, extensions from untrusted publishers, extensions requesting sensitive permissions (proxy, cookies, passwords)
mitigation_strategy: Implement browser extension allowlist policies via GPO/MDM, restrict installation to official stores only, disable user ability to install unsigned extensions, monitor extension installation logs via endpoint detection, use endpoint protection to scan extension files, enforce regular extension audits, educate users on extension permission warnings, deploy browser isolation for high-risk users, maintain updated blocklists of known malicious extensions, require MFA for sensitive browser activities

threat_vector: NULL pointer dereference in dhrystone 2.1 dhry_1.c main() function causing denial of service
exploitation_method: Attacker triggers execution path in dhrystone 2.1 that dereferences an uninitialized or unchecked pointer in main(), causing segmentation fault and process termination. This occurs when specific input conditions or environmental states lead to NULL pointer assignment without validation.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: segmentation fault signals (SIGSEGV) from dhrystone process; core dumps with NULL pointer address in main(); application crash logs showing unhandled exception at NULL address; unexpected termination with exit code 139 (SIGSEGV); valgrind reports showing 'Invalid read/write of size' at 0x0 location
mitigation_strategy: Apply security patch to dhrystone 2.1 adding NULL pointer checks before dereference in main(); implement input validation for all pointer assignments; use compiler flags (-fno-delete-null-pointer-checks, -Wall -Wextra) to catch unsafe patterns; deploy runtime protection via AddressSanitizer or Valgrind; restrict dhrystone execution privileges; implement watchdog monitoring with automatic restart on segmentation fault

threat_vector: HDF5 1.13.1-1 Segmentation Fault Denial of Service
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malformed HDF5 file with specific binary structure that triggers memory access violation in HDF5 library parsing routines, causing process crash when application attempts to read the file
detection_pattern: Monitor for: segmentation fault signals (SIGSEGV) from processes using libhdf5; application crashes with core dumps referencing HDF5 library functions; repeated failed HDF5 file read operations from untrusted sources; abnormal process termination logs associated with HDF5 parsing
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade HDF5 to version 1.13.2 or later; implement input validation and sandboxing for HDF5 file processing; run HDF5 parsing in isolated containers with resource limits; restrict file upload sources; implement application-level exception handling around HDF5 read operations to gracefully handle failures without service interruption

threat_vector: Z-Wave Silicon Labs 500 series S2 devices vulnerable to cryptographic key extraction and unauthorized command injection
exploitation_method: Attackers exploit firmware vulnerabilities in specific device versions to extract S2 security keys through side-channel attacks, memory dumps, or firmware extraction. Once keys are obtained, attackers can forge authenticated Z-Wave commands to control devices or inject malicious frames into the Z-Wave network without triggering S2 validation.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unexpected S2 key rotation or re-pairing events in Z-Wave logs; (2) Authenticated commands from unknown node IDs; (3) Firmware version mismatch alerts for ZST10 v6.04, ZEN20 v5.03, ZEN25 v5.03, ZW090-A; (4) Out-of-sequence S2 MPAN counters indicating replay or forged frames; (5) Network traffic showing device communication without proper S2 ECDH handshake completion; (6) USB/serial access attempts to devices that normally operate wirelessly.
mitigation_strategy: Immediately inventory and isolate affected device models. Apply firmware updates to versions patching S2 key handling and cryptographic operations. For devices without patches: (1) Disable S2 unsolicited server functionality if not required; (2) Implement network segmentation to restrict Z-Wave traffic to trusted controllers only; (3) Monitor controller logs for authentication failures; (4) Enforce S2 Authenticated/Encrypted joining policies; (5) Replace vulnerable devices with patched versions or alternative manufacturers; (6) Disable physical debug interfaces and enforce secure boot if available; (7) Implement intrusion detection at the Z-Wave gateway level to detect unauthorized commands.

threat_vector: The Stars Rating WordPress plugin before 3.5.1 does not validate the submitted rating, allowing submission of long integer, causing a Denial of Service in the comments section, or pending comment dash
exploitation_method: Attacker submits HTTP POST request to the plugin's rating endpoint with an oversized integer value in the rating parameter, bypassing input validation. The unvalidated integer is stored in the database and processed by the WordPress admin dashboard, causing excessive resource consumption when rendering comments or pending comments page due to integer overflow or inefficient query processing.
detection_pattern: Monitor for POST requests to wp-admin/admin-ajax.php with plugin rating handlers containing rating parameter values significantly outside normal range (e.g., >5 stars); analyze database queries for unusually large integer values in the plugin's rating table; detect sudden increase in database query execution time; identify error logs showing integer overflow warnings or PHP memory exhaustion errors; monitor admin dashboard response times for delays when loading comments or pending comments pages.
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade The Stars Rating plugin to version 3.5.1 or later; implement server-side input validation to restrict rating values to expected integer range (typically 1-5); apply numeric sanitization to all rating inputs using sanitize_text_field() or intval() with range checks; enable database query logging to identify problematic queries; implement rate limiting on rating submission endpoints; add request size limits; configure WordPress to log suspicious POST requests with malformed parameters.

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1059.008 - ESXi Administration Command
exploitation_method: Attacker gains privileged ESXi host access and executes arbitrary commands via esxcli, vim-cmd, or direct SSH shell to manipulate VMs, modify configurations, disable logging, or establish persistence on hypervisor
detection_pattern: Monitor for: esxcli commands with suspicious parameters (vm.process.kill, system.shutdown), unusual vim-cmd execution patterns, SSH sessions from non-administrative accounts, modification of /etc/ssh/sshd_config, clearing of /var/log/auth.log or vmkernel.log, unexpected privilege escalation to root, bulk VM state changes or network reconfiguration outside change windows, and dcui access logs showing unusual activity
mitigation_strategy: Implement: (1) Restrict SSH and DCUI access to approved IP ranges via firewall rules, (2) enforce strong authentication including MFA for ESXi accounts, (3) disable unnecessary services and default accounts, (4) deploy ESXi endpoint protection with behavioral monitoring, (5) configure centralized syslog forwarding with log aggregation to prevent local tampering, (6) enable audit logging and monitor command execution via vCenter audit events, (7) use role-based access control (RBAC) limiting command execution privileges, (8) conduct regular security audits of ESXi host configurations, (9) implement network segmentation for management traffic, (10) deploy file integrity monitoring on critical ESXi files

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1556.004 - Credential Access: Credentials from Password Stores (LSA Secrets)
exploitation_method: Attacker with SYSTEM privileges accesses the Local Security Authority (LSA) database stored in HKLM\SECURITY\Policy\Secrets to extract cached credentials, DPAPI-encrypted secrets, and service account passwords using tools like Mimikatz, secretsdump.py, or direct registry access
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Suspicious access to HKLM\SECURITY registry hive with READ permissions from non-system processes; (2) Execution of credential dumping tools (Mimikatz, secretsdump); (3) lsass.exe process being accessed by tools with unusual handles; (4) Event ID 4663 (Attempt to access object) on SECURITY hive; (5) Event ID 4688 showing credential access tools; (6) Unusual SYSTEM-level PowerShell/command-line activity accessing registry LSA paths; (7) DPAPI-related function calls (CryptUnprotectData) from suspicious processes
mitigation_strategy: Implement: (1) Enforce privileged access management (PAM) to minimize accounts with SYSTEM privileges; (2) Enable LSA protection via Registry key HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa\RunAsPPL and credential guard; (3) Restrict HKLM\SECURITY registry permissions to SYSTEM only; (4) Deploy Application Whitelisting to block Mimikatz and similar tools; (5) Monitor and alert on LSASS.exe access attempts; (6) Implement MFA to reduce credential reuse impact; (7) Use Windows Defender Credential Guard to isolate secrets; (8) Regular patching and disable unnecessary service accounts

threat_vector: Bluetooth link disconnection via duplicate BD address connection attempt
exploitation_method: Attacker initiates Bluetooth connection using spoofed BD address matching an already-connected device, causing the Bluetooth stack to improperly handle the duplicate address and disconnect the legitimate peer
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Rapid connection state transitions for same BD address, (2) Unexpected disconnection events preceded by connection attempts from duplicate addresses, (3) Bluetooth HCI logs showing 'Connection_Complete' or 'Disconnection_Complete' events with matching BD addresses in short time windows, (4) Device logs indicating 'duplicate device address detected' or similar warnings, (5) Anomalous increase in connection/disconnection cycles for specific paired devices
mitigation_strategy: Implement BD address validation before accepting connection requests - verify incoming connection BD address against active connection table and reject or queue duplicate address attempts; enforce Bluetooth stack firmware updates that properly validate connection state before processing new requests; implement connection state machine validation to prevent state transitions from valid peer addresses during active sessions; configure device logging to alert on connection attempts with duplicate addresses; consider Bluetooth connection filtering at the controller level to suppress duplicate address connection events during active sessions

threat_vector: mruby Heap-based Buffer Overflow
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious Ruby code or input that causes mruby's heap allocator to write beyond allocated buffer boundaries, typically through string operations, array manipulations, or deserialization of untrusted data that exceeds expected size constraints.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unexpected process termination with segmentation faults, heap corruption warnings from memory sanitizers, abnormal memory access patterns in mruby process, crashes during string/array operations with large inputs, ASLR bypass attempts, or heap metadata corruption detection in debugger output.
mitigation_strategy: Apply latest mruby security patches, implement input validation with strict size limits before passing to mruby, use AddressSanitizer (ASAN) in development/staging, enable heap protection mechanisms (ASLR, DEP/NX), run mruby in sandboxed environments with memory limits, conduct code review of custom mruby extensions, and implement fuzzing tests for buffer boundary conditions.

threat_vector: Modify or Spoof Tool UI
exploitation_method: Attacker modifies legitimate application UI elements (dialogs, windows, menus, notifications) or creates fake UI overlays to deceive users into performing unintended actions such as credential entry, authorization approval, or file operations. Common techniques include window title spoofing, fake UAC dialogs, phishing overlays, and cloned legitimate tool interfaces.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unexpected window creation from suspicious processes, mismatched window class names or styles, UI element modifications in running processes, anomalous API calls to CreateWindowEx or SetWindowText, browser DevTools console manipulation, unexpected overlay windows, process injections modifying UI handles, and user reports of unusual dialog appearance. Check for modified UI resource files and DLL injection into legitimate applications.
mitigation_strategy: Implement application whitelisting for UI-rendering processes, enforce code signing verification for legitimate applications, deploy UI Automation Access Controls to restrict unauthorized window manipulation, use Windows Defender Application Guard for high-risk applications, educate users on authentic UI characteristics, implement multi-factor authentication to reduce credential entry impact, monitor process behavior for UI API abuse, restrict DLL injection capabilities, and maintain updated legitimate application versions with integrity verification.

threat_vector: Cloud Instance Metadata API
exploitation_method: Attacker exploits misconfigured or unprotected metadata service (e.g., AWS IMDSv1, Azure IMDS) running on 169.254.169.254 to retrieve temporary credentials, API keys, or sensitive configuration data from within a compromised cloud instance or container
detection_pattern: Unusual HTTP requests to 169.254.169.254 or cloud-specific metadata endpoints; processes executing curl/wget/PowerShell commands targeting metadata service; repeated 401/403 responses followed by successful credential retrieval; outbound connections from containers or pods to metadata endpoints; environment variable exfiltration patterns; access logs showing x-aws-ec2-metadata-token or User-Data request patterns from unexpected processes
mitigation_strategy: Enforce IMDSv2 (token-based) on all cloud instances; implement network policies blocking metadata service access from containers/pods; use IAM policies restricting metadata API calls; deploy host-based controls preventing process execution of metadata requests; monitor and alert on metadata endpoint access; apply least-privilege credentials with short TTLs; disable metadata service where unnecessary; use VPC endpoints instead of direct service access

threat_vector: COMOS Web Components Authentication Bypass and Remote Code Execution
exploitation_method: Attackers exploit improper input validation in COMOS web components to bypass authentication mechanisms or inject malicious code. The vulnerability affects versions where web components are enabled, allowing unauthenticated access to sensitive functions or execution of arbitrary commands on the system.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: HTTP requests to COMOS web component endpoints lacking valid authentication tokens; unusual POST/PUT requests to admin or configuration endpoints; error logs showing '401 Unauthorized' followed by successful requests; suspicious parameters in web requests containing script tags or SQL injection patterns; unexpected process spawning from COMOS service accounts; abnormal network connections from COMOS application server to external hosts; repeated requests to sensitive URLs like /admin, /config, or /api endpoints without proper headers
mitigation_strategy: Immediately upgrade COMOS to patched versions (V10.3.3.3 or later, or appropriate V10.4 patch); disable web components if not required; implement Web Application Firewall (WAF) rules to validate COMOS requests; enforce strong authentication with multi-factor authentication; implement network segmentation to restrict COMOS access; monitor authentication logs and failed login attempts; disable default credentials; apply principle of least privilege to COMOS service accounts; implement input validation and sanitization; enable detailed logging for all web component interactions

threat_vector: HwNearbyMain module Data Processing Errors leading to process restart
exploitation_method: Attacker sends malformed or oversized data packets to HwNearbyMain service, triggering unhandled exceptions in data parsing routines that cause the process to crash and restart, potentially enabling denial of service or privilege escalation during restart
detection_pattern: Monitor for: repeated HwNearbyMain process crashes in system logs, segmentation faults or access violations in HwNearbyMain, abnormal memory access patterns preceding crashes, unexpected restart cycles of HwNearbyMain service, malformed packets received on Nearby communication ports (typically UDP 5353 or Bluetooth channels), error codes indicating parsing failures in device logs
mitigation_strategy: Apply security patches for HwNearbyMain module; implement input validation and bounds checking for all data processing routines; add exception handling for malformed packet scenarios; restrict network access to HwNearbyMain service using firewall rules; enable process crash monitoring with automatic rate-limiting to prevent restart loops; implement packet size limits and timeout mechanisms; run HwNearbyMain with minimal required privileges using sandboxing or SELinux contexts

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1566.001: Phishing - Spearphishing Attachment
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts personalized email with malicious attachment (executable, macro-enabled document, or archive) targeting specific organization or individual, leveraging social engineering to convince recipient to open/execute the file
detection_pattern: Email gateway alerts on executable/suspicious attachments; unusual sender reputation or domain spoofing; macro execution attempts in Office documents; process execution from temporary directories (%TEMP%, Downloads); sudden file modification to known-good executables; EDR alerts on unsigned binaries launched from email clients; abnormal network connections from user machines shortly after attachment execution
mitigation_strategy: Implement email gateway filtering (block executable extensions, scan macros); enforce Office macro security policies (disable by default); deploy email authentication (SPF, DKIM, DMARC); user security awareness training with simulated phishing campaigns; endpoint controls (application whitelisting, disable Office macros in untrusted locations); advanced threat protection with sandbox analysis; isolate email attachments in secure environments before opening; require manual approval for macro execution

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1592.004 - Gather Victim Org Info: Identify Business Tempo
exploitation_method: Attacker analyzes publicly available information (social media, news releases, earnings calls, conference schedules, employee activity patterns) to map organizational operating hours, peak activity periods, incident response capability windows, and security team shift schedules to identify optimal timing for attacks when defenses are minimal or response is delayed
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unusual external reconnaissance of business calendars/schedules, bulk collection of public statements about operational windows, social engineering queries targeting business hours, web scraping of company event announcements, LinkedIn activity mapping employee locations/time zones, analysis of security team public statements or conference attendance patterns, correlation of attack timing with identified low-activity periods
mitigation_strategy: Implement 24/7 security operations center coverage regardless of business hours; randomize incident response team schedules; limit public disclosure of organizational events, security staff identities, and operational calendars; establish consistent monitoring and alerting across all time zones; conduct regular security awareness training on operational security (OPSEC) for employees; use threat deception/honeypots to detect reconnaissance activity; enforce strict information governance on publicly shared organizational planning documents; monitor dark web/forums for discussion of organizational schedules

threat_vector: Insecure file permissions on config files
exploitation_method: Attacker reads sensitive configuration files (database credentials, API keys, secrets) by exploiting overly permissive file access controls (world-readable or group-readable permissions on files like .env, config.xml, settings.ini, or database connection strings)
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) File permission audits showing config files with mode 644, 666, or world-readable bits set; (2) Unauthorized file access attempts in audit logs (auditd, Windows Event ID 4663); (3) Process access to config files outside normal application initialization; (4) Configuration file reads by non-service accounts or unexpected users
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict file permissions: config files should be readable only by owner (600) and service account if necessary (640 maximum); use automated tools to enforce and alert on permission drift; store secrets in dedicated vaults (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager) rather than plaintext files; run services with minimal privilege principles; implement file integrity monitoring (AIDE, Tripwire, OSSEC) to detect permission changes; conduct regular permission audits on all configuration files

threat_vector: Apache James IMAP LIST command ReDoS via crafted mailbox names
exploitation_method: Attacker sends specially crafted IMAP LIST commands with mailbox names containing patterns that trigger catastrophic backtracking in the regular expression used for mailbox name parsing. This causes excessive CPU consumption and server unresponsiveness.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) IMAP LIST commands with unusually long or complex mailbox name patterns containing repetitive special characters; (2) CPU spikes correlated with specific IMAP sessions; (3) Regex evaluation timeouts in James logs; (4) Multiple LIST commands from same user with patterns like '.*.*.*' or similar backtracking-prone constructs; (5) IMAP session hangs or timeouts
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Apache James to patched version; implement regex timeout limits in IMAP command handlers; validate and sanitize mailbox name patterns before regex processing; use possessive quantifiers or atomic groups in mailbox name regex; implement rate limiting on IMAP LIST commands per user; monitor regex evaluation time and kill queries exceeding threshold; consider using compiled regex with explicit complexity bounds

threat_vector: Arbitrary File Read via NavigateCMS 2.9 /navigate/navigate_download.php id Parameter
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious requests to /navigate/navigate_download.php with manipulated 'id' parameter containing path traversal sequences (../) or absolute paths to access sensitive files outside intended directory scope, such as configuration files, source code, or system files.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: HTTP requests to /navigate/navigate_download.php with id parameters containing '../', '..\', encoded traversal sequences (%2e%2e%2f), absolute paths (/etc/, C:\), or unusual file extensions (.conf, .php, .sql, .env); access logs showing requests for files outside expected application directories; unexpected file read operations by the web process; unusual patterns in web server access logs with multiple failed or successful requests to navigate_download.php with varying id values.
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict input validation on the 'id' parameter using allowlist-based validation accepting only expected alphanumeric identifiers; apply proper path canonicalization before file access; restrict file access to a specific whitelist directory with chroot/jail isolation; enforce principle of least privilege on web server process file permissions; upgrade NavigateCMS to patched version; implement Web Application Firewall (WAF) rules to block path traversal patterns; conduct security code review of download functionality and implement proper access controls checking user permissions before file delivery.

threat_vector: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation (Cross-site Scripting) in Hoppscotch before 2.1.1
exploitation_method: Attacker injects malicious JavaScript code through unvalidated input fields (request parameters, headers, body content, or URL parameters) that are rendered directly into the DOM without proper HTML encoding or sanitization, allowing arbitrary script execution in users' browsers
detection_pattern: Monitor for: script tags in request/response inspection panels, event handlers in dynamically rendered content, console errors from CSP violations, unusual DOM mutations, requests containing javascript: protocol, data: URIs with script content, or HTML entity patterns suggesting bypass attempts (e.g., <script>, \x3cscript\x3e)
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Hoppscotch to version 2.1.1 or later; implement strict Content Security Policy (CSP) with script-src 'nonce' or 'strict-dynamic'; sanitize all user input using DOMPurify or similar library before DOM insertion; use textContent instead of innerHTML for dynamic content; apply HTML entity encoding to all user-controlled output; implement input validation whitelists for request parameters; enable X-XSS-Protection and X-Content-Type-Options headers

threat_vector: Fast Flux DNS
exploitation_method: Attacker rapidly changes DNS A records pointing to multiple compromised hosts (flux agents) to evade detection and maintain command & control infrastructure. Uses short TTL values to force frequent DNS lookups, distributing traffic across botnet nodes and complicating takedown efforts.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: multiple DNS A record changes for same domain within short timeframe (minutes/hours), unusually low TTL values (< 5 minutes), single domain resolving to large number of distinct IPs (>10-50), geographic clustering of resolved IPs to hosting/proxy services, DNS query volume spikes for specific domains, whois registrant changes paired with rapid IP rotation
mitigation_strategy: Implement DNS sinkhole/blackhole for known fast flux domains; deploy DNS query logging and analytics to detect unusual resolution patterns; maintain threat intel feeds on known botnet C2 domains; configure firewall rules to block multiple connections from same domain across diverse IPs; use passive DNS historical data to identify flux patterns; enforce DNS filtering with reputation-based blocking; implement DNSSEC validation; segment network to prevent lateral movement from compromised hosts; monitor outbound DNS requests for anomalous query rates

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Screen Capture (T1113)
exploitation_method: Attacker executes malware or leverages legitimate tools (PrintScreen API, Windows.Graphics.Capture, or command-line utilities like gnome-screenshot) to capture screen content containing sensitive information, credentials, or confidential data displayed on victim's monitor
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unexpected calls to GdiDibCreate, BitBlt, or GetDC APIs; suspicious processes accessing Graphics.Capture namespace; uncommon use of screenshot tools by non-admin users; file creation in temp directories with image extensions immediately after user activity; process injection into explorer.exe or winlogon.exe; high-frequency screen capture API calls; unusual child processes spawned by office applications or browsers
mitigation_strategy: Implement application whitelisting to restrict screenshot utilities; enable Windows Defender Application Guard for sensitive environments; use Data Loss Prevention (DLP) policies to monitor screen capture activity and block suspicious processes; deploy EDR solutions with behavioral analysis; restrict API access via AppLocker rules; enforce screen privacy filters for physical security; educate users on securing sensitive data display; implement privileged access workstations (PAW) for high-value users; monitor process creation and image loading through Sysmon Event ID 10 (CreateRemoteThread) and API call logging

threat_vector: Hilinksvc Data Processing Errors vulnerability leading to DoS
exploitation_method: Attacker sends malformed or oversized data packets to Hilinksvc service, triggering unhandled exceptions in data processing routines that cause service crash or resource exhaustion
detection_pattern: Monitor for: repeated Hilinksvc process crashes, abnormal memory/CPU spikes before crashes, error logs containing 'data processing exception' or 'unhandled exception', sudden increase in malformed packets to Hilinksvc ports, service restart events in quick succession
mitigation_strategy: Apply latest Hilinksvc security patches, implement input validation and sanitization for all data streams, configure process restart policies with rate limiting, set resource limits (memory/CPU) per process, deploy network segmentation to restrict Hilinksvc exposure, enable detailed service logging and alerting on crash events

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Container and Resource Discovery (T1526)
exploitation_method: Attacker executes commands within compromised container or host to enumerate running containers, cluster resources, Kubernetes API endpoints, container registries, and resource quotas using tools like kubectl, docker ps, crictl, or cloud provider CLIs to map the attack surface
detection_pattern: Monitor for: kubectl/docker/crictl commands with list, describe, or get subcommands; unusual API calls to container runtimes; repeated failed authentication attempts to Kubernetes API; commands querying container metadata services; process execution of cloud CLI tools (aws-cli, gcloud) with describe/list parameters; high volume of API discovery requests; anomalous access to /var/run/docker.sock or CRI socket files
mitigation_strategy: Implement RBAC policies restricting API discovery permissions; disable or restrict container runtime socket access; use network policies to limit API server communication; deploy runtime security monitoring (Falco, Sysdig) to alert on reconnaissance commands; isolate container runtimes behind authentication layers; audit all API server requests; restrict kubectl RBAC verbs to 'list' and 'get' only where necessary; implement pod security standards preventing privileged containers; use service account tokens with minimal permissions; monitor and log all container introspection attempts

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1574.011: Services Registry Permissions Weakness
exploitation_method: Attacker exploits weak DACL (Discretionary Access Control List) permissions on service registry keys (HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\*) to modify ImagePath, Parameters, or other service configurations, enabling privilege escalation by forcing privileged services to execute arbitrary code
detection_pattern: Monitor for: Registry modification events on HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\* with Event ID 4657 (Registry value modified) or Event ID 4656 (Registry object access), unexpected changes to ImagePath or Start values, service binary path alterations pointing to non-standard locations, weak ACLs detected via tools like accesschk.exe showing non-admin write permissions on service keys
mitigation_strategy: Restrict DACL permissions on all service registry keys to SYSTEM and Administrators only using icacls or Group Policy; deploy AppLocker/WDAC rules blocking unauthorized service image paths; enable Registry auditing on HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services with alerting on write events; regularly audit service registry ACLs via automated security scanning; implement principle of least privilege for service accounts; use Windows Defender for Endpoint to detect suspicious service modifications

threat_vector: Improper access control vulnerability in S Assistant prior to version 7.5 allows remote unauthorized access to sensitive information
exploitation_method: Attacker bypasses authentication or authorization checks by sending crafted requests to unprotected API endpoints or resources that lack proper role-based access control (RBAC). The vulnerability allows direct access to sensitive data without valid credentials or with insufficient permission validation.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unauthorized HTTP requests to API endpoints with 200/201 responses despite missing/invalid authentication tokens; (2) Access logs showing requests to /api/sensitive, /api/admin, or /data endpoints from non-whitelisted IPs; (3) Unusual data exfiltration patterns or large bulk requests to information endpoints; (4) Repeated 401/403 errors followed by successful 200 responses indicating permission bypass; (5) Access to S Assistant versions <7.5 without proper patch level detection
mitigation_strategy: Immediately: (1) Upgrade all S Assistant instances to version 7.5 or later; (2) Enforce mandatory authentication on all API endpoints using OAuth2/JWT tokens; (3) Implement strict RBAC with principle of least privilege; (4) Add request signing and rate limiting to sensitive endpoints; (5) Deploy WAF rules to block requests lacking valid authentication headers; (6) Enable comprehensive access logging and audit trails; (7) Conduct data access review to identify if compromise occurred; (8) Implement network segmentation to limit lateral movement from affected systems

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1110.001 - Brute Force: Password Guessing
exploitation_method: Attacker systematically attempts common passwords and variations against user accounts through authentication interfaces (SSH, RDP, web portals, APIs) without rate limiting, leveraging weak password policies and lack of account lockout mechanisms
detection_pattern: Multiple failed authentication attempts from single source IP within short timeframe; sequences of 5+ failed logins to different/same accounts within minutes; authentication logs showing pattern of common passwords (password123, admin, letmein); spike in 4625 events (Windows failed login) or sshd authentication failures; geographically impossible login attempts
mitigation_strategy: Implement account lockout policy (lock after 5 failed attempts for 30 minutes); enforce strong password requirements (12+ characters, complexity); deploy rate limiting on authentication endpoints; require MFA on all critical accounts; implement CAPTCHA after 3 failed attempts; monitor and alert on failed login thresholds; use fail2ban or similar tools to auto-block IPs with repeated failures; conduct regular password spraying assessments; educate users on password manager usage

threat_vector: Rogue backends causing Denial of Service of guests via high frequency events in Xen hypervisor
exploitation_method: A malicious or compromised backend domain generates excessive event notifications to guest virtual machines, overwhelming their event processing capabilities and consuming CPU resources, resulting in guest unavailability or performance degradation
detection_pattern: Monitor for: abnormally high event ring occupancy rates, sustained elevation in events per second from specific backend domains exceeding baseline by >10x, guest CPU usage spikes correlating with event storm patterns, event queue saturation events in hypervisor logs, repeated EVTCHN_SEND operations from untrusted domains
mitigation_strategy: Implement per-domain event rate limiting and throttling at the hypervisor level; establish maximum event frequency thresholds per backend-guest channel pair; deploy event filtering rules in dom0; use Xen's credit scheduler to isolate misbehaving domains; enable event notification validation; regularly audit backend domain privileges and isolate trusted backends in separate security domains

threat_vector: COMOS Web Component Remote Code Execution (CVE-2024-28235)
exploitation_method: Attackers exploit unpatched COMOS versions with web components enabled by sending specially crafted HTTP requests to vulnerable endpoints. The vulnerability allows unauthenticated remote code execution through deserialization of untrusted data or template injection in web interfaces, bypassing input validation mechanisms.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unusual HTTP POST/PUT requests to COMOS web component endpoints with suspicious payloads, (2) Error logs showing deserialization exceptions or template processing errors, (3) Unexpected process spawning from COMOS service accounts, (4) Network connections from COMOS processes to external IP addresses, (5) Web server access logs with requests containing encoded payloads or script tags, (6) File modifications in COMOS directories with unexpected timestamps
mitigation_strategy: Immediate: Disable web components if not required; isolate affected COMOS systems on network segment with strict egress filtering. Short-term: Apply available security patches (V10.3.3.3+, V10.4.x latest), implement WAF rules blocking malicious payloads, enable request logging. Long-term: Upgrade to patched versions, implement network segmentation, deploy intrusion detection, conduct security audit of COMOS configurations, establish patch management schedule for all dependencies

threat_vector: net/http HTTP/2 header canonicalization cache memory exhaustion (CVE-2021-44716)
exploitation_method: Attacker sends rapid HTTP/2 requests with unique header field names that aren't in the canonicalization cache, forcing the server to allocate unbounded memory for cache entries. Each unique header combination creates a new cache entry without size limits, eventually exhausting available memory and causing denial of service.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: rapid increase in process memory usage during HTTP/2 request handling; excessive entries in golang runtime memory profiles in the http2 header caching subsystem; connection logs showing repeated requests with variations in header field names (case variations, rare headers); abnormal heap growth patterns specifically in net/http package allocations
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Go to version 1.16.12, 1.17.5, or later where header canonicalization cache size is bounded. Implement request rate limiting at the load balancer level for HTTP/2 connections; set resource limits (ulimits, container memory caps) on Go processes; deploy reverse proxy (nginx, Envoy) with HTTP/2 header validation and limits; monitor process memory and alert on threshold breaches; disable HTTP/2 support if not required and revert to HTTP/1.1

threat_vector: Path Traversal / Directory Traversal in Opmantek Open-AudIT 4.2.0 - Authenticated File Read
exploitation_method: Authenticated attacker crafts malicious file path requests using traversal sequences (../, ..\, or URL encoding variants) in application parameters that handle file operations. The application fails to properly validate or sanitize file paths, allowing access to files outside the intended restricted directory such as /etc/passwd, configuration files, or other sensitive system files.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) HTTP requests containing encoded or literal traversal sequences in URL parameters or POST data (../, %2e%2e, ..\); (2) File access attempts targeting paths outside expected application directories in application or system logs; (3) Unusual file I/O patterns from the web service process accessing system directories; (4) Access logs showing requests to sensitive file paths like /etc, /config, /admin with traversal payloads; (5) Repeated 403/404 responses followed by successful 200 responses with path traversal patterns
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict input validation using whitelist-based path validation; canonicalize all file paths using native OS functions and verify they resolve within allowed directories; use chroot/jailed environments; apply principle of least privilege to web service process; disable directory listing; update to patched version of Opmantek Open-AudIT beyond 4.2.0; implement Web Application Firewall (WAF) rules blocking traversal patterns; enforce strong access controls and audit file access permissions; monitor and log all file operations with authenticated user context

threat_vector: Memory corruption via invalid free operations in uriparser < 0.9.6 uriFreeUriMembers and uriMakeOwner functions
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malformed URI input that triggers double-free or use-after-free conditions when uriparser processes and deallocates URI structure members, potentially causing heap corruption or code execution
detection_pattern: Monitor for: application crashes with SIGSEGV/SIGABRT in uriparser library calls; heap corruption detected by ASAN/valgrind tools; unexpected segmentation faults correlating with URI parsing operations; memory sanitizer warnings during application startup or URI processing; abnormal process termination in uriparser-dependent services
mitigation_strategy: Immediately upgrade uriparser to version 0.9.6 or later; implement input validation and sanitization for all URI inputs before passing to uriparser; enable memory safety tools (ASAN, UBSAN) in development/testing; apply compiler hardening flags (-fstack-protector-strong, -D_FORTIFY_SOURCE=2); consider alternative URI parsing libraries with better security track records; implement strict bounds checking on URI length and component validation

threat_vector: Memory leakage vulnerability in smartphone allowing memory exhaustion
exploitation_method: Attacker triggers repeated allocation of memory objects without proper deallocation through vulnerable code paths. Common vectors include: malicious app installation, processing of specially crafted media files, or triggering specific legitimate app workflows that fail to release heap/native memory. Over time, memory consumption increases until system becomes unresponsive or crashes.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: sustained increase in memory usage by single process without corresponding decrease during idle periods; out-of-memory (OOM) killer events in system logs; repeated warnings from memory manager; app crashes with 'OutOfMemoryError' or 'E_OUTOFMEMORY'; abnormal growth in 'PSS' (Proportional Set Size) metrics; system slowdown correlating with specific app activity; logcat/system logs showing memory pressure warnings: 'onTrimMemory()', 'MemoryWarning', or low-memory states
mitigation_strategy: 1) Implement strict memory lifecycle management with automated leak detection tools (LeakCanary, Address Sanitizer) during development; 2) Enforce code review for proper resource cleanup and use of try-with-resources; 3) Deploy memory monitoring at OS level with aggressive low-memory thresholds; 4) Implement app sandboxing and memory limits per process; 5) Deploy security patches and OS updates promptly; 6) Monitor app store for vulnerable versions and push updates; 7) Use memory-safe languages/frameworks where possible; 8) Implement heap hardening and ASLR protections

threat_vector: Pointer Dereference Vulnerability in GPAC 1.0.1 gf_list_count function
exploitation_method: Attacker passes a NULL pointer or improperly initialized list structure to gf_list_count(), causing the function to dereference an invalid memory address without proper null-pointer checks, resulting in a crash or undefined behavior
detection_pattern: Monitor for segmentation faults or access violations in GPAC processes; observe null pointer dereference exceptions in system logs; detect abnormal termination of GPAC worker threads with SIGSEGV signals; track crash dumps referencing gf_list_count in the call stack; identify repeated process exits correlated with specific media file processing attempts
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade GPAC to version 1.0.2 or later containing the patch; implement input validation to ensure list pointers are properly initialized before calling gf_list_count(); add null-pointer checks at function entry; enable Address Sanitizer (ASan) during development and testing; deploy exception handlers to gracefully catch dereference faults; restrict GPAC processes to minimal privilege levels and isolated environments; implement sandboxing to limit DoS impact scope

threat_vector: Improper authorization vulnerability in Galaxy Store prior to 4.5.36.5 allows remote app installation of the allowlist
exploitation_method: Attacker bypasses authorization checks in Galaxy Store versions before 4.5.36.5 to remotely trigger installation of applications that should be restricted by device allowlist policies. This exploits insufficient validation of installation requests against configured security policies.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Installation events of apps not present in device allowlist policies; (2) Galaxy Store process initiating installations without matching user consent logs; (3) APK installation requests from Galaxy Store lacking proper authorization headers; (4) Unexpected app installations occurring during non-business hours or from non-standard Galaxy Store API calls; (5) Device policy violation logs indicating allowlist bypass; (6) Network traffic from Galaxy Store to installation endpoints with invalid authorization tokens
mitigation_strategy: Update Galaxy Store to version 4.5.36.5 or later immediately. Implement mandatory Mobile Device Management (MDM) policies enforcing: (1) Allowlist-only installation mode with admin approval required; (2) Disable remote installation capabilities if not required; (3) Regular security audits of app installation logs; (4) Network segmentation to restrict Galaxy Store API endpoints; (5) Monitor for unauthorized installation attempts via EMM solutions; (6) Enforce code signing validation for all installation packages

threat_vector: Clickjacking vulnerability in Ultimaker S3/S5/3D printer local webserver (versions through 6.3 and 5.2.16)
exploitation_method: Attacker creates malicious webpage with transparent overlay or frame that tricks users into clicking UI elements on the printer's local webserver interface. Missing X-Frame-Options or Content-Security-Policy headers allow embedding of printer control interface in attacker-controlled pages, enabling unauthorized settings modification or device control.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) HTTP responses from printer webserver lacking 'X-Frame-Options' header or CSP frame-ancestors directive, (2) Unusual cross-origin requests to printer's local IP address with embedded frames, (3) Legitimate local requests followed by unexpected configuration changes, (4) Browser console warnings about frame embedding, (5) Printer logs showing settings changes without corresponding UI access from authenticated administrator
mitigation_strategy: Implement and enforce X-Frame-Options: DENY header on all webserver responses. Deploy Content-Security-Policy header with frame-ancestors 'none' directive. Apply firmware update to latest patched version (6.4+). Restrict printer webserver access to trusted network segments via firewall rules. Implement authentication token rotation and CSRF protection. Use HTTP-only and Secure flags on session cookies. Monitor for clickjacking via SIEM alerting on suspicious configuration change patterns.

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1110 - Brute Force: Password Cracking
exploitation_method: Attacker uses automated tools to systematically test multiple password combinations against user accounts through login interfaces, network protocols (SSH, RDP), or offline hash cracking against stolen credential databases
detection_pattern: Multiple failed authentication attempts from single source IP within short timeframe; rapid sequential login failures for same user account; unusual spike in authentication errors; offline detection of cracked hashes in use through breach databases; unusual process execution of cracking tools (hashcat, John the Ripper, Hydra); high-frequency LDAP/Active Directory queries; failed SSH/RDP connection logs with varying usernames or passwords
mitigation_strategy: Implement account lockout policies after N failed attempts; enforce strong password policies (minimum 12 characters, complexity requirements); deploy MFA/2FA for all critical accounts; use rate limiting on authentication endpoints; implement CAPTCHA after failed attempts; monitor and alert on multiple failed logins; hash passwords using bcrypt/Argon2; segment networks to limit protocol access; deploy honeypot accounts to detect scanning; conduct regular security awareness training; use passwordless authentication methods where possible

threat_vector: Type Confusion in MPTCP Subsystem - Resource Access Vulnerability
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malformed MPTCP packets with mismatched type declarations in packet headers or socket structures, causing the kernel to interpret data buffers as incompatible types. This allows reading/writing memory regions intended for different resource types, potentially leading to information disclosure or privilege escalation.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) MPTCP socket allocation failures followed by abnormal memory access patterns; (2) Kernel logs showing type mismatch warnings in MPTCP handlers; (3) Unexpected MPTCP subflow creation with conflicting address families; (4) Crashes in mptcp_subflow_create() or type-checking functions; (5) Memory sanitizer reports of heap buffer overflows in MPTCP data structures; (6) Consecutive failed MPTCP connection attempts with varying packet structures
mitigation_strategy: Apply kernel patches that add runtime type validation in MPTCP subsystem; implement strict type checking before resource access in subflow handlers; enable CONFIG_KASAN (Kernel Address Sanitizer) to detect type confusion at runtime; restrict MPTCP usage via sysctl controls; monitor and update to patched kernel versions (Android security patches, Linux MPTCP fixes); deploy SELinux/AppArmor policies limiting MPTCP socket operations; use network segmentation to limit MPTCP traffic exposure

threat_vector: libjxl b02d6b9 out-of-bounds write in ModularFrameDecoder::DecodeGroup
exploitation_method: Attacker supplies malformed JXL image file with crafted frame data that triggers buffer overflow in DecodeGroup function during AC group processing. Insufficient bounds checking allows write operations beyond allocated buffer boundaries, potentially enabling arbitrary code execution or memory corruption.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: segmentation faults or access violations in libjxl/libvips processes; abnormal memory allocation patterns followed by write operations outside bounds; heap corruption indicators (malloc/free anomalies); crashes in jxl::FrameDecoder::ProcessACGroup or jxl::ModularFrameDecoder::DecodeGroup stack traces; processes handling untrusted JXL files showing excessive memory access violations
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade libjxl to patched version (post-b02d6b9); update libvips to 8.11.3 or later; implement strict input validation for JXL files before processing; run image processing in sandboxed/containerized environments with memory protection; disable JXL support if unused; implement AddressSanitizer (ASan) in development/staging for early detection; apply principle of least privilege to image processing services

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1552.005 - Cloud Instance Metadata API
exploitation_method: Attacker queries cloud metadata service (AWS IMDSv1, Azure IMDS, GCP metadata server) from compromised instance or container to retrieve temporary credentials, SSH keys, role information, and configuration data without authentication. Common attack exploits SSRF vulnerabilities or direct HTTP requests to 169.254.169.254 or equivalent endpoints.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unusual HTTP requests to 169.254.169.254 or cloud-specific metadata endpoints from application processes; (2) Excessive or repeated metadata API calls with credential-related paths (/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials); (3) Anomalous outbound connections from containers or serverless functions; (4) CloudTrail/equivalent logs showing credential usage from unexpected sources shortly after metadata queries; (5) Process execution of curl/wget targeting metadata endpoints; (6) Failed IMDSv2 token acquisition attempts followed by IMDSv1 fallback requests.
mitigation_strategy: Implement: (1) Enforce IMDSv2 with require-imdsv2 tokens (one-hop, time-limited); (2) Disable unnecessary metadata service access via network policies; (3) Restrict metadata API calls at network level using security groups/NSGs to only required services; (4) Use instance/pod IAM roles with minimal permissions (least privilege); (5) Enable VPC endpoint policies to restrict metadata service access; (6) Implement Web Application Firewall rules to block SSRF attempts; (7) Deploy container-level egress filtering to prevent metadata service communication; (8) Monitor and alert on metadata API access patterns; (9) Rotate credentials regularly; (10) Use workload identity federation instead of credentials where available.

threat_vector: Stored XSS via Unsanitized Parameters in UpdraftPlus WordPress Backup Plugin < 1.16.66
exploitation_method: Attacker with admin/contributor access injects malicious JavaScript into backup_timestamp or job_id parameters. These values are stored in the database and later rendered without sanitization in admin pages, executing arbitrary JavaScript in admin context when the page loads.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: 1) POST requests to UpdraftPlus admin pages with encoded script tags or event handlers in backup_timestamp/job_id parameters; 2) Database queries containing script tags in wp_options or UpdraftPlus-related tables; 3) Admin page responses containing unescaped user input from these parameters; 4) Browser console errors indicating injected scripts; 5) Audit logs showing parameter modifications with special characters (<, >, quotes, parentheses)
mitigation_strategy: 1) Upgrade UpdraftPlus to version 1.16.66 or later; 2) Implement Content Security Policy (CSP) headers to restrict inline script execution; 3) Apply input validation: whitelist allowed characters for backup_timestamp (numeric/dash format) and job_id (alphanumeric); 4) Use wp_kses_post() or esc_html() when outputting these parameters; 5) Conduct security audit of all user input handling; 6) Restrict admin access using role-based controls; 7) Enable security headers (X-XSS-Protection, X-Content-Type-Options)

threat_vector: Booster for WooCommerce plugin (< 5.4.9) - Unsanitized wcj_create_products_xml_result parameter reflected in admin dashboard
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious URL with XSS payload in wcj_create_products_xml_result parameter. When admin visits the link, unsanitized parameter is reflected directly into HTML output without proper escaping, allowing arbitrary JavaScript execution in admin context
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) HTTP requests containing wcj_create_products_xml_result parameter with script tags or event handlers, (2) Admin dashboard responses containing unescaped parameter values in HTML context, (3) WAF logs showing XSS payloads in query strings targeting wp-admin paths, (4) Admin activity logs showing unusual actions following dashboard access with suspicious parameters
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Booster for WooCommerce to version 5.4.9 or later. Implement input validation to sanitize wcj_create_products_xml_result parameter using sanitize_text_field() or similar, apply output escaping using esc_html(), esc_attr(), or esc_url() when displaying parameter values in admin context. Deploy WAF rules to block requests with XSS patterns in plugin parameters. Use WordPress security plugins to monitor/log admin access with suspicious query parameters

threat_vector: COMOS Web Component Remote Code Execution (CVE-2024-XXXXX) - Path traversal and code injection in web-based components affecting COMOS V10.2 (all versions with web components), V10.3 (versions < V10.3.3.3 with web components)
exploitation_method: Attacker sends crafted HTTP requests with path traversal payloads (../ sequences) to bypass access controls on web components, then injects malicious code (JavaScript/server-side code) through unvalidated input parameters to achieve remote code execution on the COMOS server
