detection_pattern: Monitor for: sustained high memory/CPU usage from display processes, repeated allocation failures, growing heap size without deallocation, excessive GPU memory usage, display module crash dumps, system performance degradation, log entries showing failed memory allocations or resource limits exceeded, process handle/file descriptor leaks in display subsystem
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict resource quotas and limits per display rendering request (memory caps, timeout thresholds, max buffer sizes). Add input validation for display parameters with range/length checks. Deploy resource monitoring with automatic termination of runaway processes. Use memory pooling with pre-allocated fixed-size buffers. Implement request rate limiting and queue depth controls. Apply principle of least privilege to display module process permissions. Regularly audit and patch display rendering libraries.

threat_vector: wolfSSL wolfMQTT 1.9 heap-based buffer overflow in MqttDecode_Disconnect function
exploitation_method: Attacker sends malformed MQTT DISCONNECT packet with oversized payload that exceeds allocated heap buffer during deserialization, causing heap corruption and potential code execution through heap spray or ROP gadget chains
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) MQTT protocol fuzzing/malformed packets from external sources, (2) heap corruption signals (double-free, use-after-free), (3) segmentation faults in MqttDecode_Disconnect or MqttClient_DecodePacket stack traces, (4) unexpected process termination with SIGSEGV, (5) abnormal memory access patterns via ASAN or Valgrind, (6) incoming MQTT packets with DISCONNECT type (0xE0) containing payload length exceeding protocol specification (should be 0 bytes for standard DISCONNECT)
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade wolfMQTT to version 1.10 or later with heap overflow patch; implement input validation to reject DISCONNECT packets with non-zero payload length; deploy bounds checking in packet deserialization; use heap canaries and ASLR; run application with memory sanitizers (ASAN) in development; implement network-level packet inspection to filter malformed MQTT frames; run service in sandboxed environment with memory protection

threat_vector: Integer overflow in MDP driver memory allocation leading to heap buffer overflow and local privilege escalation
exploitation_method: Attacker provides crafted input parameters to MDP driver that cause integer overflow in size calculation, resulting in undersized heap buffer allocation. Subsequent memory writes overflow the buffer, enabling arbitrary kernel memory corruption and privilege escalation.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) MDP driver ioctl calls with unusually large dimension/size parameters; (2) Kernel memory allocation failures followed by continued write operations; (3) Heap corruption detection via KASAN/SLUB poisoning violations; (4) Unexpected kernel crashes with memory corruption signatures; (5) Process capability elevation without corresponding privilege escalation syscalls; (6) Audit logs showing unprivileged process accessing /dev/mdp or similar device nodes with suspicious parameters
mitigation_strategy: Implement integer overflow checks before all size calculations in MDP driver using safe arithmetic functions; validate input parameters against maximum reasonable bounds; use compiler-based protections (CFI, UBSAN); enable kernel memory sanitizers (KASAN) in production; restrict MDP device node access via SELinux/AppArmor policies; keep driver and kernel patched; implement capability-based access control on device file; perform static analysis and fuzzing of driver ioctl handlers

threat_vector: IBM Security Verify 10.0.0, 10.0.1.0, and 10.0.2.0 sensitive version information disclosure in HTTP response headers (IBM X-Force ID: 212038)
exploitation_method: Attacker performs HTTP requests to IBM Security Verify endpoints and examines response headers (Server, X-Powered-By, X-AspNet-Version, or custom headers) to identify exact product version and build number, enabling reconnaissance for known CVEs and targeted attack vectors
detection_pattern: Monitor HTTP response headers for Server/version disclosures; log inspection of responses containing 'IBM Security Verify', version strings like '10.0.0', '10.0.1.0', '10.0.2.0'; identify anomalous header scanning patterns or automated reconnaissance tools (User-Agent analysis, rapid sequential requests to multiple endpoints, tools like Nmap, Nikto, or custom scanners)
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade to patched IBM Security Verify versions beyond 10.0.2.0; implement HTTP header suppression/modification via reverse proxy or WAF to remove identifying headers (Server, X-Powered-By, X-AspNet-Version); configure web server to return generic or misleading version information; implement rate limiting on HTTP requests; monitor and alert on reconnaissance activity patterns; apply principle of least privilege to information exposure

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Domain Groups (T1136.002)
exploitation_method: Attacker with domain admin credentials creates new Active Directory groups to establish persistence, escalate privileges, or maintain backdoor access. Groups are added to sensitive organizational units and granted permissions to critical resources.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: new group creation events (Event ID 4731 on domain controllers), unexpected group membership changes (Event ID 4732/4733), groups created outside normal business hours or by unusual accounts, creation of groups with suspicious names mimicking legitimate groups, rapid group creation patterns
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict access controls limiting group creation to authorized admins, enable MFA for privileged accounts, audit and log all Active Directory changes, use DNS and credential guard to prevent lateral movement, regularly review domain group membership and permissions, implement just-in-time admin access with time-limited elevation, disable default group creation for non-administrative users

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1199 - Trusted Relationship: SharePoint Access Abuse
exploitation_method: Attacker leverages compromised user credentials or misconfigured permissions to access SharePoint repositories, exfiltrate sensitive documents, plant malicious files, or modify content through legitimate authentication channels
detection_pattern: Unusual bulk document downloads from SharePoint, access to files outside normal user permissions scope, downloads from multiple document libraries in short timeframe, after-hours SharePoint access, failed permission-denied attempts followed by successful access, anomalous IP geolocation for SharePoint user sessions, mass file copies to external sharing links
mitigation_strategy: Implement SharePoint-specific RBAC with principle of least privilege, enable audit logging for document access and sharing, configure conditional access policies based on risk factors (anomalous location/time), monitor and restrict external sharing permissions, enforce multi-factor authentication for SharePoint access, regularly audit user permissions and remove unnecessary access, implement data loss prevention (DLP) policies to block exfiltration, segment SharePoint sites by sensitivity level, conduct user activity analytics to establish baselines

threat_vector: Pointer Dereference Vulnerability in GPAC 1.0.1 gf_isom_box_size function
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts a malformed ISO Base Media File Format (MP4/MOV) with specially constructed box structures that cause null or invalid pointer dereference in gf_isom_box_size function during parsing, leading to application crash or memory access violation
detection_pattern: Monitor for: GPAC process segmentation faults or access violations when parsing media files; abnormal termination with SIGSEGV signals; core dumps referencing gf_isom_box_size stack frames; malformed box headers with zero or invalid size values in ISOM containers; file format validation failures before processing
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade GPAC to patched version (>1.0.1); implement input validation for box size and structure before dereferencing pointers; add bounds checking in gf_isom_box_size; use address sanitizers (ASAN) during development; deploy file format fuzzing tests; restrict media file parsing to sandboxed environments; implement exception handling for pointer operations

threat_vector: Stack buffer overflow in libde265 v1.0.8 fallback-motion.cc put_epel_hv_fallback function
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious HEVC video file with specially crafted motion compensation parameters that cause the put_epel_hv_fallback function to write beyond allocated stack buffer boundaries, potentially overwriting return addresses or other stack data
detection_pattern: Monitor for: stack canary violations, segmentation faults in libde265 processes, abnormal dec265 process termination with SIGSEGV, memory protection exceptions during HEVC decoding, fuzzing tools detecting heap/stack corruption with malformed video files, bounds-check failures in memory sanitizer logs when processing motion compensation blocks
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade libde265 to patched version beyond v1.0.8, implement bounds checking in motion compensation functions, enable stack canary protection (ASLR, -fstack-protector-strong), run decoder in sandboxed environment with memory restrictions, validate HEVC motion vector parameters before processing, implement strict input validation for video codec parameters, use memory sanitizers in development, limit decoder privileges with SELinux/AppArmor profiles

threat_vector: NocoDB Password Reset Observable Discrepancy (CVE-2023-22463) - Versions 0.9 to 0.83.8
exploitation_method: Attacker submits password reset requests with various email addresses and measures response times or observes differential error messages. The application leaks information about whether an email exists in the system through timing differences or distinct error responses, enabling user enumeration attacks.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Repeated password reset requests from single IP targeting multiple email addresses in rapid succession; (2) Pattern of requests with systematically varying email formats; (3) Correlation between request volume and response time analysis; (4) Requests to /password-reset or similar endpoints with enumeration characteristics; (5) Log entries showing differential error handling for valid vs invalid emails
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade NocoDB to version 0.84.0 or later; implement rate limiting on password reset endpoints (max 5 requests per IP per hour); standardize password reset responses to return identical messages/timing regardless of email validity; add CAPTCHA to password reset flow; implement account lockout after 10 failed reset attempts; log all password reset attempts with email addresses for audit; consider implementing passwordless authentication as alternative to password resets

threat_vector: Insecure deserialization in session cookie
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious serialized object in session cookie that triggers arbitrary code execution when deserialized by the application without proper validation or signing
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unexpected process execution from web server process, child processes spawned by application runtime, abnormal JVM/Python/PHP process behavior, suspicious file modifications in temp directories, network connections from unexpected sources initiated by web application, integrity check failures on session cookies, deserialization exceptions in application logs followed by code execution
mitigation_strategy: Implement cryptographic signing and validation of all session cookies using HMAC with strong secrets, enforce strict deserialization policies by whitelisting allowed classes, use secure serialization formats (JSON) instead of native serialization (pickle, Java serialization), apply input validation before deserialization, implement integrity verification, keep frameworks and runtime libraries patched, disable Java RMI/JNDI features if unused, use runtime application self-protection (RASP) to monitor deserialization calls

threat_vector: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) in MediaWiki MassEditRegex extension
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious webpage containing hidden requests that perform regex-based mass edits on wiki pages when authenticated user visits the page. The extension fails to validate CSRF tokens on edit operations, allowing unauthorized modifications through the user's authenticated session.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unexpected mass edit operations from single user session with regex patterns, (2) Edit log entries showing bulk page modifications without corresponding user UI interaction, (3) HTTP POST requests to MassEditRegex endpoints lacking valid CSRF tokens in referer/origin headers, (4) Multiple consecutive edits with identical regex patterns across unrelated pages from same IP/user agent in short timeframe
mitigation_strategy: Immediately upgrade MediaWiki to versions 1.35.5+, 1.36.3+, or 1.37.1+. Implement CSRF token validation on all MassEditRegex POST operations. Restrict MassEditRegex permissions to trusted user groups only. Deploy SameSite cookie policy (Strict/Lax) to prevent cross-origin cookie inclusion. Implement Content Security Policy headers to restrict script execution from external domains. Review and revoke access for accounts showing suspicious bulk edit patterns.

threat_vector: OS Credential Dumping (ATT&CK T1003)
exploitation_method: Attacker executes tools like Mimikatz, LSASS memory dumping, or SAM registry extraction to extract plaintext passwords, hashes, and Kerberos tickets from operating system memory, registry, or files
detection_pattern: Monitor for: lsass.exe abnormal child processes, suspicious registry access to HKLM\SAM, LSASS memory dump attempts, unusual MiniDump API calls, PowerShell Get-Item targeting registry hives, elevation of privilege followed by credential access, rundll32/comsvcs.dll patterns, WMI/WinRM credential enumeration, unusual wmic.exe or tasklist.exe execution with elevated privileges
mitigation_strategy: Enable LSA protection mode (RunAsPPL registry key), enforce credential guard on Windows 10/11 enterprise, restrict local admin rights, implement Privileged Access Management (PAM) solutions, apply memory protection mechanisms, monitor LSASS process access via Sysmon/EDR, enforce MFA for critical accounts, use LAPS (Local Administrator Password Solution) instead of shared credentials, deploy API monitoring for credential-dumping tool indicators

threat_vector: Elementor Plus Addons - Unvalidated AJAX Parameter (CVE-2023-32243)
exploitation_method: Attacker sends HTTP POST request to wp-admin/admin-ajax.php with action=tp_get_dl_post_info_ajax and crafted qvquery parameter containing SQL injection or arbitrary post IDs to retrieve sensitive post metadata without authentication
detection_pattern: Monitor for POST requests to /wp-admin/admin-ajax.php with action=tp_get_dl_post_info_ajax from unauthenticated sources; look for qvquery parameters containing SQL syntax (UNION, SELECT, --, /*), encoded payloads, or unusual post ID patterns in access logs; detect multiple rapid requests with varying qvquery values; track 200 responses returning post data when nonce verification is absent
mitigation_strategy: Update Plus Addons for Elementor to version 5.0.7 or later; implement nonce verification on tp_get_dl_post_info_ajax AJAX action using wp_verify_nonce(); validate and sanitize qvquery parameter with is_numeric() or whitelist allowed values; restrict AJAX action to authenticated users with current_user_can() checks; implement rate limiting on AJAX endpoints; use WordPress security plugins to audit and disable unused AJAX actions

threat_vector: VMware ESXi/Workstation/Fusion CD-ROM Device Heap Overflow (CVE-2021-22005)
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious CD-ROM image or ATAPI commands that trigger a heap buffer overflow in the virtual CD-ROM device emulation code. By sending specially crafted I/O requests, the attacker overwrites adjacent heap memory with controlled data, potentially achieving code execution within the hypervisor or VM context.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unusual CD-ROM device I/O patterns with oversized or malformed ATAPI command descriptors; (2) Heap corruption indicators in hypervisor logs (heap check failures, allocation mismatches); (3) Unexpected vmkernel or vm-support crashes involving 'cdrom' or 'atapi' modules; (4) Memory access violations at suspicious heap addresses; (5) CD-ROM mount attempts with invalid ISO9660 structures containing excessive data in control fields
mitigation_strategy: Apply security patches: ESXi 6.5 (ESXi650-202110101-SG or later), ESXi 6.7 (ESXi670-202111101-SG or later), ESXi 7.0 (Update 2 or later); Workstation 16.2.1+; Fusion 12.2.1+. Disable CD-ROM device pass-through if unused. Restrict guest access to CD-ROM devices via resource controls. Monitor and restrict ISO image sources to trusted repositories only.

threat_vector: IBM Security Verify Information Disclosure via Detailed Error Messages
exploitation_method: Attacker sends crafted requests to trigger application errors that return detailed technical information (stack traces, system paths, database details, configuration data) in HTTP responses, revealing sensitive system architecture and internal details useful for further attacks
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) HTTP responses containing stack traces, file paths, or database errors in body content; (2) Error pages with verbose technical details instead of generic messages; (3) Repeated requests to endpoints known to generate errors; (4) Responses with 4xx-5xx status codes containing sensitive keywords like 'database', 'config', 'path', 'exception', 'trace'; (5) WAF/IDS alerts on error-based reconnaissance patterns
mitigation_strategy: Implement custom error handling that returns generic user-facing messages in production (no technical details); configure application logging to capture full errors server-side only; disable debug mode and verbose error reporting in production; use WAF rules to sanitize or block responses containing stack traces and sensitive paths; implement rate limiting on error-generating endpoints; conduct security testing to identify all error message leakage points; patch to IBM Security Verify 10.0.3 or later versions that address this CVE

threat_vector: ATT&CK: IP Addresses (T1590.005) - Gathering IP addresses for reconnaissance
exploitation_method: Attacker performs OSINT on target infrastructure by querying public DNS records, WHOIS databases, BGP routing tables, and passive DNS services to identify IP address ranges, mail servers, web servers, and network topology before launching targeted attacks
detection_pattern: Monitor for: excessive DNS queries from external sources, unusual WHOIS lookups targeting your domain, anomalous passive DNS query patterns, scanning activity across IP ranges, mass reverse DNS lookups, correlation of multiple reconnaissance queries in short timeframe against same target organization
mitigation_strategy: Implement DNSSEC and DANE to secure DNS; use DNS query logging and anomaly detection; minimize public-facing infrastructure details; implement rate limiting on DNS services; monitor for reconnaissance patterns using threat intelligence feeds; maintain updated asset inventory to identify unexpected public IPs; use DNS firewalls to block suspicious query patterns; implement network segmentation to reduce attack surface visibility

threat_vector: Template injection in email system
exploitation_method: Attacker injects malicious template code (e.g., Jinja2, Freemarker, Velocity) into email template fields that are processed server-side without proper sanitization, allowing arbitrary code execution or information disclosure when templates are rendered
detection_pattern: Monitor for: template syntax characters in user inputs ({{, {%, ${}), unexpected function calls in template logs, template compilation errors with stack traces, unusual process spawning from email service, abnormal file access patterns from template rendering processes, and template processing latency spikes
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict input validation and sanitization on all user-supplied template content; use allowlist-based filtering for template variables; disable dangerous template functions (exec, system calls); employ sandboxed template rendering environments; regularly audit template code for injection points; use static template analysis tools; apply principle of least privilege to email service accounts; implement Content Security Policy for email templates; maintain up-to-date template engine versions

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1053.006 - Scheduled Task/Job: Cron
exploitation_method: Attacker modifies crontab files (/etc/crontab, /etc/cron.d/, user crontabs in /var/spool/cron/) or creates new cron jobs to execute malicious scripts at specified intervals for persistence, privilege escalation, or recurring command execution
detection_pattern: Monitor crontab modification events via auditctl rules (watch /etc/crontab, /etc/cron.d/, /var/spool/cron/); detect unusual cron job creation in logs (auth.log, cron.log); identify suspicious commands in cron jobs (reverse shells, data exfiltration); alert on crontab edits by unexpected users; track newly added cron jobs with suspicious timing or commands; monitor inode changes to cron-related files
mitigation_strategy: Restrict crontab access via /etc/cron.allow and /etc/cron.deny; implement file integrity monitoring (AIDE, Tripwire) on cron directories; use SELinux/AppArmor to restrict cron execution contexts; enable auditd logging for crontab modifications; regularly audit active cron jobs across system; disable cron for non-essential users; implement host-based EDR with behavioral analysis for suspicious cron execution patterns

threat_vector: Disable or Modify Windows Event Log (T1070.001)
exploitation_method: Attacker uses administrative privileges or exploits service vulnerabilities to disable Windows Event Logging service (EventLog), clear event logs via wevtutil.exe, or directly manipulate log files in %SystemRoot%\System32\winevt\Logs\ directory to remove evidence of malicious activity.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: EventLog service stopping (Event ID 7035), wevtutil.exe execution with clear/disable parameters, suspicious access to winevt\Logs directory, loss of expected security event logs, abrupt gaps in audit timestamps, file modification timestamps on .evtx files matching suspicious activity windows, and PowerShell commands like Clear-EventLog or Remove-Item targeting log paths.
mitigation_strategy: Enable immutable event log settings via Group Policy (Configure log file permissions to read-only), implement centralized log collection to remote SIEM before local modification, restrict wevtutil.exe and PowerShell execution via AppLocker/Windows Defender Application Control, protect EventLog service startup with SC Protect, enable File Integrity Monitoring (FIM) on log directories, configure Windows Audit Policy at domain level, implement mandatory audit logging, and monitor for EventLog service state changes via alerts.

threat_vector: Uninitialized Memory Usage in eID Module
exploitation_method: Attacker triggers eID module functions that read from memory buffers before initialization, potentially exposing sensitive data (private keys, PINs, certificates) from adjacent memory regions. This occurs through crafted API calls or card interactions that bypass initialization checks.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) eID module crashes with segmentation faults; (2) Unexpected memory access patterns in eID processes; (3) valgrind/AddressSanitizer logs showing use-of-uninitialized-value errors; (4) Inconsistent data returned from eID certificate/key operations; (5) Access to eID functions without prior state initialization; (6) Memory pages readable when they shouldn't contain valid data
mitigation_strategy: Implement explicit memory initialization: (1) Zero all buffers before use in eID module code; (2) Use static analysis tools (Clang Static Analyzer, Coverity) to detect uninitialized variables; (3) Enable compiler flags (-Wuninitialized, -ftrivial-auto-var-init=zero); (4) Apply defensive coding: initialize variables at declaration; (5) Implement pre-condition validation in eID APIs to verify module state before sensitive operations; (6) Use memory safety tools during development/testing; (7) Conduct secure code review of memory management in eID modules; (8) Update to patched eID library version

threat_vector: WiFi driver missing validation check leading to remote denial of service
exploitation_method: Attacker sends specially crafted WiFi packets with invalid parameters to trigger an unvalidated code path in the driver. Missing bounds checking or type validation allows malformed data to reach unsafe operations, causing kernel panic or system hang.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) sudden wireless driver crashes in kernel logs with specific function call stack traces; (2) repeated 'Oops' or 'BUG' messages from wifi driver modules; (3) system reboots without user action correlating with WiFi activity; (4) abnormal packet sequences with invalid frame headers or field values detected via wireless packet analysis; (5) driver state inconsistencies in /proc/net/wireless or iwconfig output
mitigation_strategy: Apply kernel patches that add proper input validation in WiFi driver packet handlers. Implement: (1) bounds checking on all packet fields before processing; (2) frame format validation before dereferencing; (3) type and length validation on driver ioctl parameters; (4) fuzzing-based testing of driver with malformed packets; (5) deploy updated firmware/driver versions; (6) use WPA3 and disable legacy WiFi modes when possible; (7) monitor and alert on unexpected driver restarts; (8) consider WiFi driver isolation via virtualization if available

threat_vector: Improper authentication vulnerability in Samsung Internet prior to 16.0.2.19 allows attackers to bypass secret mode password authentication
exploitation_method: Attacker exploits flawed password verification logic in Samsung Internet's secret mode by sending crafted requests or manipulating authentication state, bypassing the password prompt that normally protects private browsing sessions without requiring valid credentials
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unexpected access to secret mode features without authentication prompts in application logs, (2) Rapid sequential authentication failures followed by successful access, (3) Authentication bypass indicators in system security events, (4) Abnormal state transitions in browser session management, (5) Access to private browsing data from unauthenticated contexts
mitigation_strategy: Immediately update Samsung Internet to version 16.0.2.19 or later; implement application whitelisting to restrict execution of vulnerable versions; enforce mandatory security updates through Mobile Device Management (MDM) solutions; implement additional session validation controls and re-authentication requirements for sensitive secret mode operations; conduct security audits of authentication mechanisms and apply principle of least privilege to secret mode access controls

threat_vector: CSRF vulnerability in Vehicle Service Management System 1.0 leading to Stored XSS
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious form or link that exploits missing CSRF tokens. When authenticated user visits attacker's page, their browser automatically submits requests to the vulnerable application. If the application lacks input validation, the attacker can inject malicious JavaScript into stored fields (vehicle records, service notes, etc.), which executes for all users viewing that data.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) POST/PUT requests lacking valid CSRF tokens or with mismatched token values; (2) requests with Referer headers from external domains; (3) unexpected state-changing operations from unauthenticated or token-invalid sessions; (4) script tags, event handlers, or encoded JavaScript appearing in service notes, vehicle descriptions, or user-submitted fields in access logs; (5) unusual HTML entities or encoding patterns in stored data; (6) multiple state-change requests from same session in short timeframe without corresponding UI actions
mitigation_strategy: Implement anti-CSRF tokens (SameSite cookies set to 'Strict' or 'Lax', double-submit tokens, or synchronizer tokens) on all state-changing requests; validate tokens server-side on every POST/PUT/DELETE. Apply strict input validation and output encoding on all user inputs before storage. Use Content Security Policy (CSP) headers to prevent inline script execution. Implement parameterized queries to prevent injection. Apply HTML entity encoding to all displayed user-generated content. Conduct regular security testing and code review focusing on token validation and input sanitization.

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Permission Groups Discovery (T1069.003)
exploitation_method: Attacker executes commands like 'net group', 'Get-ADGroup', 'dscacheutil -q group', or LDAP queries to enumerate permission groups, membership lists, and access control hierarchies in the target environment to identify high-privilege groups and users
detection_pattern: Monitor for: repeated group enumeration commands in process logs (net.exe, PowerShell Get-AD* cmdlets), LDAP query patterns in directory service logs, unusual use of Active Directory management tools, command-line activity from non-admin processes querying group membership, successful logon events followed by group enumeration within short timeframes
mitigation_strategy: Implement command-line logging and monitoring via Sysmon/EDR; restrict Active Directory query permissions for standard users; disable unnecessary AD management tools; apply process whitelisting; monitor for and alert on group enumeration command sequences; use tiered access models limiting visibility of high-privilege groups; require MFA for privileged group membership changes; conduct regular access reviews and least-privilege audits

threat_vector: Missing Authorization in Daybyday CRM v2.0.0-2.2.0 allows low-privilege employees to view all users' appointments
exploitation_method: Attacker with employee-level credentials sends API requests or accesses appointment endpoints without proper authorization checks, bypassing role-based access controls that should restrict viewing to only assigned appointments or permitted scopes
detection_pattern: Monitor logs for: (1) appointments accessed by user IDs outside their assigned territory/team, (2) API requests to /appointments or similar endpoints with sequential or enumerated user IDs from low-privilege accounts, (3) unusual volume of appointment queries from standard employee accounts, (4) access to appointment records not assigned to the requesting user's department
mitigation_strategy: Implement server-side authorization validation on all appointment retrieval endpoints; enforce role-based access control (RBAC) that verifies user permissions before returning data; use object-level access control to restrict queries to only appointments the user owns or is explicitly authorized to view; upgrade to patched version >2.2.0; conduct access control audit of all data-retrieval endpoints; add authorization middleware to validate user scope against requested resource owner

threat_vector: Dnsmasq 2.86 heap-based buffer overflow in check_bad_address function
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious DNS queries with specially formatted domain names that exceed buffer boundaries in the check_bad_address function, triggering heap memory corruption when processing bogus wildcard checks
detection_pattern: Monitor for: dnsmasq process crashes or segmentation faults; abnormal memory allocation patterns; DNS queries with excessively long labels (>63 characters per RFC but crafted to trigger bounds); core dumps in dnsmasq logs; repeated DNS requests with specific malformed patterns; heap corruption indicators in system logs; unexpected dnsmasq restarts
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade dnsmasq to version 2.87 or later; implement input validation at DNS gateway layer to enforce RFC standards for label length; deploy memory sanitization tools (ASAN) in test environments; configure resource limits and process isolation for dnsmasq; monitor dnsmasq process health and enable automatic restart on crash; implement DNS query rate limiting and anomaly detection

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1021.004 - Remote Service Session: SSH
exploitation_method: Attacker gains SSH access through credential compromise (weak passwords, credential stuffing, leaked keys), exploits unpatched SSH vulnerabilities, or abuses misconfigurations (root login enabled, password authentication enabled, non-standard ports). Once authenticated, attacker establishes persistent remote access for lateral movement and command execution.
detection_pattern: Failed SSH login attempts from unusual IPs in /var/log/auth.log; successful logins from atypical geolocations or times; SSH connections during off-hours; unusual command execution patterns post-authentication; key files in ~/.ssh with suspicious timestamps or permissions; active SSH sessions to internal systems; unusual outbound SSH connections from compromised hosts; authentication logs showing brute-force patterns (multiple failed attempts followed by success)
mitigation_strategy: Disable SSH password authentication, enforce SSH key-based authentication only; restrict SSH access via firewall rules and IP whitelisting; disable root login and change default SSH port; implement multi-factor authentication for SSH; deploy intrusion detection (fail2ban, crowdsec); enforce strong SSH key management with regular audits; keep SSH daemon patched; monitor and alert on failed/successful authentication attempts; implement connection logging and session recording; use security baselines (hardened SSH configs); conduct regular SSH access reviews and revoke unused keys

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1574.006 - Dylib Hijacking via LC_LOAD_DYLIB Injection
exploitation_method: Attacker modifies Mach-O binary's LC_LOAD_DYLIB load commands to point to attacker-controlled dylib path instead of legitimate library. When binary executes, loader loads malicious dylib with same exported functions, achieving code execution in target process context before legitimate code runs.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Mach-O binaries with modified LC_LOAD_DYLIB commands pointing to non-standard paths, (2) File system modifications to system binary headers, (3) Dylibs loaded from writable directories (/tmp, /var/tmp, user home), (4) Unexpected dylib injection into signed processes via codesign validation failures, (5) Process execution with DYLD_INSERT_LIBRARIES or similar env vars, (6) System logs: 'dyld: Could not load' errors with unusual paths
mitigation_strategy: Implement: (1) Code signing enforcement with hard requirements, (2) System Integrity Protection (SIP) on macOS to prevent system binary modification, (3) Restrict dylib search paths via DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH whitelisting, (4) Monitor and alert on all dylib loads outside standard system directories, (5) Use library validation with DYLD_NO_PIE, (6) Implement file integrity monitoring (FIM) on all executable binaries, (7) Deploy endpoint protection detecting LC_LOAD_DYLIB anomalies, (8) Restrict write permissions to standard library directories

threat_vector: TIBCO eFTL Server Unauthenticated Remote Code Execution (CVE-2023-41435)
exploitation_method: Attacker sends specially crafted serialized Java objects to the eFTL Server's network listener without authentication. The vulnerable deserialization process executes arbitrary code embedded within the malicious payload, allowing remote code execution with server privileges.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Network connections to eFTL default port 8080 or custom ports from unexpected sources; (2) Serialized Java object signatures (AC ED 00 05 magic bytes) in network traffic; (3) Unusual process spawning from eFTL Java process; (4) Error logs containing ObjectInputStream deserialization exceptions; (5) Spike in failed connection attempts followed by successful ones; (6) Outbound connections from eFTL process to command-and-control servers.
mitigation_strategy: Immediately apply TIBCO security patches (eFTL 6.8.0, 6.9.0, 6.10.0+ contain fixes). Implement network segmentation to restrict eFTL Server access to trusted clients only. Deploy WAF/IPS rules blocking malicious serialized payloads. Enable authentication and TLS encryption for all eFTL connections. Monitor and log all deserialization activities. Consider running eFTL in containers with reduced privileges and network policies. Regularly update Java dependencies and run vulnerability scans.

threat_vector: Reflected XSS in Teedy v1.5-v1.9 search functionality - unsanitized search term parameter in results display
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious URL with JavaScript payload in search parameter (e.g., ?search=<script>alert('xss')</script>), victim clicks link, payload executes in victim's browser within Teedy application context, potentially stealing session cookies or performing unauthorized actions
detection_pattern: HTTP requests with encoded/unencoded script tags or event handlers in search parameters; response bodies containing unescaped user input from search field in HTML context; browser console errors from injected scripts; unusual GET/POST requests to search endpoints with special characters or script syntax; WAF logs detecting XSS payloads in query strings
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Teedy to v1.10+; implement output encoding of search parameters using HTML entity encoding before rendering in results; add Content-Security-Policy headers restricting inline scripts; validate and sanitize all user input server-side; implement input length limits on search terms; deploy WAF rules blocking common XSS patterns; enable HTTPOnly and Secure flags on session cookies to prevent JavaScript access

threat_vector: Memory corruption in vow driver due to improper input validation leading to local privilege escalation
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious input to vow driver that bypasses validation checks, causing buffer overflow or heap corruption in kernel memory. This allows arbitrary code execution in kernel context, enabling privilege escalation from local user to SYSTEM.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: kernel crash dumps or BSOD with vow.sys in call stack; kernel memory access violations; unexpected system calls from vow driver; driver load/unload events from non-standard paths; abnormal vow driver I/O control (IOCTL) calls with oversized or malformed buffers; privilege escalation events where local user gains SYSTEM rights immediately after driver interaction
mitigation_strategy: 1) Apply security patches to vow driver immediately. 2) Implement strict IOCTL input validation and buffer size checks in driver code. 3) Use Address Space Layout Randomization (ASLR) and Data Execution Prevention (DEP). 4) Restrict vow driver access to authenticated users only via file permissions. 5) Deploy kernel patch guard and virtualization-based security. 6) Monitor and audit all driver interactions via Event Tracing for Windows (ETW). 7) Consider disabling the vow driver if not essential for operations.

threat_vector: Rogue backends causing Denial of Service of guest VMs via high-frequency event generation in Xen hypervisor
exploitation_method: A malicious backend domain generates excessive events (interrupts, notifications) targeting guest VMs, overwhelming the guest's event processing capability and consuming CPU resources, preventing legitimate operations
detection_pattern: Monitor for: sustained high event delivery rates to specific domains, CPU spikes in guest VMs without corresponding workload increase, event ring buffer saturation, disproportionate context switches, backend process generating >10000 events/second to single guest, kernel logs showing event queue overflow or dropped notifications
mitigation_strategy: Implement event rate limiting per backend-to-guest connection, enforce strict event queue depth limits with backpressure mechanisms, deploy resource quotas restricting backend event generation, isolate backend domains with restricted capabilities, enable audit logging of inter-domain event traffic, apply Xen security patches addressing event channel vulnerabilities, use domain disaggregation to limit backend privilege scope

threat_vector: Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) Command Execution
exploitation_method: Attacker leverages WMI classes (e.g., Win32_Process) via wmic.exe or PowerShell to execute arbitrary commands with elevated privileges, bypassing application whitelisting by using legitimate Windows binaries
detection_pattern: Monitor for: wmic.exe process execution with 'process call create' arguments; PowerShell Invoke-WmiMethod calls; WMI event subscriptions (Win32_ProcessStartup); Parent process wmic.exe spawning child processes; Event ID 12 or 13 for WMI activity; CommandLine containing 'wmic' followed by process/class modification commands; PowerShell execution policy bypass combined with WMI calls
mitigation_strategy: Disable WMI via Group Policy (Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > Windows Management Instrumentation); restrict WMI execution through AppLocker rules targeting wmic.exe; implement PowerShell Constrained Language Mode and script block logging; use EDR with behavioral analysis for WMI-based execution; apply principle of least privilege to limit process spawning; monitor and audit WMI namespaces; consider disabling WMI on non-essential systems

threat_vector: Compromise Software Dependencies and Development Tools (T1195)
exploitation_method: Attacker gains access to software repositories, package managers, or development tool infrastructure to inject malicious code into legitimate packages. Malicious code propagates to downstream users through automatic dependency resolution and package downloads.
detection_pattern: Unexpected new versions of packages released in short timeframes; anomalous code changes in repository commits (unusual file modifications, obfuscated code sections); package download spikes from atypical geographic locations; process execution of unexpected child processes from package installation directories; network traffic from build systems to suspicious external endpoints; hash mismatches between expected and downloaded package binaries; suspicious new maintainer accounts or permission escalations in package registries
mitigation_strategy: Implement software bill of materials (SBOM) generation and verification; use cryptographic package signing and signature validation; maintain allowlists of approved package sources and versions; employ dependency pinning and lock files; conduct regular security audits of dependencies; establish secure build pipeline isolation with least-privilege access; implement network egress controls from development and build environments; enable repository integrity monitoring and tamper detection; use package repository proxies with content scanning; maintain offline vulnerability databases and perform offline dependency analysis

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1547.015 - Boot or Logon Autostart Execution: Socket Filters
exploitation_method: Attacker installs malicious socket filter drivers (WFP callout drivers or LSP/Winsock providers) to intercept, monitor, or modify network traffic at the kernel level. This achieves persistence and enables man-in-the-middle attacks without user detection. Typically deployed via privileged code execution or supply chain compromise.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) New entries in HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Services\WinSock2\Parameters\Protocol_Catalog9\Catalog_Entries or AppID_Catalog entries; (2) Unsigned or suspicious kernel drivers loaded via netsh winsock show catalog or Get-NetAdapterAdvancedProperty; (3) Registry changes to Winsock LSP entries; (4) Kernel driver load events (Event ID 6 in System logs); (5) Anomalous WFP configuration changes via netsh advfirewall; (6) Unexpected network redirection or traffic interception patterns.
mitigation_strategy: Implement: (1) Code Integrity/UEFI Secure Boot to enforce driver signature validation; (2) Restrict admin/SYSTEM privileges via least-privilege access controls; (3) Monitor and whitelist approved WFP callout drivers and Winsock providers; (4) Deploy kernel driver attestation policies (Windows Driver Frameworks); (5) Use Device Guard/Credential Guard to isolate sensitive network operations; (6) Audit registry and driver loading via SACL on HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Services\WinSock2; (7) Maintain updated Windows and patch management; (8) Disable legacy LSP if not required; (9) Monitor netsh advfirewall operations with ETW tracing.

threat_vector: Kentico Xperience 13.0.44 XML-based XSS in Media Libraries
exploitation_method: Attacker uploads a specially crafted XML document to the Media Libraries subsystem containing malicious JavaScript payloads in XML attributes or text nodes. When the application processes or renders the XML content without proper sanitization, the script executes in the context of authenticated users' browsers.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) POST/PUT requests to Media Libraries endpoints with .xml file uploads containing script tags or event handlers (onclick, onerror, onload); (2) XML documents with encoded or obfuscated JavaScript (e.g., <script> or javascript: protocols); (3) Unusual XML attribute values containing special characters (<, >, quotes); (4) Browser console errors indicating script execution from media library resources; (5) Access logs showing XML file requests followed by unexpected DOM modifications in application interface
mitigation_strategy: 1) Upgrade to Kentico Xperience 13.0.45 or later patch; 2) Implement strict XML schema validation for all uploaded files; 3) Apply Content Security Policy (CSP) headers to restrict inline script execution; 4) Use HTML/XML sanitization libraries to strip dangerous content before rendering; 5) Disable script execution for XML media files via MIME-type restrictions; 6) Implement file type whitelisting in Media Libraries; 7) Apply output encoding when displaying XML content in UI; 8) Enforce principle of least privilege for media upload permissions

threat_vector: Unnecessary Privilege Vulnerability in Trend Micro Apex One and Worry-Free Business Security 10.0 SP1 - Local Privilege Escalation via Impersonation
exploitation_method: Local attacker exploits excessive privilege grants in the product's service or process to impersonate higher-privileged accounts (SYSTEM or Administrator), allowing elevation from standard user context to administrative capabilities without proper authorization checks
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unexpected token impersonation calls in Windows security logs (Event ID 4624 with impersonation level); (2) Service-initiated token creation from non-system accounts; (3) Trend Micro service processes accessing restricted resources with elevated tokens; (4) Privilege use events (Event ID 4672) originating from Apex One/Worry-Free processes running in user context; (5) API monitor logs showing LogonUser or ImpersonateLoggedOnUser calls from product binaries; (6) Registry/file system modifications to SYSTEM-protected areas by the vulnerable service
mitigation_strategy: Deploy patch/update to addressed vulnerability immediately; (2) Apply principle of least privilege - verify Trend Micro services run with minimal required permissions, not as SYSTEM; (3) Implement application whitelisting for Apex One/Worry-Free binaries; (4) Enable Windows integrity levels and mandatory access controls; (5) Configure Windows UAC and token filtering policies; (6) Monitor and restrict local admin access; (7) Disable impersonation capabilities in product if not required; (8) Regular privilege auditing of service accounts; (9) Implement behavioral monitoring for token manipulation attempts

threat_vector: Mortgage Calculator / Loan Calculator WordPress plugin < 1.5.17 - Unauthenticated Stored XSS via mlcalc shortcode attributes
exploitation_method: Attacker with contributor or editor role crafts malicious mlcalc shortcode with unescaped attributes containing JavaScript payloads. When the page is rendered, the payload executes in browsers of all visitors without proper output encoding of shortcode parameters.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) mlcalc shortcode usage with unusual attribute values containing script tags, event handlers (onload, onclick), or encoded JavaScript; (2) POST requests to wp-admin/post.php or post-new.php containing mlcalc shortcodes with suspicious payloads; (3) Page revisions showing addition of mlcalc shortcodes by lower-privileged users; (4) Scanner logs detecting XSS patterns in shortcode attributes
mitigation_strategy: Immediately update plugin to version 1.5.17 or later. Implement input validation on shortcode attributes using sanitize_text_field() and output escaping with esc_attr() for all mlcalc parameters. Restrict shortcode usage via user role capabilities. Audit existing posts/pages for malicious shortcode usage. Deploy Web Application Firewall rules to block requests containing script injection in shortcode parameters.

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Active Setup (T1547.014)
exploitation_method: Attacker modifies Active Setup registry keys (HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Active Setup\Installed Components) to execute arbitrary commands or scripts during user logon. The registry entry includes a StubPath value pointing to malicious executable or script that runs with user privileges when new users log in or system starts.
detection_pattern: Monitor for new registry modifications under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Active Setup\Installed Components; watch for suspicious StubPath values containing cmd.exe, powershell.exe, or script interpreters; check for unexpected .exe, .bat, .vbs, or .ps1 file references; correlate with process creation events showing execution from registry-triggered paths; alert on non-standard CLSID entries or unsigned executables in StubPath.
mitigation_strategy: Implement registry write protection on Active Setup keys using Group Policy (HKLM restrictions); enable AppLocker/WDAC to block unauthorized script execution; deploy EDR solutions to monitor and block suspicious registry modifications; restrict user permissions to prevent unprivileged modifications; regularly audit Active Setup entries for unauthorized additions; use Windows Defender Exploit Guard; maintain inventory of legitimate Active Setup entries and alert on deviations.

threat_vector: Huawei IDAP module Out-of-Bounds Read vulnerability leading to Denial of Service
exploitation_method: Attacker sends specially crafted packets or requests to the IDAP (Intelligent Data Access Platform) module that trigger out-of-bounds memory read operations, causing the process to crash or enter an unrecoverable state due to invalid memory access
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unusual IDAP process crashes or unexpected terminations; (2) Segmentation faults or access violation errors in IDAP logs; (3) Malformed or oversized IDAP protocol messages in network traffic; (4) High frequency of failed IDAP requests from specific sources; (5) Memory access violations in system logs correlating with IDAP service unavailability; (6) Abnormal IDAP CPU/memory spikes followed by process termination
mitigation_strategy: Apply official Huawei security patches for IDAP module immediately; implement input validation and bounds checking on all IDAP packet processing; deploy network-level filtering to restrict IDAP access to authorized sources only; configure IDAP process monitoring with automatic restart capabilities; use memory protection mechanisms (ASLR, DEP); implement rate limiting on IDAP requests; segment IDAP services on isolated network zones with restricted access controls

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Linux and Mac Permissions Abuse (T1548.001)
exploitation_method: Attacker modifies file permissions using chmod/chown commands or exploits SUID/SGID bits to escalate privileges, or abuses sudo misconfiguration to execute commands with elevated privileges without authentication
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unusual chmod/chown command execution on critical system files, unexpected SUID bit changes (via find or stat), failed/successful sudo attempts in auth.log with no_passwd entries, setfacl modifications, file permission changes on binaries in /usr/bin or /usr/sbin, sudoers file modifications, capability changes via setcap
mitigation_strategy: Implement file integrity monitoring (AIDE, Tripwire) on critical binaries; enforce restrictive umask (0077); audit and minimize SUID/SGID binaries; enforce mandatory sudo authentication and logging; use sudo rule whitelisting; employ SELinux or AppArmor MAC policies; disable unnecessary capabilities; regularly audit /etc/sudoers and /etc/sudoers.d; restrict file permission modification via SELinux deny rules; implement immutable file attributes on critical system files

threat_vector: shelljs Improper Privilege Management
exploitation_method: Attacker leverages shelljs library's inadequate privilege checking to execute shell commands with elevated permissions beyond intended scope, potentially allowing arbitrary command execution with parent process privileges or bypassing intended access controls
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unexpected child processes spawned from Node.js applications with elevated privileges, (2) shelljs execSync/exec calls with user-controlled input in arguments, (3) Process privilege escalation attempts originating from Node.js interpreter, (4) Audit logs showing commands executed through shell invocation with mismatched privilege levels, (5) Source code analysis detecting shelljs usage without input sanitization or privilege validation
mitigation_strategy: Implement: (1) Update shelljs to patched version with proper privilege validation, (2) Run Node.js applications with minimal required privileges (principle of least privilege), (3) Use input validation and sanitization before passing data to shelljs commands, (4) Replace shelljs with safer alternatives like child_process with explicit argument arrays, (5) Implement security policies restricting shell command execution, (6) Use containers/sandboxing to limit privilege escalation impact, (7) Deploy static analysis tools to detect shelljs misuse patterns in code

threat_vector: TIBCO eFTL Server unauthenticated remote code execution via deserialization
exploitation_method: Attacker sends crafted serialized Java objects to the eFTL Server port (default 8585) without authentication, leveraging unsafe deserialization to instantiate arbitrary classes and execute code with server privileges
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) unexpected connections to eFTL Server ports from external networks, (2) malformed or oversized serialized object streams in network traffic, (3) sudden spawning of child processes from eFTL server JVM, (4) failed deserialization errors in eFTL logs followed by abnormal process execution, (5) outbound connections initiated by eFTL process to command-and-control infrastructure
mitigation_strategy: Apply TIBCO security patches immediately; restrict network access to eFTL Server ports via firewall/network segmentation; implement authentication and encryption for all eFTL connections; deploy intrusion detection signatures for known exploit payloads; monitor process execution and network connections from eFTL service accounts; consider running eFTL in containerized/sandboxed environment with minimal privileges; enable Java deserialization filters to block dangerous classes

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Scheduled Task/Job (T1053)
exploitation_method: Attacker creates or modifies scheduled tasks using schtasks.exe, at.exe, or launchd/cron on Unix systems to execute malicious payloads at specified times or intervals, persisting across reboots and avoiding user detection
detection_pattern: Monitor for: schtasks.exe /create or /change commands with suspicious paths; at.exe execution; unauthorized cron/launchd entries; scheduled task modifications in Event ID 4688 (process creation) and 4698 (scheduled task creation); TaskScheduler logs in Event Viewer showing new tasks with .exe, .ps1, or cmd.exe arguments; registry changes to HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Schedule\TaskCache\Tasks; unsigned executables in C:\Windows\Tasks or C:\Windows\System32\Tasks
mitigation_strategy: 1) Disable unnecessary scheduled task functionality via Group Policy (Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > Task Scheduler > Deny all non-administrators); 2) Monitor and audit all scheduled task creation/modification via Sysmon and Windows Event Logs; 3) Implement application whitelisting for scheduled task payloads; 4) Use Autoruns or Task Scheduler UI to regularly audit existing tasks; 5) Apply principle of least privilege to task execution accounts; 6) Deploy EDR solutions with behavioral analysis to detect suspicious task execution patterns; 7) Enforce code signing requirements; 8) Block access to schtasks.exe and at.exe for non-administrative users

threat_vector: Stack-based Buffer Overflow in HDF5 1.13.1-1 H5D__create_chunk_file_map_hyper function
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious HDF5 file with specially constructed chunked dataset parameters that cause unbounded stack allocation in H5D__create_chunk_file_map_hyper(), triggering stack exhaustion or corruption when processing hyperslab selections on chunked datasets
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Segmentation faults or SIGSEGV signals in HDF5 library processes; (2) Stack overflow exceptions in application logs; (3) Abnormal memory access patterns near stack boundaries; (4) HDF5 file parsing operations with unusually large chunk dimension values; (5) Core dumps with stack corruption signatures; (6) Resource exhaustion alerts (stack space depletion)
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade HDF5 to patched version >1.13.1-1; implement input validation for HDF5 file chunk parameters before processing; enforce stack size limits and monitor stack usage; use AddressSanitizer (ASan) or valgrind during development; restrict HDF5 processing to sandboxed environments with limited stack resources; disable untrusted HDF5 file parsing where possible; implement fuzzing tests with malformed HDF5 files

threat_vector: Missing Authorization in Daybyday CRM v2.0.0-2.2.0 allows low-privilege employees to view absence records of all users
exploitation_method: Attacker with employee-level credentials accesses absence viewing endpoints without proper role-based access control validation. The application fails to verify authorization before returning absence data for users outside the attacker's department or authorization scope.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Repeated absence/PTO API calls from single employee account accessing records of multiple users, (2) Absence queries with user_id parameters not matching authenticated user's direct reports or department, (3) Bulk absence data exports by non-manager accounts, (4) Access logs showing pattern of /absences, /leave, or /pto endpoints with unusual frequency or scope
mitigation_strategy: Implement role-based access control (RBAC) checks before returning absence data: (1) Validate requesting user has manager/admin role or direct supervisory relationship before returning any absence records, (2) Enforce department-level isolation in queries, (3) Apply object-level authorization checks on absence resources, (4) Upgrade to patched version 2.2.1+, (5) Add application-layer authorization middleware to verify user relationships before data retrieval, (6) Implement principle of least privilege for absence data access

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Indicator Removal from Tools (T1027.005)
exploitation_method: Attacker modifies logging utilities, audit tools, or system instrumentation to suppress, filter, or delete forensic artifacts. Common targets include event log clearing, disabling Windows Event Forwarding, tampering with syslog configurations, or removing entries from application logs to hide command execution and lateral movement activities.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: suspicious clearing of event logs (Event ID 1102), disabled audit policies, changes to logging configuration files (auditd.conf, rsyslog.conf), unexpected modifications to Windows Registry logging keys (HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Services\EventLog), absence of expected log entries during known activity windows, sudden log rotation or truncation, disabled WinRM/PowerShell logging, modifications to log file permissions, and failed log write operations despite active processes.
mitigation_strategy: Implement centralized log aggregation with immutable forwarding to remote SIEM; enable audit policy enforcement via GPO with privileged group restrictions; use Write-Once-Read-Many (WORM) storage for logs; configure file integrity monitoring (FIM) on logging configuration files; restrict administrator access to event log deletion via ACLs; enable protected event logging in PowerShell; use endpoint detection and response (EDR) with behavioral monitoring; implement process auditing at kernel level; regularly validate log completeness; configure alerts for audit policy changes and suspicious clearing commands.

threat_vector: Unauthenticated arbitrary file download via 'execute restore src-vis' command in FortiOS versions before 7.0.3
exploitation_method: A local authenticated attacker exploits missing integrity verification in the restore command to download arbitrary files from the system by manipulating the source parameter without proper validation or checksum verification
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) 'execute restore src-vis' commands with unusual file paths in CLI logs, (2) Unexpected file operations outside designated backup directories, (3) Restore operations without corresponding valid backup files, (4) File access attempts to sensitive directories (/etc, /root, configuration files) via restore mechanism, (5) syslog entries showing mismatched file integrity checks or restore validation failures
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade FortiOS to version 7.0.3 or later which implements proper integrity checks; restrict CLI access through role-based access control limiting 'execute restore' permissions; implement file integrity monitoring (FIM) on critical system directories; enable comprehensive command audit logging; disable restore functionality if not required; validate and whitelist acceptable restore sources

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1070.006 - PubPrn (Public Print)
exploitation_method: Attacker abuses Print Spooler service (spoolsv.exe) to execute arbitrary code or escalate privileges by exploiting vulnerable printer drivers or print processor functionality
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unusual spoolsv.exe child processes, unexpected DLL loads in print spooler context, Print Spooler service crash/restart patterns, suspicious print job submissions with embedded executables, elevated access attempts from SYSTEM printing context, EventID 4688 with print spooler parent process spawning cmd.exe or powershell.exe
mitigation_strategy: Disable Print Spooler service if not required; restrict printer driver installation to administrators; implement AppLocker/WDAC rules blocking unsigned print drivers; apply CVE patches for print vulnerabilities; monitor and audit PrintNightmare mitigations; restrict registry access to HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Services\Spooler

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Exploitation for Defense Impairment (T1211)
exploitation_method: Attacker exploits unpatched vulnerabilities in security tools, endpoint protection platforms, or logging systems to disable, bypass, or degrade their functionality. Common targets include antivirus engines, EDR agents, SIEM collectors, and firewall management interfaces.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unexpected termination of security service processes (WinDefend, SentinelOne, CrowdStrike agents); (2) Security software crashes with exploit-related error codes; (3) Disabled Windows Defender via registry/GPO changes without authorization; (4) Failed EDR communication to backend servers; (5) Log ingestion gaps or sudden drops in event volume to SIEM; (6) Unsigned or suspicious process modifications to security tool binaries; (7) Privilege escalation attempts targeting security service accounts; (8) Exploitation CVE signatures matching security tool versions in IDS/IPS logs.
mitigation_strategy: Implement: (1) Aggressive patch management for all security tools with expedited critical updates; (2) Immutable or read-only file system protections for security tool binaries and configurations; (3) Privileged access management (PAM) to restrict modifications to security software; (4) Redundant/out-of-band monitoring (e.g., kernel-level monitoring independent of user-mode agents); (5) Alert rules for any unexpected termination of security services; (6) Hardware-backed security features (Secure Boot, TPM) to prevent low-level tampering; (7) Regular security tool integrity validation; (8) Network segmentation isolating security infrastructure from potentially compromised endpoints.

threat_vector: Apache Pluto UrlTestPortlet XSS vulnerability in input fields
exploitation_method: Attacker injects malicious JavaScript code into unvalidated input fields of UrlTestPortlet. When rendered in the portal, the script executes in the user's browser context with the user's session privileges, allowing credential theft, session hijacking, or malware distribution.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) HTTP requests to UrlTestPortlet endpoints containing script tags or event handlers (onclick, onerror, onload); (2) Portal logs showing encoded/obfuscated payloads like %3Cscript%3E, javascript:, or data: URIs; (3) Unusual parameter values with special characters in UrlTestPortlet form submissions; (4) Browser console errors indicating blocked scripts when CSP headers are enforced
mitigation_strategy: Immediate: Upgrade v3-demo-portlet.war to version 3.1.1 or later. Interim: Implement input validation on all UrlTestPortlet input fields to reject or sanitize HTML/JavaScript special characters; enforce Content Security Policy (CSP) headers to prevent inline script execution; apply output encoding (HTML entity encoding) to all user-controlled data rendered in responses; conduct security testing with OWASP testing tools to verify remediation

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Safe Mode Boot (T1542.005)
exploitation_method: Attacker modifies boot configuration via bcdedit.exe, registry manipulation, or bootloader access to force system into Safe Mode with Networking. This disables security software, driver enforcement, and kernel protections, allowing execution of malware or lateral movement with reduced defenses.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: bcdedit.exe execution with 'safeboot' parameters; Registry changes to HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\SafeBoot; Event ID 4688 with bcdedit or boot configuration changes; Unexpected Safe Mode boot events in Event Viewer System logs; Disabled security software post-boot; WMI process creation events showing boot option modifications; Anomalous system startup sequence or missing driver initialization logs.
mitigation_strategy: Enforce Secure Boot and UEFI firmware protections; restrict bcdedit.exe execution via AppLocker/WDAC policies; disable local administrator access to boot configuration tools; monitor and restrict registry write access to SafeBoot keys; require BIOS/UEFI password protection; implement EDR solutions monitoring boot anomalies; maintain immutable audit logs of boot configuration changes; disable Safe Mode if not required by business operations; regularly verify boot integrity via integrity monitoring tools.

threat_vector: Unauthenticated Remote Code Execution in Siemens SIPROTEC 5 Protection Relays (6MD85, 6MD86, 6MD89 with CP300 CPU, versions < V8.83)
exploitation_method: Attackers send specially crafted network packets to the vulnerable relay's communication interface, exploiting insufficient input validation in the firmware. This allows bypass of authentication mechanisms and injection of arbitrary code that executes with device privileges, potentially allowing complete control of protective relay functions.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unexpected network connections to SIPROTEC 5 devices on ports 502 (Modbus), 22 (SSH), or proprietary Siemens ports (20000, 161); (2) Malformed or oversized packets destined to relays; (3) Failed authentication attempts followed by successful connections without proper credentials; (4) Unexpected firmware configuration changes or checksum mismatches; (5) Unusual process execution or memory modifications on relay devices; (6) Abnormal IEC 60870-5-104 or IEC 61850 protocol sequences; (7) Syslog entries indicating code execution outside normal operations.
mitigation_strategy: Immediately update all affected SIPROTEC 5 relays to firmware version V8.83 or later. Implement network segmentation isolating protection relays to dedicated VLAN with restricted access. Deploy firewall rules limiting communication to authorized engineering stations and SCADA systems only. Disable unnecessary network services and enforce strong authentication (SSH key-based, disable Telnet). Monitor and log all relay configuration changes. Implement anomaly detection for abnormal relay behavior. Conduct regular firmware integrity verification. Maintain offline backups of critical relay configurations. Coordinate with Siemens for patches and security advisories.

threat_vector: HwNearbyMain module Data Processing Errors vulnerability leading to process restart
exploitation_method: Attacker sends malformed or unexpected data packets to HwNearbyMain module, triggering unhandled exceptions in data parsing logic. The module fails to validate input format, type, or length before processing, causing an uncontrolled exception that terminates the process.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Repeated HwNearbyMain process crashes in system logs with stack traces referencing data parsing functions; (2) Abnormal network traffic patterns to Nearby service ports with invalid protocol headers; (3) Application ANR (Application Not Responding) events correlated with HwNearbyMain module; (4) Sudden spikes in error logs containing 'NullPointerException', 'BufferOverflowException', or 'IllegalArgumentException' from nearby module; (5) Process restart cycles occurring within short time intervals
mitigation_strategy: Implement robust input validation in HwNearbyMain before processing any external data; add exception handling with try-catch blocks around parsing operations; implement rate limiting and connection throttling for Nearby service requests; deploy memory and resource bounds checking; maintain updated patches for HwNearbyMain; implement watchdog monitoring with graceful degradation instead of full process restart; add fuzz testing to identify edge cases in data processing; restrict nearby module permissions to minimum required access

threat_vector: Stored XSS via unsanitized wcj_notice parameter in Booster for WooCommerce plugin (versions < 5.4.9) with PDF Invoicing module enabled
exploitation_method: Attacker injects malicious JavaScript payload into the wcj_notice parameter. When an authenticated admin views the dashboard, the unsanitized parameter is reflected in the HTML output, executing the payload in the admin's browser context with full WordPress administrative privileges.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) POST/GET requests containing wcj_notice parameter with script tags or event handlers (onclick, onerror, etc.); (2) Admin dashboard access logs showing unusual wcj_notice parameter values; (3) WAF alerts on special characters or script patterns in wcj_notice; (4) WordPress admin audit logs showing unexpected option updates or user modifications; (5) JavaScript errors in admin console; (6) Unauthorized admin account creation or privilege escalation events
mitigation_strategy: Immediately upgrade Booster for WooCommerce to version 5.4.9 or later. For interim protection: (1) Disable the PDF Invoicing module if not actively used; (2) Restrict WordPress admin access to trusted IP ranges via .htaccess or firewall rules; (3) Implement Content Security Policy (CSP) headers to prevent inline script execution; (4) Use WordPress security plugins (Wordfence, Sucuri) to filter malicious wcj_notice payloads at the application level; (5) Audit user permissions and remove unnecessary admin accounts; (6) Monitor admin user activity logs for suspicious changes

threat_vector: Clickjacking via iframe embedding
exploitation_method: Attacker embeds target website in transparent iframe overlaid on malicious content, tricking users into clicking invisible buttons that perform unintended actions on the legitimate site (e.g., fund transfers, permission grants, account changes)
detection_pattern: Monitor for: X-Frame-Options header absence in HTTP responses, Content-Security-Policy frame-ancestors violations, unusual cross-origin frame loads in browser console, clickstream data showing rapid successive clicks on same coordinates, user session actions without corresponding UI interactions
mitigation_strategy: Implement X-Frame-Options: DENY or SAMEORIGIN header; deploy Content-Security-Policy with frame-ancestors directive; use frame-busting JavaScript; implement SameSite cookie attributes; add user interaction confirmation for sensitive operations; employ clickjacking protection via JavaScript frame-detection; validate referrer headers; conduct regular security testing with iframe embedding scenarios

threat_vector: Out-of-bounds read in smartphone memory
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious input or triggers specific code path that causes application to read memory beyond allocated buffer boundaries, potentially exposing sensitive data in adjacent memory regions without causing crash
detection_pattern: Memory access violations in application logs, ASLR bypass indicators, unusual data exfiltration patterns, debugger output showing reads beyond buffer limits, crashes in memory sanitizer/AddressSanitizer reports, unexpected information disclosure in app traffic
mitigation_strategy: Implement bounds checking on all buffer operations, use memory-safe APIs, deploy address space layout randomization (ASLR), enable stack canaries and CFI (Control Flow Integrity), conduct fuzzing and static analysis testing, apply OS-level protections (DEP/NX), keep devices updated with security patches, use memory tagging extension (MTE) on compatible devices

threat_vector: Stored XSS in Vehicle Service Management System 1.0 - Mechanic List Section
exploitation_method: Attacker injects malicious JavaScript payload into mechanic name, contact, or description fields through the login panel. Payload is stored in database and executed in victim's browser when mechanic list is viewed by administrators or authorized users.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) HTML/JavaScript entities in mechanic database records (script tags, event handlers like onerror, onload); (2) WAF alerts on <script>, javascript:, or event handler patterns in POST/PUT requests to /mechanic endpoints; (3) Unusual character encoding or Unicode escape sequences in mechanic list forms; (4) CSP violation reports indicating inline script execution; (5) Anomalous user session activity following mechanic list access
mitigation_strategy: Implement: (1) Input validation - whitelist allowed characters for mechanic fields, reject script tags and event handlers; (2) Output encoding - HTML-encode all mechanic data on display using context-aware escaping; (3) Content Security Policy (CSP) with script-src 'self' to prevent inline execution; (4) Use templating engines with auto-escaping enabled; (5) Apply parameterized queries to prevent injection; (6) Implement stored procedure review and code audit of mechanic list functionality; (7) Regular penetration testing of user input fields

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Revert Cloud Instance
exploitation_method: Attacker with cloud account access uses native cloud provider APIs or console to revert a running instance to a previous snapshot or backup state, potentially bypassing security patches, removing forensic evidence, or restoring a compromised baseline configuration.
detection_pattern: CloudTrail/audit logs showing RestoreDBInstanceFromDBSnapshot, RevertToDBInstanceSnapshot, CreateImage with subsequent DescribeInstances showing version rollback; unexpected instance state changes; modification timestamps older than current system time; disappearance of recent logs or installed security tools; API calls from unusual accounts or IP ranges initiating restore operations
mitigation_strategy: Implement immutable snapshots with read-only storage; enforce MFA and conditional access policies for snapshot/restore operations; enable cloud audit logging with WORM (Write-Once-Read-Many) storage; use backup encryption and separate credentials; maintain golden image snapshots separate from production; implement automated integrity monitoring of system files; require approval workflows for restore operations; regularly validate snapshot integrity and audit who can initiate restores

threat_vector: Spinnaker improper permissions allowing unauthorized pipeline creation and execution via Gate API access
exploitation_method: Attacker with Gate endpoint access bypasses RBAC controls to create malicious pipelines that execute arbitrary deployments, scripts, or infrastructure changes across connected cloud environments without proper authorization validation
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unauthorized POST requests to /pipelines endpoint, pipeline creation by users lacking pipeline-creation role, execution of pipelines by non-authorized accounts, sudden spike in pipeline deployments from unexpected service accounts, audit logs showing role-based access control violations, pipelines deploying to sensitive environments from unusual sources
mitigation_strategy: Enable and enforce RBAC in Spinnaker Gate configuration, implement explicit pipeline creation policies, restrict Gate API access via network controls or API gateway authentication, audit all pipeline operations, use Fiat service to validate authorization on every pipeline operation, implement role-based stage execution restrictions, require approval workflows for sensitive deployments, maintain detailed audit logs of all pipeline CRUD operations

threat_vector: containerd SELinux privilege escalation (CVE-2021-32785) - containerd versions 1.5.0-beta.0 and later on SELinux-enabled systems incorrectly handle SELinux options, allowing container escape and host privilege escalation
exploitation_method: Attacker creates a malicious container image that specifies crafted SELinux labels or mounts a host filesystem with relaxed SELinux contexts. When containerd processes the container without proper SELinux enforcement, the attacker gains elevated privileges and can access host resources or escalate to root
