mitigation_strategy: Implement Application Whitelisting (AppLocker/Windows Defender Application Control) to restrict startup execution; deploy Group Policy to restrict registry modifications; enable Startup folder auditing and alerting; use EDR solutions to monitor process execution chains at boot; conduct regular audits of startup items via 'msconfig' and Task Scheduler; restrict Write permissions to startup directories; disable unnecessary startup services via Group Policy.

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1546.002: Event Triggered Execution - Screensaver
exploitation_method: Attacker modifies screensaver registry keys (HKCU\Control Panel\Desktop\SCRNSAVE.EXE or group policy) to point to malicious executable. When screensaver activates after idle timeout, the malicious code executes with user privileges.
detection_pattern: Monitor registry modifications to HKCU\Control Panel\Desktop\SCRNSAVE.EXE and related screensaver keys; track unsigned or suspicious executables launched from screensaver paths; analyze Event ID 4657 (registry value modifications); detect deviation from baseline screensaver files; flag .scr files with abnormal parent processes or network connections immediately after execution
mitigation_strategy: Enforce Group Policy to disable screensaver functionality or restrict to legitimate built-in screensavers only; implement application whitelisting for screensaver executables; monitor and log all SCRNSAVE.EXE registry modifications; disable screensaver via GPO if not required; use Windows Defender Application Guard or AppLocker to prevent unsigned executable execution from screensaver paths; conduct regular registry audits for unauthorized screensaver modifications

threat_vector: Improper input validation in TrustZone memory transfer interface allows out-of-bounds memory access leading to information disclosure across Snapdragon platforms
exploitation_method: Attacker sends specially crafted memory transfer requests to TrustZone interface with invalid buffer sizes, offsets, or addresses that bypass validation checks, causing the secure OS to read and return sensitive data from adjacent memory regions outside intended boundaries
detection_pattern: Monitor for: TrustZone SMC calls with buffer parameters exceeding allocated size ranges; abnormal memory access patterns in secure monitor logs; repeated failed validation errors followed by successful transfers with modified parameters; unexpected data returns from syscalls; TEE audit logs showing out-of-bounds access attempts; statistical anomalies in memory transfer request sizes compared to baseline
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict input validation with explicit bounds checking on all TrustZone memory transfer parameters before processing; use secure memory management APIs that enforce buffer boundaries; apply address space layout randomization (ASLR) in TrustZone; conduct regular fuzzing of memory transfer interfaces; deploy runtime memory access validation; apply available Qualcomm security patches; implement capability-based access control limiting memory ranges accessible per request; perform regular code audits of TEE interface handlers

threat_vector: showdoc is vulnerable to Generation of Error Message Containing Sensitive Information
exploitation_method: Attacker triggers application errors by submitting malformed input, invalid parameters, or accessing non-existent resources. The application returns detailed error messages containing sensitive information such as file paths, database connection strings, internal IP addresses, software versions, or stack traces that reveal system architecture and potential attack vectors.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: HTTP responses with 4xx/5xx status codes containing database credentials, file system paths, server software versions, or stack trace information in response bodies; unusual error response sizes; error messages containing words like 'Exception', 'Error', 'SQL', 'Connection', 'Database', 'Path', or 'Stack'; responses with Content-Type of text/html/plain containing technical details; repeated error requests from same IP targeting different endpoints
mitigation_strategy: Implement generic error handling: return user-friendly error messages to clients while logging detailed errors server-side only; disable debug mode in production; configure web server to hide version information and technical details in HTTP headers; implement input validation and sanitization to prevent triggering errors; use a Web Application Firewall (WAF) to filter error responses before transmission; implement centralized logging with sensitive data masking; regular security testing to identify information disclosure vulnerabilities; enforce principle of least privilege for error message content

threat_vector: Heap-based buffer overflow in GPAC 1.0.1 MP4Box via media.c
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts a malicious MP4 file with oversized or malformed media box structures that exceed allocated heap buffer boundaries in the media.c parsing logic, causing heap memory corruption during file processing
detection_pattern: Monitor for: heap corruption exceptions in MP4Box/GPAC processes; abnormal memory access violations in media.c functions; unexpected process termination with segmentation faults when processing MP4 files; memory sanitizer warnings (ASAN/MSAN) on crafted MP4 inputs; anomalous heap metadata corruption signatures
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade GPAC to patched version >1.0.1; implement strict input validation for MP4 box sizes before heap allocation; deploy bounds checking and safe memory allocation practices in media.c; use Address Sanitizer (ASAN) and memory protection mechanisms (CFI, shadow stack); restrict MP4Box file processing to sandboxed environments; validate file signatures and apply file size limits before processing

threat_vector: Privilege escalation vulnerability in smartphone file system component
exploitation_method: Attacker exploits improper permission validation or race conditions in file system operations to gain elevated privileges, potentially leveraging symlink attacks, hard link exploitation, or TOCTOU (Time-of-Check-Time-of-Use) vulnerabilities to access protected system files or modify sensitive data structures
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unauthorized access to /system or /data/system directories, unexpected privilege level changes in process logs, suspicious symlink creation in world-writable directories, repeated failed permission checks followed by successful access, anomalous file descriptor usage patterns, SELinux/AppArmor denial logs indicating policy violations, unexpected capability additions to low-privilege processes
mitigation_strategy: Apply latest security patches to file system driver and permission validation logic; implement strict DAC/MAC controls with least privilege principles; enable audit logging for all file operations on sensitive directories; use secure file handling practices (avoid world-writable temp directories, validate symlinks); deploy runtime integrity monitoring; implement ASLR and code signing enforcement; restrict access to sensitive APIs; perform regular penetration testing on file system operations

threat_vector: QXIP SIPCAPTURE homer-app before 1.4.28 uses hardcoded JWT secret key across multiple customer installations
exploitation_method: Attacker obtains the known JWT secret from one installation or public sources, then uses it to forge authentication tokens with arbitrary claims (user ID, admin privileges) to authenticate to any other customer's homer-app instance sharing the same secret
detection_pattern: Monitor for: JWT tokens with unexpected issuer/audience claims; authentication success from unusual source IPs using service accounts; API requests with elevated privileges from non-admin users; unusual admin operations in audit logs; verification failures when comparing JWT signatures against expected key rotation dates; multiple customers reporting identical JWT secret values in security scans
mitigation_strategy: Immediately upgrade homer-app to version 1.4.28 or later; generate unique JWT secret keys per customer installation during deployment; implement runtime JWT secret rotation with versioning; validate JWT issuer/audience claims against registered customer identities; monitor and alert on JWT verification failures; implement additional authentication factors beyond JWT; conduct forensic review of authentication logs for token-based lateral movement across customer instances

threat_vector: Automated Collection (ATT&CK T1123)
exploitation_method: Attacker deploys scripts or tools that automatically gather data from compromised systems at scheduled intervals or continuous streams. Common targets include clipboard contents, screenshots, keystrokes, email archives, file system contents, and browser history. Tools like Evasion frameworks or custom PowerShell/Python scripts execute collection routines without user interaction.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: persistent scheduled tasks (schtasks.exe, cron jobs) with suspicious binaries; elevated process creation with collection-related arguments; unusual file access patterns to sensitive directories (AppData, Documents, Downloads); clipboard access via Windows API calls; screenshot tool execution (PrintScreen, bitblt functions); screen capture library imports in running processes; large data staging in temp directories; unusual network exfiltration of archive files; Registry key modifications enabling screenshot/recording capabilities
mitigation_strategy: Implement application allowlisting for scheduled task execution; restrict scheduled task creation via Group Policy; deploy behavioral monitoring to detect screenshot/clipboard APIs; enforce DLP (Data Loss Prevention) rules on sensitive data staging; monitor and block execution of common collection tools (screencap utilities, clipboard readers); restrict file system access to user directories; implement memory protection to prevent injection of collection routines; maintain audit logging for system API calls; use behavioral EDR to detect automated data access patterns inconsistent with user behavior

threat_vector: Unrestricted File Upload in Sourcecodester Vehicle Service Management System 1.0 leading to HTML Injection
exploitation_method: Attacker bypasses file upload validation by submitting files without proper extension/content-type checks. Malicious HTML or script files are uploaded to web-accessible directories, allowing execution of arbitrary HTML/JavaScript in victim browsers when accessed
detection_pattern: Monitor upload directories for: unexpected file types (html, js, svg with embedded scripts), MIME type mismatches, files with double extensions, suspicious file permissions (777), POST requests to upload endpoints with abnormal file sizes, access logs showing requests to uploaded files from unexpected user agents, error logs showing failed validation attempts, stored XSS payloads in uploaded content
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict whitelist validation: verify file extensions against allowed types, validate MIME types server-side, store uploads outside web root or in non-executable directories, rename uploaded files with random names, set proper file permissions (644), disable script execution in upload directories via .htaccess or web server config, implement Content-Security-Policy headers, sanitize filenames, enforce maximum file size limits, scan uploads with antivirus/malware detection, log all upload attempts with user attribution

threat_vector: Open Redirect via X-Forwarded-Host Header in Rails Action Pack >= 6.0.0
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious X-Forwarded-Host headers that bypass host validation when allowed_hosts configuration uses wildcards or overly permissive patterns. This causes the application to redirect users to attacker-controlled domains while appearing to originate from the trusted application.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) X-Forwarded-Host headers with domain values not matching configured allowed_hosts; (2) HTTP 301/302 redirects to domains outside whitelist; (3) Log entries showing mismatch between request.host and request.forwarded_host; (4) Multiple X-Forwarded-Host headers in single request; (5) X-Forwarded-Host containing special characters or wildcard patterns
mitigation_strategy: Update Rails/Action Pack to patched version. Implement strict allowed_hosts configuration using explicit full domain names instead of wildcards. Validate X-Forwarded-Host against explicit whitelist before trusting it. Disable X-Forwarded-Host processing if not needed. Implement Content-Security-Policy headers to restrict redirect destinations. Add request logging for all redirect operations including originating host header.

threat_vector: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) in HUAWEI WS318n network settings configuration interface due to insufficient input validation
exploitation_method: Attacker with local authentication access injects malicious JavaScript payload into network settings form fields (SSID, DNS, gateway, etc.). The unvalidated input is reflected or stored in the web interface and executed in victim's browser when settings page is accessed, allowing session hijacking, credential theft, or unauthorized configuration changes.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) HTML/JavaScript special characters in network settings parameters (script tags, event handlers like onload, onerror); (2) HTTP POST/GET requests with encoded payloads (%3Cscript%3E, \u003c variants); (3) Content-Security-Policy violation logs; (4) Abnormal DOM manipulation in browser console logs; (5) Unexpected JavaScript execution in web admin interface; (6) Access logs showing settings modifications from unexpected sources
mitigation_strategy: Implement: (1) Input validation - whitelist allowed characters for each field (alphanumeric, dots, hyphens for DNS/hostnames); (2) Output encoding - HTML-encode all user input before rendering in responses; (3) Content-Security-Policy headers restricting script execution; (4) HTTPOnly and Secure flags on session cookies; (5) Regular security patching of firmware; (6) Disable unnecessary admin interface features; (7) Implement CSRF tokens for configuration changes; (8) Use parameterized templating to prevent injection; (9) Conduct code review of input handling routines

threat_vector: Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR) in profile endpoint
exploitation_method: Attacker modifies user ID parameter in API requests (e.g., /api/profile/123) to access other users' profiles by incrementing, guessing, or enumerating valid IDs without proper authorization checks
detection_pattern: Monitor for sequential or rapid ID parameter changes in profile endpoint requests from single source; log access attempts to profile IDs not matching authenticated user context; flag HTTP 200 responses when requested user_id differs from session user_id; analyze access patterns showing enumeration of consecutive ID values
mitigation_strategy: Implement authorization checks verifying authenticated user ID matches requested resource ID before returning data; use indirect references (UUIDs, opaque tokens) instead of sequential integers; implement role-based access controls; add rate limiting on profile endpoint; log and alert on cross-user access attempts; conduct periodic access control audits

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Application Exhaustion Flood (T1499.004)
exploitation_method: Attacker sends high volume of seemingly legitimate application-layer requests (HTTP GET/POST, API calls, database queries) to consume server resources such as CPU, memory, and connection pools, causing legitimate users to experience degraded performance or service unavailability
detection_pattern: Sudden spike in application logs showing repeated requests from same or distributed source IPs; elevated response times (>5s); increased error rates (5xx responses); connection pool exhaustion warnings; database query queue buildup; CPU/memory utilization approaching 100%; request rate 10x+ above baseline; patterns of requests targeting expensive operations (complex searches, file uploads, report generation)
mitigation_strategy: Implement rate limiting per IP/user (e.g., 100 requests/minute); deploy WAF with request throttling and connection limits; use request queuing with backpressure handling; enable connection timeouts; implement CAPTCHA for suspicious traffic patterns; cache expensive responses; add query cost analysis to limit database strain; configure auto-scaling to absorb traffic spikes; monitor and alert on request rate anomalies; implement request signatures to identify malicious patterns; use CDN to absorb volumetric attacks at edge

threat_vector: OpenID Connect provider accepts 'none' algorithm for token signatures; pac4j v5.3.0 and prior fails to reject unsigned tokens without explicit configuration
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts JWT with 'alg': 'none' header and no signature. Vulnerable pac4j accepts token without cryptographic verification, allowing attacker to forge arbitrary identity claims and bypass authentication
detection_pattern: Monitor for: JWT tokens with 'alg': 'none' in header during OIDC authentication flows; log entries showing token acceptance without signature verification; authentication events where token validation warnings are absent; security event logs from pac4j indicating algorithm acceptance without strict validation
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade pac4j to v5.4.0 or later which enforces algorithm validation by default; explicitly configure 'none' algorithm as rejected in OIDC provider settings; implement server-side JWT validation that rejects any token with 'alg': 'none' before pac4j processing; enforce algorithm allowlist configuration requiring RS256, ES256, or equivalent asymmetric algorithms only; add WAF rules to block authentication requests containing 'none' algorithm tokens

threat_vector: HHEE system Code Injection vulnerability
exploitation_method: Attacker injects malicious code through unsanitized input parameters (HTTP requests, API calls, or database queries) that are executed by the HHEE system without proper validation or encoding, allowing arbitrary command execution or unauthorized code execution within the application context
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unusual characters or escape sequences in input logs (backslashes, quotes, semicolons, shell metacharacters); (2) Web application firewall alerts on suspicious payloads; (3) Unexpected process execution from HHEE application processes; (4) Failed input validation errors in application logs; (5) Correlation between specific input patterns and abnormal code execution; (6) Error messages revealing code structure or system details; (7) Increased exception handling logs with syntax-related errors
mitigation_strategy: Implement input validation using allowlist approach for all user inputs; use parameterized queries/prepared statements for database interactions; employ proper output encoding based on context (HTML, URL, JavaScript); implement Web Application Firewall (WAF) rules to block injection patterns; conduct secure code review and static application security testing (SAST); use principle of least privilege for HHEE process execution; apply security patches promptly; implement runtime application self-protection (RASP); maintain comprehensive logging and monitoring of code execution sources

threat_vector: Invalid or withdrawn CVE candidate exploitation attempt
exploitation_method: Attacker references non-existent or withdrawn CVE identifiers to exploit ambiguity in vulnerability tracking systems, potentially masking actual exploits or causing confusion in patch management workflows
detection_pattern: Log entries showing CVE references that resolve to withdrawn status, anomalies in vulnerability scanning tools reporting invalid CVE IDs, discrepancies between claimed vulnerabilities and official CVE database records, gap in patch management timelines where false CVEs cause delays
mitigation_strategy: Validate all CVE references against authoritative NVD/CVE database in real-time before initiating remediation; implement verification workflow requiring CNA confirmation; monitor for references to withdrawn candidates in security communications; train security teams to cross-reference CVE IDs with official sources before allocating remediation resources

threat_vector: Incorrect authorization vulnerability in KONICA MINOLTA bizhub series multifunction devices allowing unauthorized access to sensitive functions and data
exploitation_method: Attacker bypasses authentication/authorization checks through direct API calls or web interface requests to administrative functions without proper credential validation, potentially exploiting missing or insufficient access control checks on device management endpoints
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unauthorized web requests to /admin, /settings, /api endpoints from non-authenticated sessions; repeated 200 OK responses to privileged operations without prior authentication tokens; access logs showing administrative function execution without corresponding login events; unusual device configuration changes without admin user correlation; network traffic to management ports (8080, 9090, 631) from unexpected sources
mitigation_strategy: Apply latest firmware updates from KONICA MINOLTA immediately; enforce network segmentation isolating bizhub devices to trusted administrative networks only; implement strict access controls requiring multi-factor authentication for all administrative interfaces; disable unnecessary protocols and services; configure device firewall rules to restrict management port access; implement monitoring and alerting for unauthorized configuration changes; regularly audit device access logs and user permissions; use VPN for remote device management

threat_vector: DNS ID mismatch vulnerability in dnslib <= 0.9.16 allows DNS cache poisoning and response spoofing
exploitation_method: Attacker sends crafted DNS replies with mismatched transaction IDs that the vulnerable dnslib library accepts, allowing injection of malicious DNS records into the resolver's cache or application memory
detection_pattern: Monitor for: DNS responses with transaction IDs not matching corresponding queries in application logs; unexpected DNS record changes in cache; multiple failed DNS lookups followed by successful responses from unexpected sources; packet captures showing DNS reply ID values outside expected range or sequence
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade dnslib to version 0.9.17 or later; implement DNS response validation at application layer to verify transaction ID matches query; use DNSSEC validation; restrict DNS queries to trusted resolvers only; implement DNS response rate limiting; monitor DNS traffic for anomalies using network IDS

threat_vector: Denial of Service in Binaryen 104 - Assertion Abort in wasm::WasmBinaryBuilder::readFunctions
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts a malformed WebAssembly binary file with invalid function metadata that triggers an assertion failure in the readFunctions parser, causing the process to abort unexpectedly and denying service to legitimate users
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unexpected crashes/SIGABRT signals in Binaryen processes, (2) Error logs containing 'readFunctions' and assertion failures, (3) Sudden termination of wasm compilation/validation services, (4) Repeated failed wasm binary parsing attempts with same malformed signatures, (5) Stack traces referencing wasm::WasmBinaryBuilder::readFunctions
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Binaryen to version 105 or later that includes assertion handling fixes; implement input validation and sanitization of WebAssembly binaries before parsing; use try-catch exception handling around wasm binary processing; deploy rate limiting on wasm compilation requests; run Binaryen in isolated processes with resource constraints to limit blast radius of crashes; implement graceful error handling that logs issues without aborting

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1003.002 - OS Credential Dumping: Security Account Manager (SAM)
exploitation_method: Attacker extracts the SAM registry hive file (typically located at %SystemRoot%\System32\config\SAM) containing local user account password hashes, then performs offline password cracking or pass-the-hash attacks. Often requires SYSTEM privileges or physical access to boot into alternate OS.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: registry hive access via Volume Shadow Copy Service (vssadmin), cmd.exe/powershell.exe spawning with registry operations, unusual SAM file reads, failed attempts to access HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SAM, suspicious reg.exe or regedit.exe usage, Event ID 4657 (Registry object was accessed), Event ID 4663 (Attempt to access object), abnormal file copy operations targeting System32\config\, mimikatz/wce process execution, SYSTEM privilege escalation followed by registry access
mitigation_strategy: Enable BitLocker full-disk encryption to protect SAM at rest; restrict SYSTEM-level access via privileged access management (PAM); enforce Windows Defender Credential Guard to protect LSASS process; apply principle of least privilege; monitor and restrict RunAs/SeDebugPrivilege; disable local account usage where possible; enforce strong password policies; use Windows Hello/PIN instead of password hashes; regularly patch systems; implement EDR solutions detecting credential dumping tools; configure audit policies for registry access (HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SAM); isolate legacy systems; maintain offline password cracking defenses with bcrypt/PBKDF2 iteration counts

threat_vector: IBM PowerVM Hypervisor (FW860, FW940, FW950, FW1010) VM isolation bypass via malformed management operations
exploitation_method: Attacker with hypervisor management access sends crafted VM management operation sequences that exploit state management flaws in the hypervisor kernel, allowing memory or resource access across VM boundaries. The specific sequence manipulates VM state transitions during partition configuration changes, exposing peer VM memory pages or device I/O mappings.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unusual sequences of VM management API calls with rapidly changing partition configuration states; (2) HMC/PHYP logs showing consecutive partition state change operations without normal inter-operation delays; (3) Unexpected memory access violations in hypervisor logs; (4) VM migration or configuration change operations followed by cross-VM memory parity errors; (5) Audit trail gaps in partition state consistency checks; (6) Performance anomalies indicating unauthorized shared resource access between VMs
mitigation_strategy: Immediately apply IBM security updates to affected firmware versions (upgrade to FW1020 or later patched builds). Implement strict RBAC on hypervisor management operations, restricting partition configuration changes to trusted administrative accounts. Enable hypervisor audit logging with integrity protection. Deploy network segmentation isolating HMC management interface. Implement VM-level memory encryption (DIME) if available. Monitor and alert on all partition state change sequences. Conduct periodic VM isolation verification testing and consider temporary workload segregation pending patch validation.

threat_vector: NocoDB CSV Injection via Formula Injection (CVE-2023-26897) - versions 0.81.0 to 0.83.8
exploitation_method: Attacker with low privileges creates a table and injects malicious formulas into cell data (e.g., =cmd|'/c calc'!A0 or similar spreadsheet formula syntax). When the table is exported to CSV or imported into spreadsheet applications, the formula is executed with user privileges.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Cell data containing formula prefixes (=, +, -, @, tab character) in audit logs; (2) Unusual CSV export requests from low-privilege accounts; (3) Requests to create tables followed immediately by bulk row inserts with formula-like patterns; (4) Exported CSV files containing formula injection indicators in content scanning; (5) Application logs showing formula evaluation attempts in data cells
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade NocoDB to version 0.84.0 or later. Implement input validation to strip or escape formula prefix characters (=, +, -, @, tab) from cell values before storage. Apply cell data sanitization on export by prefixing suspicious values with single quote. Restrict CSV export functionality to authorized users only. Implement content security policy for spreadsheet imports. Monitor and alert on suspicious formula patterns in database contents.

threat_vector: Beaver Themer conditional logic bypass in post archives allowing unauthorized content visibility
exploitation_method: Attacker exploits improper evaluation of conditional logic rules when Beaver Themer layout is applied to archive pages. The theme fails to properly enforce content visibility conditions during archive rendering, potentially exposing restricted content that should be hidden based on user role, post status, or other conditional criteria.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unexpected content appearing in archive page HTML that matches hidden/restricted post patterns; (2) Discrepancies between post visibility rules in theme settings vs. actual rendered output on archive pages; (3) Access logs showing archive page requests followed by retrieval of posts marked as conditionally hidden; (4) Database queries showing posts with conditional visibility flags being returned for archive templates; (5) Beaver Themer render logs showing conditional rules skipped or bypassed during archive processing
mitigation_strategy: Implement server-side content filtering on archive pages independent of theme rendering; enforce conditional visibility checks at the WordPress query layer before content reaches the theme; regularly audit Beaver Themer version for security patches; disable dynamic theme rendering for archives if conditional logic cannot be verified; add capability checks before post data is passed to archive templates; use post status and role-based filtering at the WP_Query level rather than relying solely on theme-level conditionals

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Local Account (T1087.001)
exploitation_method: Attacker discovers and leverages local user accounts on compromised system through credential enumeration (net user, /etc/passwd parsing), credential access from stored files, or account takeover. May use weak passwords, privilege escalation paths, or lateral movement between local accounts.
detection_pattern: Successful login attempts from unusual times/locations, rapid failed authentication attempts followed by success, unexpected elevation of privileges for local accounts, creation of new local accounts outside normal provisioning processes, scheduled task creation under different user context, access to sensitive files by previously dormant accounts, unusual process execution under different user session (Process Explorer parent/child relationships)
mitigation_strategy: Implement strong password policies (complexity, length, expiration), disable unnecessary local accounts, use multi-factor authentication on local accounts where possible, enforce principle of least privilege, monitor and restrict local account creation via Group Policy (Windows) or PAM/sudoers (Linux), enable and analyze Windows Event Log 4624/4625 (login attempts), implement endpoint detection and response (EDR) for privilege escalation detection, conduct regular account audits and access reviews

threat_vector: ATT&CK: File System Permissions Weakness (T1222)
exploitation_method: Attacker exploits overly permissive file system ACLs or chmod settings to gain read/write/execute access to sensitive files or directories. This may involve modifying world-readable configurations, escalating privileges through writable system directories, or accessing credential files with improper protections.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unexpected changes to file permissions using auditd rules on chmod/chown/setfacl, (2) Access to sensitive files by unprivileged users (e.g., /etc/shadow, /root/.ssh), (3) World-writable or group-writable permissions on critical system paths detected by filesystem scanning, (4) Privilege escalation attempts following permission modifications in process execution logs, (5) Modifications to umask values in shell configurations
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict file system permissions: (1) Apply principle of least privilege with 0755 for directories and 0644 for files by default, (2) Use mandatory access controls (SELinux/AppArmor) to enforce fine-grained policies, (3) Audit and remediate world-writable or group-writable files regularly with tools like find -perm /022, (4) Restrict access to sensitive files (/etc/shadow, private keys) to owning user only, (5) Configure secure umask (0077) system-wide, (6) Implement file integrity monitoring (AIDE, Tripwire) to detect unauthorized permission changes, (7) Use immutable flags on critical system files, (8) Regular permission audits via configuration management

threat_vector: Stored XSS in Vehicle Service Management System 1.0 - Service List Section login panel
exploitation_method: Attacker injects malicious JavaScript payload into the Service List input field during login. The unsanitized payload is stored in the database and executed in the browsers of all users who view the Service List, enabling session hijacking, credential theft, or malware distribution.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) HTML/JavaScript tags in Service List database entries (script, iframe, onerror, onload tags); (2) Unusual Base64 or URL-encoded strings in service list fields; (3) Content Security Policy (CSP) violations in browser console logs; (4) Spike in outbound connections from user sessions to external domains; (5) Repeated failed input validation warnings in application logs; (6) Suspicious modifications to service list entries by administrative accounts
mitigation_strategy: Implement: (1) Input validation - whitelist allowed characters and reject any HTML/JavaScript metacharacters; (2) Output encoding - HTML-encode all Service List data before rendering in browser using context-aware encoding functions; (3) Content Security Policy (CSP) header with script-src 'self' to prevent inline script execution; (4) Use templating engines with auto-escaping enabled; (5) Regular security testing and code review of input handling; (6) Implement HTTP-only and Secure flags on session cookies; (7) Apply parameterized queries for database operations; (8) Web Application Firewall (WAF) rules to detect and block XSS payloads

threat_vector: CVE-2020-27339 - Duplicate CVE Assignment Confusion
exploitation_method: Attacker exploits security team confusion by referencing rejected or duplicate CVE identifiers, causing delayed patching or misdirected vulnerability tracking across systems and ticketing platforms
detection_pattern: Monitor CVE database queries for references to rejected/reserved CVE numbers; detect inconsistent vulnerability tracking across ticketing systems; identify security bulletins referencing invalid CVE identifiers; audit patch management systems for unresolved CVE references pointing to duplicates
mitigation_strategy: Implement CVE canonical source validation requiring patches to reference only NVD-confirmed CVE numbers; establish automated alerts for duplicate or rejected CVE references in security communications; maintain centralized CVE mapping database; train security teams on CVE lifecycle and validation procedures; implement CI/CD checks blocking patches that reference invalid CVE identifiers

threat_vector: Extra Window Memory Injection (T1181)
exploitation_method: Attacker injects malicious code into extra memory space allocated to a window object. Leverages WriteProcessMemory or similar APIs to write shellcode into window-associated memory regions, then triggers execution through window messaging or event handlers without triggering typical code injection detection.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unusual WriteProcessMemory calls targeting window processes; suspicious WM_* message handling with non-standard callbacks; memory allocation anomalies in USER32.dll regions; unsigned code execution in windows subsystem processes; unexpected changes to window procedure addresses; debug traces showing memory writes to 0x400000+ ranges in explorer.exe or other windowed processes
mitigation_strategy: Implement Control Flow Guard (CFG) and Arbitrary Code Guard (ACG) on Windows; enforce strict window message filtering and validation; disable unnecessary window messaging APIs where possible; use memory tagging extensions (MTE) on supported platforms; restrict WriteProcessMemory via AppLocker/WDAC policies; monitor and restrict hand-crafted window class registrations; apply principle of least privilege to UI-level processes; conduct code integrity checking on window procedure pointers

threat_vector: PendingIntent Hijacking in Reminder Application (Versions < 12.2.05.0 on Android R, < 12.3.02.1000 on Android S)
exploitation_method: Attackers exploit improper PendingIntent flag configuration lacking FLAG_IMMUTABLE or FLAG_UPDATE_CURRENT protections, allowing third-party applications to intercept, modify intent payloads, and execute privileged actions with Reminder app permissions through intent interception.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unexpected permission elevation in logcat showing Reminder app performing actions outside normal scope; (2) Multiple applications registering broadcast receivers for Reminder intents; (3) Intent interception attempts in SecurityException logs; (4) Unusual IPC calls between untrusted apps and Reminder service; (5) Persistent intent modifications captured via AndroidManifest permission audits; (6) Anomalous reminder execution patterns not triggered by user interaction.
mitigation_strategy: Enforce immediate patching to versions 12.2.05.0+ (Android R) or 12.3.02.1000+ (Android S). Implement defensive measures: (1) Use FLAG_IMMUTABLE for all PendingIntent creations; (2) Implement intent signature verification; (3) Restrict exported components via android:exported='false'; (4) Deploy runtime permission enforcement; (5) Monitor app version compliance via MDM solutions; (6) Apply SELinux policies restricting inter-process communication for Reminder service.

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1547.012 - Print Processors
exploitation_method: Attacker modifies or creates malicious print processor DLLs in System32\spool\prtprocs\w3x86 or w3x64 directories. Windows loads these unsigned DLLs at startup or print service initialization with SYSTEM privileges, executing arbitrary code before security checks.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unauthorized DLL creation/modification in spool\prtprocs directories with suspicious timestamps; (2) Print spooler service (spoolsv.exe) spawning unexpected child processes; (3) File integrity monitoring alerts on prtprocs folder; (4) Registry modification to HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Print\Environments\Windows x64\Print Processors; (5) Unsigned or invalid digital signatures on processor DLLs; (6) Event ID 7045 (service installation) related to print services
mitigation_strategy: (1) Implement application whitelisting for System32\spool\prtprocs directories; (2) Enable Code Integrity/Device Guard to enforce signed driver and DLL loading; (3) Set file permissions restricting write access to prtprocs folders to administrators only; (4) Disable Print Spooler service if not required, or restrict it to trusted networks; (5) Deploy file integrity monitoring (FIM) on critical spool directories; (6) Regularly audit and validate legitimate print processors; (7) Keep Windows and printer drivers fully patched

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Modify Cloud Resource Hierarchy
exploitation_method: Attacker with elevated cloud credentials uses management console, APIs, or infrastructure-as-code tools to alter organizational units, resource groups, subscriptions, or folder structures. Changes allow privilege escalation, policy circumvention, or billing isolation to hide malicious activities.
detection_pattern: Monitor CloudTrail/audit logs for CreateFolder, MoveNode, UpdateFolder, UpdateSubscription, UpdateResourceGroup, or ModifyOrganizationalUnit API calls from non-standard principals or at unusual times. Alert on rapid hierarchy changes, cross-tenant movements, or modifications that bypass compliance baselines. Detect policy inheritance changes affecting security controls.
mitigation_strategy: Enforce role-based access control limiting hierarchy modification to specific admin roles. Require multi-factor authentication and approval workflows for resource hierarchy changes. Implement resource locks and policy-as-code to prevent unauthorized organizational structure modifications. Enable immutable audit logging with separate retention. Use organization policy to restrict hierarchy changes and centralize governance. Regular audits of organizational structure against baseline.

threat_vector: livehelperchat is vulnerable to Generation of Error Message Containing Sensitive Information
exploitation_method: Attacker triggers application errors through malformed requests, SQL injection attempts, or invalid input to livehelperchat endpoints. The application returns verbose error messages containing sensitive details such as database connection strings, file paths, SQL queries, internal IP addresses, or stack traces that reveal system architecture and vulnerabilities.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) HTTP responses with status codes 400-599 containing database credentials or connection strings in response body; (2) Log entries showing stack traces in user-facing responses; (3) Error messages containing absolute file paths or internal server paths; (4) Responses revealing software versions or framework details; (5) Repeated error triggering from same source IP; (6) WAF/IDS alerts for error-based information disclosure patterns
mitigation_strategy: Implement generic error pages that do not expose technical details to end users. Log detailed errors server-side only for administrators. Set custom error handlers for common HTTP status codes. Configure application to display user-friendly messages while logging full stack traces separately. Disable debug mode and verbose error reporting in production. Use Web Application Firewall rules to sanitize error responses before sending to clients. Regularly audit error messages in application logs and responses for sensitive information leakage.

threat_vector: Symlink following vulnerability in Trend Micro Worry-Free Business Security on-premises allowing arbitrary file overwrite with SYSTEM privileges
exploitation_method: Local attacker creates malicious symlinks in predictable temporary directories or cache locations that the Trend Micro service monitors or processes. When the service follows these symlinks without proper validation, it overwrites arbitrary files (system binaries, configuration files, or security tools) with attacker-controlled content, executing code with SYSTEM privileges.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unusual symlink creation in %TEMP%, %ProgramData%, or Trend Micro cache directories; file write operations to system-protected paths from Trend Micro processes (tmproxy.exe, ntrtscan.exe); access denied errors followed by successful overwrites; integrity changes to critical system files; symlink creation/follow operations in application logs with mismatched source/target paths
mitigation_strategy: Apply latest Trend Micro security patches immediately; restrict local user permissions to prevent symlink creation in service-accessible directories; implement File Integrity Monitoring (FIM) on critical system binaries and Trend Micro configuration files; run Trend Micro services with minimal required privileges; use OS-level protections (Windows Defender Application Guard); monitor and restrict temporary directory access; deploy SACL auditing on sensitive system paths; isolate on-premises installations from untrusted local user accounts

threat_vector: Denial of Service in Binaryen 104 - Assertion abort in wasm::WasmBinaryBuilder::visitRethrow()
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts a malformed WebAssembly binary with invalid rethrow instruction context (e.g., rethrow outside try-catch block or with corrupted exception handling metadata). When Binaryen processes this binary during parsing/compilation, the visitRethrow() function hits an assertion that aborts the process, causing DoS.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unexpected process crashes with SIGABRT when processing WASM binaries; (2) Error logs containing 'visitRethrow' or assertion failures during WASM parsing; (3) Abnormal termination of Binaryen tools (wasm-opt, wasm-as) with core dumps; (4) Repeated failed WASM compilation attempts from same source with exception handling instructions; (5) Resource exhaustion patterns in WASM processing pipelines
mitigation_strategy: Update Binaryen to version 105+ which patches the assertion vulnerability. Implement input validation: (1) Validate WASM binary structure before processing, specifically exception handling constructs; (2) Use exception handling around Binaryen library calls to gracefully handle crashes; (3) Implement resource limits and timeouts for WASM processing; (4) Run WASM parsing in isolated sandbox/container; (5) Add pre-processing checks to detect malformed rethrow instructions before passing to visitRethrow(); (6) Enable robust error handling instead of assertions in production builds

threat_vector: Guest can force Linux netback driver to hog large amounts of kernel memory
exploitation_method: Malicious guest VM sends specially crafted network packets or requests to the netback driver that cause it to allocate excessive kernel memory buffers without proper limits or cleanup, potentially leading to denial of service on the host system
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Abnormal kernel memory growth correlated with specific guest VM network activity; (2) netback driver memory allocation spikes in /proc/meminfo or kernel logs; (3) Increased skbuff pool exhaustion messages; (4) dmesg entries indicating 'out of memory' conditions or netback-related allocation failures; (5) Persistent high page cache pressure from single guest VM; (6) netstat or ethtool showing queue buildup from specific guest
mitigation_strategy: Apply Linux kernel patches addressing netback memory limits; implement per-VM network resource quotas via cgroups v2 memory limits; configure xenbus network rate limiting; enable memory.max enforcement for guest domains; implement network packet rate limiting at the netback layer; monitor and cap skbuffer allocations per guest; use memory pressure monitoring to detect and throttle misbehaving guests; regularly update Xen and Linux kernel to versions with netback driver hardening patches

threat_vector: Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI) in Jinja2
exploitation_method: Attacker injects malicious Jinja2 template syntax (e.g., {{7*7}}, {{config}}, {{self.__init__.__globals__.__builtins__.__import__('os').popen('cmd').read()}}) into user-controlled input that gets rendered by the Jinja2 template engine without proper sanitization, allowing arbitrary code execution on the server
detection_pattern: Monitor for: template expressions in user input ({{, {%, {#), unusual function calls in logs (.__init__, __globals__, __builtins__), subprocess/os module imports in web request contexts, template rendering errors with stack traces revealing internal structure, abnormal process spawning from web application processes, template syntax characters in HTTP parameters/headers
mitigation_strategy: 1) Never pass unsanitized user input directly to template render functions; 2) Use Jinja2's autoescape feature (autoescape=True); 3) Implement strict input validation with allowlists for template variables; 4) Use sandboxed environment (jinja2.sandbox.SandboxedEnvironment) to restrict dangerous functions; 5) Disable dangerous built-ins via restricted_python; 6) Apply principle of least privilege to application process; 7) Use Web Application Firewall (WAF) rules to block template syntax patterns in requests; 8) Implement Content Security Policy (CSP) headers; 9) Regular code review for template rendering patterns; 10) Keep Jinja2 and dependencies patched

threat_vector: Spinnaker AppEngine TAR extraction path traversal vulnerability
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious TAR archive with directory traversal sequences (e.g., ../../../) in filenames. When Spinnaker extracts the TAR during AppEngine deployment, files are written outside intended directory, potentially overwriting critical application files or configuration
detection_pattern: Monitor for: TAR extraction errors with path traversal indicators in logs; file writes outside deployment sandbox directory; unexpected file modifications in parent directories during deployment; archive entries containing ../ sequences; Spinnaker deployment logs showing symlink or absolute path warnings
mitigation_strategy: Update Spinnaker to patched version; implement TAR extraction validation to reject entries with ../ or absolute paths; use sandboxed extraction with chroot/containerization; enforce strict file permissions on deployment directories; implement pre-deployment TAR integrity checks; configure AppEngine deployment policies to deny file writes outside designated zones

threat_vector: Memory corruption via race condition in VOW driver enabling local privilege escalation
exploitation_method: Attacker triggers concurrent access to shared kernel memory in VOW driver without proper synchronization, causing use-after-free or buffer overflow conditions that allow arbitrary kernel code execution with SYSTEM privileges
detection_pattern: Monitor for: kernel panic dumps with VOW driver in call stack, multiple rapid IOCTLs to VOW device from same process, memory access violations in driver logs, unexpected privilege elevation from unprivileged user process, kernel address sanitizer (KASAN) warnings for VOW driver, simultaneous write operations to /dev/vow* devices
mitigation_strategy: Apply kernel patches adding proper mutex/spinlock synchronization to VOW driver shared memory access, implement reference counting for memory objects, enable kernel ASLR and DEP/NX protections, restrict /dev/vow* device access via MAC policy (SELinux/AppArmor), use containers/VMs with reduced privileges, monitor and log all VOW driver interactions, consider disabling VOW driver if not required

threat_vector: HTML injection via MAIN_MAX_DECIMALS_TOT parameter in admin/limits.php (Dolibarr 7.0.2)
exploitation_method: Attacker with admin access submits malicious HTML/JavaScript payload in the MAIN_MAX_DECIMALS_TOT configuration parameter. The application fails to sanitize user input before storing and displaying the value, allowing arbitrary HTML/script execution in the admin interface when the configuration page is viewed.
detection_pattern: Monitor access logs for POST requests to /admin/limits.php with unusual parameter values containing HTML tags (<, >, script, onerror, onclick). Check database for config entries with MAIN_MAX_DECIMALS_TOT containing HTML entities or script tags. Review browser console errors indicating injected scripts. Alert on config changes to sensitive parameters containing markup characters.
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Dolibarr to version 7.0.3 or later with input validation patches. Implement strict output encoding of all configuration parameters using htmlspecialchars() with ENT_QUOTES flag. Enforce Content Security Policy headers (script-src 'self'). Restrict admin/limits.php access via IP whitelisting and require additional authentication factors for configuration changes. Use parameterized input validation to accept only numeric values for MAIN_MAX_DECIMALS_TOT parameter.

threat_vector: Improper sanitization of incoming intent in Dressroom prior to SMR Jan-2022 Release 1 allows local attackers to read and write arbitrary files without permission
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious intent objects with unsanitized file path parameters that bypass input validation, allowing direct file system operations through Dressroom's intent handling without proper permission checks or path canonicalization
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Intent objects with file path parameters containing traversal sequences (../, ..\ , encoded variants), (2) Dressroom process attempting file operations outside expected application directories, (3) Unexpected file read/write access to sensitive system or user data locations, (4) System calls from Dressroom with file descriptors pointing to unauthorized paths, (5) SELinux/AppArmor denials for Dressroom file access attempts
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade to SMR Jan-2022 Release 1 or later; implement strict input validation on all intent parameters with whitelisting of allowed characters and path components; enforce canonical path resolution before any file operation; apply principle of least privilege restricting Dressroom process to specific directory trees; use OS-level access controls (SELinux, AppArmor) to confine file system access; conduct code review of intent parsing logic and implement automated fuzz testing of intent handlers

threat_vector: MediaWiki WikibaseMediaInfo XSS via caption fields (CVE-2021-44521)
exploitation_method: Attacker injects malicious JavaScript code into media file caption fields in WikibaseMediaInfo. Due to insufficient input sanitization and output encoding, the payload executes in victims' browsers when they view the media file page or caption data.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) POST/PUT requests to WikibaseMediaInfo caption endpoints containing script tags, event handlers (onclick, onerror), or encoded JavaScript; (2) HTML entities like <script>, javascript: protocol in caption fields; (3) Unusual characters or encoding patterns in caption parameters; (4) Browser console errors indicating blocked script execution; (5) CSP violation reports if Content-Security-Policy is enforced; (6) Database audit logs showing caption values containing HTML/JavaScript syntax
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade MediaWiki to version 1.35.5, 1.36.3, 1.37.1 or later. Implement strict input validation and sanitization on all caption fields using MediaWiki's Html::escapeTagsOnly() or equivalent. Enable Content-Security-Policy headers with script-src restrictions. Apply output encoding to all caption display contexts. Review and restrict user permissions for caption editing. Consider disabling WikibaseMediaInfo if unused.

threat_vector: Improper input validation in Shockwall system programming function allowing authenticated local attacker to send malicious responses
exploitation_method: Authenticated attacker on LAN sends crafted responses to Shockwall server that bypass input validation checks in the programming function handler, allowing injection of malicious commands or corrupted data that causes service disruption or system instability
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) unexpected response packets from untrusted LAN sources to Shockwall service ports; (2) malformed data in programming function API calls with special characters or oversized payloads; (3) error logs showing input validation failures or parsing exceptions in Shockwall logs; (4) sudden service crashes or restarts following specific request patterns; (5) anomalous process behavior after receiving programming function calls; (6) audit logs showing authenticated sessions initiating unusual programming operations
mitigation_strategy: (1) Implement strict whitelist-based input validation for all programming function parameters with length limits and character restrictions; (2) Deploy network segmentation isolating Shockwall systems to trusted LAN zones only; (3) Require mutual TLS authentication for all API requests; (4) Apply latest security patches and conduct code review of input handlers; (5) Implement rate limiting on programming function endpoints; (6) Enable comprehensive audit logging of all programming operations; (7) Use Web Application Firewall rules to block requests with suspicious payloads; (8) Perform regular security testing and fuzzing of input validation

threat_vector: Qt SVG out-of-bounds write in QCommonArrayOps::growAppend via malformed SVG path elements (CVE-2021-45930)
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious SVG file with specially constructed path elements that trigger buffer overflow in QPainterPath::addPath or QPathClipper operations, causing out-of-bounds memory write during array growth operations in affected Qt versions
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Crashes in QtSvg/QtGui modules with stack trace containing QCommonArrayOps::growAppend or QPainterPath::addPath, (2) Memory corruption events in SVG rendering processes, (3) Unexpected termination of applications processing SVG files, (4) AddressSanitizer/Valgrind reports of heap-buffer-overflow in Qt painting code, (5) SVG files with unusually deep or complex path nesting, (6) Application segmentation faults when rendering specific SVG inputs
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade to Qt 5.15.3+ or 6.2.2+ immediately; implement SVG input validation to reject files with excessive path complexity or nesting depth; run SVG rendering in sandboxed processes with memory protection enabled; use AddressSanitizer in development; implement file type whitelisting and disable SVG rendering if not required; apply principle of least privilege to SVG processing services

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Dynamic Data Exchange (DDE)
exploitation_method: Attacker embeds DDE formulas in Office documents (Excel, Word) that automatically execute when opened. The formula references external data sources or executes system commands through cmd.exe or PowerShell without user interaction or macro warnings.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Office files containing '=cmd' or '=powershell' strings in document properties/content, (2) Office applications spawning cmd.exe or powershell.exe processes without user interaction, (3) Network connections from Office applications to unusual external resources, (4) Event logs showing Office processes creating child processes with suspicious command lines, (5) DDE protocol traffic on ports 135-139 or 445
mitigation_strategy: Disable DDE in Office via Group Policy (HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Microsoft\Office\[version]\Word\Security set DisableDDE=1), block Office macro execution via AppLocker, implement AMSI-based detection for script execution, require macro security settings to prompt all macros, maintain updated Office patches, restrict Office application permissions using AppContainer/sandboxing, and deploy email filtering to scan document attachments for DDE formulas before delivery

threat_vector: Zoho ManageEngine Desktop Central <10.0.662 - Authenticated Information Disclosure via Reports Page
exploitation_method: Authenticated attacker accesses the Reports page in vulnerable Desktop Central versions to query and extract sensitive database information such as credentials, device details, or configuration data through insufficient access controls or SQL injection vectors in report generation functionality
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unusual database queries from Reports page with parameters accessing credential tables or sensitive configuration data; (2) Multiple report generation requests with varying filter parameters targeting user/device/credential data; (3) Export or download of reports containing plaintext credentials or sensitive information; (4) Access logs showing authenticated users accessing Reports page followed by database access patterns inconsistent with normal operations; (5) Anomalous SQL patterns in application logs containing UNION, SELECT from sys tables, or credential-related queries
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade to ManageEngine Desktop Central 10.0.662 or later; implement role-based access controls restricting report access to necessary personnel only; disable report export functionality for sensitive data; enable database query logging and alerts for suspicious SELECT statements; implement input validation and parameterized queries for all report filters; conduct database access reviews to identify overly-permissive account privileges; segment database access by user role; regularly audit report generation logs for unauthorized data access patterns

threat_vector: Apache Pluto 3.1.0 MVCBean JSP portlet maven archetype first name and last name fields vulnerable to Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
exploitation_method: Attacker injects malicious JavaScript code (e.g., <script>alert('XSS')</script> or event handlers) into the first name or last name input fields. The application fails to sanitize or encode these values before rendering them in the JSP template, causing the injected script to execute in the victim's browser context with the application's privileges.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: HTML/JavaScript special characters in form submissions (< > ' " ; etc.), JSP rendering of unsanitized user input variables, missing XSS filter headers (X-XSS-Protection, Content-Security-Policy), reflected JavaScript execution in response bodies, browser console errors indicating script injection attempts, HTTP requests containing encoded payloads (%3Cscript%3E, <script>)
mitigation_strategy: Implement output encoding in JSP templates using JSTL <c:out> tag with escapeXml='true', apply input validation whitelist for name fields (alphanumeric, spaces, hyphens only), configure Content-Security-Policy headers to prevent inline script execution, use Spring Security or Apache Commons Text StringEscapeUtils for encoding, enable X-XSS-Protection header, perform code review of MVCBean template to identify all user input rendering points, upgrade to patched Apache Pluto version if available, implement context-aware output encoding based on HTML, JavaScript, or URL context where data appears

threat_vector: Race condition in QoS request handler due to improper validation when multiple simultaneous add or update requests are received
exploitation_method: Attacker sends multiple concurrent QoS add/update requests with crafted parameters that bypass individual request validation. The race condition allows multiple requests to pass validation checks before mutual exclusion locks are acquired, causing assertion failures or memory corruption when requests execute out of order
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Multiple QoS requests from same source within <100ms window; (2) Kernel assertions or panics in QoS subsystem with stack traces referencing validation or state management functions; (3) Inconsistent QoS configuration states in system logs; (4) High-frequency add/update operations on same QoS flow identifier; (5) Out-of-sequence request processing indicated by request ID ordering mismatches in audit logs
mitigation_strategy: Implement thread-safe request queuing with proper locking before validation; serialize QoS requests using mutexes or atomic operations that cover both validation and state update phases; add idempotency checking using request tokens to reject duplicate concurrent submissions; implement bounds checking and state validation within critical sections; deploy input rate limiting per source (max requests/second); add comprehensive assertion logging with context preservation for debugging; apply vendor security patches and enable runtime memory protection features

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1027.011 - Embedded Payloads (Obfuscation/Stealth)
exploitation_method: Attacker embeds malicious code within legitimate files (documents, images, archives, firmware) using steganography, polyglot files, or container formats. Payload executes when file is processed by vulnerable application or custom handler.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: file entropy anomalies in documents/images, mismatched file headers/magic bytes, unusual embedded object execution, process spawning from document/media handlers, suspicious temporary file extraction, abnormal file size increases, metadata inconsistencies, and execution of code from unexpected file types via WMI/COM objects.
mitigation_strategy: Implement: strict file type validation via magic bytes not extensions, disable macros/active content in office documents via group policy, use application whitelisting for file handlers, sandbox file processing, maintain updated document/media parsers, disable unnecessary codecs/handlers, enforce code signing verification, monitor process lineage from office/media apps, educate users on file handling, and maintain file integrity monitoring on critical assets.

threat_vector: IBM Security Verify 10.0.0, 10.0.1.0, 10.0.2.0 sensitive information disclosure via improper input validation in QR code generation (IBM X-Force ID: 212040)
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious input to the QR code generation function that bypasses input validation controls. Improperly sanitized user-supplied data is embedded directly into QR code generation processes, causing sensitive authentication tokens, session identifiers, or configuration data to be exposed in generated QR codes or error responses.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) QR code generation requests with unusual parameter lengths or special characters in input fields; (2) Error messages containing authentication tokens, API keys, or session IDs in QR generation endpoints; (3) Repeated requests to QR generation APIs with progressively larger or malformed payloads; (4) Log entries showing QR code data containing secrets matching regex patterns for tokens/credentials; (5) HTTP responses from /qrcode or /generate endpoints exceeding expected size; (6) Abnormal characters in query parameters passed to QR generation functions
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade IBM Security Verify to patched versions (10.0.3.0 or later). Implement strict input validation: whitelist allowed characters for QR input, enforce maximum length limits, and reject special characters unnecessary for QR encoding. Apply output encoding to all user inputs before QR generation. Implement web application firewall rules to block requests with suspicious payloads to QR endpoints. Disable QR generation functionality if not required. Conduct security scanning of generated QR codes in test environments. Enable detailed audit logging for QR generation requests and review logs regularly for anomalies.

threat_vector: Stored XSS in Bludit 3.13.1 TAGS section of login panel
exploitation_method: Attacker injects malicious JavaScript payload into the TAGS input field during authentication. The payload is stored in the database and executed in the browsers of users who view the affected page, potentially stealing session cookies or performing actions on behalf of victims.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) HTML/JavaScript entities in TAGS database fields (script tags, event handlers like 'onerror', 'onload'); (2) Web server logs showing POST requests to login/tag endpoints with encoded payloads; (3) Unusual DOM modifications or unexpected script execution in browser developer console; (4) WAF logs flagging requests containing <script>, javascript:, or event handlers in TAGS parameters
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Bludit to version 3.14.0 or later with XSS protections. Implement output encoding/HTML entity encoding for all TAGS display. Use Content Security Policy (CSP) headers to restrict inline script execution. Apply input validation to reject or sanitize tags containing HTML/JS syntax. Conduct security code review of TAGS input/output handling. Implement HTTPOnly and Secure flags on session cookies to prevent JavaScript access.

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Archive via Utility (T1560.001)
exploitation_method: Attacker uses built-in compression utilities (tar, zip, rar, 7z) to archive sensitive files before exfiltration, bypassing file-by-file detection and reducing data transfer volume
detection_pattern: Process execution logs showing compression utilities invoked with suspicious arguments; archive files created in temp directories or user profile paths; high volume of files accessed immediately before compression; network transfers of .zip/.tar/.rar files from unexpected locations; command line contains wildcard patterns or credential paths
mitigation_strategy: Deploy application whitelisting to restrict compression utility execution to authorized users/processes; monitor process creation events for compression tools with EDR; implement DLP rules to block archival of sensitive file types before egress; restrict compression utility access via group policy; log and alert on any archive creation containing credential files; disable or sandbox compression utilities on high-value systems; enforce code signing requirements for compression tools

threat_vector: Apache James STARTTLS Buffering Vulnerability (CVE-2021-26119) - Man-in-the-Middle Command Injection
exploitation_method: Attacker intercepts SMTP connection and injects commands into the TLS buffer during STARTTLS negotiation. The server processes buffered plaintext commands as encrypted traffic after TLS upgrade, allowing unauthorized SMTP commands (e.g., RCPT TO, DATA) to be executed without authentication.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) STARTTLS commands followed by unusual command sequences in connection logs; (2) SMTP commands appearing before successful TLS handshake completion; (3) Authentication bypass attempts post-STARTTLS; (4) Abnormal RCPT/DATA commands from unauthenticated sessions; (5) Buffer overflow errors in James SMTP handler logs; (6) Simultaneous cleartext and encrypted protocol states in single session.
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Apache James to version 3.6.1 or later immediately. Implement: (1) TLS-only policy requiring encryption before SMTP commands; (2) Clear input buffer on STARTTLS transition; (3) Network segmentation isolating SMTP service; (4) TCP connection rate limiting; (5) IDS/IPS signatures detecting STARTTLS buffer injection patterns; (6) Disable STARTTLS if not required, enforce implicit TLS on alternate port 465 instead; (7) Monitor James error logs for TLS state violations.

threat_vector: Session fixation after login
exploitation_method: Attacker pre-generates a known session ID, tricks user into authenticating with that ID, then hijacks the authenticated session using the known session identifier
detection_pattern: Session ID remains unchanged before and after authentication, multiple login events with identical session tokens, session IDs matching predictable patterns, session ID reuse across different user accounts, login events without corresponding session ID regeneration in auth logs
mitigation_strategy: Implement mandatory session ID regeneration immediately after successful authentication, use cryptographically random session tokens (minimum 128 bits entropy), set Secure and HttpOnly flags on session cookies, implement session binding to user agent and IP address, maintain whitelist of valid session IDs post-authentication, implement short session timeout periods with re-authentication requirements

threat_vector: ManageEngine ADSelfService Plus <6116 predictable password policy file exposure in web root
exploitation_method: Attacker enumerates domain names and constructs predictable URLs (e.g., /html/domainname_policy.txt) to access plaintext or weakly encoded password policy configurations stored in the web-accessible directory, potentially revealing policy details or credentials
detection_pattern: Monitor for: HTTP GET requests to /html/ directory with pattern *_policy.txt or *_policy.xml; unusual file access attempts with domain name enumeration patterns; access logs showing repeated 200 responses for non-existent policy files; file integrity monitoring on html/ subdirectories for unauthorized policy file creation or modification
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade to ManageEngine ADSelfService Plus build 6116 or later; move policy configuration files outside web root; implement strict access controls (deny /html/ directory listing and direct file access via web server configuration); restrict /html/ access to authenticated users only using .htaccess or firewall rules; implement file permission controls (remove world-readable on policy files); regularly audit web root contents for sensitive configuration files

threat_vector: Unauthenticated Datalogic DXU Service Configuration Access
exploitation_method: Attacker connects to the DXU service port on a Datalogic device (typically port 9000-9010 range) without credentials and sends service protocol commands to read sensitive configuration data or modify device settings including network parameters, scan modes, output formats, and operational thresholds.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Network connections to DXU service ports from unexpected sources; (2) DXU protocol commands in network traffic without prior authentication handshake; (3) Repeated GET/SET configuration requests from single source IP; (4) Unexpected changes to device configuration parameters logged in device audit trails; (5) DXU service traffic containing sensitive parameter strings (IP addresses, credentials, configuration identifiers); (6) Access to DXU ports from non-management network segments.
mitigation_strategy: Implement network segmentation restricting DXU service access to authorized management VLANs only; deploy firewall rules blocking DXU ports (typically 9000-9010) from untrusted networks; enable DXU service authentication if supported by firmware version; upgrade to patched firmware versions that enforce authentication; monitor and log all DXU service interactions; disable DXU service if not required for operations; implement network-level access controls using MAC filtering or certificate-based authentication; conduct regular configuration audits comparing running config against approved baselines.

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Vulnerabilities (T1190)
exploitation_method: Attacker identifies unpatched software vulnerabilities through vulnerability scanning, public exploit databases, or zero-day research, then delivers exploit code via malicious payloads, phishing attachments, or malicious websites to gain code execution, privilege escalation, or system compromise
detection_pattern: Unexpected process spawning from vulnerable applications; abnormal network connections from patched software; crash dumps or error logs indicating exploitation attempts; signature-based alerts matching known CVE exploits; unusual file modifications in application directories; failed authentication attempts followed by privilege escalation; traffic patterns matching known exploit delivery mechanisms
mitigation_strategy: Implement automated patch management with rapid deployment timelines; maintain comprehensive asset inventory with software versioning; deploy web application firewalls and intrusion prevention systems with vulnerability-specific signatures; conduct regular vulnerability assessments and penetration testing; apply principle of least privilege to limit exploit impact; isolate critical systems; enable exploit protection mechanisms (ASLR, DEP, CFG); use vulnerability management platforms with real-time CVE intelligence; establish incident response procedures for active exploitation

threat_vector: Password reset token predictability
exploitation_method: Attacker analyzes password reset tokens to identify patterns in generation (sequential, time-based, weak entropy). They predict or brute-force valid tokens for target accounts to perform unauthorized password resets.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: multiple failed token validation attempts from same IP, tokens reused across sessions, tokens with low entropy/simple patterns in audit logs, successful password resets without corresponding MFA challenges, reset requests followed by immediate login from unusual locations
mitigation_strategy: Implement cryptographically secure token generation using high-entropy randomness (minimum 256-bit). Set short expiration windows (15-30 minutes). Rate-limit password reset attempts per account/IP. Require email verification and time-based constraints. Log all reset token generation with hash values (never plaintext). Enforce MFA verification before accepting password reset. Use single-use tokens that invalidate after first attempt.

threat_vector: Heap-based buffer overflow in GPAC 1.0.1 MP4Box gp_rtp_builder_do_mpeg12_video function via crafted input
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts a malicious MPEG-12 video stream with oversized RTP payload data that exceeds allocated heap buffer size in the gp_rtp_builder_do_mpeg12_video function, causing heap memory corruption and potential code execution
detection_pattern: Monitor for: segmentation faults or SIGABRT signals from MP4Box process, heap corruption detection via ASAN/valgrind, unusual RTP packet sizes exceeding protocol limits in network traffic to MP4Box, crashes with stack traces referencing gp_rtp_builder_do_mpeg12_video, abnormal memory access patterns in heap regions allocated for video processing buffers
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade GPAC to version 1.1.0 or later with buffer overflow patch applied; implement input validation to reject MPEG-12 video streams with RTP payloads exceeding maximum buffer size; run MP4Box in sandboxed environment with memory protection enabled (ASLR, DEP/NX); disable RTP builder functionality if not required; use fuzzing regression tests on video input processing; apply compiler-level protections (stack canaries, bounds checking)

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1578.001 - Modify Cloud Compute Infrastructure (Create Compute Instance)
exploitation_method: Attacker with valid cloud credentials uses cloud provider API/console to create unauthorized compute instances, modify instance configurations, attach persistent storage, or alter security group rules to establish persistence or expand attack surface
detection_pattern: CloudTrail/audit logs showing: RunInstances, CreateInstance, ModifyInstanceAttribute, AuthorizeSecurityGroupIngress from unusual IAM principals; instances launched outside business hours; compute resource creation by service accounts; security group changes allowing unexpected protocols (RDP 3389, SSH 22 from 0.0.0.0); unusual instance types (GPU-accelerated); tags or naming patterns deviating from organizational standards; creation events followed by immediate IAM policy modification
mitigation_strategy: Implement least-privilege IAM policies restricting EC2/compute creation permissions; enable CloudTrail with log analysis and alerting on RunInstances/ModifyInstance events; use Service Control Policies (SCPs) blocking instance creation in non-approved regions; enforce MFA for privileged operations; implement automated resource tagging policies; regular audit of running instances against inventory; use AWS Config rules to enforce baseline configurations; implement VPC Flow Logs to detect unexpected traffic patterns from new instances; set resource budgets with alerts for unexpected compute provisioning costs

threat_vector: Arbitrary File Access leading to Directory Traversal with Sensitive Information Exposure
exploitation_method: Attacker manipulates file path parameters using traversal sequences (../, ..\, or encoded variants) to access files outside intended directory boundaries, potentially exposing configuration files, credentials, source code, or other sensitive data stored on the server filesystem.
