detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unusual property access patterns on __proto__ or constructor.prototype in application logs; (2) Exception handling bypasses or unexpected function access in sandboxed contexts; (3) Attempts to access global objects from within isolated realms; (4) Stack traces showing prototype chain traversal; (5) Runtime errors indicating prototype pollution (e.g., 'Cannot redefine property' warnings); (6) Use of Object.getPrototypeOf or setPrototypeOf calls with suspicious arguments
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade to patched versions of realms-shim beyond the vulnerable release. Implement Object.freeze() on built-in prototypes and critical prototype chains. Use Object.preventExtensions() on realm boundary objects. Deploy Content Security Policy to restrict script behavior. Implement input validation to block suspicious prototype-related property names (__proto__, constructor, prototype). Consider using alternative sandboxing mechanisms (Web Workers, separate processes) for critical isolation requirements. Apply strict object property whitelisting in realm-shim configuration.

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Query Public AI Services
exploitation_method: Attacker queries public AI services (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, etc.) to gather intelligence on target systems, craft malicious prompts, generate malware code, or obtain sensitive information that may have been published in training data or accessible through the AI's knowledge base.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: Unusual API calls to public AI service endpoints from internal networks; High volume of queries from single user/host to AI services; Queries containing organization-specific technical details, employee names, or system architectures; Outbound HTTPS traffic to ai service domains (openai.com, anthropic.com, google.com/generativeai) during off-hours; Correlation of AI query timing with subsequent attack indicators; User agents or request patterns inconsistent with legitimate tool usage.
mitigation_strategy: Implement egress filtering to restrict or monitor connections to public AI services; Deploy data loss prevention (DLP) rules to prevent pasting sensitive information into AI services; Conduct security awareness training on AI service risks; Monitor and log all web traffic for AI service access; Implement network segmentation separating sensitive systems from internet-facing networks; Use endpoint detection and response (EDR) to flag unusual AI service interactions; Establish acceptable use policies restricting AI service access for sensitive roles; Implement API key management and authentication controls if internal AI services are used instead.

threat_vector: CVE-2021-45960 - Expat libexpat < 2.4.3 integer overflow in storeAtts function
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malformed XML with excessive attributes causing left shift by 29+ bits in storeAtts() function, triggering integer overflow in realloc size calculation, resulting in heap buffer underallocation or incomplete memory deallocation
detection_pattern: Monitor for: libexpat library errors during XML parsing; heap allocation failures or mismatches in debugger/ASAN logs; XML documents with unusually high attribute counts (>2^29 attributes); memory corruption indicators in crash dumps; version detection showing expat < 2.4.3; unexpected process termination with SIGSEGV during XML processing
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade libexpat to version 2.4.3 or later; implement input validation to reject XML documents with excessively nested elements or attribute counts exceeding practical limits; use memory sanitizers (ASAN/MSAN) in testing; apply strict resource limits (max attributes per element); consider using alternative XML parsers with security audits if feasible; monitor library dependencies in build systems to prevent older versions being linked

threat_vector: Integer overflow to buffer overflow in FTM ARA commands across Snapdragon platforms due to improper input validation
exploitation_method: Attacker sends specially crafted FTM (Fast Time Matrix) ARA (Authentication and Reporting Architecture) commands with oversized input parameters that trigger integer overflow in size calculations, causing subsequent buffer writes to overflow adjacent memory regions, potentially achieving code execution or denial of service
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) FTM ARA command messages with parameter values near integer boundaries (0xFFFFFFFF, 0x7FFFFFFF); (2) unexpected memory access violations or segmentation faults following specific ARA command sequences; (3) stack canary corruption detected by runtime protections; (4) anomalous heap allocations with sizes resulting from integer wraparound (small allocations after large calculations); (5) audit logs showing ARA command processing failures with buffer-related errors
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict input validation: (1) validate all FTM ARA command parameters against defined maximum values before arithmetic operations; (2) use safe integer arithmetic libraries that detect overflow conditions; (3) enforce bounds checking on all buffer operations; (4) apply compiler-level protections (stack canaries, ASLR, DEP/NX); (5) deploy memory tagging extensions (MTE) on supported Snapdragon variants; (6) implement firmware update mechanisms to patch vulnerable ARA command handlers; (7) use fuzzing-based testing on FTM ARA interfaces during development

threat_vector: Out-of-bounds write vulnerability in AOD (Always-On Display) module
exploitation_method: Attacker sends malformed input to AOD module that causes a buffer overflow, writing data beyond allocated memory boundaries. This corrupts adjacent memory structures, potentially affecting display service functionality or enabling privilege escalation.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: kernel panic logs related to AOD module, segmentation faults in display service, unexpected memory corruption errors, excessive memory write operations to AOD buffer regions, system service crashes following display-related function calls, SELinux denials involving AOD process memory operations
mitigation_strategy: Apply available security patches for AOD module immediately. Implement AddressSanitizer (ASan) or Memory Tagging Extension (MTE) to detect out-of-bounds writes. Use stack canaries and ASLR. Restrict AOD module permissions via SELinux policies. Implement input validation and bounds checking for all AOD buffer operations. Monitor system logs for anomalous display service behavior and restart display service on detected anomalies.

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Lifecycle-Triggered Deletion
exploitation_method: Attacker establishes persistence mechanism that monitors for specific lifecycle events (user logoff, system shutdown, process termination) and automatically executes deletion commands to remove evidence of compromise, logs, malware binaries, or artifacts when those triggers occur
detection_pattern: Monitor for: scheduled tasks or cron jobs with deletion payloads; WMI event subscriptions targeting system shutdown/logoff events; PowerShell scripts containing deletion logic tied to event listeners; unusual file deletion patterns clustered around logoff times; registry entries (HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\System\CurrentControlSet\Services) with suspicious cleanup parameters; deletion of event logs immediately before system shutdown; correlation between process termination events and subsequent artifact deletion; presence of cleanup scripts in startup folders or RunOnce registry keys
mitigation_strategy: Implement immutable logging with write-once storage to prevent deletion of audit trails; enforce file integrity monitoring (FIM) with out-of-band verification; use endpoint detection and response (EDR) with behavioral analysis to flag deletion activity tied to lifecycle events; restrict permissions on system startup/shutdown hooks and WMI event subscriptions; maintain centralized syslog collection on isolated infrastructure; enable Windows Event Log forwarding with write protection; regularly validate backup integrity of critical logs; use file retention policies and legal holds on security logs; monitor for unexpected scheduled task or cron job creation; block PowerShell/script execution during shutdown sequences except authorized processes

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Clipboard Data (T1115)
exploitation_method: Attacker executes code (malware, script, or compromised process) that reads the system clipboard to capture sensitive data like passwords, API keys, authentication tokens, financial information, or source code that users have copied
detection_pattern: Monitor for: Unusual process access to clipboard APIs (GetClipboardData, XGetSelectionOwner); processes from suspicious locations accessing clipboard; unexpected clipboard access by non-standard applications; correlate with user copy operations; EDR alerts on clipboard enumeration; check Windows Event Viewer for Sysmon events (Event ID 10 for inter-process access); audit process creation with unusual parent-child relationships attempting clipboard operations
mitigation_strategy: Implement application allowlisting to restrict clipboard access; deploy EDR/XDR with clipboard access monitoring; enforce principle of least privilege for application permissions; use clipboard managers with access controls; regularly audit and update security policies for privileged processes; disable clipboard sharing in remote desktop/virtual machine environments unless required; conduct user security awareness training on clipboard risks; monitor and restrict script execution (PowerShell, VBScript) in environments; use sandboxing for untrusted applications

threat_vector: ATT&CK: T1021.005 - Remote Service Session Initiation via VNC
exploitation_method: Attacker establishes unauthorized Remote Frame Buffer (RFB) connections to port 5900/5901 or alternate VNC ports. Exploits weak/default credentials, unpatched VNC software vulnerabilities, or network exposure of VNC services. Once authenticated, attacker gains interactive GUI access for lateral movement and command execution.
detection_pattern: Unexpected network connections to VNC ports (5900-5910); RFB protocol handshakes in network traffic; Authentication failures followed by success in VNC logs; Unusual process execution from VNC processes (vncserver, vncviewer); New VNC session initiation outside business hours; Multiple connection attempts from external IPs; VNC process children spawning shells or system tools.
mitigation_strategy: Disable VNC or restrict to internal networks only via firewall rules; Require strong authentication (enforce minimum 12+ character passwords, disable default credentials); Implement network segmentation with VNC on isolated jump-host tier; Deploy intrusion detection for RFB protocol anomalies; Use SSH tunneling for remote access instead of direct VNC exposure; Enable VNC logging and centralize logs for SIEM analysis; Patch VNC software regularly; Implement multi-factor authentication if VNC is required; Monitor and alert on VNC process creation and child processes.

threat_vector: McAfee Application and Change Control (MACC) pre-8.3.4 local privilege escalation via application solidification bypass
exploitation_method: Locally authenticated attacker exploits insufficient validation in MACC's application control enforcement mechanism to execute unsigned or unapproved binaries, bypass application whitelisting policies, or modify protected application configurations by circumventing solidification controls that prevent unauthorized changes
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unexpected binary execution outside approved application whitelist; (2) MACC policy enforcement failures in security logs; (3) Attempts to modify application configuration files or registry entries related to application control; (4) Signed vs unsigned executable mismatch alerts; (5) Processes spawning from unexpected parent processes; (6) Failed MACC validation events followed by successful execution; (7) Anomalous system calls attempting to bypass driver-level enforcement
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade MACC to version 8.3.4 or later immediately; implement application hardening via strict code signing requirements; enforce driver-level integrity checks; configure MACC policies in enforcement mode (not audit); restrict local administrative privileges; deploy endpoint detection and response (EDR) to monitor for unsigned code execution; maintain audit logging of all application control decisions; perform regular security assessments of application solidification configurations; implement application behavior analysis alongside whitelisting

threat_vector: Bluetooth module out-of-bounds write vulnerability leading to remote code execution
exploitation_method: Attacker sends specially crafted Bluetooth packets with oversized payloads that exceed buffer boundaries in the Bluetooth stack, overwriting adjacent memory regions containing function pointers or executable code, allowing arbitrary command execution on the target device
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unexpected Bluetooth packet sizes exceeding MTU limits in bluetooth_core logs, (2) Memory protection faults or segmentation violations in kernel logs coinciding with Bluetooth activity, (3) Abnormal Bluetooth device connection attempts from previously unknown addresses, (4) Stack canary corruption detection events, (5) Unexpected process spawning or privilege escalation following Bluetooth pairing events, (6) Core dumps or crash reports in bluetooth daemon processes
mitigation_strategy: Apply firmware patches that implement bounds checking on all Bluetooth packet handlers; enable stack canary protection and ASLR in the Bluetooth module; implement input validation for all Bluetooth packet fields; use memory-safe languages for critical Bluetooth stack components; deploy address space layout randomization; enforce Bluetooth Classic and BLE packet size limits at the hardware abstraction layer; implement hardware-backed memory tagging extension (MTE) if available; establish connection-level rate limiting and anomaly detection for malformed packets; maintain updated firmware with security patches from the device manufacturer

threat_vector: Improper broadcast permission settings in Android/iOS applications allowing unauthorized access to broadcast information
exploitation_method: Attacker registers a broadcast receiver with insufficient permission requirements or exploits default overly-permissive broadcast settings to intercept sensitive system broadcasts (location, SMS, device state, authentication tokens) that should be restricted to system components only
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unexpected broadcast receiver registrations in AndroidManifest.xml without proper permission declarations, logcat entries showing 'broadcasts' sent to multiple packages, suspicious applications requesting BROADCAST_INTENT permissions, runtime receiver registration via registerReceiver() without matching permission enforcement, Intent interception logs, abnormal data access patterns from non-system applications accessing protected broadcasts
mitigation_strategy: Enforce explicit permission checks on all broadcasts using sendBroadcast() with required permissions parameter, implement signature-based permission verification, restrict broadcasts to system-only via FLAG_RECEIVER_REGISTERED_ONLY or FLAG_RECEIVER_SYSTEM_ONLY flags, conduct security code review of broadcast implementations, validate AndroidManifest.xml broadcast receiver declarations require explicit permissions, implement runtime permission validation using checkCallingPermission(), monitor and restrict implicit broadcast reception, update to enforce targetSdkVersion with stricter broadcast isolation requirements

threat_vector: kubectl terminal output does not sanitize escape sequences in raw data fields, allowing injection of ANSI/control characters that manipulate terminal behavior
exploitation_method: Attacker embeds malicious escape sequences in Kubernetes object fields (e.g., pod names, annotations, error messages). When kubectl outputs these fields to terminal without sanitization, sequences execute commands like cursor movement, text clearing, or terminal mode changes to obscure logs or manipulate user interaction
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Kubernetes objects with field values containing unusual escape sequences (\x1b, \033, \e patterns), (2) kubectl output logs showing unexpected cursor positioning or text clearing, (3) anomalous pod/resource names containing ANSI codes (regex: [\x1b\033]\[[0-9;]*[a-zA-Z]), (4) user reports of garbled or missing kubectl output
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade kubectl to version with output sanitization; configure terminal output mode with --output=json/yaml when piping to scripts; implement admission webhook to validate and sanitize object field values before persistence; use terminal multiplexers (tmux/screen) with escape sequence filtering; train users to use structured output formats (--output=json) for programmatic parsing instead of relying on terminal rendering

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1562.008 - Disable or Modify Cloud Logs
exploitation_method: Attacker with compromised cloud credentials uses cloud provider APIs (e.g., DeleteLogGroup, PutEventSelectors, StopLogging) to disable CloudTrail, remove log groups, modify retention policies, or delete audit trail records to cover tracks after lateral movement or data exfiltration
detection_pattern: Alert on: API calls to disable/delete logging services (DeleteTrail, DeleteLogGroup, StopEventSelectors), rapid deletion of log entries, DisableLogging or ModifyLogRetention operations, sudden gaps in audit logs, changes to CloudTrail/CloudWatch configurations by unusual principals, failed or denied log access attempts followed by successful deletion calls
mitigation_strategy: Enable immutable log storage (S3 Object Lock, Azure Blob Immutable Storage), implement separate log aggregation to offline SIEM, enforce MFA and IP restrictions on logging service API calls, use resource-based policies to prevent log deletion, enable CloudTrail organization trails with read-only access, monitor IAM permission changes for logging services, implement log redundancy across multiple storage backends, enable versioning on log buckets, and configure CloudWatch alarms for logging configuration changes

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Port Monitors (T1547.013)
exploitation_method: Attacker registers malicious DLL as port monitor via Registry (HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Print\Monitors) or adds monitor through SetPort API. Code executes with SYSTEM privileges during printer subsystem initialization and on each print job.
detection_pattern: Monitor Registry additions under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Print\Monitors for unknown entries; EventID 13 (Registry object added/modified) with print monitor paths; unsigned DLLs loaded by spoolsv.exe; unexpected network connections from print spooler process; unusual child processes spawned by spoolsv.exe
mitigation_strategy: Implement Registry monitoring and alerting for Print\Monitors changes; enforce Code Integrity policy requiring signed drivers; disable print spooler service if unnecessary; apply Microsoft patch for CVE-2020-1048; use Application Whitelisting for spoolsv.exe child processes; restrict registry write permissions to Administrators; conduct regular audits of installed port monitors via 'Get-PrinterPort' and registry inspection

threat_vector: HwPCAssistant path traversal vulnerability
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious file path input containing directory traversal sequences (../) to access files outside the intended directory structure, potentially reading sensitive configuration files, credentials, or system data that the HwPCAssistant process has permissions to access
detection_pattern: Monitor for: file access attempts with ../ or ...\ sequences in HwPCAssistant process logs, unexpected file reads outside application directory, error messages indicating invalid path traversal attempts, access to /etc, /proc, Windows\System32, or credential storage directories through HwPCAssistant, unusual argument patterns in process execution, file access audit logs showing traversal patterns (../ sequences) in file operations initiated by HwPCAssistant
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict input validation using allowlist-based path normalization; use os.path.realpath() or equivalent to resolve paths and verify they remain within permitted directories before access; apply principle of least privilege to HwPCAssistant process permissions; disable directory listing; implement chroot/jail sandboxing; maintain updated patched versions; use Web Application Firewall (WAF) rules to block path traversal patterns; conduct regular code audits focusing on file operations

threat_vector: IBM Engineering Workflow Management 7.0-7.0.2 and Rational Team Concert 6.0.6-6.0.6.1 information disclosure via build definition access
exploitation_method: Authenticated attacker exploits insufficient access controls on build definition endpoints to retrieve sensitive data (credentials, API keys, environment variables, connection strings) stored in unencrypted or inadequately protected build configuration objects through direct API calls or UI navigation
detection_pattern: Log entries showing: authenticated users accessing build definition endpoints with unusual frequency; API requests to /buildDefinition or similar endpoints from non-service accounts; database queries retrieving plaintext secrets from configuration tables; audit logs missing expected permission denials; HTTP response codes 200 to build config endpoints for users lacking explicit build admin role; config file extraction attempts in application logs; unusual serialization of sensitive build parameters in request/response payloads
mitigation_strategy: Apply security patches immediately (upgrade to patched versions); implement role-based access control (RBAC) restricting build definition read access to authorized personnel only; encrypt all secrets at rest using AES-256; implement field-level encryption for sensitive build parameters; audit and rotate all embedded credentials in build definitions; enable detailed access logging for build configuration retrieval; implement least-privilege service accounts; use secret management tools instead of embedded credentials; regularly scan build definitions for exposed secrets using automated secret scanning tools; restrict API access via network segmentation and IP whitelisting

threat_vector: Mass assignment vulnerability in ORM
exploitation_method: Attacker submits unexpected form fields or API parameters that directly map to database model attributes, allowing modification of fields not intended for user input (e.g., is_admin, role, price, discount). ORM automatically assigns all submitted parameters to model properties without validation.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unexpected model attribute changes in audit logs, privilege escalation events where user role changes without admin action, price modifications in transactions that don't match API endpoints, database update queries with unusual field combinations, API requests with parameter names matching sensitive model fields (is_admin, role_id, account_status)
mitigation_strategy: Implement attribute whitelisting at ORM level using allowlist/permit methods; use Data Transfer Objects (DTOs) to explicitly define which fields accept user input; disable mass assignment globally and require explicit property assignment; validate and sanitize all user inputs against schema; implement role-based access controls on model properties; conduct code reviews focusing on ORM binding patterns; use static analysis tools to detect mass assignment risks

threat_vector: Django UserAttributeSimilarityValidator ReDoS via crafted password input (CVE-2021-33571)
exploitation_method: Attacker submits extremely long password strings or passwords with repetitive patterns to UserAttributeSimilarityValidator, causing catastrophic backtracking in regex operations and consuming excessive CPU/memory during password validation, leading to denial of service
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) HTTP requests with unusually long password fields (>1000 characters), (2) Spike in authentication endpoint response times (>5s), (3) CPU usage spikes during password validation phases, (4) Multiple sequential failed authentication attempts with incrementally longer payloads, (5) Application logs showing prolonged regex evaluation in auth validation code
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Django to 2.2.26+, 3.2.11+, or 4.0.1+. Implement input validation limiting password length (max 128 characters). Add request-level timeouts on authentication endpoints (5-10 seconds). Deploy rate limiting on login/password reset endpoints. Monitor validator performance and set alerts for validation times exceeding baseline. Consider disabling UserAttributeSimilarityValidator if not required and using alternative validators with proven performance characteristics

threat_vector: IBM Security Verify 10.0.0-10.0.2.0 Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) in Web UI
exploitation_method: Attacker injects malicious JavaScript payloads through unvalidated input fields in the Web UI. The application fails to properly sanitize or encode user-supplied data before rendering it in HTML responses, allowing arbitrary script execution in victim browsers with the privileges of authenticated users.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) HTTP requests with script tags or JavaScript event handlers (onerror=, onclick=, onload=) in query parameters or POST bodies; (2) Unusual HTML/JavaScript in Web UI form submissions; (3) WAF alerts for XSS patterns; (4) Browser console errors indicating unexpected script execution; (5) Audit logs showing data modification without corresponding legitimate user actions; (6) Session hijacking attempts or privilege escalation events following suspicious Web UI interactions
mitigation_strategy: Immediately upgrade to patched versions beyond 10.0.2.0. Implement: (1) Input validation and output encoding on all user-supplied data; (2) Content Security Policy (CSP) headers to restrict inline script execution; (3) HTTPOnly and Secure flags on session cookies; (4) Web Application Firewall (WAF) rules blocking XSS payloads; (5) Regular security scanning of Web UI endpoints; (6) Principle of least privilege for application accounts; (7) Monitor and restrict JavaScript execution in trusted domains only

threat_vector: Changlian Blocklist Business Logic Error - Message Class Attack Surface Expansion
exploitation_method: Attacker identifies flaws in the blocklist validation logic where improperly formatted or specially crafted message entries bypass filtering rules. By exploiting conditional logic errors or state management issues in the blocklist, attackers can inject messages into classes that should be blocked, expanding their ability to target additional message types or user groups.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Messages matching blocklist criteria but successfully delivered; (2) Inconsistent blocklist enforcement across message classes; (3) Sudden increase in message volumes in previously blocked categories; (4) Log entries showing blocklist evaluation failures or exceptions; (5) Messages with malformed headers that bypass validation; (6) Repeated attempts with incrementally modified message formats; (7) Database queries showing blocklist table access with unusual parameters
mitigation_strategy: Implement: (1) Input validation at multiple layers with explicit whitelist approach for message classes; (2) Unit and integration tests specifically targeting blocklist edge cases and boundary conditions; (3) Blocklist enforcement as atomic transactions with rollback on any validation failure; (4) Rate limiting on message submission per user/class; (5) Comprehensive audit logging of all blocklist decisions with decision rationale; (6) Regular security code review of blocklist evaluation logic; (7) Blocklist integrity checks using cryptographic signatures; (8) Implement fail-secure defaults where uncertain classifications default to rejection

threat_vector: Insufficient Session Expiration in Talkyard v0.2021.20 through v0.2021.33 (regular) and v0.2021.20 through v0.2021.34 (dev)
exploitation_method: Attacker obtains a valid session token/cookie from an authenticated user and continues using it after the user logs out or after an extended idle period. The application fails to properly invalidate sessions, allowing token reuse for unauthorized access to user accounts and data.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Multiple authentication requests with same session ID after logout events; (2) Session activity from different IP addresses/geolocation with same session token; (3) API calls using session tokens that should have expired based on last activity timestamp; (4) Absence of session invalidation log entries following logout actions; (5) Requests with cookies containing dates predating typical session lifespans (>30 days for most applications)
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Talkyard to patched version v0.2021.34 or later. Implement mandatory session timeout (15-30 minutes idle), server-side session store validation on every request, immediate token revocation on logout with blacklist checking, secure HttpOnly/Secure cookie flags, and implement CSRF tokens. Add logout event logging and monitor for anomalous session reuse patterns.

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1218.009 - System Binary Proxy Execution via Regsvcs/Regasm
exploitation_method: Attacker executes regsvcs.exe or regasm.exe with malicious .NET assemblies to bypass execution policies and UAC. These legitimate Windows binaries load and execute arbitrary code through COM registration mechanisms, evading detection of direct script/executable execution.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: regsvcs.exe or regasm.exe launched with suspicious command-line arguments (non-standard paths, /U flag with unknown assemblies); parent processes not typical (explorer.exe, cmd.exe instead of installer services); network connections from regsvcs/regasm processes; .NET assembly files in unusual locations; Event ID 11 (file creation) for temporary assemblies; Sysmon Event ID 1 showing these binaries with LoadLibrary of untrusted .NET assemblies
mitigation_strategy: 1) Restrict execution via AppLocker/WDAC rules allowing regsvcs/regasm only from System32 with legitimate use cases. 2) Disable .NET runtime on systems that don't require it. 3) Monitor registry for suspicious COM registration patterns. 4) Implement code signing enforcement for .NET assemblies. 5) Use Windows Defender Application Guard or similar sandboxing. 6) Block these binaries via EDR if not business-critical. 7) Monitor and alert on any regsvcs/regasm execution in your environment by default.

threat_vector: Protobuf-java UnknownFieldSet parsing DoS via malicious field interleaving
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts a protobuf message with specially interleaved UnknownFieldSet fields that cause the parser to process fields out of order, triggering excessive CPU consumption and memory allocation during deserialization
detection_pattern: Monitor for: sudden CPU spikes during protobuf deserialization, parser thread hang detection, memory allocation anomalies during message processing, repeated deserialization failures with specific field patterns, increased latency in protobuf parsing operations
mitigation_strategy: 1) Upgrade protobuf-java to patched version (3.19.6+, 3.20.2+, 3.21.7+ or later). 2) Implement parser timeout mechanisms and per-message resource limits. 3) Add message size validation before deserialization. 4) Deploy rate limiting on message ingestion endpoints. 5) Monitor and alert on parsing duration anomalies. 6) Consider adding deserialization sandboxing for untrusted data sources.

threat_vector: SphinxSearch directory traversal via CALL SNIPPETS and load_file operations (CVE-2019-14511 related)
exploitation_method: Attacker uses MySQL client protocol to issue CALL SNIPPETS commands combined with load_file operations against Sphinx daemon, allowing traversal of filesystem boundaries to read arbitrary files outside intended directories. The vulnerability chains with CVE-2019-14511 to bypass path restrictions.
detection_pattern: Monitor Sphinx logs for: CALL SNIPPETS queries containing '../' path sequences, load_file operations with absolute paths, queries accessing /etc, /var, or other sensitive directories, unexpected file access patterns in Sphinx audit logs, SQL queries attempting directory traversal syntax (../, ..\), repeated failed file access attempts to restricted paths, unusual mysql client connections to Sphinx port (default 9306) with file operation keywords
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Sphinx to version 3.1.2 or later; restrict Sphinx daemon process permissions using dedicated low-privilege user account; implement filesystem ACLs to limit Sphinx read access to designated index directories only; disable or strictly control load_file functionality at application level; implement network-level access controls restricting client connections to Sphinx port; validate and sanitize all user input before constructing CALL SNIPPETS queries; run Sphinx in containerized environment with restricted mount points; enable and monitor Sphinx query logging for suspicious patterns; apply SELinux or AppArmor profiles to confine Sphinx process capabilities

threat_vector: wolfSSL wolfMQTT 1.9 heap-based buffer overflow in MqttClient_DecodePacket
exploitation_method: Attacker sends a malformed MQTT packet with an oversized payload field that exceeds allocated heap buffer size. When MqttClient_DecodePacket processes the packet during connection handshake (via MqttClient_WaitType or MqttClient_Connect), it writes beyond buffer boundaries, corrupting heap metadata and enabling code execution.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Abnormal MQTT protocol packets with payload length exceeding expected buffer sizes (typically <4KB for standard MQTT), (2) Heap corruption indicators in application logs or crash dumps showing invalid heap chunk sizes, (3) Unexpected process termination with segmentation faults or access violations during MQTT_Connect/MQTT_WaitType calls, (4) Memory sanitizer output flagging heap-buffer-overflow in MqttClient_DecodePacket, (5) Unusual network traffic from MQTT brokers containing non-standard control packet structures
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade wolfMQTT to version 1.10 or later with the heap overflow patch applied. Implement input validation: enforce strict maximum packet size limits before processing (RFC 3.1.2.3 specifies 268MB but applications should use much smaller limits like 4KB), use address space layout randomization (ASLR) and stack canaries to mitigate exploitation, deploy memory safety tools (AddressSanitizer) in development/testing, implement network-level packet filtering to reject malformed MQTT frames, and restrict MQTT broker connectivity to trusted networks only.

threat_vector: Host Header Injection in UserFrosting v0.3.1 to v4.6.2 via password reset functionality
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious link with injected Host header value in forgot-password endpoint. When victim clicks link, the application uses the attacker-controlled Host header to generate password reset URLs in emails, redirecting to attacker-controlled domain instead of legitimate application domain.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) GET/POST requests to /forgot-password endpoints with suspicious or mismatched Host headers; (2) Host header values differing from whitelist of legitimate domains; (3) Password reset emails containing URLs with unexpected domain names; (4) Multiple password reset requests with varying Host header values from single source IP; (5) Log entries showing Host header values containing attacker domains or IP addresses
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade to UserFrosting v4.6.3 or later; implement strict Host header validation by maintaining whitelist of allowed hostnames; configure web server (Apache/Nginx) to reject requests with invalid Host headers; use $_SERVER['HTTP_HOST'] validation against configuration values rather than user-supplied data; implement CSRF tokens on password reset forms; configure email templates to use configuration-based domain URLs instead of dynamically generated Host headers; monitor and alert on Host header anomalies

threat_vector: eID module out-of-bounds memory write vulnerability
exploitation_method: Attacker sends specially crafted input to eID module that bypasses bounds checking in memory operations, causing writes to unintended memory locations. This can overwrite adjacent data structures, function pointers, or control flow metadata, potentially leading to privilege escalation or arbitrary code execution.
detection_pattern: Memory protection violations (segmentation faults, access violations), canary corruption detection, heap/stack overflow indicators in system logs, unusual process termination of eID service, integrity check failures on protected data structures, ASLR bypass attempts, unexpected changes to process memory mappings, anomalous behavior following eID API calls
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict input validation and bounds checking on all eID module inputs, use address space layout randomization (ASLR), enable stack canaries and heap protections, apply memory tagging extensions (ARM MTE) if available, use AddressSanitizer during development, keep eID module updated with security patches, implement sandboxing/containerization for eID service, apply principle of least privilege to eID process permissions, monitor memory allocations and enforce size limits

threat_vector: openwhyd Improper Authorization
exploitation_method: Attacker bypasses authorization checks by directly accessing API endpoints or resources without proper permission validation. This may involve manipulating user IDs in requests, accessing other users' playlists/data, or performing administrative actions without required roles.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unauthorized API requests with mismatched user IDs, access logs showing users accessing resources outside their permission scope, unusual cross-user data access patterns, privilege escalation attempts in application logs, repeated 403 errors followed by successful requests with modified parameters
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict server-side authorization checks on all endpoints, validate user permissions before returning data, use role-based access control (RBAC), enforce principle of least privilege, implement comprehensive audit logging, conduct regular security reviews of authorization logic, and add rate limiting on failed authorization attempts

threat_vector: Shopware session persistence after password change
exploitation_method: Attacker who gains temporary access to a user account can change the password, then retain session access even after the legitimate user discovers the compromise and changes their password back. The attacker's active session remains valid and can be used for continued unauthorized access.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Session activity continuing after password change events in auth logs, (2) Multiple concurrent sessions from different IPs for same user account within short timeframe, (3) User login event followed by password change with subsequent activity from different IP/user-agent, (4) Audit logs showing password modification but no corresponding session invalidation/restart events
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Shopware to version 5.7.7 or later. Implement forced session termination on password changes by: (1) Invalidating all active sessions when password is modified, (2) Requiring re-authentication after password change, (3) Maintaining session change logs linked to account modifications, (4) Implementing session binding to password hash or credential version, (5) Conducting proactive password resets for all users to terminate any attacker-held sessions

threat_vector: Unquoted Service Path Privilege Escalation in SICAM PQ Analyzer < V3.18
exploitation_method: Attacker with local write privileges exploits unquoted registry service path containing spaces. By creating a malicious executable in a directory that matches a substring of the service path (e.g., placing evil.exe in C:\Program Files\ when path is C:\Program Files\Siemens\SICAM\...), the service startup routine executes the attacker's binary instead of the legitimate service, achieving privilege escalation if service runs as SYSTEM or elevated user.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Registry Key modification in HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services for SICAM entries; (2) Executable creation in C:\Program Files\ or C:\Program Files (x86)\ directories with suspicious names matching service path substrings; (3) Service start failures followed by unexpected process execution; (4) File integrity monitoring alerts on Program Files directory; (5) Process creation events where service process spawns from non-standard paths; (6) Application whitelisting violations for unsigned binaries in system paths.
mitigation_strategy: Immediately upgrade SICAM PQ Analyzer to V3.18 or later. As interim control: (1) Manually update registry entries HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services to use fully quoted service paths with quotes around the entire path and parameters; (2) Implement restrictive NTFS permissions on C:\Program Files\ and C:\Program Files (x86)\ to prevent non-administrative writes; (3) Deploy application whitelisting/AppLocker policies to restrict executable execution in system directories; (4) Use Windows Defender Application Guard or similar sandboxing; (5) Monitor service registry hives with configuration management tools; (6) Enforce principle of least privilege—run SICAM service with minimal required permissions, not SYSTEM account.

threat_vector: Out-of-bounds read in APK close session request processing due to insufficient domain input validation in Snapdragon platforms
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious APK close session request with invalid domain parameters that exceed buffer boundaries, causing the parser to read memory outside allocated regions without proper bounds checking
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) APK session close requests with domain field length exceeding expected maximums, (2) Memory access violations or segmentation faults in APK session handler, (3) Crashes in domain parsing routines, (4) Unusual domain values containing null bytes or non-printable characters, (5) Log entries showing validation bypass in session request handlers
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict input validation: (1) Define and enforce maximum domain length limits before processing, (2) Use bounded string parsing functions (strncpy, snprintf with size limits), (3) Add explicit range checks before memory access operations, (4) Implement fuzz testing on APK session close handlers, (5) Enable address sanitization (ASAN) in development/testing, (6) Apply vendor security patches for Snapdragon firmware, (7) Implement input sanitization that rejects malformed domain parameters

threat_vector: Out-of-bounds array read in smartphone security storage module
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts input that causes array index calculations to exceed allocated memory bounds, reading adjacent memory containing sensitive credentials, encryption keys, or authentication tokens from the security storage module without proper bounds validation
detection_pattern: Monitor for: segmentation faults or memory protection violations in security storage processes; abnormal memory access patterns in security module logs; unexpected data leakage in crash dumps; fuzzing detection tools identifying non-validated array indexing; static analysis flags on unchecked array access operations in security module code
mitigation_strategy: Implement bounds checking on all array accesses in security storage module; use safe array access functions with length validation; employ AddressSanitizer or similar memory safety tools in testing; apply input validation and sanitization before array indexing; use memory-safe languages or safe wrapper libraries; conduct regular code audits focusing on array operations; implement stack canaries and ASLR to limit memory disclosure impact; isolate security storage in restricted memory regions with access controls

threat_vector: Null pointer dereference in smartphone kernel leading to denial of service
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious input or triggers specific code path that causes application to dereference a null pointer without validation, resulting in kernel panic or process crash
detection_pattern: Kernel panic logs with null pointer dereference stack traces; segmentation fault (SIGSEGV) signals; unexpected process termination in system logs; crash dump files referencing null memory access; repeated kernel restarts within short timeframes
mitigation_strategy: Implement null pointer checks before all dereference operations; use static code analysis tools to identify potential null dereferences; enable Address Sanitizer (ASan) during development; apply security patches promptly; implement kernel hardening features; use memory protection mechanisms; conduct regular code reviews focusing on pointer handling

threat_vector: URL Redirection to Untrusted Site in Forge
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts a malicious URL containing a redirect parameter pointing to an external untrusted domain. When a user clicks the link or is automatically redirected, they are taken to an attacker-controlled site that may host phishing pages, malware, or credential harvesting forms. The vulnerability exists when Forge fails to validate or whitelist redirect destinations.
detection_pattern: Monitor HTTP 302/301 redirect responses with Location headers pointing to external domains not in whitelist; log requests containing redirect parameters with suspicious URLs; track user sessions that redirect from legitimate Forge domains to known malicious or suspicious external hosts; alert on redirect chains or multiple consecutive redirects
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict URL validation: maintain a whitelist of approved redirect destinations, use allowlist-based validation on all redirect parameters, implement relative URL redirects only, validate redirect URLs against OWASP guidelines. Add Content Security Policy (CSP) headers to restrict navigation, implement URL sanitization libraries, require explicit user consent before cross-domain redirects, and conduct regular security audits of redirect handling code

threat_vector: Latte Template Engine Sandbox Escape (CVE-2023-29404)
exploitation_method: Attacker injects malicious code into Latte templates that exploits sandbox bypass vulnerabilities in versions 2.8.0+. By manipulating template syntax or leveraging unsafe filter/macro implementations, attackers can escape the sandbox restrictions and execute arbitrary PHP code on the server.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unusual template variable assignments containing system function calls (exec, shell_exec, passthru), suspicious filter chains accessing restricted classes via reflection, template syntax with unexpected nested function calls, PHP error logs showing sandbox violation exceptions, abnormal file write/execute operations originating from template processing, and access to $_SERVER or environment variables within untrusted templates.
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Latte to version 2.13.9+ or 3.0.6+ where sandbox escapes are patched. Implement strict input validation for all user-supplied template content. Disable dangerous functions (exec, system, passthru, eval) via PHP configuration. Use separate unprivileged user accounts for template processing. Implement content security policies. Audit and restrict available filters and macros in template environments. Run template engines in isolated containers with minimal privileges.

threat_vector: LiteSpeed Cache WordPress plugin (< 4.4.4) insufficient QUIC.cloud origin verification
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts HTTP requests to vulnerable endpoints without proper origin validation. By spoofing requests or manipulating headers, they bypass authentication checks intended for QUIC.cloud servers, gaining unauthorized access to cache management, purge, or configuration endpoints.
detection_pattern: Monitor WordPress logs for: requests to /wp-admin/admin-ajax.php with litespeed cache actions from non-QUIC.cloud IPs; access to cache purge endpoints without valid QUIC.cloud headers or IP ranges (103.21.244.0/22, 103.22.200.0/22, 103.31.4.0/22, 141.101.64.0/18, 108.162.192.0/18, 190.93.240.0/20, 188.114.96.0/20, 197.234.240.0/22, 198.41.128.0/17, 162.158.0.0/15); repeated cache manipulation attempts from suspicious sources.
mitigation_strategy: Update LiteSpeed Cache plugin to version 4.4.4 or later immediately. Implement strict IP whitelisting for QUIC.cloud communication using official IP ranges only. Add WAF rules to restrict cache-related endpoints to verified QUIC.cloud sources. Validate request origin through multiple factors: IP verification, cryptographic signatures, and request authentication tokens. Monitor for unauthorized cache operations and implement rate limiting on cache endpoints. Consider disabling remote cache management if not required.

threat_vector: Denial of Service in Binaryen 104 - Assertion Abort in wasm::Tuple::validate
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malformed WebAssembly (WASM) bytecode with invalid tuple type structures that trigger an unhandled assertion failure in the Tuple::validate function during WASM module parsing/validation, causing the Binaryen validator process to abort and deny service.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unexpected process termination with SIGABRT signal when Binaryen processes WASM modules, (2) Core dumps or crash logs from wasm::Tuple::validate stack trace, (3) Repeated failed WASM module validation attempts from single source, (4) Malformed WASM magic number 0x00 0x61 0x73 0x6d preceded by unusual tuple type definitions (opcode 0x5b), (5) Resource exhaustion patterns where validation processes spawn and crash repeatedly
mitigation_strategy: Update Binaryen to version 105 or later containing the assertion fix. Implement input validation: (1) Pre-validate WASM binary structure before passing to Binaryen, (2) Add timeout controls for validation operations, (3) Implement rate limiting on WASM module submission endpoints, (4) Run Binaryen validator in sandboxed process with resource limits and crash recovery, (5) Deploy WAF rules to detect malformed WASM magic headers, (6) Monitor and alert on validation process crashes with automatic restart mechanisms

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1568.002 - Dynamic Resolution via Domain Generation Algorithm (DGA)
exploitation_method: Malware generates a large number of domain names algorithmically at runtime to establish command and control (C2) communications, evading static domain-based blocking by rotating through pseudo-random domains derived from seeds like current date/time
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) DNS queries to numerous non-existent domains with similar patterns or character entropy, (2) Failed DNS lookups followed by successful connections to derived domains, (3) Unusual DNS query volume from single host in short time window, (4) Domains with high algorithmic similarity clustering, (5) OSINT correlation with known DGA seed lists, (6) NetFlow/proxy logs showing connections to algorithmically-generated domain patterns
mitigation_strategy: Implement: (1) DNS sinkholing with DGA sinkhole feeds updated regularly, (2) Machine learning-based DNS anomaly detection analyzing domain characteristics (entropy, length patterns, TLD distribution), (3) Strict egress filtering limiting external DNS queries to approved resolvers, (4) YARA/Sigma rules matching known DGA algorithms, (5) Passive DNS monitoring and threat intelligence integration, (6) Network segmentation reducing lateral movement if DGA callback succeeds, (7) Host-based controls blocking execution of known malware families using DGAs

threat_vector: Path traversal in file download endpoint
exploitation_method: Attacker manipulates file path parameters using sequences like ../ or ..\, absolute paths, or URL encoding to access files outside intended download directory, such as /etc/passwd, configuration files, or application source code
detection_pattern: HTTP requests with encoded traversal sequences (%2e%2e, %252e%252e), backslash variations (..\), null bytes (%00), double encoding, or suspicious file paths in logs; monitor for requests accessing files outside designated directories; alert on repeated failed access attempts to sensitive system paths
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict input validation using whitelist-based filename verification; canonicalize file paths and verify resolved path stays within intended directory; use dedicated file serving mechanisms that prevent directory traversal; avoid exposing raw file paths in URLs; implement least-privilege file system permissions; consider using a file serving library with built-in path traversal protections rather than custom implementations

threat_vector: Process Hollowing (ATT&CK T1055.012)
exploitation_method: Attacker creates a legitimate process in suspended state, unmaps its memory sections, injects malicious code into the hollowed address space, resumes execution causing the malicious payload to run under the legitimate process name and security context
detection_pattern: Monitor for: Process creation with CREATE_SUSPENDED flag followed by NtUnmapViewOfSection/VirtualProtectEx calls; mismatched parent-child process relationships; legitimate process image path mismatch with actual loaded modules; suspicious memory write patterns to newly created processes; absence of expected legitimate DLLs in process memory; entry point deviation analysis showing execution not starting from legitimate image base
mitigation_strategy: Enable Exploit Protection (Code Integrity Guard, Mandatory ASLR); deploy EDR with memory integrity monitoring; implement application whitelisting restricting process creation to known binaries; enforce Code Signing Enforcement; monitor process injection via Windows event logging (Event ID 10 via Sysmon); restrict administrative privileges; use Windows Defender SmartScreen; patch promptly to address kernel-level protections

threat_vector: Password vault External Control of System or Configuration Setting vulnerability
exploitation_method: Attacker exploits improper input validation or insecure deserialization in vault configuration endpoints to inject malicious parameters that override security settings, encryption keys, or authentication mechanisms, allowing unauthorized access to stored credentials
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unexpected changes to vault configuration files, suspicious API calls to /config or /settings endpoints with non-standard parameters, modified authentication policies or encryption settings in audit logs, unauthorized deserialization events, requests with script injection payloads in config parameters, and privilege escalation attempts following configuration changes
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict input validation and whitelisting for all configuration parameters; disable or sandbox deserialization of untrusted data; enforce role-based access controls on configuration endpoints requiring multi-factor authentication; maintain immutable audit logs of all vault settings changes; regularly validate vault configuration integrity against known-good baselines; apply principle of least privilege to configuration modification permissions; keep vault software patched and monitor vendor security bulletins

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Securityd Memory (macOS)
exploitation_method: Attacker injects malicious code into the securityd daemon process memory to intercept authentication credentials, encryption keys, or certificate data. This is typically done through process injection, DLL injection analogues, or exploiting memory vulnerabilities in securityd to gain unauthorized access to sensitive security material stored in process memory.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unexpected child processes spawned by securityd; securityd consuming abnormal memory resources; ptrace/process_attach syscalls targeting securityd; code injection attempts via mach messages; unauthorized reads from /var/db/timezone or Keychain-related directories; securityd crashes followed by rapid restart; suspicious loaded dylibs in securityd address space (via DYLD_INSERT_LIBRARIES); audit log entries showing credential access without legitimate user interaction.
mitigation_strategy: Enforce code signing and notarization verification for all binaries; implement System Integrity Protection (SIP) to protect securityd; disable code injection mechanisms (DYLD_INSERT_LIBRARIES restrictions); use address space layout randomization (ASLR); restrict ptrace capabilities; monitor and restrict Keychain access via Security Policies; employ memory encryption for sensitive credential storage; implement securityd process monitoring and restart detection; apply OS patches promptly; restrict developer tools access; enable audit logging for Keychain operations.

threat_vector: WooCommerce PDF Invoices & Packing Slips plugin (< 2.10.5) - Reflected XSS via unescaped tab and section parameters
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious URL with JavaScript payload in 'tab' or 'section' GET parameters. Plugin reflects these parameters into HTML attributes without sanitization, allowing script execution in victim's browser when they visit the link.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) HTTP GET requests to plugin pages containing 'tab' or 'section' parameters with script tags, quotes, or event handlers (e.g., onload=, onerror=); (2) WAF alerts on parameter values containing <, >, ', ", javascript:; (3) Browser console errors from injected scripts; (4) Sudden spike in referrer logs from external domains pointing to plugin pages with suspicious parameters
mitigation_strategy: Immediate: Update plugin to version 2.10.5+. Short-term: Disable plugin if update unavailable. Defense-in-depth: (1) Implement WAF rules to block requests with unescaped special characters in URL parameters; (2) Set Content-Security-Policy headers to restrict inline script execution; (3) Use PHP sanitization functions like sanitize_text_field() and esc_attr() on all user inputs before output; (4) Enable WordPress security headers; (5) Monitor for plugin exploitation attempts in access logs

threat_vector: WordPress low-privileged authenticated user remote code execution via plugin/theme exploitation
exploitation_method: Authenticated users with Author or Contributor roles exploit unpatched vulnerabilities in plugins or themes to execute arbitrary PHP code through file upload, SQL injection, or unsafe function calls that bypass capability checks
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unexpected file uploads to wp-content/uploads or wp-content/plugins directories by low-privilege users, POST requests to plugin/theme editor endpoints from non-admin accounts, error logs showing PHP execution in upload directories, database queries with UNION SELECT or INTO OUTFILE from low-privilege sessions, suspicious wp_options modifications, and failed capability checks in error logs
mitigation_strategy: Restrict file upload capabilities by role using role-based access control plugins, disable theme/plugin editors entirely via define('DISALLOW_FILE_EDIT', true), implement Web Application Firewall rules blocking executable file uploads, maintain strict plugin/theme allowlists, apply security patches immediately, run automated vulnerability scanners against installed plugins, configure proper file permissions (644 for files, 755 for directories), use security plugins that monitor admin actions, and implement file integrity monitoring on wp-content directory

threat_vector: Guest-initiated kernel memory exhaustion via Linux netback driver
exploitation_method: Malicious guest VM sends specially crafted network packets or requests that cause the netback driver to allocate excessive kernel memory buffers without proper limits, leading to denial of service or system instability on the host
detection_pattern: Monitor for: sudden spike in kernel memory usage from netback driver (check /proc/meminfo, slab allocations via 'slabtop'), excessive skbuff or page allocations in kernel logs, guest VM sending malformed or oversized network frames, oom-killer invocation correlated with netback activity, vmstat showing increased page faults and swap usage
mitigation_strategy: Apply kernel patches that enforce memory limits and rate limiting in netback driver; configure per-guest network resource quotas; implement memory cgroup limits for VM domains; enable kernel module parameter tuning (adjust allowed buffer pools); monitor and alert on anomalous netback memory allocation patterns; consider network traffic shaping at hypervisor level to prevent resource exhaustion attacks

threat_vector: Arbitrary physical memory read/write via vulnerable kernel driver (BS_RCIO64.sys) in Biostar RACING GT Evo
exploitation_method: Low-integrity process opens vulnerable driver's device object and issues crafted IOCTLs to read/write arbitrary physical memory locations, bypassing access controls and enabling privilege escalation, kernel code injection, or credential theft
detection_pattern: Monitor for: 1) Unsigned or suspicious CreateFile calls to \\.\BS_RCIO64 or similar device paths from non-system processes; 2) Anomalous DeviceIoControl calls with IOCTL codes associated with memory operations; 3) Low-integrity processes (AppContainer, Low IL) attempting device handle acquisition; 4) Driver load events for BS_RCIO64.sys with version <= 2.1.1905.1700; 5) Unexpected physical memory access patterns in kernel logs
mitigation_strategy: 1) Update Biostar RACING GT Evo to patched version > 2.1.1905.1700; 2) Implement Device Guard/HVCI to prevent unsigned kernel driver loading; 3) Restrict device object access via DACL to system/admin only; 4) Deploy application whitelisting to block unsigned executables; 5) Monitor and block low-integrity process creation; 6) Consider uninstalling unnecessary overclocking/system utilities if not required; 7) Apply Windows kernel mitigations (DEP, ASLR); 8) Monitor for driver exploits using EDR tools with kernel-level visibility

threat_vector: ATT&CK: File/Path Exclusions (T1027.010)
exploitation_method: Attacker configures antivirus or security software exclusions to prevent scanning of malicious files, directories, or file types. Often leverages legitimate administrative interfaces or registry modifications to whitelist malware execution paths, temporary directories, or specific file extensions from real-time protection.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unexpected modifications to antivirus exclusion lists in registry (HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows Defender\Exclusions), (2) PowerShell commands using Add-MpPreference or Set-MpPreference, (3) Suspicious WMI queries targeting antivirus configuration, (4) Legitimate security tools being configured with unusual paths or broad wildcards, (5) Exclusion additions by non-admin users, (6) Rapid sequential exclusion changes, (7) Exclusions pointing to temp directories or user writable locations
mitigation_strategy: Implement: (1) Group Policy restrictions on antivirus exclusion modifications (disable user/admin ability to modify), (2) Real-time audit logging of all antivirus configuration changes with alerting, (3) File integrity monitoring on antivirus config files, (4) Whitelist only necessary exclusions required for business operations, (5) MDE/EDR behavioral analysis to detect suspicious activity even if antivirus bypassed, (6) Endpoint detection and response (EDR) that operates independently of antivirus exclusions, (7) Regular compliance audits of security tool configurations

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1134.005 - Access Token Manipulation: SID-History Injection
exploitation_method: Attacker injects forged SID (Security Identifier) values into the SidHistory attribute of an Active Directory user or computer object, allowing them to impersonate group memberships and escalate privileges without modifying primary group memberships. Typically requires Domain Admin or account operator privileges and exploits the fact that SidHistory is used for migration scenarios but not properly validated during token generation.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unexpected SidHistory attribute modifications via Directory Service Changes event 5136, especially SidHistory additions for privileged groups; (2) Logon events (4624) where token contains SIDs not matching user's current group memberships; (3) Access to sensitive resources by accounts whose SidHistory contains high-privilege SIDs; (4) Use of Active Directory modification tools (dsmod, Set-ADUser) targeting SidHistory; (5) Directory replication events (5929) showing SidHistory changes from non-replication sources.
mitigation_strategy: (1) Enable 'SidHistory must match' validation via Group Policy on domain controllers; (2) Implement strict RBAC limiting who can modify SidHistory (typically disabled except during migrations); (3) Monitor and audit all SidHistory changes with real-time alerting; (4) Use authentication policies to restrict token claims validation; (5) Implement token binding and sign-on risk analytics; (6) Disable SidHistory attribute when not actively performing migrations; (7) Conduct regular AD integrity audits comparing SidHistory against legitimate migration records; (8) Enforce multi-factor authentication for high-value accounts vulnerable to this escalation.

threat_vector: Code Signing Abuse (T1553.002)
exploitation_method: Attacker obtains or forges a legitimate code signing certificate to sign malicious executables, DLLs, or scripts. This bypasses SmartScreen filters, security warnings, and whitelisting controls that trust signed binaries. The signed malware appears legitimate to endpoint security tools and users.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: signed executables with unusual certificate issuers or expired/revoked certs; mismatches between file hash and signature; binaries signed with stolen or subordinate CAs; suspicious files signed within abnormal timeframes; certificate with legitimate issuer but suspicious subject; execution of recently signed files from unusual locations; certificate used to sign multiple malware variants across telemetry
mitigation_strategy: Implement certificate pinning for trusted publishers; maintain and regularly update a blocklist of revoked certificates and malicious signers; deploy application whitelisting with code signing enforcement; require Extended Validation (EV) certificates for critical software; monitor certificate issuance logs from internal PKI; enforce Windows Driver Signature Enforcement and Secure Boot; conduct code signing hygiene audits; implement runtime verification of signatures before execution; use threat intelligence to track malicious signing certificates

threat_vector: CP-8000/CP-8021 Master Module firmware vulnerability affecting versions prior to V16.20
exploitation_method: Attacker exploits unpatched firmware vulnerability in industrial control system master modules to achieve remote code execution, privilege escalation, or denial of service through crafted network packets or authenticated session manipulation
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unexpected firmware version strings in device identification queries, anomalous network traffic to CP-8000/CP-8021 devices, failed authentication attempts followed by successful connections, unauthorized configuration changes in system logs, unexpected process execution or memory access patterns on affected modules, version detection showing <V16.20, unusual I/O state changes without corresponding legitimate commands
mitigation_strategy: Immediately upgrade all CP-8000 and CP-8021 Master Modules to firmware version V16.20 or later; implement network segmentation isolating industrial control systems; deploy firmware integrity verification mechanisms; enforce strict access controls and multi-factor authentication for module management interfaces; monitor for unauthorized access attempts; maintain offline backups of critical configurations; establish firmware update verification procedures including digital signature validation

threat_vector: Pointer Dereference Vulnerability in GPAC 1.0.1 gf_hinter_finalize function
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts a malformed media file (MP4/3GP) that triggers improper pointer initialization or validation in the gf_hinter_finalize function. When GPAC processes the file, a null or invalid pointer is dereferenced, causing a crash or potential code execution through memory corruption.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: segmentation faults in libgpac processes; core dumps from gf_hinter_finalize; abnormal process termination on specific media file inputs; valgrind/sanitizer reports of invalid memory access; suspicious file operations before crashes; repeated crash attempts from same source with varying media files
mitigation_strategy: Update GPAC to version 1.0.2 or later; implement input validation for media files before processing; run GPAC in sandboxed environments with memory protection (ASLR, DEP); use static analysis tools to scan codebase for pointer dereference issues; implement bounds checking in hint track processing; deploy Web Application Firewall rules to block malformed media submissions; monitor process execution with EDR tools for abnormal termination patterns

threat_vector: Cookie theft in Vehicle Service Management System 1.0 leading to Full Account Takeover
exploitation_method: Attacker exploits missing HttpOnly and Secure flags on session cookies, uses XSS vulnerabilities to extract cookies via JavaScript, or intercepts unencrypted HTTP traffic. Stolen cookies are then replayed to authenticated endpoints to impersonate the legitimate user.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Session cookies transmitted over HTTP instead of HTTPS, (2) Cookie values appearing in error logs or application logs, (3) Multiple authentication attempts from different IP addresses using identical session tokens, (4) Rapid sequential API requests from different geographic locations within impossible travel timeframes using same session cookie, (5) Successful authentication followed by session token usage from new IP/device/browser combination, (6) Unexpected changes to user profile or service requests correlated with session cookie reuse
mitigation_strategy: Implement: (1) HttpOnly and Secure flags on all session cookies to prevent JavaScript access, (2) SameSite=Strict attribute to prevent CSRF and cross-site cookie leakage, (3) Enforce HTTPS-only transmission with HSTS headers, (4) Implement session binding to IP address and User-Agent, (5) Short session timeouts with refresh token rotation, (6) Implement Content Security Policy (CSP) to prevent XSS, (7) Add cookie encryption at rest, (8) Deploy anomaly detection for impossible travel, (9) Require re-authentication for sensitive operations, (10) Regular security testing and vulnerability scanning for XSS vectors

threat_vector: PartKeepr ≤v1.4.0 file:// URI scheme in attachment loading allows authenticated local file disclosure
exploitation_method: Authenticated attacker crafts malicious part creation request with attachment URL using file:// scheme (e.g., file:///etc/passwd) to bypass URI validation and load arbitrary local files from the server filesystem into the application
detection_pattern: Monitor application logs for: (1) attachment load requests with 'file://' protocol in HTTP parameters, (2) unexpected local filesystem paths in attachment URLs, (3) failed/successful retrieval of /etc/, /var/, or other sensitive directories, (4) file:// requests originating from non-local sources, (5) application errors attempting to process file:// URIs, (6) audit logs showing parts created with unusual attachment URL formats
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade to PartKeepr v1.4.1 or later; implement strict URI scheme whitelist (http/https only) at application level with regex validation before processing attachment URLs; add network-level controls restricting outbound local filesystem access; enforce principle of least privilege on application service account; implement additional validation layer to reject any attachment URL containing 'file://', 'ftp://', or other non-approved schemes before processing

threat_vector: OpenEXR 3.1.x before 3.1.4 heap-based buffer overflow in LineCompositeTask::execute
exploitation_method: Malformed EXR image files with specially crafted tile/line data trigger buffer overflow in the line composite rendering task when thread pool processes image composition. Attacker crafts EXR with scanline or tile dimensions exceeding allocated buffer bounds, causing heap memory corruption during pixel composition operations.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) crashes/segfaults in OpenEXR process with stack trace containing 'LineCompositeTask::execute' or 'IlmThread'; (2) heap corruption signals (ASAN reports, valgrind errors); (3) abnormal process termination when processing EXR files; (4) file access logs showing processing of EXR files with suspicious dimensions in metadata; (5) memory access violations at addresses near heap allocations for image buffers
mitigation_strategy: Update OpenEXR to version 3.1.4 or later immediately. Implement input validation: (1) verify EXR file headers before processing - reject files with scanline/tile dimensions exceeding documented limits; (2) enable AddressSanitizer (ASAN) or similar runtime detection in development; (3) run image processing in sandboxed containers with memory limits; (4) use static analysis tools to detect buffer handling issues; (5) restrict EXR file processing to trusted sources; (6) implement file size limits and timeout mechanisms for processing operations

threat_vector: H2 Database JNDI Injection via JdbcUtils.getConnection
exploitation_method: Attacker supplies malicious JNDI driver class name and remote URL to org.h2.util.JdbcUtils.getConnection(), causing the H2 database to instantiate an attacker-controlled JNDI datasource, potentially leading to remote code execution through deserialization or LDAP/RMI protocol abuse
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) H2 database connection strings containing 'jndi:' protocol or JNDI references in driver parameters; (2) Unexpected JNDI lookups to external LDAP/RMI servers in application logs; (3) ClassLoader attempts to load unusual driver classes from remote sources; (4) Outbound network connections from H2 process to non-standard ports (389 for LDAP, 1099 for RMI); (5) Stack traces containing JdbcUtils.getConnection with non-standard driver class names
mitigation_strategy: Implement input validation: (1) Whitelist allowed JDBC driver classes and reject any JNDI-based drivers; (2) Use parameterized/prepared database connections with hardcoded driver class names; (3) Disable JNDI datasource lookup capabilities if not required; (4) Apply H2 security patches and upgrade to latest version; (5) Restrict network egress from application servers to prevent outbound JNDI lookups; (6) Implement Java deserialization filtering; (7) Run application with restrictive SecurityManager permissions limiting ClassLoader access

threat_vector: Smarty Template Engine - Arbitrary PHP Code Execution via Template Injection
exploitation_method: Template authors inject malicious PHP code through Smarty template syntax before versions 3.1.42 and 4.0.2. By exploiting insufficient input validation in template processing, attackers can break out of template context and execute arbitrary PHP code with application privileges.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unusual {php} tags or {eval} directives in template files, (2) Template files modified outside normal deployment processes, (3) Suspicious template syntax containing backticks or system() calls, (4) Unexpected PHP execution originating from template directories in error logs, (5) Web application logs showing template processing errors followed by unusual system calls or file operations
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Smarty to versions 3.1.42+ or 4.0.2+. Implement: (1) Disable {php} and {eval} tags via Smarty configuration, (2) Restrict template file write permissions to deployment processes only, (3) Implement code review for all template changes, (4) Use template sandboxing features, (5) Apply principle of least privilege to web application user account, (6) Monitor template directories with file integrity checking (AIDE/Tripwire), (7) Implement Web Application Firewall (WAF) rules to detect template injection patterns

threat_vector: Vim Heap-based Buffer Overflow Vulnerability
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious input (typically through file content or command sequences) that causes vim to allocate insufficient heap memory for a buffer operation. By providing specially crafted data exceeding expected bounds, the overflow overwrites adjacent heap structures, potentially enabling code execution or denial of service.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: vim process crashes with segmentation faults; abnormal memory consumption spikes; valgrind/AddressSanitizer reports of heap-buffer-overflow; unusual vim core dumps; file access patterns with unexpectedly large or malformed content; system logs showing vim terminated by signal 11 (SIGSEGV); unexpected child process spawning from vim context
mitigation_strategy: 1) Update vim to latest patched version immediately. 2) Enable ASLR (Address Space Layout Randomization) and DEP/NX protections at OS level. 3) Run vim in restricted mode when processing untrusted files. 4) Implement file input validation and sanitization before opening in vim. 5) Use memory protection tools (valgrind, AddressSanitizer) in development. 6) Restrict vim usage on systems processing untrusted content. 7) Monitor vim process behavior with AppArmor/SELinux profiles limiting syscall capabilities.

threat_vector: Samba pre-4.13.16 SMB1/NFS race condition allowing directory creation outside exported shares
exploitation_method: Attacker exploits race condition between SMB1 protocol operations and NFS access to create directories in non-exported filesystem areas by manipulating symlinks or concurrent file operations during share traversal
detection_pattern: Monitor for: symlink creation attempts in share paths, concurrent SMB1 MKDIR operations with rapid succession, directory creation in unexpected filesystem locations outside configured shares, audit logs showing file operations outside share boundaries, NFS and SMB operation interleaving in syslog
mitigation_strategy: Immediately upgrade Samba to version 4.13.16 or later; disable SMB1 protocol if not required; implement strict filesystem ACLs limiting Samba daemon permissions; use AppArmor/SELinux profiles to confine Samba to exported share directories only; monitor filesystem changes outside shares with inotify-based tools; segregate NFS and SMB access paths

threat_vector: Apache Kylin CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) misconfiguration allowing credentialed requests from any origin
exploitation_method: Attacker creates malicious webpage that makes authenticated requests to Kylin API endpoints using victim's browser session. Due to overly permissive CORS headers (Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * with Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true), browser allows requests with session cookies, enabling CSRF-like attacks and unauthorized data access.
