detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) CORS headers in HTTP responses containing 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *' combined with 'Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true'; (2) OPTIONS preflight requests from unexpected origins in access logs; (3) cross-origin POST/PUT/DELETE requests to /kylin/api/* endpoints with valid authentication tokens; (4) requests to Kylin endpoints originating from non-whitelisted domains in Referer headers
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade to patched versions (Kylin 2.6.7+ or 3.1.3+). Configure CORS headers to specify explicit allowed origins instead of wildcard; set Access-Control-Allow-Credentials to true only when necessary and paired with specific origins. Implement server-side request validation, restrict CORS to essential endpoints, require SameSite cookie attributes (Strict/Lax), and deploy Web Application Firewall rules to block suspicious cross-origin requests.

threat_vector: Uninitialized AOD (Always-On Display) driver structure in smartphone kernel
exploitation_method: Attacker triggers AOD driver initialization code path that fails to properly initialize memory structures, leaving stack/heap data containing sensitive information accessible through subsequent driver operations or information disclosure primitives
detection_pattern: Monitor for: uninitialized variable access warnings in kernel logs, AOD driver probe failures followed by successful device access, memory access violations with AOD subsystem addresses, unusual return values from AOD ioctl calls, kernel sanitizer reports (KASAN) flagging uninitialized reads in AOD driver code paths
mitigation_strategy: Implement mandatory structure initialization at declaration (memset to zero or designated initializers) in AOD driver code; enable kernel memory sanitizers (KASAN/MSAN) in production builds; conduct static code analysis on driver initialization routines; implement security reviews of all driver probe/initialization functions; apply kernel hardening patches; enforce compiler flags that detect uninitialized variables (-Wuninitialized, -Werror)

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Fileless Storage
exploitation_method: Attacker stores malicious code, configuration, or payloads in memory-only structures, registry hives, WMI repositories, or other non-traditional storage locations that bypass disk-based file monitoring and do not create artifacts on persistent storage.
detection_pattern: Monitor for unsigned code execution from memory regions, unusual WMI event subscriptions or classes, suspicious registry modifications to Run/RunOnce/Startup keys, PowerShell script blocks with encoded/obfuscated content, process memory injections via API calls (WriteProcessMemory, VirtualAllocEx), and detection of code execution without corresponding file creation events. Use ETW (Event Tracing for Windows) monitoring and memory forensics.
mitigation_strategy: Deploy Application Control (AppLocker, Windows Defender Application Control) with strict policies. Enable code integrity enforcement and Virtualization Based Security. Monitor and restrict WMI event subscriptions and COM object instantiation. Use PowerShell Module Logging and Script Block Logging. Implement memory-based detection tools (EDR solutions). Restrict registry write access to sensitive keys. Use YARA rules for in-memory detection and maintain updated threat signatures.

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Drive-by Target (T1189)
exploitation_method: Attacker compromises legitimate website or advertising network to inject malicious code/scripts. When victim visits the compromised site, JavaScript or browser exploits automatically execute without user interaction, potentially delivering malware or establishing command & control.
detection_pattern: Abnormal outbound connections to known malicious IPs; unexpected script execution in browser console; process creation from browser (powershell.exe, cmd.exe, rundll32.exe); unusual DNS queries; spike in antivirus/EDR alerts for memory injection; network traffic to C2 domains; browser cache containing suspicious .tmp or .exe files; JavaScript errors in web logs indicating obfuscated payloads
mitigation_strategy: Deploy Content Security Policy (CSP) headers to restrict script execution; implement web filtering to block known malicious domains; maintain updated browser versions and plugins with auto-patching enabled; use browser isolation technology for high-risk users; deploy endpoint detection & response (EDR) agents to monitor process creation and network behavior; apply network segmentation to limit lateral movement post-compromise; educate users to avoid untrusted websites; monitor third-party script dependencies for compromises

threat_vector: Reflected XSS in Booking Calendar WordPress plugin via unsanitized booking_type parameter
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious URL containing JavaScript payload in booking_type parameter (e.g., ?booking_type=<script>alert('XSS')</script>). When admin visits the link, unsanitized parameter is reflected in admin page HTML without encoding, causing browser to execute attacker's JavaScript in admin context.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) HTTP requests to admin pages containing booking_type parameter with encoded/unencoded script tags (<, >, script, onerror, onload keywords); (2) Admin page responses containing unescaped user input in HTML output; (3) WAF alerts for XSS payloads in query parameters; (4) Plugin error logs showing booking_type parameter handling; (5) Unexpected JavaScript execution in browser console during admin sessions
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Booking Calendar plugin to version 8.9.2 or later. Implement input sanitization using WordPress sanitize_text_field() and output escaping with esc_attr() or esc_html() for all user-supplied parameters in admin pages. Apply Content Security Policy (CSP) headers to prevent inline script execution. Implement nonce verification for admin actions. Conduct security code review of parameter handling and deploy Web Application Firewall rules to block XSS patterns in booking_type parameter.

threat_vector: JWT algorithm confusion attack
exploitation_method: Attacker modifies JWT header to change algorithm from RS256 (asymmetric) to HS256 (symmetric), then re-signs using the public key as HMAC secret, bypassing signature verification that expects RSA verification
detection_pattern: Log entries showing algorithm type changes in JWT headers between requests, unexpected HS256 usage in systems configured for RS256, signature verification failures followed by successful verification with different algorithm, or audit logs revealing public key being used in HMAC operations
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict algorithm whitelisting in JWT validation library, explicitly specify expected algorithm during verification (not accepting algorithm from untrusted header), maintain separate key management for symmetric vs asymmetric algorithms, use modern JWT libraries that disable algorithm confusion by default, implement JWT validation that rejects unexpected algorithm changes, and monitor for algorithm anomalies in authentication logs

threat_vector: Atlassian Jira Server/Data Center RCE via Email Template Injection
exploitation_method: Administrator with privileged access injects malicious code into email template fields. The template engine processes unsanitized input during email rendering, allowing arbitrary code execution on the server with application privileges.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unusual email template modifications in audit logs with suspicious syntax or code tags; (2) Template configuration changes by administrators during non-business hours; (3) Jira process spawning unexpected child processes; (4) File creation/modification in Jira directories outside normal operations; (5) Outbound connections from Jira service; (6) Error logs containing template processing exceptions with code fragments
mitigation_strategy: Immediately apply Atlassian security patches for affected versions. Restrict email template editing to minimal admin accounts. Implement change approval workflows for template modifications. Monitor template audit logs continuously. Disable email template functionality if unused. Apply input validation and output encoding in template processing. Run Jira with principle of least privilege (dedicated service account). Implement network segmentation to limit Jira outbound connections.

threat_vector: Implicit Intent Hijacking in Android Dialer (pre-January 2022 SMR)
exploitation_method: Unprivileged application registers intent filter matching Dialer's implicit intents without requiring permissions, allowing interception of contact data through intent extras or activity result callbacks. Attacker app declares matching action/category/data filters in AndroidManifest.xml to receive intents destined for legitimate Dialer components.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Multiple apps with overlapping intent filters matching android.intent.action.CALL, android.intent.action.VIEW with tel: or content: schemes; (2) Unprivileged apps accessing ContactsContract.Contacts or phone number data without READ_CONTACTS permission; (3) Unusual inter-process communication (IPC) between Dialer and unexpected third-party applications via intent interception; (4) Logcat entries showing intent resolution ambiguity or activity chooser dialogs for dialing actions
mitigation_strategy: Update Dialer to January 2022 SMR Release 1 or later. Implement explicit intents when launching sensitive contact operations. For defense in depth: (1) Use PendingIntent flags IMMUTABLE or MUTABLE_RECENCY_ONLY; (2) Validate intent origin before processing contact data; (3) Enforce READ_CONTACTS permission checks; (4) Implement runtime permission verification; (5) Monitor and restrict apps with multiple tel: or call-related intent filters; (6) Deploy SELinux policies restricting inter-process data flow involving contact information

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1553.001 - Gatekeeper Bypass (macOS)
exploitation_method: Attacker removes extended attributes (com.apple.quarantine) from downloaded files using xattr command, or exploits code signature validation weaknesses to bypass macOS Gatekeeper checks that prevent execution of unsigned/untrusted applications
detection_pattern: Monitor for: xattr commands removing quarantine flags, files lacking valid code signatures, execution of unsigned binaries from unexpected locations, audit logs showing gatekeeper policy violations, files with manipulated or missing extended attributes, processes spawned from /tmp or /var/tmp with missing signatures
mitigation_strategy: Enforce System Integrity Protection (SIP), require valid developer certificates and notarization, implement code signature validation enforcement via Endpoint Detection & Response, use MDM to enforce Gatekeeper settings and disable unsigned app execution, audit and monitor xattr command usage, maintain updated security policies, educate users on avoiding manual quarantine flag removal

threat_vector: CGI::Cookie.parse in Ruby through 2.6.8 mishandles security prefixes in cookie names, potentially allowing attackers to bypass cookie security mechanisms by manipulating prefix parsing
exploitation_method: An attacker crafts HTTP requests with malformed cookie names containing security prefixes (e.g., __Secure-, __Host-) that are improperly validated by vulnerable CGI::Cookie.parse implementations, allowing injection of cookies that should be restricted or bypassing intended security boundaries
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) HTTP requests with cookie names containing unusual prefix combinations or formatting inconsistencies; (2) __Secure- or __Host- prefixed cookies being set without HTTPS or appearing from unexpected sources; (3) CGI error logs showing cookie parsing anomalies; (4) Web application firewall logs detecting cookie injection patterns; (5) Application security events related to unexpected cookie attribute mismatches
mitigation_strategy: Immediately upgrade Ruby to 2.7.0+ or apply security patches for 2.6.8; upgrade CGI gem to 0.3.1 or later; implement strict cookie validation at the application layer; enforce cookie security attributes through Set-Cookie headers directly rather than relying on CGI parsing; use HTTP-only and Secure flags for sensitive cookies; implement Content Security Policy headers; conduct security testing specifically for cookie handling with malformed prefix inputs

threat_vector: Improper socket state validation during client event transmission leading to memory access violations in Snapdragon platforms
exploitation_method: Attacker sends socket events when socket is in invalid state (closed, uninitialized, or transitioning), causing out-of-bounds memory access, use-after-free, or null pointer dereference when event handlers attempt to access socket structures
detection_pattern: Monitor for: kernel memory faults (segmentation faults, page faults) associated with socket operations; unexpected socket state transitions in rapid succession; socket event processing with NULL socket pointers; kernel sanitizer reports (KASAN/UBSAN) showing heap-use-after-free or out-of-bounds access in socket event code paths; correlate timing of socket close operations with subsequent event delivery attempts
mitigation_strategy: Implement state machine validation: verify socket state is valid (ESTABLISHED, LISTENING) before processing any client events; use reference counting or atomic state flags to prevent race conditions; add bounds checking before dereferencing socket structures; implement event queuing with state validation at dequeue time; apply kernel memory protection (SMEP/SMAP); conduct fuzzing of socket state transitions; use address space layout randomization (ASLR) and stack canaries to limit exploitation impact

threat_vector: Unrestricted File Upload leading to Stored XSS in Sourcecodester Vehicle Service Management System 1.0
exploitation_method: Attacker bypasses file upload validation by uploading files with malicious script payloads (e.g., .svg, .html, or polyglot files containing JavaScript). When legitimate users access or view the uploaded file, the stored malicious script executes in their browser context with their privileges.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) HTTP POST requests to upload endpoints with unusual file extensions (.svg, .html, .js); (2) File uploads containing script tags, event handlers (onclick, onerror), or JavaScript code in metadata; (3) Sudden increase in file uploads from single user; (4) Files stored in web-accessible directories with executable MIME types; (5) Browser console errors or JavaScript execution from file URLs; (6) Access logs showing requests to uploaded files followed by XSS payload indicators
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict server-side file validation: (1) Whitelist only safe file types (.pdf, .jpg, .png); (2) Validate file content against magic bytes, not just extensions; (3) Store uploads outside web root or in non-executable directories; (4) Disable script execution in upload directories via web server config (e.g., disable PHP/JavaScript execution); (5) Rename uploaded files with random strings; (6) Implement Content-Security-Policy headers to prevent inline script execution; (7) Perform antivirus/malware scanning on uploads; (8) Apply output encoding when displaying filenames or file metadata to users

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1137: Office Application Startup
exploitation_method: Attacker creates malicious add-ins, templates, or modifies Office application startup paths (HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Office\[Version]\[Application]\Addins) to execute arbitrary code when Office applications launch, or embeds macros in startup templates.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: Registry modifications under Office\Addins paths, unexpected .XLL or .OLL files in Office startup directories, suspicious entries in Word/Excel/Outlook STARTUP folders, abnormal Office process child processes, network connections initiated immediately after Office launch, unusual Office add-in registry values with suspicious command-line parameters
mitigation_strategy: Disable Office macro execution by default via Group Policy (User Configuration > Administrative Templates > Microsoft Office > [Application] > Security > Macro Security); restrict Office add-in installation via allowlist policies; monitor and lock down HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Office registry paths with AppLocker/Windows Defender Application Control; regularly audit installed add-ins; implement application whitelisting for Office startup directories; educate users to disable untrusted add-ins

threat_vector: wolfSSL wolfMQTT 1.9 heap-based buffer overflow in MqttClient_DecodePacket
exploitation_method: Attacker sends malformed MQTT packets with oversized payloads or incorrect length fields that bypass bounds checking in the packet decoding function, causing heap memory corruption when processing Subscribe/Unsubscribe responses or other packet types
detection_pattern: Monitor for: heap corruption indicators (malloc/free anomalies), segmentation faults in wolfMQTT processes, abnormal memory access patterns in packet processing, MQTT packets with length fields exceeding RFC 3.1.1 constraints, sudden process termination with signal 11, AddressSanitizer/valgrind reports of heap buffer overflow, correlated failed MqttClient_DecodePacket calls followed by memory errors
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade wolfSSL wolfMQTT to version 1.10 or later; implement strict packet length validation before decoding; use memory safety tools (AddressSanitizer) in development/testing; deploy network segmentation to restrict MQTT broker communication; implement packet size limits at network boundary; enable ASLR and stack canaries; conduct fuzzing of packet parsing logic with malformed MQTT inputs

threat_vector: ManageEngine ADSelfService Plus pre-6116 UMCP ChangePasswordAPI Observable Response Discrepancy
exploitation_method: Attacker sends crafted requests to the ChangePasswordAPI endpoint via UMCP (Unified Management Control Protocol) and analyzes response timing, HTTP status codes, or error message variations to enumerate valid usernames or determine password validity without authentication
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Multiple rapid requests to /ChangePasswordAPI or UMCP endpoints from single source IP, (2) Requests with varying username parameters returning inconsistent HTTP status codes (200 vs 400 vs 500), (3) Response time variance exceeding threshold for identical request types, (4) Error messages revealing user existence or password state information, (5) Unauthenticated API calls to sensitive endpoints, (6) Log entries showing UMCP protocol abuse or abnormal operation sequence
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade ManageEngine ADSelfService Plus to build 6116 or later; implement response normalization (identical HTTP codes/delays for success/failure); enforce rate limiting on ChangePasswordAPI (max 5 requests/min per IP); disable UMCP access from untrusted networks; implement WAF rules to detect enumeration patterns; add API authentication requirements; monitor and alert on response discrepancies; disable verbose error messages in production

threat_vector: Stack-based Buffer Overflow in HDF5 1.13.1-1 (CVE-2021-46249) at H5Eint.c causing Denial of Service
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malformed HDF5 file or sends specially constructed input that triggers unbounded stack memory write in error handling code at H5Eint.c. The vulnerable function fails to properly validate buffer sizes when processing error messages, allowing stack corruption and process crash.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Segmentation fault or abnormal termination of HDF5 processing applications; (2) Stack corruption indicators in core dumps showing overwritten return addresses or stack canaries; (3) Unexpected error message generation patterns; (4) Applications processing untrusted HDF5 files crashing with signal 11 (SIGSEGV); (5) ELF core dump analysis showing corrupted stack frames near H5Eint functions
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade HDF5 to version 1.14.0 or later with stack overflow fixes applied. Implement: (1) Input validation for HDF5 file format before processing; (2) Stack canary protections and ASLR on target systems; (3) Process isolation and resource limits (ulimit -s) to minimize DoS impact; (4) Disable HDF5 processing of untrusted files from external sources; (5) Deploy Web Application Firewall rules to reject malformed HDF5 uploads; (6) Apply security patches immediately and maintain version inventory

threat_vector: Improper access control during dynamic XPU reconfiguration allowing unauthorized access to secure resources in Snapdragon platforms
exploitation_method: Attacker with local or elevated privileges exploits insufficient permission checks during XPU (execution processing unit) dynamic reconfiguration to bypass access control boundaries and gain unauthorized access to protected memory, hardware resources, or secure execution environments
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unauthorized XPU configuration change requests, privilege escalation attempts preceding XPU reconfig calls, unexpected modifications to XPU permission tables or ACLs, access attempts to secure memory regions from non-privileged processes, anomalous XPU register writes, failed access control validation logs, rapid repeated configuration attempts
mitigation_strategy: Implement mandatory permission verification at every XPU reconfiguration point; enforce role-based access control (RBAC) with strict privilege requirements; use secure boot and attestation to validate configuration state; enable runtime access control enforcement with hardware-backed protections; maintain detailed audit logging of all XPU reconfig operations; implement mutual authentication between configuration requestors and the secure resource manager; regularly validate configuration integrity using cryptographic checksums

threat_vector: IBM AIX 7.0, 7.1, 7.2, and VIOS 3.1 lscore command privilege escalation (IBM X-Force ID: 212953)
exploitation_method: Non-privileged local user exploits a buffer overflow or improper input validation vulnerability in the lscore command to write arbitrary code or modify executable memory, leading to privilege escalation and arbitrary code execution with elevated privileges
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unusual lscore command invocations with malformed or excessively long arguments in process logs; (2) lscore process spawning unexpected child processes; (3) Abnormal system calls from lscore process (execve, mprotect, mmap with PROT_EXEC); (4) Core dumps or segmentation faults from lscore; (5) File permission changes on lscore binary; (6) Unexpected network connections initiated by lscore process; (7) Audit logs showing non-privileged users executing lscore with suspicious parameters
mitigation_strategy: Apply IBM security patches: AIX 7.0.5.0+, 7.1.5.15+, 7.2.5.0+ addressing CVE-2023-2680 and related vulnerabilities. Implement: (1) Principle of least privilege - restrict lscore execution; (2) File integrity monitoring on /usr/bin/lscore and related libraries; (3) AppArmor/SELinux profiles restricting lscore capabilities; (4) Regular vulnerability scanning; (5) Disable lscore if not required; (6) Monitor audit logs for lscore execution attempts; (7) Implement strict input validation and fuzzing tests if custom wrappers are used

threat_vector: Break Process Trees (Process Termination/Hiding)
exploitation_method: Attacker terminates parent processes or uses techniques like process hollowing, process injection, or detaching from parent process trees to obscure process lineage and evade detection systems that rely on parent-child process relationships for threat hunting
detection_pattern: Orphaned processes with missing parents, sudden termination of legitimate parent processes followed by suspicious child activity, processes with invalid or suspicious PPID values, svchost.exe or explorer.exe spawning unusual children after parent termination, Event ID 4688 with ProcessParentId pointing to non-existent processes, anomalous process creation without expected parent context
mitigation_strategy: Implement process creation monitoring with enforcement of expected parent-child relationships; use endpoint detection and response (EDR) tools that track process genealogy and alert on orphaned or reparented processes; enforce application whitelisting to prevent unauthorized process execution; monitor for suspicious process termination followed by execution; maintain baseline process tree models and alert on deviations; restrict permissions for process termination operations; enable Sysmon Event ID 1 logging with detailed parent process tracking

threat_vector: Laundry Booking Management System 1.0 - Remote Code Execution via Arbitrary File Upload in profile.php
exploitation_method: Attacker sends crafted POST request to profile.php with malicious PHP code embedded in the 'image' parameter. Due to insufficient file type validation and lack of execution restrictions on uploaded files, the webshell executes with application privileges, allowing arbitrary command execution on the server.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) POST requests to profile.php with suspicious 'image' parameter values containing PHP code patterns (<?php tags, base64 encoded payloads); (2) File uploads with double extensions (.php.jpg, .phtml); (3) New PHP files created in upload directories with unexpected timestamps; (4) Unexpected outbound connections or command execution logs immediately following profile.php requests; (5) Web server error logs showing PHP execution in user-uploaded file directories
mitigation_strategy: Implement server-side file validation: (1) Whitelist only safe image MIME types (image/jpeg, image/png, image/gif) using functions like fileinfo_file(); (2) Strip file extensions and rename uploads with random names; (3) Store uploads outside web root or in directory with execution disabled (Apache: .htaccess with 'php_flag engine off'); (4) Use Content-Disposition: attachment headers on uploads; (5) Validate image file signatures (magic bytes); (6) Implement strict file permissions (644); (7) Apply input sanitization to profile.php parameters; (8) Update application to patched version immediately

threat_vector: ATT&CK: External Defacement
exploitation_method: Attacker gains unauthorized access to web server or content management system through compromised credentials, unpatched vulnerabilities, or injection attacks, then modifies publicly visible website content to deface branding, inject malicious content, or spread propaganda
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unexpected changes to file modification timestamps on web directories, unauthorized commits to version control repositories, modified .htaccess or index files, suspicious file creation in web root, failed authentication attempts followed by successful logins from unusual IPs, WAF logs showing successful injection attacks, CDN cache invalidation requests, user reports of website appearance changes, abnormal outbound connections from web server processes
mitigation_strategy: Implement web application firewall (WAF) with input validation rules; enforce strong authentication with MFA on CMS/FTP accounts; deploy file integrity monitoring (FIM) on web directories with alerting; maintain automated backups of website content with version control; patch all web application vulnerabilities promptly; restrict file upload capabilities and validate file types; implement code review processes; enable detailed logging of file modifications; use Content Security Policy (CSP) headers; conduct regular security assessments; establish change management procedures requiring approval before production updates

threat_vector: Disk Wipe (T1561)
exploitation_method: Attacker executes commands or tools to overwrite/erase disk sectors, partition tables, or master boot records (MBR) using utilities like cipher.exe, format, dd, shred, or proprietary wiper malware to destroy evidence and cause system unavailability
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unusual disk I/O patterns with high write volume to raw disk sectors; execution of disk formatting/wiping utilities (cipher, format, dd, shred); Windows events 4688 showing process creation with suspicious command-line arguments; kernel-level disk access from unexpected processes; alerts on privileged handle requests to \Device\HarddiskVolume; sudden filesystem errors or corrupted MBR; DFS/NTFS journal anomalies indicating sector overwrites
mitigation_strategy: Implement file integrity monitoring on critical boot/system sectors; restrict execution permissions on disk utility binaries via AppLocker/Windows Defender Application Control; enforce privileged access management requiring approval for raw disk operations; maintain offsite immutable backups tested regularly; deploy endpoint detection and response (EDR) with behavioral analysis of disk access patterns; configure audit policies for handle creation events; use UEFI Secure Boot with TPM to detect bootloader tampering; segment networks to prevent lateral movement of disk-wiper malware

threat_vector: Dnsmasq 2.86 heap-based buffer overflow in extract_name function via DNS query processing
exploitation_method: Attacker sends specially crafted DNS queries with malformed domain names that trigger buffer overflow in extract_name when processing question sections, allowing heap memory corruption and potential code execution
detection_pattern: Monitor for: dnsmasq process crashes or segmentation faults; abnormal memory access patterns via valgrind/sanitizers; malformed DNS packets with oversized or invalid name compression offsets in pcap logs; dnsmasq error logs showing parse failures on specific query patterns; heap corruption indicators in system logs
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade dnsmasq to patched version 2.87+; implement DNS query validation at firewall/IDS level to reject malformed packets; run dnsmasq with restricted privileges and memory limits (ulimit); deploy ASLR and stack canaries; monitor dnsmasq process for unexpected terminations; consider WAF/DNS filtering appliances upstream of dnsmasq

threat_vector: Stored Data Manipulation
exploitation_method: Attacker gains write access to stored data through compromised credentials, SQL injection, insecure APIs, or direct database access, then modifies data at rest to alter records, corrupt backups, or inject malicious content without triggering standard integrity checks.
detection_pattern: Unexpected data value changes in audit logs, mismatched checksums or hash values for data at rest, modification timestamps during unauthorized hours, database transaction logs showing unusual UPDATE/DELETE patterns, integrity verification failures on backup files, discrepancies between primary and replicated data stores
mitigation_strategy: Implement write-once-read-many (WORM) storage for critical data, enforce database encryption with access controls and row-level security, maintain cryptographic hashing of stored records with integrity monitoring, enable comprehensive database audit logging with alerting on suspicious modifications, enforce principle of least privilege for database accounts, implement immutable backup copies with separate access credentials, conduct regular integrity checks and checksums, use database activity monitoring (DAM) solutions

threat_vector: Heap-based buffer overflow in openhtj2k T1OpenHTJ2K::decompress during JPEG 2000 image decompression
exploitation_method: Malformed JPEG 2000 file with crafted tile/codeblock dimensions causes heap buffer overflow when T1DecompressScheduler processes decompression tasks via packaged_task execution, potentially overwriting heap metadata or adjacent objects
detection_pattern: Monitor for: heap corruption indicators (ASAN reports, malloc errors), abnormal memory allocation patterns before decompression, crashes in grk::T1OpenHTJ2K::decompress stack frames, application abort signals from heap canary violations, unusually large codeblock/tile dimension values in JPEG 2000 headers preceding crashes
mitigation_strategy: Update openhtj2k to patched version; implement strict validation of JPEG 2000 tile and codeblock dimensions before decompression; use memory sanitizers in testing; enable heap hardening (ASLR, DEP/NX); run image processing in sandboxed process with resource limits; reject files with malformed dimension headers; implement bounds checking wrappers around T1 decompression calls

threat_vector: Heap-based buffer overflow in HDF5 1.13.1-1 via H5F_addr_decode_len function in H5Fint.c
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious HDF5 file with specially formatted address encoding that exceeds heap buffer boundaries when decoded by H5F_addr_decode_len, causing memory corruption and process crash
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) HDF5 library process crashes with segmentation faults when processing specific file inputs; (2) ASAN/valgrind heap buffer overflow reports during HDF5 file parsing; (3) Unexpected termination of HDF5 applications with core dumps; (4) Memory access violations in H5Fint.c stack traces; (5) Abnormal resource consumption spikes followed by sudden process termination
mitigation_strategy: Apply security patches: upgrade HDF5 to version 1.13.2 or later containing CVE fixes; implement input validation for HDF5 files before processing; run HDF5 applications in sandboxed environments with memory protection (ASLR, DEP/NX); deploy application-level exception handling to gracefully fail on malformed files; restrict file access permissions to minimize exposure; use fuzzing tools to identify similar buffer overflows in custom HDF5 integrations

threat_vector: libming 0.4.8 parseSWF_DEFINELOSSLESS2 boundary check bypass in util/parser.c
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious SWF file with oversized DEFINELOSSLESS2 bitmap data that exceeds allocated buffer boundaries. When parseSWF_DEFINELOSSLESS2 processes the chunk without validating declared size against actual buffer limits, it triggers out-of-bounds memory access causing process crash or heap corruption.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Application crash/segmentation fault when processing SWF files; (2) AddressSanitizer/Valgrind heap-buffer-overflow reports in parseSWF_DEFINELOSSLESS2; (3) Abnormally large DEFINELOSSLESS2 chunk size declarations in SWF file structure (analyze magic bytes 0x4643 or 0x5743 followed by chunk type 0x14 and suspiciously large width/height values); (4) Repeated SWF parsing failures from untrusted sources; (5) Core dumps referencing util/parser.c
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade libming to version 0.4.9 or later with boundary checks implemented. Implement input validation: (1) Strict size checks before allocating buffers for bitmap data; (2) Validate DEFINELOSSLESS2 declared dimensions against file size before processing; (3) Use safe parsing libraries with bounds checking; (4) Implement file format sandboxing/isolated SWF processing; (5) Apply resource limits (max bitmap dimensions, timeouts) for SWF parsing; (6) Deploy file type validation and rejection of untrusted SWF sources at network boundary

threat_vector: Stored XSS in Vehicle Service Management System 1.0 Category List Section
exploitation_method: Attacker injects malicious JavaScript payload into the Category List input field during login panel interaction. The payload is stored in the backend database without proper sanitization. When legitimate users access the Category List section, the stored script executes in their browser context, potentially stealing session tokens, credentials, or redirecting to phishing pages.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unusual JavaScript patterns in category field database entries (script tags, event handlers like onerror/onclick); (2) HTTP requests containing HTML/JS entities in category parameters (e.g., %3Cscript%3E, <script>); (3) Unexpected DOM modifications or console errors in browser developer tools; (4) Sudden increase in requests to external domains from legitimate user sessions; (5) WAF alerts for script injection attempts in POST/PUT requests to category endpoints
mitigation_strategy: Implement: (1) Input validation using strict whitelisting for category names (alphanumeric, hyphens, underscores only); (2) Output encoding - use HTML entity encoding for all category display (e.g., < instead of <); (3) Content Security Policy headers (CSP) to restrict inline script execution; (4) Parameterized queries/prepared statements to prevent injection; (5) Regular security code reviews and SAST scanning; (6) Apply latest security patches to Vehicle Service Management System 1.0; (7) Implement HTTP-only and Secure flags on session cookies; (8) User-space input sanitization libraries (e.g., DOMPurify)

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Email Hiding Rules (T1564.010)
exploitation_method: Attacker creates or modifies email client rules (Outlook, Gmail, etc.) to automatically hide, delete, or forward incoming emails to attacker-controlled accounts or hidden folders. This is typically done post-compromise to hide command-and-control communications, credential harvesting responses, or exfiltration confirmations from the legitimate user.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unexpected email rules creation in Exchange logs (NewInboxRule events with suspicious forward/delete actions), (2) Rules redirecting to external domains, (3) Rules with keywords matching data exfiltration patterns, (4) Rapid rule creation by non-admin users, (5) Access to hidden/custom folders in mailbox auditing, (6) Forwarding rules to unfamiliar addresses detected via mailbox delegation changes
mitigation_strategy: Implement: (1) Mailbox auditing policies logging all inbox rule modifications, (2) Alert on email forwarding rules to external addresses, (3) Restrict user permissions to create rules via group policy or email security policies, (4) Regular audit of existing email rules across all user mailboxes, (5) Disable automatic forwarding to external domains, (6) Monitor Exchange for suspicious NewInboxRule PowerShell cmdlet executions, (7) Deploy email DLP solutions to detect hidden exfiltration patterns

threat_vector: Spring Framework Log Injection via malicious input in affected versions (5.3.0-5.3.13, 5.2.0-5.2.18, and older)
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts input containing newline characters (\n, \r\n) and log formatting sequences that are not properly sanitized by vulnerable Spring Framework versions. When logged, this creates false log entries that can mask malicious activities, inject forged audit trails, or trigger downstream log parsing vulnerabilities.
detection_pattern: Monitor logs for: unexpected newline sequences in single-log-line fields, log entries with suspicious formatting injected into user-controlled input fields, multiple consecutive log entries with identical timestamps and inconsistent sequence, log lines containing raw \n or \r\n characters in expected string values, false authentication success/failure entries appearing after suspicious input submission, discrepancies between application activity and corresponding log entries
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Spring Framework to patched versions (5.3.14+, 5.2.19+, 6.0.0+). Implement input validation to reject or sanitize newline characters and control characters in user-submitted data before logging. Configure structured logging (JSON format) to prevent log injection. Use log aggregation with anomaly detection. Implement strict output encoding for all logged user input. Enable log integrity monitoring and checksums. Apply WAF rules to detect and block newline injection patterns in HTTP requests.

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Match Legitimate Resource Name or Location (T1036.005)
exploitation_method: Attacker creates malicious files, processes, or services with names that closely mimic legitimate system resources (e.g., 'svvhost.exe' instead of 'svchost.exe', or placing malware in directories that appear legitimate) to evade detection and user scrutiny
detection_pattern: Monitor for: process creation with names differing by 1-2 characters from known legitimate processes, executables in unexpected directories (non-standard system paths), file hash mismatches for known-good binaries, process command-line arguments inconsistent with legitimate versions, elevated privileges for normally low-privilege processes, unusual parent-child process relationships, digital signature verification failures
mitigation_strategy: Implement application whitelisting with hash-based validation, enforce code signing requirements, configure process monitoring with baseline profiles of legitimate resources, use file integrity monitoring on critical system binaries, restrict write permissions to system directories, deploy endpoint detection and response (EDR) with behavioral analysis, maintain updated hash databases of known-good system files, enforce strict UAC policies, and conduct regular process/file audits against authorized inventory

threat_vector: Pointer Dereference Vulnerability in GPAC 1.0.1 _fseeko Function
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malformed MP4 or other media files that trigger improper pointer initialization in the _fseeko function, causing the application to dereference a null or invalid pointer when processing file seek operations, resulting in crash or undefined behavior
detection_pattern: Monitor for: segmentation faults or access violations originating from _fseeko stack traces; abnormal process termination with signal 11 (SIGSEGV) when processing media files; repeated failed file parsing attempts with memory access violations; crash dumps indicating invalid pointer dereference at specific _fseeko offset; system logs showing media player/transcoder process crashes correlating with specific malformed file uploads
mitigation_strategy: Apply GPAC security updates beyond version 1.0.1; implement input validation for media files before processing (file format verification, structure scanning); use memory-safe sanitizers during testing; deploy application sandboxing or containerization to isolate GPAC processes; implement watchdog mechanisms to restart crashed media processing services; configure file upload restrictions limiting file types and sizes; use static analysis tools to detect pointer dereference flaws in GPAC codebase

threat_vector: follow-redirects is vulnerable to Exposure of Private Personal Information to an Unauthorized Actor
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts a malicious redirect chain that causes the follow-redirects library to leak sensitive headers (Authorization, Cookies, credentials) or request body data to an attacker-controlled domain by following HTTP redirects to cross-domain destinations without proper validation of redirect targets
detection_pattern: Monitor for: HTTP 3xx redirect responses with Location headers pointing to external domains, Authorization/Cookie headers present in requests to non-original-domain targets, POST/PUT requests being downgraded to GET during redirects, requests to suspicious domains following initial legitimate request, multiple redirect hops in single request chain, sensitive headers in referer logs pointing to credential-containing requests
mitigation_strategy: Update follow-redirects to patched version; implement strict redirect validation by whitelisting allowed redirect destinations, disable cross-origin redirects unless explicitly needed, strip sensitive headers (Authorization, Cookie, X-API-Key) before following redirects to different domains, limit maximum redirect hops to 5, log all redirect attempts, use Content Security Policy to restrict redirect targets, validate redirect URLs against a allowlist, enforce HTTPS for all redirects, implement header filtering based on same-origin policy

threat_vector: libbpf 0.6.0 and 0.6.1 heap-based buffer overflow in __bpf_object__open
exploitation_method: Attacker provides a specially crafted BPF object file to bpf_object__open_mem() that triggers a 4-byte heap buffer overflow during object parsing, potentially allowing memory corruption and code execution in kernel or privileged userspace contexts
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) ASAN/MSAN heap overflow alerts in libbpf calls; (2) segmentation faults in __bpf_object__open or caller functions; (3) unexpected heap memory corruption detected by heap canary checks; (4) abnormal return values from bpf_object__open_mem with malformed BPF ELF input; (5) debug logs showing buffer boundary violations during BPF object deserialization
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade libbpf to version 0.6.2 or later containing the heap overflow patch; implement input validation on BPF object files before parsing; run BPF object loading in sandboxed/unprivileged contexts; enable ASLR and stack canaries at compile time; use memory sanitizers (ASAN) in non-production for detection; restrict BPF object loading to authenticated, trusted sources only

threat_vector: @replit/crosis versions <7.3.1 sensitive information exposure through container protocol
exploitation_method: Attacker intercepts or accesses unencrypted/improperly authenticated communications between JavaScript client and Replit container, extracting API tokens, credentials, or environment variables transmitted over the protocol
detection_pattern: Monitor for: plaintext credential transmission in network logs, unusual @replit/crosis version usage in dependencies, suspicious container protocol connections lacking proper TLS validation, environment variable access in application logs, authentication tokens appearing in unencrypted protocol buffers
mitigation_strategy: Immediately upgrade @replit/crosis to version 7.3.1 or later; enforce dependency version pinning in package-lock.json; audit all Replit integrations for credential exposure; implement network segmentation and TLS inspection; rotate all credentials that may have been exposed; use secret management systems instead of environment variables for sensitive data

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Component Object Model (COM) Hijacking / Abuse
exploitation_method: Attacker modifies COM registry entries (HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Classes\CLSID or HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\Software\Classes\CLSID) to redirect legitimate COM object instantiation to malicious DLLs. Applications querying COM objects execute attacker-controlled code with the privilege level of the calling process.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unexpected modifications to HKCU\Software\Classes\CLSID and HKLM\Software\Classes\CLSID registry keys via Sysmon Event ID 12/13, (2) COM object registration pointing to non-standard or suspicious DLL paths, (3) DLL loads from unusual locations triggered by COM queries, (4) Process execution spawned by legitimate applications loading unexpected DLLs, (5) Changes to InprocServer32, LocalServer32, or ProgID registry values
mitigation_strategy: Implement Application Whitelisting to restrict COM object instantiation; enable Registry access controls limiting modifications to COM CLSID keys; deploy endpoint detection/response (EDR) with registry monitoring; maintain COM object inventory and baseline; apply principle of least privilege to user accounts; patch COM-related CVEs promptly; monitor and restrict unsigned DLL loads; use Code Integrity policies and Device Guard to enforce code execution restrictions; audit COM object usage with Windows Event Log monitoring (Event IDs 4656, 4657)

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Hidden Window
exploitation_method: Attacker uses API calls (ShowWindow with SW_HIDE flag, SetWindowDisplayAffinity) or registry modifications to hide malicious process windows from user view, preventing detection through visual inspection while maintaining execution
detection_pattern: Monitor for ShowWindow API calls with SW_HIDE (0) parameter, WinEventHook registrations for EVENT_SYSTEM_FOREGROUND, processes with no visible windows but active network/file I/O, registry modifications to HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Control Panel\Desktop\WindowMetrics, presence of hidden processes in tasklist /v output lacking window titles
mitigation_strategy: Deploy application whitelisting to restrict window manipulation APIs, enable Audit Process Creation with command line logging to detect suspicious process execution patterns, use EDR solutions to monitor API call sequences indicative of window hiding, implement Group Policy restrictions on registry modifications related to window display settings, enforce process transparency tools that detect and report hidden windows in real-time

threat_vector: PublishPress Capabilities WordPress plugin (versions before 2.3.1) lacks authorization and CSRF checks in settings update functionality
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious form or script that submits settings modification requests to the plugin's admin endpoint. Due to missing nonce verification and capability checks, requests succeed even from unauthenticated users or low-privilege accounts. Attacker can modify plugin settings to escalate privileges, disable security features, or alter role capabilities.
detection_pattern: Monitor WordPress logs and audit trails for: (1) POST requests to PublishPress Capabilities settings endpoints without valid nonce tokens; (2) Settings modification events from user accounts lacking admin/manage_options capability; (3) Rapid or batch settings changes from single session; (4) Role or capability modifications that deviate from documented change requests; (5) Web application firewall alerts for CSRF patterns to wp-admin endpoints
mitigation_strategy: Immediately update PublishPress Capabilities plugin to version 2.3.1 or later. Additionally: (1) Implement Web Application Firewall (WAF) rules to enforce CSRF token validation on all admin POST requests; (2) Use security headers (SameSite cookies) to restrict cross-site request submission; (3) Apply principle of least privilege - limit admin role access to only necessary users; (4) Enable WordPress security plugins that audit and alert on unauthorized settings changes; (5) Configure logs to capture and review all capability and role modifications; (6) Schedule regular plugin and core WordPress updates; (7) Implement rate limiting on admin settings endpoints

threat_vector: ATT&CK: T1548.002 - Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism: Bypass User Account Control
exploitation_method: Attacker exploits UAC bypass techniques using Windows APIs (ShellExecute with 'runas' verb), COM interfaces (CLSID elevation abuse), or DLL hijacking in system directories to execute malicious code with SYSTEM privileges without user interaction or visible UAC prompt
detection_pattern: Monitor for: sysmon event ID 3 (network connection from unexpected services), process creation with parent/child mismatch (rundll32.exe spawning cmd.exe), registry modifications to HKLM\Software\Classes\CLSID, elevation of non-system binaries, Event ID 4688 with TokenElevationType=TokenElevationTypeLimited followed by Full, or missing/suppressed UAC event ID 4688
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict UAC policy enforcement (no bypassing), disable unnecessary COM elevation, apply principle of least privilege (users run as standard accounts), deploy Application Whitelisting (AppLocker/WDAC), patch Windows regularly, monitor and restrict DLL search paths, audit and remove unnecessary admin group memberships, deploy EDR with behavioral analysis for elevation anomalies

threat_vector: Apache James IMAP Infinite Loop via Crafted APPEND/STATUS Commands
exploitation_method: Attacker sends specially crafted IMAP APPEND or STATUS commands with malformed parameters that trigger infinite loops in the IMAP parsing stack. The parser fails to properly validate command structure or message boundaries, causing CPU to spin indefinitely while processing the malicious input.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Single IMAP connection consuming sustained high CPU without completing commands, (2) James process CPU usage spikes correlated with incomplete IMAP command sequences, (3) Parser state machines stuck in loops detected via profiling tools, (4) APPEND/STATUS commands with abnormal parameter lengths or nested structures in IMAP session logs, (5) Connections that send commands but never receive completion responses
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Apache James to patched version with input validation fixes. Implement: (1) Command parsing timeout thresholds to abort processing after N milliseconds, (2) Maximum limits on IMAP command parameter lengths and nesting depth, (3) Rate limiting per IMAP connection to restrict command frequency, (4) Parser state machine guards with iteration limits to prevent infinite loops, (5) Monitor and alert on sustained per-connection CPU usage, (6) Implement connection-level resource quotas to kill runaway sessions

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Digital Certificates (T1588.004)
exploitation_method: Attacker acquires valid digital certificates through legitimate channels, compromised CAs, or self-signed certificates to sign malware, establish HTTPS C2 channels, or perform man-in-the-middle attacks while appearing legitimate to security tools and users
detection_pattern: Monitor for: newly issued certificates with unusual organization names, certificates signed by untrusted CAs, certificate transparency log anomalies, multiple certificates for single domain within short timeframe, certificates with mismatched validity periods, revoked certificates still in use, unusual Subject Alternative Names (SANs), and mismatches between certificate issuer and expected domain registrant
mitigation_strategy: Implement certificate pinning for critical applications, enforce certificate validation across all TLS connections, monitor CT logs for unauthorized certificate issuance, maintain updated CRL/OCSP responder configurations, require organization-level CA verification, restrict internal CA signing privileges with MFA, audit CA access logs regularly, deploy YARA rules for signed malware detection, and use security tools that verify certificate chain legitimacy and check against known malicious certificates

threat_vector: Pipenv requirements file parsing vulnerability (CVE-2022-24765) - malicious package specifications in requirements files
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts specially formatted requirement entries with escape sequences or path traversal patterns in pipenv lock/requirements files that are parsed unsafely, allowing execution of arbitrary code during dependency resolution or installation phases
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unusual characters in requirements file entries (backticks, shell metacharacters), file modifications to Pipfile/Pipfile.lock with suspicious package names, unexpected subprocess calls from pipenv process, network connections to non-standard package repositories during dependency installation, permission errors or file access outside project directories
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade pipenv to version 2022.1.8 or later; implement file integrity monitoring on Pipfile/Pipfile.lock; restrict pipenv execution to isolated environments with minimal privileges; validate and sanitize all requirements files before processing; use software composition analysis tools to scan for known vulnerable dependency specifications; enforce code review processes for dependency file changes

threat_vector: Z-Wave devices with Silicon Labs 500 series chipsets using vulnerable CRC-16 encapsulation (Linear LB60Z-1 v3.5, Dome DM501 v4.26, Jasco ZW4201)
exploitation_method: Attackers craft malformed Z-Wave frames that bypass CRC-16 validation due to polynomial collision vulnerabilities in the checksum algorithm. This allows injection of unauthorized commands into the mesh network without cryptographic validation, potentially enabling device takeover or network compromise.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Z-Wave frames with mismatched CRC-16 values that still execute commands; (2) unexpected device state changes without corresponding authenticated commands in logs; (3) frame retransmissions with identical payloads but different CRC values; (4) devices responding to commands from unauthorized nodes; (5) Z-Wave sniffer captures showing frames that violate protocol integrity checks.
mitigation_strategy: Immediately: (1) Isolate affected devices from production mesh networks; (2) update firmware to patched versions from manufacturers; (3) implement Z-Wave network segmentation with dedicated security keys; (4) deploy Z-Wave sniffer monitoring for frame integrity anomalies; (5) enable application-layer encryption where supported; (6) restrict network joining to manually-approved devices only; (7) replace affected devices with alternative vendors if patches unavailable; (8) conduct network-wide re-keying after remediation.

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1588.004: Digital Certificates
exploitation_method: Attacker obtains or creates fraudulent digital certificates to sign malware, spoof legitimate services, perform man-in-the-middle attacks, or establish trusted communication channels for command and control infrastructure
detection_pattern: Monitor for: certificate validation failures in logs, unexpected new certificates in trusted stores, certificates with mismatched domain names, recently issued certificates from unusual CAs, multiple certificates for same domain in short timeframe, certificates with validity periods spanning years, revoked certificates still in use, certificate transparency log entries for your domains from unknown requesters
mitigation_strategy: Implement certificate pinning in applications, enforce OCSP stapling and CRL checking, monitor Certificate Transparency logs for your domains, maintain strict CA trust policies, validate certificate chains in real-time, deploy HSTS headers, implement certificate scanning in email gateways, regularly audit installed root certificates, use DNS CAA records to restrict certificate issuance, maintain updated CRL/OCSP responder configurations

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Browser Extensions (T1176)
exploitation_method: Attacker installs malicious browser extension through social engineering, supply chain compromise, or by gaining local access. Extension gains access to browsing data, credentials, session tokens, and can intercept/modify web traffic and perform actions on behalf of the user.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unexpected browser extension installations in user profiles; extensions from untrusted sources in extension manifest logs; suspicious permissions requests (clipboard access, storage, webRequest); unusual outbound traffic from extension processes; modifications to browser profile directories; entries in browser extension history showing unauthorized installs; permission changes to extension directories; API calls to credential storage APIs from extension contexts
mitigation_strategy: Implement centralized extension management policies via Group Policy (Windows) or MDM (macOS/Linux); maintain allowlist of approved extensions and block all others; disable extension installation for standard users; regularly audit installed extensions and permissions; educate users on extension risks; monitor extension traffic via network IDS; use browser security tools that detect malicious extension behavior; enforce automatic extension updates; implement CSP headers to restrict extension capabilities; use endpoint detection and response (EDR) to monitor browser process anomalies

threat_vector: NULL pointer dereference in dhrystone 2.1 dhry_1.c main() function causing denial of service
exploitation_method: Attacker provides malformed input or triggers code path in main() where uninitialized or unchecked pointer is dereferenced without NULL validation, causing immediate segmentation fault and process termination
detection_pattern: Monitor for: SIGSEGV signal on dhrystone process, core dumps with backtrace pointing to dhry_1.c main(), sudden unexpected termination of benchmark process, segmentation fault messages in syslog/journalctl with dhrystone executable name, abnormal exit code 139 or 136
mitigation_strategy: Update to patched dhrystone version; implement input validation before pointer use in main(); add NULL pointer checks before all dereference operations; use compiler flags (-ftrapv, -fno-delete-null-pointer-checks) to catch NULL dereferences; employ memory safety tools (AddressSanitizer, Valgrind) during testing; restrict execution permissions and isolate benchmark in sandboxed environment with resource limits

threat_vector: HDF5 1.13.1-1 Segmentation Fault Denial of Service
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malformed HDF5 file with specific structure that triggers memory access violation in HDF5 library parsing routines, causing process crash when legitimate application processes the file
detection_pattern: Monitor for: sudden process termination with SIGSEGV signal, core dumps from HDF5-dependent applications, repeated crashes on specific file inputs, abnormal exit codes in application logs, segmentation fault messages in syslog/journalctl with HDF5 library references
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade HDF5 to version 1.13.2 or later; implement input validation and sandboxing for HDF5 file processing; enforce file format verification before processing; deploy application-level exception handling to gracefully handle crashes; isolate HDF5 processing in containerized environments with resource limits; implement file integrity checks and reject suspicious HDF5 structures

threat_vector: Z-Wave Silicon Labs 500 series S2 protocol implementation vulnerability affecting ZooZ ZST10 v6.04, ZEN20 v5.03, ZEN25 v5.03, and Aeon Labs ZW090-A devices
exploitation_method: Attacker leverages cryptographic weaknesses or protocol parsing flaws in S2 security framework implementation on affected chipsets to forge Z-Wave frames, bypass device authentication, or execute unauthorized commands without proper key material validation
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unexpected Z-Wave frame sequences with invalid S2 HMAC signatures, repeated authentication failures from same source, out-of-sequence frame IDs, devices accepting commands without proper security class escalation, unusual device-to-device communication patterns, firmware version queries to ZooZ/Aeon devices from unauthorized controllers, syslog entries indicating Z-Wave stack errors or security violations
mitigation_strategy: Immediately upgrade affected devices to patched firmware versions; restrict Z-Wave network access through controller whitelist; disable S0 legacy security and enforce S2 with highest security class (authenticated + encrypted); implement Z-Wave network segmentation using secondary controllers; monitor Z-Wave traffic for anomalies; disable remote management features; consider replacing devices if patches unavailable; audit Z-Wave device inclusion logs for unauthorized pairings

threat_vector: The Stars Rating WordPress plugin before 3.5.1 allows unauthenticated users to submit unvalidated integer values in rating submissions, causing Denial of Service through comment system overload or dashboard performance degradation
exploitation_method: Attacker sends HTTP POST requests with oversized or malformed integer values in the rating parameter to the plugin's AJAX handler. The unvalidated input is stored in the database and processed on each page load, consuming excessive server resources when rendering comments or the pending comments dashboard
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) POST requests to wp-admin/admin-ajax.php with rating parameter containing unusually large integers or non-standard numeric values; (2) database records in wp_commentmeta table with abnormally large integer values for rating keys; (3) slow database queries filtering comments with specific rating criteria; (4) increased CPU/memory usage on admin dashboard when viewing comments; (5) MySQL error logs showing integer overflow or conversion warnings
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade The Stars Rating plugin to version 3.5.1 or later. Implement input validation: sanitize and validate rating submissions with type casting to integer and range validation (e.g., 1-5 stars). Add rate limiting on rating submission endpoints. Monitor wp_commentmeta table for anomalous rating values and implement database constraints to enforce valid rating ranges. Consider using Web Application Firewall rules to block requests with suspiciously large numeric payloads in rating parameters

threat_vector: ATT&CK: ESXi Administration Command (T1059.008)
exploitation_method: Attacker gains access to ESXi host via compromised credentials or vulnerability, then executes arbitrary commands through SSH, DCUI, or vSphere API to achieve lateral movement, persistence, or data exfiltration within virtualized infrastructure
detection_pattern: Monitor ESXi for: unusual SSH login attempts with privileged accounts; esxcli command execution with suspicious parameters; unexpected vMotion or VM modification activities; shell.session events in vpxa logs; execution of non-standard esxcli modules; SSH sessions originating from non-management networks; repeated authentication failures followed by success; commands targeting VM datastores or network configuration outside normal maintenance windows
mitigation_strategy: Implement: disable SSH by default and enable only during authorized maintenance; enforce strong MFA for ESXi host access; restrict SSH to specific management IP ranges via firewall rules; deploy host-based intrusion detection monitoring ESXi command audit logs; use role-based access control (RBAC) limiting privileges; regularly patch ESXi to latest versions; implement vSphere with Tanzu Security hardening; enable ESXi lockdown mode; monitor and alert on privilege escalation attempts; maintain audit logs with syslog forwarding to centralized SIEM

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1556.004 - LSA Secrets Extraction
exploitation_method: Attacker uses tools like Mimikatz or direct registry access to dump credentials stored in HKLM\SECURITY\Policy\Secrets (domain cached credentials, DPAPI master keys, service account passwords). Requires SYSTEM privileges and typically occurs post-compromise.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: 1) Process creation with names like mimikatz.exe, procdump.exe accessing lsass.exe; 2) Registry access to HKLM\SECURITY\Policy\Secrets via abnormal processes; 3) Eventlog 4657 (registry modification) targeting SECURITY hive; 4) Sysmon Event 10 (ProcessAccess) showing non-system processes accessing lsass.exe with suspicious handles; 5) Credential Guard bypass attempts if enabled; 6) Unusual DPAPI-related API calls.
mitigation_strategy: 1) Enable Windows Defender Credential Guard on Windows 10+ to isolate LSA process memory; 2) Restrict local admin privileges and enforce privileged access management (PAM); 3) Deploy application whitelisting to block mimikatz and similar tools; 4) Enable LSA protection (RunAsPPL registry key) to run LSA as Protected Process Light; 5) Monitor and log all lsass.exe access attempts; 6) Use password-less authentication (Windows Hello, FIDO2); 7) Implement EDR solutions with behavioral analysis for credential dumping; 8) Enforce strong audit logging on registry modifications.

threat_vector: Bluetooth link disconnection via duplicate BD address connection attempt
exploitation_method: Attacker initiates a Bluetooth connection using the same BD (Bluetooth Device) address as an already-connected legitimate device, causing the Bluetooth stack to improperly handle the duplicate address scenario and disconnect the active link
detection_pattern: Monitor Bluetooth logs for: (1) multiple connection attempts with identical BD addresses in short time window, (2) unexpected link disconnection events preceded by connection requests from same BD address, (3) rapid HCI (Host Controller Interface) connection/disconnection cycles, (4) kernel logs showing 'duplicate device address' warnings or BT_ERR entries related to address conflicts
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict BD address validation in Bluetooth stack: (1) reject connection attempts from already-connected BD addresses before link establishment, (2) add connection request deduplication logic with timestamp checking, (3) update Bluetooth firmware/driver to latest patches addressing CVE-related BD address handling, (4) enable BD address spoofing detection by validating device identity via additional authentication factors (link keys, ECDH), (5) configure connection timeout and retry limits to prevent rapid reconnection abuse

threat_vector: mruby Heap-based Buffer Overflow
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious Ruby code or input that causes mruby's memory management to write beyond allocated heap buffer boundaries, typically through string operations, array manipulations, or unmarshalling of untrusted data that exceeds expected size limits
detection_pattern: Monitor for: segmentation faults or access violations in mruby processes; heap corruption detection via Address Sanitizer (ASAN) or Valgrind; unexpected process termination with SIGSEGV; abnormal memory growth patterns; core dumps with heap metadata corruption; suspicious input sizes exceeding documented limits for string/array operations
mitigation_strategy: Apply security patches from mruby project immediately; implement input validation and size limits before passing to mruby; run mruby in sandboxed environments with memory protection; use modern compiler flags (-fstack-protector-strong, -D_FORTIFY_SOURCE=2); enable AddressSanitizer in development/testing; restrict mruby script execution to trusted sources only; monitor process resource limits (ulimit); consider using safer Ruby implementations for untrusted input processing

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Modify or Spoof Tool UI
exploitation_method: Attacker modifies legitimate application UI elements, dialogs, or visual components to deceive users into performing unintended actions. This includes fake authentication prompts, spoofed system dialogs, or altered warning messages that trick users into granting permissions, entering credentials, or executing malicious commands.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unexpected child processes spawning GUI dialogs, mismatched process-to-window mappings, UI automation API calls from non-standard processes, unexpected WinAPI calls (CreateWindowEx, ShowWindow) from suspicious sources, inconsistent window class names or titles, accessibility API abuse (OLEACC, UIAutomation), screenshot capture followed by dialog creation, or user reports of unfamiliar authentication prompts appearing outside normal application context.
mitigation_strategy: Implement UI integrity verification through application whitelisting of approved UI frameworks, enforce code signing for GUI components, use operating system-level UI privilege separation, deploy security awareness training on phishing via fake dialogs, implement credential guard to prevent credential harvesting from fake prompts, monitor and restrict accessibility API access, require multi-factor authentication to prevent single-prompt compromise, and conduct regular security audits of custom UI implementations.

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1552.005 - Unsecured Credentials: Cloud Instance Metadata API
exploitation_method: Attacker makes HTTP requests to cloud metadata endpoints (e.g., 169.254.169.254 on AWS/Azure/GCP) from compromised instance to retrieve temporary credentials, API keys, or role assumptions without authentication
detection_pattern: Monitor for: HTTP requests to 169.254.169.254, 127.0.0.1:80/metadata, or cloud-specific metadata endpoints; unusual GET requests to /latest/meta-data/ or similar paths; processes spawning curl/wget/powershell accessing metadata IPs; IAM credential usage from unexpected source IPs or times; CloudTrail logs showing API calls from overly-permissive instance roles
mitigation_strategy: 1) Enforce IMDSv2 requiring session tokens; 2) Disable metadata API access where unused; 3) Restrict metadata endpoint via network policies/host firewalls; 4) Apply least-privilege IAM roles to instances; 5) Use credential federation and short-lived tokens; 6) Monitor and alert on metadata API access patterns; 7) Implement runtime security to detect credential exfiltration

threat_vector: COMOS Web Components Remote Code Execution - Insufficient Input Validation
exploitation_method: Attacker sends crafted HTTP requests to vulnerable COMOS web components with malicious payloads in parameters or request bodies. The web interface fails to properly validate and sanitize inputs before processing them, allowing injection of code that executes in the context of the COMOS application with elevated privileges.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: HTTP POST/GET requests to COMOS web component endpoints with unusual parameter values or encoded payloads; error messages indicating code execution attempts; unexpected spawning of child processes from COMOS service accounts; changes to web application logs showing malformed requests with high entropy data; network connections from COMOS server to unusual external IPs; database query logs showing injected commands
mitigation_strategy: Immediately upgrade affected COMOS versions: V10.2 apply patch, V10.3 upgrade to V10.3.3.3 or later, V10.4 upgrade to patched version. Implement Web Application Firewall (WAF) rules to block requests with suspicious payloads to COMOS endpoints. Disable web components if not required. Apply principle of least privilege to COMOS service accounts. Implement input validation and output encoding at application level. Monitor and restrict outbound connections from COMOS servers. Conduct security audit of all COMOS web interface access logs for indicators of exploitation attempts.

threat_vector: HwNearbyMain module Data Processing Errors vulnerability leading to process restart
exploitation_method: Attacker sends malformed or specially crafted data packets to the HwNearbyMain module's nearby communication interface. The module fails to properly validate input data structure, triggering unhandled exceptions in data processing routines that cause the process to crash and restart.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Repeated HwNearbyMain process restarts within short time intervals; (2) Exception logs containing 'HwNearbyMain' with data parsing or deserialization errors; (3) Nearby communication protocol errors followed immediately by process termination; (4) Spike in malformed nearby service packets from specific source; (5) System logs showing unexpected service restart of nearby daemon; (6) Increase in OutOfMemory or buffer overflow exceptions in HwNearbyMain logs
mitigation_strategy: Implement input validation and sanitization for all data received by HwNearbyMain module before processing; deploy bounds checking and exception handling around data parsing operations; configure process restart limits to prevent rapid restarts (circuit breaker pattern); enable security updates for HwNearbyMain; implement network segmentation to restrict nearby communication to trusted sources; monitor and alert on process restart anomalies; use SELinux/AppArmor policies to limit HwNearbyMain process capabilities and restart frequency

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Spearphishing Attachment (T1566.001)
exploitation_method: Attacker sends targeted email with malicious attachment (executable, macro-enabled document, or archive) crafted to appear legitimate. Recipient opens attachment, triggering code execution or enabling credential harvesting through social engineering.
detection_pattern: Email gateway alerts on suspicious file types; EDR detects unsigned executable launch from email client temp directory; file hash matches known malware; macro execution from Office application; unusual process spawning from email attachment; user reports unsolicited attachment; abnormal network connection from newly executed process
mitigation_strategy: Implement email security with attachment sandboxing and detonation analysis; disable macros by default in Office applications; enforce application whitelisting; deploy endpoint detection and response (EDR); conduct security awareness training on phishing recognition; implement DMARC/SPF/DKIM authentication; require multi-factor authentication to prevent credential misuse; use content filtering to block executable file types; maintain updated antivirus signatures

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Identify Business Tempo (T1592.004)
exploitation_method: Attacker performs passive reconnaissance to observe organizational patterns including working hours, shift schedules, peak activity times, and business cycle patterns through public information, employee social media, website analytics, network traffic timing, or OSINT tools to identify optimal windows for attacks when security monitoring is reduced
detection_pattern: Unusual WHOIS queries, DNS enumeration spikes, sustained network scanning during off-hours, repeated probing of employee social media profiles, automated scraping of company websites, timing-based reconnaissance of VPN/email login patterns, Google Dorking queries targeting organizational information, abnormal geographic access attempts aligned with timezone patterns
mitigation_strategy: Implement consistent 24/7 security monitoring and incident response capabilities; maintain uniform employee operational security training regardless of shift; enforce rate-limiting on WHOIS/DNS queries and web requests; monitor social media for employee mentions of schedules or business patterns; implement security awareness training focused on OPSEC; use deception (honeypots, canaries) on business-related information; establish baseline network traffic patterns to detect reconnaissance anomalies; restrict public visibility of company operational schedules and employee information

threat_vector: Insecure file permissions on config files
exploitation_method: Attacker reads world-readable or group-readable configuration files containing database credentials, API keys, encryption keys, or connection strings. By exploiting overly permissive file permissions (e.g., 644 or 666), unprivileged users or compromised processes gain unauthorized access to sensitive data.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Files with permissions 644, 666, or broader in config directories (/etc, /app/config); (2) Unexpected file access to config files by non-owner processes; (3) Audit log entries showing permission changes to config files with chmod commands removing restrictive permissions; (4) Regular filesystem scans identifying config files with world or group-readable bits set
mitigation_strategy: Enforce restrictive file permissions (600 or 640) on all configuration files using automated configuration management tools. Implement umask 0077 for application processes. Use SELinux/AppArmor mandatory access controls. Deploy automated compliance scanning to detect and alert on permission drift. Implement file integrity monitoring (FIM) to detect unauthorized permission modifications. Store sensitive credentials in dedicated secret management systems rather than config files.

threat_vector: Apache James IMAP LIST command regex denial of service via Jazzer fuzzer-identified vulnerability
exploitation_method: Attacker sends maliciously crafted IMAP LIST commands with specially designed mailbox name patterns that trigger catastrophic backtracking in the vulnerable regular expression used by James to parse LIST responses. This causes excessive CPU consumption and memory usage, exhausting server resources.
