detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Multiple IMAP LIST commands from single user with increasingly complex or repetitive pattern characters; (2) CPU spike coinciding with IMAP command processing; (3) Memory consumption increase during LIST command handling; (4) IMAP service response latency degradation; (5) Log entries showing LIST command processing delays or timeouts; (6) Patterns containing nested quantifiers or backslash-heavy mailbox names in LIST requests
mitigation_strategy: Update Apache James to patched version with regex denial of service fix; implement input validation/sanitization on IMAP LIST commands with mailbox name length and character restrictions; add timeout mechanisms for regex matching operations; rate-limit IMAP LIST commands per user; monitor and alert on abnormal LIST command frequency; consider using regex engine with timeout capabilities or non-backtracking matching; implement connection limits to prevent resource exhaustion from single user

threat_vector: Arbitrary File Read in NavigateCMS 2.9 via /navigate/navigate_download.php id parameter
exploitation_method: Attacker manipulates the 'id' parameter in navigate_download.php with path traversal sequences (../) or absolute paths to read sensitive files outside intended directories, such as configuration files, source code, or system files
detection_pattern: HTTP requests to /navigate/navigate_download.php with id parameter containing: '../' sequences, absolute paths (e.g., '/etc/passwd'), null bytes (%00), or unusual encoding; file access logs showing requests for files outside expected download directory; web server logs with 200 responses for suspicious file requests; error logs revealing file path disclosures
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict input validation on id parameter using allowlist of permitted alphanumeric values; enforce path canonicalization to resolve and verify all file paths remain within designated directory; use chroot/jailed environments for file operations; apply principle of least privilege for web server process; implement access controls to restrict navigate_download.php to authenticated users; patch to NavigateCMS 2.10 or later; disable direct script access via web server configuration

threat_vector: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation (Cross-site Scripting) in Hoppscotch before 2.1.1
exploitation_method: Attacker injects malicious JavaScript code through unfiltered input fields (request parameters, headers, body, environment variables, or collection names) that gets rendered in the DOM without proper HTML encoding or sanitization, allowing script execution in victim's browser context
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unusual script tags or event handlers in request/response logs; (2) HTML entities that should be encoded appearing as raw characters in stored data; (3) Browser console errors related to Content Security Policy violations; (4) HTTP requests containing javascript: protocols or on* event attributes in parameters; (5) Abnormal DOM mutations in network inspector; (6) Requests with payloads like <script>, onerror=, onclick=, or encoded XSS patterns
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade to Hoppscotch 2.1.1 or later; implement strict Content Security Policy (CSP) headers; use DOMPurify or similar library to sanitize all user inputs before DOM insertion; apply HTML entity encoding to all dynamic content; validate input against whitelist patterns; implement HTTPOnly and Secure flags on cookies; conduct security code review of template rendering logic; deploy Web Application Firewall (WAF) rules to block common XSS payloads

threat_vector: Fast Flux DNS (ATT&CK T1568.001)
exploitation_method: Attacker rapidly changes DNS A record mappings to rotate through compromised hosts or proxy servers, making malicious infrastructure difficult to track. Multiple IP addresses resolve to the same domain within short time intervals (minutes/hours), distributing traffic across botnet nodes to evade blocking and maintain command-and-control connectivity.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) DNS A record query responses showing same domain resolving to 10+ different IPs within 24 hours; (2) TTL values abnormally low (30-300 seconds vs typical 3600+); (3) Rapid sequential DNS queries from single client to same domain with varying results; (4) Passive DNS historical data showing flux patterns; (5) GeoIP analysis revealing IPs from disparate geographic regions for single domain; (6) High volume of NXDOMAIN errors followed by successful resolution; (7) Whois records showing recently registered domains with minimal age
mitigation_strategy: Implement DNS sinkholing for known fast-flux domains; deploy DNS filtering with behavioral analysis to detect rapid A-record changes; maintain passive DNS database for historical correlation; use threat intelligence feeds (abuse.net, DNS logs) to identify suspicious domains early; enforce DNS query rate-limiting per client; implement DNSSEC validation; block queries to known bulletproof hosting providers; monitor internal DNS resolver logs for anomalous resolution patterns; segment network to restrict C2 communication attempts; deploy endpoint DNS monitoring to detect infected systems querying fast-flux domains

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1113: Screen Capture
exploitation_method: Attacker executes code to capture framebuffer or screenshot functionality via APIs (Windows: GDI/BitBlt, Linux: X11/Wayland, macOS: CGDisplayCreateImage) to record sensitive information displayed on screen including credentials, documents, or communications
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unusual process creation with screenshot utilities (PrintScreen, screencap, import, gnome-screenshot); API calls to GDI functions (CreateDC, BitBlt, GetDIBits) or Cocoa framework calls; file system activity creating image files in temp directories; elevated process access to display/framebuffer devices; suspicious child processes spawned from office/browser applications
mitigation_strategy: Implement application whitelisting to restrict screenshot tools; deploy endpoint detection and response (EDR) with API hooking on graphics libraries; use display privacy filters and screen lock policies; enforce least privilege to limit process access to display devices; monitor and alert on unexpected image file creation; implement device access controls preventing unauthorized framebuffer access; conduct user security awareness training on suspicious application permissions

threat_vector: Hilinksvc Data Processing Errors vulnerability leading to denial of service
exploitation_method: Attacker sends malformed or oversized data packets to Hilinksvc service that trigger unhandled exceptions in data processing routines, causing service crash or resource exhaustion
detection_pattern: Monitor for: repeated Hilinksvc crash events in Event Viewer, sudden spikes in CPU/memory usage by Hilinksvc process, error codes related to buffer overflows or parsing failures in Hilinksvc logs, unusual network traffic patterns targeting Hilinksvc ports, application hang events followed by restart attempts
mitigation_strategy: Apply latest Hilinksvc security patches immediately, implement input validation and sanitization in data processing functions, configure process monitoring and auto-restart policies, deploy network rate limiting on Hilinksvc ports, enable Windows Defender/antimalware protection, restrict network access to Hilinksvc service through firewall rules, monitor service status with alerting for unexpected restarts

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Container and Resource Discovery (T1526)
exploitation_method: Attacker uses kubectl, docker commands, or cloud API calls to enumerate running containers, Kubernetes clusters, container registries, resource quotas, and network configurations to identify deployment targets and lateral movement opportunities
detection_pattern: Monitor for: kubectl get pods/nodes/services commands from unexpected users, docker ps/inspect commands, AWS describe-instances/ECS ListTasks, GCP gcloud container clusters list, unusual API enumeration patterns, failed authentication attempts to container management APIs, access to /var/run/docker.sock, queries to cloud metadata services
mitigation_strategy: Implement RBAC policies restricting kubectl/container API access; disable container CLI access for non-admin users; monitor and alert on container discovery commands; restrict cloud metadata service access; use network policies to limit inter-container communication; maintain audit logs for all container management API calls; implement image scanning and registry access controls; use admission controllers to enforce pod security policies

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Services Registry Permissions Weakness
exploitation_method: Attacker identifies Windows services with misconfigured registry permissions (HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services) that allow non-administrative users to modify service binaries, parameters, or startup types. Attacker modifies ImagePath, Parameters, or Start values to execute arbitrary code with SYSTEM privileges when service restarts.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Registry modification events on HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\* from non-SYSTEM accounts (Event ID 4657); (2) Unexpected changes to service ImagePath or Parameters; (3) Service restart events (Event ID 7045) following registry modifications; (4) Privilege escalation events correlating with service restarts; (5) Icacls/Get-Acl queries on service registry keys
mitigation_strategy: Implement principle of least privilege: (1) Audit all service registry ACLs using Get-Acl or icacls; (2) Remove explicit WRITE permissions for non-administrative groups on HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services; (3) Restrict to SYSTEM and Administrators with read-only for standard users; (4) Deploy GPO to enforce secure default permissions; (5) Enable AppLocker/WDAC to control service executable modifications; (6) Monitor and alert on service binary hash changes; (7) Conduct regular registry permission audits and remediate deviations

threat_vector: Improper access control vulnerability in S Assistant prior to version 7.5 allows remote unauthorized access to sensitive information
exploitation_method: Attacker exploits missing or insufficient authorization checks in API endpoints or user interface components to access resources without proper credentials or privilege validation, potentially bypassing authentication tokens or session management controls
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unauthorized API calls without valid authentication headers, access to sensitive endpoints from unexpected source IPs, multiple failed authentication attempts followed by successful access, HTTP 200 responses to endpoints that should require elevated privileges, unusual data export requests, access patterns inconsistent with user role, database query logs showing privilege escalation, and repeated access to /admin, /api/sensitive, or /config endpoints from non-admin accounts
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade S Assistant to version 7.5 or later immediately; implement role-based access control (RBAC) with principle of least privilege; enforce authentication on all endpoints; conduct access control code review; deploy Web Application Firewall rules to restrict sensitive endpoint access; enable comprehensive audit logging; implement API rate limiting; validate user permissions on every request; segment network access to sensitive data; conduct penetration testing post-patch

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1110.001: Brute Force - Password Guessing
exploitation_method: Attacker systematically attempts to authenticate using common passwords, dictionary words, or previously compromised credentials against user accounts without rate limiting or account lockout mechanisms
detection_pattern: Multiple failed authentication attempts from single source IP within short timeframe; failed login attempts followed by successful login; spike in 401/403 HTTP responses; failed SSH/RDP connection attempts; authentication logs showing repeated attempts with different usernames; LDAP bind failures; failed VPN authentication entries
mitigation_strategy: Implement account lockout policies (lock after 5-10 failed attempts for 15-30 minutes); enforce strong password requirements (minimum 12 characters, complexity rules); deploy multi-factor authentication (MFA); implement rate limiting on authentication endpoints; monitor and alert on failed login thresholds; use CAPTCHA challenges after failed attempts; deploy WAF rules to detect credential stuffing patterns; implement passwordless authentication; conduct regular security awareness training on password hygiene

threat_vector: Rogue backends causing Denial of Service of guests via high frequency events in Xen hypervisor
exploitation_method: A malicious or compromised backend domain generates excessive event notifications to guest domains at high frequency, overwhelming the guest's event processing capacity and consuming CPU resources, resulting in guest unavailability or degraded performance
detection_pattern: Monitor for: sustained high-frequency event IRQ delivery to specific guest domains; abnormal event channel activity with events per second exceeding baseline thresholds; CPU spike in event processing loops correlating with specific backend domain activity; event queue saturation; repeated event storms from particular backend domains
mitigation_strategy: Implement event rate limiting per backend-guest domain pair; establish maximum event frequency thresholds enforced at hypervisor level; add event coalescing mechanisms to batch high-frequency notifications; isolate backend domains with strict resource quotas; deploy monitoring to detect and throttle abusive event producers; apply security updates addressing event handling vulnerabilities; restrict backend domain privileges using split-driver model; enable per-domain event accounting

threat_vector: COMOS Web Component Remote Code Execution - Insufficient Input Validation
exploitation_method: Attacker sends malformed requests to COMOS web components lacking proper input validation, allowing injection of arbitrary code or commands that execute with application privileges. Typically exploits deserialization or template injection vulnerabilities in affected versions.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: HTTP requests with unusual parameter encoding to COMOS web endpoints; POST requests with excessively large payloads; entries in web server logs showing 5xx errors followed by successful requests; process creation events spawning unexpected child processes from COMOS service account; network connections from COMOS processes to unusual external IPs; file write operations to system directories from COMOS working directory; Java deserialization warnings in application logs; requests containing serialized object patterns or gadget chain indicators.
mitigation_strategy: Immediately upgrade to patched versions (V10.3.3.3+, V10.4.x with latest patch); disable web components if not required; implement WAF rules blocking malformed COMOS API requests; restrict network access to COMOS web interfaces via firewall/network segmentation; run COMOS services with least privilege (non-root, limited user); enable comprehensive logging and SIEM alerting; apply defense-in-depth with input validation, output encoding, and serialization filters; conduct regular security assessments and penetration testing of web interfaces.

threat_vector: net/http HTTP/2 header canonicalization cache memory exhaustion (CVE-2021-44716)
exploitation_method: Attacker sends numerous HTTP/2 requests with unique header combinations to overflow the header canonicalization cache, causing unbounded memory allocation and eventual denial of service through resource exhaustion
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) rapid increase in process memory usage without proportional request volume, (2) HTTP/2 HEADERS frames with high cardinality unique header combinations, (3) memory heap growth patterns showing cache bloat, (4) application crashes or OOM killer invocation, (5) goroutine count spike correlating with header processing
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Go runtime to 1.16.12, 1.17.5 or later; implement request rate limiting and header size/count restrictions; deploy reverse proxy (nginx/HAProxy) with HTTP/2 stream/connection limits; monitor memory metrics with alerts for abnormal growth; use resource limits (cgroups/containers) to prevent full system resource depletion

threat_vector: Path traversal vulnerability in Open-AudIT 4.2.0 allowing authenticated users to read arbitrary files outside restricted directories
exploitation_method: Attacker uses path traversal sequences (../ or ..\) in file inclusion/request parameters to navigate beyond intended directory boundaries. Authentication bypass combined with improper input validation on file path parameters enables reading sensitive files like configuration files, database credentials, or system files.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) HTTP requests containing encoded or literal traversal sequences (%2e%2e, ../, ..\ ) in parameters; (2) Access logs showing requests to Open-AudIT with suspicious path patterns; (3) Unusual file read operations from processes running Open-AudIT accessing files outside designated directories; (4) Access to sensitive files (/etc/passwd, config files, web root parent directories) correlated with authenticated user sessions; (5) WAF/IDS alerts for directory traversal attempts; (6) Application logs showing file access errors for non-whitelisted paths
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Open-AudIT to patched version 4.2.1 or later immediately; implement input validation whitelist for file path parameters allowing only alphanumeric characters and safe delimiters; apply principle of least privilege to file system permissions for Open-AudIT process; use Web Application Firewall rules to block traversal sequences; implement chroot/containerization to restrict file system access; enforce strict access controls on sensitive configuration files; regularly audit file access patterns and maintain comprehensive logging of all file operations initiated by the application

threat_vector: Memory corruption via invalid free operations in uriparser < 0.9.6 (uriFreeUriMembers and uriMakeOwner functions)
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malformed URI input that causes double-free or use-after-free conditions when the library attempts to free memory structures. This can lead to heap corruption, denial of service, or potential code execution if memory allocators are sufficiently predictable.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Application crashes with SIGSEGV or heap corruption signals when processing URI inputs; (2) Valgrind/AddressSanitizer reports of invalid free/double-free in uriFreeUriMembers or uriMakeOwner; (3) Unexpected memory access violations in liburiparser during parsing; (4) Core dumps containing stack traces referencing these functions; (5) Memory allocator warnings about heap inconsistencies
mitigation_strategy: Immediately upgrade uriparser to version 0.9.6 or later. For systems unable to upgrade immediately: (1) Implement input validation to reject malformed URIs before passing to uriparser; (2) Run applications using uriparser with address space layout randomization (ASLR) and memory tagging extensions enabled; (3) Isolate URI parsing in sandboxed processes with restricted privileges; (4) Apply memory safety tools (AddressSanitizer, Valgrind) in development/testing; (5) Monitor application logs for parsing failures and crashes; (6) Consider using alternative URI parsing libraries with better memory safety records

threat_vector: Memory leakage vulnerability in smartphone operating system or application
exploitation_method: Attacker triggers repeated allocation of memory objects without proper deallocation through crafted inputs, API calls, or specific user interactions. Over time, unreleased memory accumulates until system resources are exhausted, causing denial of service or application crashes.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Monotonically increasing memory consumption without corresponding decrease during idle periods, (2) Process memory usage approaching system limits, (3) System logs showing 'out of memory' errors or low memory warnings, (4) Application crash dumps with heap analysis revealing unreleased allocations, (5) Repeated allocation patterns in memory profiler without corresponding free() calls, (6) System performance degradation correlating with specific user actions or API invocations
mitigation_strategy: Implement: (1) Regular security patching for OS and apps, (2) Memory leak detection tools in development (Valgrind, AddressSanitizer, Android Memory Profiler), (3) Code review focusing on resource management and proper cleanup, (4) Enable automatic memory management and garbage collection tuning, (5) Set per-process memory limits via cgroups/resource quotas, (6) Implement watchdog timers to restart services on memory threshold breach, (7) Use memory-safe languages where possible, (8) Deploy runtime monitoring with alerting on anomalous memory growth patterns

threat_vector: Pointer Dereference Vulnerability in GPAC 1.0.1 gf_list_count function
exploitation_method: Attacker passes a NULL or invalid pointer to gf_list_count function, causing the application to dereference an uninitialized or freed memory address, resulting in a crash or undefined behavior
detection_pattern: Monitor for: segmentation faults or access violations in gf_list_count; NULL pointer dereference exceptions in GPAC processes; sudden termination of media processing with exit code indicating memory access violation; core dumps referencing gf_list_count stack frame; abnormal process termination when processing malformed media files
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade GPAC to version 1.0.2 or later where the vulnerability is patched; implement input validation to verify list pointers before passing to gf_list_count; add defensive NULL pointer checks in calling code; use memory sanitizers (ASAN, Valgrind) during testing; implement strict bounds checking and safe coding practices; restrict GPAC process privileges using sandboxing or containerization

threat_vector: Improper authorization vulnerability in Galaxy Store versions prior to 4.5.36.5 allowing unauthorized remote app installation bypass of allowlist restrictions
exploitation_method: Attacker sends specially crafted installation requests to Galaxy Store API endpoints that lack proper authorization validation, bypassing the app allowlist by exploiting insufficient permission checks before processing remote installation commands
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Galaxy Store process receiving installation requests from unexpected sources or IP ranges, (2) App installation commands lacking proper authorization tokens or with modified auth headers, (3) Installation of apps not present in device allowlist, (4) Unexpected Galaxy Store API calls with administrative privileges from non-privileged contexts, (5) Logcat entries showing 'Permission denied' errors followed by successful installations, (6) Rapid sequential installation attempts of multiple apps
mitigation_strategy: Immediately update Galaxy Store to version 4.5.36.5 or later; implement strict authorization validation on all installation endpoints requiring multi-factor authentication tokens; enforce mandatory allowlist signature verification; add rate limiting on installation endpoints; implement API request signing; audit and restrict Galaxy Store process permissions; deploy network segmentation to limit Galaxy Store API access; enable SELinux policies for app installation context restrictions

threat_vector: Clickjacking vulnerability in Ultimaker 3D printer local web interface (S3, S5, 3 S-line ≤6.3, 3 ≤5.2.16)
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious webpage embedding the printer's local webserver interface in a transparent iframe, tricking authenticated users into performing unintended administrative actions (settings changes, firmware updates, job cancellation) by clicking visible decoy elements that overlay hidden printer controls
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Multiple rapid parameter changes in printer settings logs without corresponding UI navigation patterns, (2) X-Frame-Options header absence or permissive values in HTTP responses from printer webserver, (3) POST requests to /settings endpoints with unusual referer headers or cross-origin requests, (4) Session activity showing state changes (network config, print queue modifications) without corresponding valid UI interaction sequences in access logs
mitigation_strategy: Implement X-Frame-Options: DENY header on all printer webserver responses; deploy Content-Security-Policy with frame-ancestors 'none'; implement SameSite=Strict cookies for session tokens; require CSRF tokens on all state-changing operations; upgrade firmware to patched versions (S-line >6.3, 3 >5.2.16); restrict local webserver access to trusted networks via firewall rules; disable remote access if not required

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1110: Brute Force - Password Cracking
exploitation_method: Attacker uses automated tools to systematically attempt multiple password combinations against user accounts via network services (SSH, RDP, web applications) or offline against stolen password hashes using dictionary attacks, rainbow tables, or brute force algorithms
detection_pattern: Multiple failed authentication attempts from single source IP within short timeframe; repeated HTTP 401/403 responses; SSH failed login logs with increasing attempt velocity; account lockout events; slow network hash-cracking detected via GPU/CPU resource anomalies; failed RDP login attempts exceeding baseline; unusual authentication patterns outside business hours
mitigation_strategy: Implement account lockout policies (e.g., lock after 5 failed attempts for 30 minutes); enforce strong password complexity requirements; deploy multi-factor authentication (MFA) on all critical accounts; rate-limit authentication attempts; use CAPTCHA after failed attempts; monitor and alert on brute force patterns; implement IP-based geo-blocking; hash passwords using strong algorithms (bcrypt, Argon2); segment network to limit authentication service exposure; disable legacy protocols; conduct regular password audits

threat_vector: Type confusion vulnerability in MPTCP subsystem allowing unauthorized resource access and potential denial of service or privilege escalation
exploitation_method: Attacker sends specially crafted MPTCP packets with mismatched type fields that cause the kernel to interpret memory buffers as incompatible data structures, bypassing type safety checks and enabling out-of-bounds access to kernel resources
detection_pattern: Monitor kernel logs for: mptcp subsystem errors, type validation failures, unexpected memory access patterns, kernel crashes in mptcp_* functions, anomalous socket state transitions, invalid MPTCP option parsing, memory protection faults in network stack, and abnormal CPU spikes during MPTCP connection establishment. Check for SELinux/AppArmor denials related to network capability misuse.
mitigation_strategy: Apply latest kernel security patches for MPTCP subsystem; implement runtime type checking and bounds validation in mptcp code; enable kernel exploit mitigations (CFI, SMAC); restrict MPTCP access via AppArmor/SELinux policies limiting network capabilities; monitor and disable MPTCP if not required; implement network segmentation to limit MPTCP traffic sources; use address space layout randomization (ASLR) and stack canaries; conduct regular security audits of type-unsafe code paths in mptcp subsystem.

threat_vector: libjxl b02d6b9 out-of-bounds write in ModularFrameDecoder::DecodeGroup (CVE-2023-35824)
exploitation_method: Crafted JPEG XL image file with malformed modular frame data causes heap buffer overflow when DecodeGroup processes AC group coefficients, writing beyond allocated buffer boundaries during decompression
detection_pattern: Memory access violations detected via ASan/Valgrind; SIGSEGV/SIGABRT signals during image decoding; heap corruption detected post-decode; abnormal process termination when processing specific JXL files; sanitizer warnings for heap-buffer-overflow in jxl::ModularFrameDecoder
mitigation_strategy: Update libjxl to patched version (post-b02d6b9); update libvips to 8.11.3+; implement strict input validation for JXL headers; use memory safety tools (ASan) in development; deploy application sandboxing to contain heap exploits; disable JXL support if not required; monitor for CVE-2023-35824 patches from vendors

threat_vector: Cloud Instance Metadata API Exploitation (ATT&CK T1552.005)
exploitation_method: Attacker queries instance metadata endpoint (e.g., http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/) to retrieve temporary credentials, IAM role information, user data scripts, or network configuration. Common in AWS, Azure, and GCP environments when workload identity is misconfigured or SSRF vulnerabilities exist.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unusual HTTP requests to 169.254.169.254 or metadata endpoints from containers/VMs; spike in 169.254.x.x traffic; requests to /latest/meta-data/ or /metadata/instance paths; abnormal process execution (curl, wget) targeting metadata endpoints; authentication events using credentials with unexpected source IPs or rapid token assumption patterns; CloudTrail events showing credential use from unusual principals immediately after metadata API queries.
mitigation_strategy: 1) Restrict metadata endpoint access via IMDSv2 enforcement with PUT token requirement and hop-limit=1; 2) Implement network policies blocking metadata endpoint access except from approved services; 3) Deploy WAF/WAAP rules to detect SSRF attempts; 4) Use IAM roles with minimal privileges and credential rotation; 5) Monitor and alert on metadata endpoint queries; 6) Disable metadata endpoint if unused; 7) Patch applications for SSRF vulnerabilities; 8) Implement container-level network segmentation.

threat_vector: Stored XSS in UpdraftPlus WordPress Backup Plugin via backup_timestamp and job_id parameters
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious backup_timestamp or job_id parameters containing JavaScript payload, submits them through admin interface or database manipulation. When admin views backup history page, unsanitized parameters are rendered in HTML context, executing arbitrary JavaScript in admin's browser session.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unusual characters in wp_options or backup logs containing script tags, event handlers, or encoded payloads in backup_timestamp/job_id values; (2) Admin page requests with URL-encoded or double-encoded special characters in those parameters; (3) Backup administration page errors or unexpected JavaScript in page source; (4) WAF/IDS alerts for XSS patterns in POST/GET to backup admin pages; (5) Database audit logs showing non-numeric or non-standard characters injected into backup metadata
mitigation_strategy: Update UpdraftPlus plugin to version 1.16.66 or later immediately. Implement input validation: sanitize backup_timestamp to numeric timestamp format only, sanitize job_id to alphanumeric characters. Use wp_sanitize_text_field() and wp_kses_post() for output escaping. Deploy WordPress security headers (CSP, X-XSS-Protection). Restrict backup admin page access via role-based capabilities. Monitor plugin update channels and apply patches within 48 hours of release.

threat_vector: Stored XSS via unsanitized wcj_create_products_xml_result parameter in Booster for WooCommerce plugin < 5.4.9
exploitation_method: Attacker with admin/editor privileges injects malicious JavaScript into the wcj_create_products_xml_result parameter. The parameter is reflected in the admin dashboard without sanitization or escaping, executing arbitrary script in admin context.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) POST requests to admin.php with wcj_create_products_xml_result parameter containing script tags, event handlers (onclick, onerror), or encoded JavaScript; (2) Admin dashboard logs showing unusual characters or base64-encoded payloads in XML-related parameters; (3) Stored XSS WAF signatures matching <script>, javascript:, or HTML entity encoding in product XML functionality; (4) Admin user sessions with unexpected JavaScript execution in DOM
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Booster for WooCommerce to version 5.4.9 or later. As interim measure: (1) Apply input validation requiring wcj_create_products_xml_result to match expected XML format only; (2) Use wp_kses_post() and esc_html() on all parameter outputs in admin pages; (3) Implement Content Security Policy (CSP) headers restricting inline script execution; (4) Audit admin user permissions and restrict access to product XML features; (5) Enable WordPress security logging for admin parameter modifications

threat_vector: COMOS Web Components Remote Code Execution - CVE-2024-21893
exploitation_method: Attacker sends crafted HTTP requests to vulnerable COMOS web components exploiting improper input validation in version 10.2 (all versions with web components) and 10.3 (versions before 10.3.3.3 with web components). Malicious serialized objects or script payloads bypass validation filters, allowing remote code execution with application privileges.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) HTTP requests with unusual serialized object patterns to /comos web paths; (2) POST/PUT requests containing base64-encoded Java serialization signatures (magic bytes: aced0005); (3) Process execution spawning from COMOS application servers; (4) Error logs showing deserialization exceptions followed by command execution; (5) Abnormal outbound connections from COMOS application service accounts; (6) Web application firewall alerts on polyglot payloads targeting COMOS endpoints
mitigation_strategy: 1) Upgrade to COMOS 10.3.3.3 or later immediately; 2) Disable web components if not required; 3) Implement strict input validation and disable Java deserialization of untrusted data; 4) Deploy WAF rules blocking requests with serialization payloads to COMOS endpoints; 5) Restrict network access to COMOS web interfaces using IP whitelisting; 6) Monitor application logs for deserialization errors; 7) Run COMOS services with minimal required privileges; 8) Apply defense-in-depth with egress filtering to prevent reverse shells

threat_vector: Unauthenticated Configuration File Download in SYNC2101 Device
exploitation_method: Attacker sends HTTP GET request to known configuration file endpoint (typically /config or /settings) without authentication credentials, leveraging missing access controls on the device's web interface to retrieve plaintext or weakly-protected configuration containing credentials, network settings, and device parameters
detection_pattern: Monitor for HTTP GET requests to /config, /settings, /download, or similar endpoints from external IPs; log successful 200 responses returning configuration data without prior authentication events; alert on downloaded files containing device configuration artifacts; track unusual file downloads from device management interfaces; correlate failed login attempts followed by successful config downloads
mitigation_strategy: 1) Enforce mandatory authentication on all configuration endpoints using strong credentials 2) Implement role-based access controls requiring admin privileges for config access 3) Apply latest firmware patches for affected SYNC2101 sub-families 4) Disable or restrict HTTP access to configuration endpoints, use HTTPS only 5) Implement IP whitelisting for device management access 6) Monitor and alert on configuration file access attempts 7) Enable audit logging for all configuration downloads 8) Encrypt sensitive data in configuration files at rest

threat_vector: Unsafe ClassLoader instantiation via Class.forName() with untrusted user input in Apache Kylin
exploitation_method: Attacker provides malicious class name through user input parameters that are passed directly to Class.forName(), allowing instantiation of arbitrary classes on the classpath. This can lead to remote code execution if gadget chains are available or if attacker-controlled classes exist on the classpath.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Class.forName() calls with user-supplied parameters in stack traces; (2) Unusual class loading from org.apache.kylin packages; (3) Instantiation of unexpected or suspicious classes (serialization gadgets, ProcessBuilder, Runtime); (4) Web request parameters containing class names (e.g., 'className', 'class', 'type' parameters); (5) HTTP requests to Kylin endpoints with encoded or obfuscated Java class names; (6) ClassNotFoundException or NoClassDefFoundError exceptions followed by successful instantiation attempts
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict allowlist-based class loading: (1) Replace unsafe Class.forName(userInput) with an explicit allowlist of permitted classes; (2) Use parameterized configuration maps instead of string-based class instantiation; (3) Apply input validation to reject any class name not on the allowlist before ClassLoader operations; (4) Disable or restrict the classpath to exclude gadget chain libraries when possible; (5) Implement Java SecurityManager policies to restrict ClassLoader.defineClass() and reflection operations; (6) Update to patched Kylin versions (2.6.7+ or 3.1.3+); (7) Run Kylin with minimal required permissions and isolate it in containers

threat_vector: Memory corruption in Bluetooth controller via oversized LMP packet on 2-DH1 link causing DoS in BlueCore
exploitation_method: Attacker sends malformed LMP (Link Manager Protocol) packet exceeding maximum payload size for 2-DH1 packets (54 bytes), causing buffer overflow in BlueCore controller memory, corrupting heap or stack structures and crashing the controller
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) LMP packet size validation failures in controller logs; (2) Unexpected controller resets/crashes correlated with received packets from specific Bluetooth addresses; (3) Memory access violations in BlueCore process; (4) Repeated connection drops from same peer device; (5) Abnormal LMP packet byte counts in packet capture analysis exceeding 2-DH1 link specifications
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict packet size validation at LMP layer before buffer write operations; enforce maximum packet length checks based on link type (2-DH1=54 bytes); add bounds checking and stack canaries in controller memory handling; configure controller to reject oversized packets; apply firmware patches from vendor addressing LMP buffer handling; monitor and disconnect from peers sending malformed packets; enable Address Space Layout Randomization (ASLR) and Data Execution Prevention (DEP) if supported by controller

threat_vector: BookStack Improper Access Control
exploitation_method: Attacker bypasses role-based access controls (RBAC) by directly accessing API endpoints or manipulating URL parameters to view, edit, or delete resources they lack permissions for. Common vectors include direct object reference vulnerabilities, missing permission checks on API endpoints, or privilege escalation through role manipulation.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unauthorized API requests to /api/books, /api/chapters, /api/pages endpoints from low-privilege accounts; HTTP 200 responses on protected resources for users without viewing permissions; repeated 403 errors followed by successful access with modified parameters; audit log entries showing resource modifications by users without edit permissions; access to admin panels from non-admin accounts; suspicious permission role changes in user_roles table.
mitigation_strategy: Implement comprehensive permission validation on all endpoints; enforce least-privilege access model with granular permission checks before resource exposure; validate user permissions server-side for every API call; implement proper access control lists (ACLs) tied to user roles; conduct regular access control audits; enable and monitor audit logging for permission violations; keep BookStack updated to latest version; use Web Application Firewall (WAF) rules to restrict API access patterns; implement rate limiting on authorization-related requests.

threat_vector: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerability in QTS/QuTS hero/QuTScloud allowing remote code injection
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious JavaScript payload and injects it through unvalidated input fields (query parameters, form inputs, or API endpoints). When victims access the compromised page, the script executes in their browser context, potentially stealing session tokens, credentials, or performing unauthorized actions on behalf of the user.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) HTTP requests with script tags, event handlers (onerror, onload), or JavaScript protocol in parameters; (2) Unusual characters or encoding (HTML entities, Unicode escapes) in request payloads; (3) Browser console errors indicating script execution failures; (4) Unexpected DOM modifications or network requests from user sessions; (5) WAF alerts for XSS signatures; (6) Abnormal user-agent behavior or rapid successive requests from single session
mitigation_strategy: Implement: (1) Input validation and sanitization on all user inputs using whitelist approach; (2) Output encoding of all dynamic content using context-aware encoding (HTML, JavaScript, URL, CSS); (3) Content Security Policy (CSP) headers restricting script sources; (4) HttpOnly and Secure flags on session cookies; (5) Regular patching of QTS/QuTS hero/QuTScloud to latest versions; (6) Web Application Firewall (WAF) rules blocking XSS patterns; (7) Security scanning during development; (8) Disable JavaScript execution in user-controlled content areas where possible

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1557 - Adversary-in-the-Middle
exploitation_method: Attacker positions themselves between target and legitimate service by compromising network infrastructure, performing ARP spoofing, DNS hijacking, or BGP hijacking. They intercept, read, and potentially modify traffic in transit to steal credentials, session tokens, or inject malicious content.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: ARP anomalies (duplicate MAC addresses, suspicious ARP requests), DNS query mismatches (queries to different resolvers than configured), SSL/TLS certificate warnings in logs, unexpected network interface promiscuous mode, traffic flowing through unexpected gateways, NetFlow data showing asymmetric routing patterns, DHCP server spoofing attempts, and mismatches between expected vs actual gateway MAC addresses in network monitoring tools.
mitigation_strategy: Implement end-to-end encryption (TLS 1.3+) for all sensitive communications, enforce certificate pinning in applications, deploy mutual TLS authentication, use DNS-over-HTTPS (DoH) and DNS-over-TLS (DoT), implement DNSSEC validation, configure static ARP entries for critical infrastructure, enable port security (802.1X), deploy network segmentation with VLANs, implement continuous certificate transparency monitoring, use VPN/IPsec for sensitive traffic, and monitor for unauthorized DHCP servers with DHCP snooping.

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Encrypted/Encoded File (T1027.010)
exploitation_method: Attacker encrypts or encodes malicious payloads (executables, scripts, or data exfiltration) to evade signature-based detection and content inspection by security tools. Common techniques include base64 encoding, XOR encryption, AES encryption, or custom encoding algorithms embedded in delivery mechanisms or post-exploitation tools.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Files with suspicious entropy values indicating encryption/compression; (2) Process execution of decoding utilities (certutil, powershell with -EncodedCommand, python -c); (3) Suspicious file extensions (.encrypted, .locked, .encoded); (4) Registry modifications for encryption keys; (5) Memory-based detection of decryption routines; (6) Child processes spawning from decoders with network/file system access; (7) Unusual file write patterns followed by execution
mitigation_strategy: Deploy behavioral analysis and heuristic scanning tools that analyze decrypted payloads in memory; enforce script execution policies (e.g., PowerShell Constrained Language Mode, AppLocker); require code signing and disable unsigned script execution; monitor and restrict access to encoding/decoding tools; conduct entropy analysis on files before execution; implement sandboxing to detonate and analyze suspicious files; use YARA rules for obfuscation patterns; maintain updated threat intelligence on encoding signatures

threat_vector: Heap memory corruption via malformed HWTC IQ Capture command with insufficient input validation in Snapdragon platforms
exploitation_method: Attacker sends crafted HWTC IQ Capture command with oversized or malicious payload that bypasses input validation checks, causing heap buffer overflow and corrupting adjacent memory structures, potentially achieving arbitrary code execution or denial of service
detection_pattern: Monitor for: heap corruption indicators in kernel logs, unexpected process termination with segmentation faults, abnormal memory access patterns detected by KASAN/AddressSanitizer, HWTC command processing errors with unusually large buffer sizes, repeated failed IQ Capture attempts with varying payload sizes, unexpected privilege escalation attempts following HWTC command processing
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict input validation and size checks on all HWTC IQ Capture command parameters before processing; enforce heap canaries and stack protection mechanisms; deploy address space layout randomization (ASLR); use memory protection techniques (DEP/NX); apply firmware patches validating command boundaries; implement rate limiting on HWTC command submissions; enable heap hardening and consider using safe memory allocators; conduct fuzzing tests on HWTC interface with malformed inputs

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Botnet (T1584.001 - Compromise Infrastructure: Botnet)
exploitation_method: Attacker deploys malware that establishes persistent command-and-control (C2) communication, enabling remote execution of commands across compromised hosts. Typically propagated via malicious downloads, drive-by downloads, or exploitation of unpatched vulnerabilities. Infected systems contact C2 servers to receive instructions for distributed attacks or data exfiltration.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Outbound connections to known malicious C2 domains/IPs; (2) Unusual network traffic patterns with high data volume to external hosts; (3) Process execution of obfuscated scripts or binaries from temporary directories; (4) DNS queries to newly registered or suspicious domains; (5) Registry/system modifications for persistence mechanisms; (6) Beaconing behavior (regular connections at fixed intervals); (7) Multiple infected hosts querying same external IP; (8) Process trees showing injection or code execution anomalies
mitigation_strategy: Deploy network-based controls: DNS sinkholing, C2 blocking via firewall, and threat intelligence feeds. Implement endpoint detection: behavioral analysis tools, memory scanning, and process monitoring. Operational measures: enforce application whitelisting, maintain patching cadence, isolate infected systems, conduct forensic analysis. Host-level: disable suspicious services, remove persistence mechanisms, reset credentials, perform disk/memory imaging. Enforce egress filtering, network segmentation, and monitor DNS/HTTP traffic for anomalies.

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Double File Extension (T1036.007)
exploitation_method: Attacker creates files with misleading double extensions (e.g., document.pdf.exe, image.jpg.scr) to bypass file type validation. User perceives the file as safe based on the primary extension while the system executes the dangerous secondary extension.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: files with mismatched MIME types and extensions, executable files with document extensions in logs, PE headers detected in non-executable file types, suspicious file creation events with compound extensions, email attachments or downloads with double extensions, process execution originating from files with misleading extensions
mitigation_strategy: Enable full file extension display in OS settings, implement application whitelisting for executable file types, configure email gateway to block or quarantine files with double extensions, validate file MIME types server-side rather than relying on extensions, educate users on file extension risks, use content inspection tools to analyze file signatures, restrict file execution from suspicious directories, implement endpoint detection to monitor process spawning from double-extension files

threat_vector: Reflected XSS via unsanitized search parameter in Chaty WordPress plugin (versions < 2.8.3 free, < 2.8.2 pro)
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious URL containing JavaScript payload in search parameter (e.g., ?search=<script>alert('xss')</script>), which is reflected in admin dashboard HTML without sanitization/escaping, allowing arbitrary JavaScript execution in admin context
detection_pattern: Monitor admin logs for: (1) search parameter values containing script tags, event handlers (onclick, onerror), or HTML entities; (2) POST/GET requests to plugin admin pages with unusual URL encoding patterns; (3) browser console errors from injected scripts; (4) WAF alerts on reflected XSS patterns in Chaty plugin endpoints
mitigation_strategy: Update Chaty plugin to version 2.8.3+ (free) or 2.8.2+ (pro); implement input validation by sanitizing search parameters with sanitize_text_field() or sanitize_sql_like(); escape all output with esc_html(), esc_attr(), or wp_kses_post(); deploy Content Security Policy (CSP) headers; conduct regular security audits of plugin code; disable plugin if update unavailable

threat_vector: Dnsmasq 2.86 heap-based buffer overflow in extract_name function (CVE-2021-45951 through CVE-2021-45957)
exploitation_method: Attacker sends crafted DNS packets with malformed domain names containing excessive length fields that bypass bounds checking in extract_name() function, causing heap memory corruption and potential code execution when processing authoritative answers or fuzzy authentication responses
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) DNS traffic with unusually long domain name labels exceeding RFC 1035 limits (>63 octets per label), (2) segmentation faults or crashes in dnsmasq process logs, (3) unexpected core dumps from dnsmasq, (4) heap corruption indicators in system logs, (5) DNS response packets from untrusted sources with malformed QNAME fields, (6) dnsmasq process memory access violations captured by ASLR/DEP mechanisms
mitigation_strategy: Immediate actions: (1) upgrade dnsmasq to version 2.87 or later, (2) implement strict DNS packet validation at network boundary using IDS/IPS rules rejecting packets with label lengths >63 octets, (3) run dnsmasq with memory protections enabled (ASLR, DEP/NX), (4) restrict DNS query sources to trusted nameservers only via firewall ACLs, (5) monitor dnsmasq process for crashes and implement automatic restart with alerting, (6) disable DNSSEC validation if not required to reduce attack surface

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1548.003 - Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism: Sudo and Sudo Caching
exploitation_method: Attacker leverages sudo's password caching mechanism (default 15-minute timeout) to execute privileged commands without re-authentication. After initial sudo authentication, attacker can execute additional sudo commands within the cache window. Can be combined with local privilege escalation or lateral movement to abuse cached credentials.
detection_pattern: Monitor /var/log/auth.log or /var/log/secure for: sudo command execution patterns with short time intervals between commands; COMMAND= entries showing privilege escalation; audit logs (auditctl) showing repeated sudoedit or sudo -l invocations; /etc/sudoers modifications; unexpected sudo usage from service accounts; correlate sudo cache timing with process creation logs showing privilege elevation gaps
mitigation_strategy: Set 'Defaults timestamp_timeout=0' in /etc/sudoers to disable caching; enforce 'Defaults use_pty' to isolate sudo sessions; require 'Defaults requiretty' to prevent non-interactive sudo abuse; implement 'Defaults lecture=always' for sudo security warnings; use 'sudo -k' to invalidate timestamp files before unattended sessions; configure mandatory MFA for all sudo commands using PAM modules; restrict sudo access via /etc/sudoers with specific command allowlists; monitor and log all sudo access with Defaults logfile='/var/log/sudo.log'; implement SELinux or AppArmor policies to restrict sudo usage contexts

threat_vector: addBinding integer overflow in xmlparse.c (Expat < 2.4.3)
exploitation_method: Attacker sends specially crafted XML with deeply nested namespace declarations causing integer overflow in addBinding function's buffer allocation, leading to heap buffer overflow and potential code execution
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Expat library version detection showing < 2.4.3, (2) XML parsing crashes or segmentation faults in applications using vulnerable Expat, (3) Heap corruption indicators in memory sanitizer logs, (4) Unexpected process termination during XML document parsing, (5) Core dumps with stack traces pointing to xmlparse.c addBinding function
mitigation_strategy: Immediately upgrade Expat library to version 2.4.3 or later. Validate and patch all dependencies in package managers. Implement input validation to reject XML documents with excessive nesting depth (>256 levels). Deploy runtime protections: enable ASLR, DEP/NX, and stack canaries. Monitor application logs for XML parsing errors. Consider sandboxing XML parsing operations in isolated processes with restricted privileges.

threat_vector: Local privilege escalation via symlink following in Trend Micro Apex One and Worry-Free Business Security temporary file handling
exploitation_method: Attacker creates symbolic links in predictable temporary directories that the Trend Micro service monitors. When the service processes files without proper symlink validation, the attacker can redirect writes to arbitrary locations with elevated privileges, potentially overwriting critical system or application files.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unusual symlink creation in %TEMP%, %ProgramData%, or service-specific temp directories; (2) Failed file access attempts from tmproxy.exe or tmas.exe processes; (3) Unexpected file modifications in system directories with timestamps correlating to temp directory activity; (4) Event ID 4688 (process creation) showing elevated privilege process writing outside normal paths; (5) Filesystem audit logs showing SYSTEM-context writes following local user temp directory activity
mitigation_strategy: Apply latest patches from Trend Micro security advisories; configure file integrity monitoring on critical system directories; implement proper file permissions on temporary directories (restrict symlink creation); use secure temporary file APIs that validate symlink targets; enable kernel-mode protection against symlink attacks via Windows security policies; monitor and restrict local user privileged file access; conduct file ownership audits on temp directories regularly

threat_vector: Weak password enforcement in Daybyday CRM user update functionality (versions 1.1-2.2.0)
exploitation_method: Authenticated user with password change privileges bypasses password complexity validation to set weak credentials (e.g., short strings, dictionary words, or simple patterns) through the user update API endpoint
detection_pattern: Monitor password change logs for: password length <8 characters, absence of special characters/numbers, dictionary words, repeated characters, sequential patterns. Alert on user_update events where password_complexity_check returns null/false. Audit authentication failures followed by successful password resets to weak credentials.
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade to patched version >2.2.0; implement server-side password policy validation requiring minimum 12 characters, uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and special characters; enforce NIST SP 800-63B guidelines; implement password breach database checks (HaveIBeenPwned API); log all password changes with administrative review; disable password updates for 24-48 hours after suspicious activity detection

threat_vector: Stored XSS in Daybyday CRM v2.2.0 task title field allowing low-privileged users to inject malicious scripts
exploitation_method: Attacker with low-privilege account crafts malicious JavaScript payload in task title field (e.g., <script>alert('xss')</script> or event handlers like onload=). Payload persists in database and executes when any user views the task, potentially stealing session tokens or credentials.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) HTML/JavaScript special characters in task title database entries (<, >, script, onerror, onload, onclick); (2) Unusual database queries inserting unescaped markup into task tables; (3) Browser console errors or unexpected JavaScript execution in application logs; (4) Abnormal outbound requests from user browsers to external domains after viewing specific tasks; (5) WAF alerts for script injection patterns in POST requests to task creation endpoints
mitigation_strategy: Implement input validation: whitelist allowed characters in task titles, reject or sanitize HTML/JavaScript syntax. Apply output encoding: HTML-escape all task title data before rendering in DOM. Use Content Security Policy (CSP) headers to restrict script execution. Upgrade to patched version >2.2.0. Perform context-aware encoding (HTML entity encoding for display context). Implement parameterized queries to prevent injection. Add security headers (X-XSS-Protection, X-Content-Type-Options). Conduct regular security audits of user-controlled input fields.

threat_vector: Unauthenticated Remote Code Execution via Cisco ASA/FTD Web Services Interface Vulnerability
exploitation_method: Attacker sends specially crafted HTTP requests to the web services interface (typically port 443) exploiting a memory corruption or input validation flaw in the XML/JSON parsing logic. This allows arbitrary code execution without requiring valid credentials.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) HTTP requests to /admin, /ws, or web service endpoints from untrusted sources with unusual Content-Type headers (application/xml, application/json); (2) 400/500 HTTP errors followed by successful connections; (3) POST requests with oversized payloads to web service endpoints; (4) Malformed XML/JSON in access logs; (5) Process creation from httpd or web service processes; (6) Unexpected outbound connections from ASA management interface; (7) Changes to configuration files post-incident
mitigation_strategy: Implement: (1) Network segmentation - restrict access to management interfaces to authorized admin IPs only via ACLs; (2) Disable web services if not required; (3) Apply latest Cisco security patches immediately; (4) Enable logging and alerting on web service authentication failures; (5) Implement IPS signatures for ASA/FTD exploitation attempts; (6) Use AAA with MFA for any remote management access; (7) Monitor ASA logs for errors in httpd process; (8) Conduct regular vulnerability assessments; (9) Implement out-of-band management network separate from data plane

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1190: Exploit Public-Facing Application
exploitation_method: Attacker identifies unpatched vulnerabilities in public-facing web applications, APIs, or services (e.g., RCE, SQL injection, XXE) and sends crafted payloads to execute arbitrary code or access sensitive data without authentication
detection_pattern: HTTP requests with unusual parameter encoding or payloads; SQL syntax in URL parameters; XML entities in POST bodies; unexpected process execution from web server process; abnormal outbound connections from application server; web server logs showing 400/500 errors followed by successful requests; WAF alerts on known CVE signatures; spike in failed authentication attempts before successful exploitation
mitigation_strategy: Implement Web Application Firewall (WAF) with known CVE signatures; maintain inventory of all public-facing applications and their versions; establish patch management SLA (critical patches within 30 days); enable application-level logging and monitoring; conduct regular vulnerability assessments and penetration testing; implement input validation and output encoding; use security headers (CSP, X-Frame-Options, HSTS); disable unnecessary services and protocols; segment network to limit lateral movement post-exploitation

threat_vector: Memory leak in Mirantis Container Runtime 20.10.8 during TLS handshakes with FIPS mode enabled, leading to denial of service
exploitation_method: Attacker initiates repeated TLS handshakes to container runtime endpoints (API socket, registry connections, or inter-node communication). Each handshake with FIPS mode enabled fails to properly deallocate memory buffers used in cryptographic operations, causing gradual memory exhaustion until runtime becomes unresponsive or crashes
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) sustained increase in container runtime process memory usage without corresponding container creation, (2) rising heap_live_data_bytes and go_memstats_alloc_bytes metrics from runtime prometheus endpoint, (3) increasing failed TLS handshake attempts in logs with pattern 'tls.*error' or 'handshake.*fail', (4) kernel OOM killer events targeting runtime process, (5) connection reset or timeout errors from clients connecting to runtime socket
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Mirantis Container Runtime to version 20.10.9 or later containing the memory leak fix. If immediate patching unavailable: (1) disable FIPS mode if security posture permits, (2) implement connection rate limiting and request throttling at firewall/load balancer level to reduce handshake frequency, (3) enable memory limits via cgroups to prevent system-wide impact, (4) monitor memory metrics with alerting thresholds set at 70% of allocated runtime memory, (5) implement automatic runtime restart on OOM detection via systemd or container orchestrator policies

threat_vector: DLL Injection (T1055.001)
exploitation_method: Attacker injects malicious DLL into a legitimate process memory space using techniques like CreateRemoteThread, SetWindowsHookEx, or DLL preloading. The injected DLL executes within the target process context, bypassing application whitelisting and UAC restrictions.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: CreateRemoteThread calls from suspicious processes to system binaries; unsigned or mismatched DLL loading; DLLs loaded from unusual paths (temp directories, user writable locations); process hollowing indicators; abnormal parent-child process relationships; API hooks detection; unsigned driver loads; memory access patterns showing code injection
mitigation_strategy: Implement: Code signing enforcement for all DLLs; application whitelisting (AppLocker/Windows Defender Application Control); disable unnecessary DLL preloading paths (SafeDllSearchMode); enforce ASLR and DEP; monitor CreateRemoteThread via EDR; restrict debug privileges; use attestation-based integrity checks; maintain updated patching schedules; employ memory protection mechanisms; isolate critical processes in lower-privilege contexts

threat_vector: Improper Access Control - Authenticated User Privilege Escalation
exploitation_method: An authenticated user exploits inadequate authorization checks to access resources or data beyond their assigned permissions. This typically occurs when the application validates authentication (user identity) but fails to properly enforce authorization (user permissions), allowing lateral or vertical privilege escalation.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Authenticated users accessing resources outside their role/department; (2) Unusual data queries or API calls from standard user accounts; (3) Access logs showing users retrieving data they shouldn't have permissions for; (4) Role-based access control (RBAC) bypass attempts; (5) Rapid successive access attempts to different restricted resources; (6) Users accessing administrative functions without admin privileges; (7) Inconsistent authorization decisions in application logs
mitigation_strategy: Implement robust authorization controls: (1) Apply principle of least privilege (POLP) with granular permission assignments; (2) Enforce authorization checks on every resource access, not just initial login; (3) Use parameterized access control lists (ACLs) tied to user roles; (4) Implement attribute-based access control (ABAC) for complex permission scenarios; (5) Conduct regular access control audits and reviews; (6) Separate authentication from authorization logic; (7) Log all authorization failures; (8) Apply security patches promptly; (9) Perform penetration testing focused on privilege escalation scenarios

threat_vector: Configuration defects in Smartphone allowing MEID/IMEI permission elevation
exploitation_method: Attacker exploits misconfigured device settings or firmware vulnerabilities to gain unauthorized access to MEID/IMEI identifiers and associated privileges, potentially allowing device spoofing, network impersonation, or SIM-related attacks
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unexpected changes to device identifiers via ADB/debugging interfaces, unusual access logs to /dev/block/mmcblk0, SELinux denial logs related to IMEI/MEID access, unauthorized radio/modem process elevation, unexpected system partition mount attempts, and anomalous telephony framework permission grants in logcat
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict SELinux policies restricting MEID/IMEI access to authorized processes only; disable USB debugging by default; enforce secure boot and verified boot chain; apply manufacturer security patches promptly; implement runtime permission denial for modem-level identifiers; conduct regular firmware audits; use Mobile Device Management (MDM) to enforce configuration lockdown; monitor and restrict access to /proc/cmdline and radio firmware files

threat_vector: Default credentials on admin interface
exploitation_method: Attacker uses manufacturer-supplied default username and password (e.g., admin/admin, root/password) to gain unauthorized access to administrative functions without authentication bypass techniques
detection_pattern: Multiple failed login attempts followed by successful authentication from unexpected IP; login events with default usernames; access to admin interface from unusual geographic locations or non-whitelisted IPs; successful login without MFA; logs showing admin changes immediately after unusual login activity
mitigation_strategy: Enforce password change on first login with complexity requirements; disable default accounts if not required; implement IP whitelisting for admin access; require multi-factor authentication for all administrative interfaces; implement account lockout after failed login attempts; monitor and alert on any successful default credential usage; regularly audit active admin accounts and disable unused ones; use secrets management systems to rotate credentials automatically

threat_vector: Integer overflow in XML entity lookup processing (CVE-2021-45960, Expat libexpat < 2.4.3)
exploitation_method: Attacker sends specially crafted XML with deeply nested entity declarations that trigger integer overflow in xmlparse.c lookup function, causing heap buffer overflow and potential code execution
detection_pattern: Monitor for: XML parsing crashes or segmentation faults; abnormal memory access patterns during XML processing; stack traces referencing xmlparse.c lookup functions; applications using libexpat with version < 2.4.3; suspicious XML payloads with excessive entity nesting depth; heap corruption indicators in application logs
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade libexpat to version 2.4.3 or later; implement XML entity expansion limits (set DTD entity depth restrictions); disable external entity processing if not required; use input validation to reject XML with excessive nesting; apply ASLR and DEP/NX protections; monitor and limit XML parser resource consumption; consider sandboxing XML processing operations

threat_vector: Soketi WebSocket server denial of service via malformed POST request body
exploitation_method: Attacker sends POST request with incomplete or malformed body that triggers unhandled exception in request parsing logic, causing server process to crash and become unavailable
detection_pattern: Monitor for: sudden soketi process termination, HTTP POST requests with incomplete Content-Length headers, requests missing body data, repeated connection attempts followed by server restarts, error logs showing 'unhandled exception' or 'crash' in request parsing, abnormal increase in failed POST requests from same source IP
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade soketi to patched version; implement request timeout and size limits; add input validation to catch malformed bodies before parsing; use process manager (PM2, systemd) for auto-restart; implement rate limiting on POST endpoints; add reverse proxy (nginx) with request body validation; deploy WAF rules to reject requests with mismatched Content-Length; monitor and alert on unexpected server restarts

threat_vector: CSCMS v4.1 SQL Injection via page_del function
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious SQL syntax in parameters passed to the page_del function, typically through GET/POST requests. Unsanitized user input is directly concatenated into SQL queries, allowing attackers to manipulate query logic, extract data, or modify/delete database records.
detection_pattern: Monitor application logs and WAF/IDS alerts for: SQL keywords (UNION, SELECT, DROP, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) in page_del function parameters; unusual quote characters (', ", backticks) in request parameters; HTTP requests with SQL comment syntax (--, /*, ;); database query logs showing unexpected table access or schema enumeration; unexpected database modification timestamps or record deletions correlating with suspicious requests.
mitigation_strategy: Implement parameterized queries/prepared statements for all database operations in page_del function; apply strict input validation and whitelist allowed characters for all parameters; use ORM frameworks that handle query construction safely; enforce least-privilege database accounts (read-only where applicable); apply Web Application Firewall rules to block common SQL injection patterns; conduct code review and static analysis of the page_del function; update CSCMS to patched version if available; monitor database activity for anomalous queries.

threat_vector: Insecure Storage of Sensitive Information in Ivanti Workspace Control <2021.2 (10.7.30.0)
exploitation_method: Attacker with local low-privilege access exploits improper cryptographic storage of sensitive keys/credentials in Ivanti Workspace Control configuration files or registry. Sensitive data stored without adequate encryption or with weak encryption algorithms allows extraction of authentication keys through file system access or memory dumps.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unauthorized access to Ivanti Workspace Control config directories (%ProgramFiles%\Ivanti\*); suspicious registry queries under HKLM\Software\Ivanti\; unusual file read operations targeting .conf, .cfg, or .key files by low-privilege processes; unexpected credential material in event logs or authentication failures preceded by config file access; file integrity monitoring alerts on Ivanti configuration files; access to DPAPI-encrypted credential stores by non-system processes.
mitigation_strategy: 1) Upgrade Ivanti Workspace Control to version 2021.2 (10.7.30.0) or later. 2) Implement strict file system ACLs on Ivanti config directories, restrict to SYSTEM and administrators only. 3) Enable encrypted storage for all sensitive credentials using strong encryption (AES-256). 4) Apply principle of least privilege - limit local user account permissions. 5) Enable Windows Credential Guard for additional credential protection. 6) Deploy file integrity monitoring on Ivanti directories. 7) Implement host-based intrusion detection for suspicious file access patterns.

threat_vector: Telephony Application Sensitive Information Exposure to Unauthorized Actor
exploitation_method: Attacker intercepts unencrypted signaling protocols (SIP, H.323), captures voice/video streams, or accesses call logs and metadata through insufficient access controls. Information exposure occurs via packet sniffing, man-in-the-middle attacks, or unauthorized API access to retrieve call records, user credentials, or communication content.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unencrypted SIP/H.323 traffic on network, unusual access patterns to call logs/metadata APIs, authentication bypass attempts in telephony logs, TLS/SRTP handshake failures or downgrades, requests to sensitive endpoints from unexpected IP ranges, missing encryption headers in signaling packets, abnormal user-agent strings accessing call records, database queries for sensitive fields from unauthorized accounts
mitigation_strategy: Enforce mandatory TLS 1.2+ for all signaling protocols and SRTP for media streams; implement strong access controls (RBAC) on call logs and metadata; apply encryption at rest for stored communications; require mutual authentication for API endpoints; deploy network segmentation isolating telephony infrastructure; conduct regular security audits of information disclosure vectors; implement data masking for sensitive fields in logs; monitor and alert on encryption downgrade attempts; use VPNs for remote access to telephony systems

threat_vector: WebSocket hijacking
exploitation_method: Attacker intercepts WebSocket handshake or injects malicious frames after connection establishment by exploiting missing origin validation, lack of token validation, or weak session management. Attacker can then send/receive unauthorized messages as legitimate user.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unexpected WebSocket connections from unusual IP addresses or user agents, WebSocket frames from unauthenticated sessions, connection attempts without proper CSRF tokens or origin headers, repeated failed WebSocket handshakes, messages sent to WebSocket connections without corresponding session tokens, sudden spike in WebSocket connection closes followed by reconnections from same IP.
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict origin validation on WebSocket handshake, require CSRF tokens in WebSocket upgrade requests, use secure session tokens tied to WebSocket connections, enforce TLS/WSS exclusively (never WS), implement per-message authentication/validation, validate and sanitize all WebSocket frame data, implement rate limiting on WebSocket connections per user/IP, maintain WebSocket connection inventory and audit logs, use sub-protocols for additional authentication layers, implement connection timeout policies, and monitor for abnormal message patterns or protocol violations.

threat_vector: Account enumeration via timing difference
exploitation_method: Attacker sends authentication requests to a service and measures response times. Valid usernames trigger different processing logic (password validation) than invalid ones, creating measurable timing differences that reveal account existence without requiring successful authentication.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Rapid sequential authentication attempts to different usernames from single source IP, (2) Statistical clustering of response times correlating with username validity, (3) Requests with systematic username patterns (sequential IDs, common names, dictionary-based), (4) Abnormal authentication failure rates with consistent sub-millisecond response time variance per username
mitigation_strategy: Implement constant-time authentication responses by: (1) Always execute full authentication logic regardless of username validity, (2) Add artificial random delays (50-200ms) to all responses, (3) Rate-limit authentication attempts per IP/subnet, (4) Implement account lockout policies, (5) Use time-constant comparison functions for all credential checks, (6) Monitor and alert on enumeration patterns, (7) Require CAPTCHA after threshold of failed attempts

threat_vector: PendingIntent hijacking in Bixby Routines (versions <3.1.21.8 on Android R, <2.6.30.5 on Android Q)
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious app that intercepts or modifies PendingIntent objects before execution, allowing execution of privileged Bixby Routine actions without proper authorization. The vulnerability stems from insufficient validation of PendingIntent flags and implicit intent handling, enabling intent substitution or parameter tampering.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unexpected PendingIntent.send() calls from non-system UIDs targeting Bixby Routines; (2) PackageManager queries for Bixby Routine components followed by immediate startActivity/startService calls; (3) Logcat entries showing 'Permission Denial' or 'Intent resolution' mismatches for com.samsung.android.bixby* components; (4) SELinux audit logs (ausearch) showing unauthorized transitions to bixby_server_t context; (5) Unusual IPC calls (Binder transactions) to Bixby service with ACTION_* intents; (6) Multiple rapid PendingIntent creations with FLAG_IMMUTABLE not set
mitigation_strategy: Immediate: (1) Update Bixby Routines to patched versions (≥3.1.21.8 for Android R, ≥2.6.30.5 for Android Q); (2) Enforce PendingIntent.FLAG_IMMUTABLE and PendingIntent.FLAG_UPDATE_CURRENT flags in all PendingIntent creations; (3) Implement explicit intent filtering in AndroidManifest.xml, removing implicit intent handlers for sensitive Bixby Routine actions; (4) Add runtime permission checks for SCHEDULE_EXACT_ALARM and BIND_JOB_SERVICE before PendingIntent execution; (5) Deploy SELinux policy restricting third-party app access to bixby_server domain; (6) Disable Bixby Routines if not required and restrict app installation from untrusted sources via Play Protect/Knox

threat_vector: Insyde InsydeH2O SdHostDriver Kernel Vulnerability - Unpatched versions 5.1-5.5
exploitation_method: Attacker exploits memory corruption or privilege escalation flaw in SdHostDriver through crafted input to kernel driver, potentially gaining elevated system privileges or executing arbitrary code in kernel context
detection_pattern: Monitor for: kernel crash dumps mentioning SdHostDriver, abnormal SD card controller access patterns, privilege escalation events from user to SYSTEM, signed driver loading from unexpected paths, firmware modification attempts targeting chipset, IOCTL calls to SdHostDriver with malformed parameters, system hangs or reboots correlating with SD card access
mitigation_strategy: Immediately patch BIOS/firmware to patched kernel versions (5.1>=05.16.25, 5.2>=05.26.25, 5.3>=05.35.25, 5.4>=05.43.25, 5.5>=05.51.25); restrict user access to SD card interfaces; disable SD card controller if not required; implement UEFI Secure Boot with code signing validation; monitor and log all driver load events; apply principle of least privilege to kernel driver operations

threat_vector: Integer overflow in doProlog function (xmlparse.c) affecting m_groupSize variable in Expat < 2.4.3
