detection_pattern: Monitoring for: unusual cryptographic operation patterns, unexpected cipher suite negotiations, repeated key derivation attempts, entropy analysis anomalies in random number generation, timing attacks on cryptographic functions, or statistical patterns in encrypted outputs. Review security logs for failed or degraded encryption configurations.
mitigation_strategy: Implement cryptographic review processes including third-party security audits of algorithm implementations, enforce use of NIST-approved or industry-standard algorithms with sufficient key lengths (minimum 256-bit for symmetric, 2048-bit for asymmetric), implement proper key management practices, use cryptographically secure RNG libraries, apply defense-in-depth with redundant encryption layers, maintain regular security patching, and implement runtime integrity checking of cryptographic components.

threat_vector: UltraJSON (ujson) <= 5.1.0 stack-based buffer overflow in Buffer_AppendIndentUnchecked via excessive indentation
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious JSON input with deeply nested structures or excessive indent parameters passed to ujson.encode(), causing stack buffer overflow when Buffer_AppendIndentUnchecked writes indentation characters without proper bounds checking
detection_pattern: Monitor for: ujson.encode() calls with indent parameter > 1000; application crashes with segmentation faults or stack corruption; memory access violations in ujson C extension; abnormal process termination during JSON encoding; stack canary violations in debugger output; correlated with JSON inputs containing deep nesting (>500 levels) or explicit large indent values
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade ujson to version > 5.1.0 immediately; implement input validation to reject JSON encoding requests with indent values exceeding 128 or nesting depth > 256; use sandboxing/containerization to limit crash impact; enable stack canary protections (compiler flags: -fstack-protector-all); implement resource limits on JSON processing (timeouts, memory caps); consider alternative JSON libraries with memory safety guarantees if ujson updates unavailable

threat_vector: Stack-based buffer overflow in giftrans 1.12.2 giftrans function via malformed GIF file with oversized data length field
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious GIF file with inflated length value in header that exceeds allocated buffer size, causing stack memory corruption when giftrans reads and writes beyond buffer boundaries without bounds checking
detection_pattern: Monitor for: segmentation faults or abnormal process termination of giftrans, memory access violations at specific stack addresses, canary value corruption detected by AddressSanitizer/FORTIFY_SOURCE, abnormal GIF file properties (length fields exceeding file size), stack smashing detected in core dumps showing overwritten return addresses
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade giftrans to patched version >1.12.2; implement strict input validation verifying length fields against actual file size and allocated buffer capacity before processing; enable stack protection mechanisms (ASLR, stack canaries, DEP/NX); run giftrans in sandboxed environment with reduced privileges; use memory safety tools (AddressSanitizer, Valgrind) during development; implement file size limits and timeouts for GIF processing

threat_vector: Unauthenticated Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) in BeyondTrust Secure Remote Access Base Software v6.0.1 and older
exploitation_method: Attacker injects malicious JavaScript code through unvalidated input parameters in the web interface. The injected script executes in the context of other users' browsers when they visit a crafted URL or interact with compromised content, allowing session hijacking, credential theft, or malware distribution without requiring prior authentication.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) HTTP requests with unusual URL parameters containing script tags, javascript: protocols, or event handlers; (2) Web server logs showing script injection attempts in GET/POST parameters; (3) Browser console errors or unexpected script execution in application traffic; (4) WAF alerts for XSS payload patterns; (5) Anomalous session tokens or cookies being exfiltrated to external domains; (6) Multiple failed authentication attempts followed by successful session takeovers
mitigation_strategy: Immediately upgrade BeyondTrust to version 6.0.2 or later. Implement: (1) Input validation and output encoding on all user-supplied data; (2) Content Security Policy (CSP) headers restricting script sources; (3) HTTPOnly and Secure flags on session cookies; (4) Web Application Firewall (WAF) rules blocking XSS patterns; (5) Regular security scanning and vulnerability assessments; (6) Network segmentation to limit exposure; (7) User training on phishing and suspicious links; (8) Audit all active sessions for unauthorized access and revoke compromised tokens

threat_vector: Unprotected WifiEvaluationService in TencentWifiSecurity application (pre-SMR Jan-2022 Release 1) allows untrusted applications to access WiFi information without authorization
exploitation_method: Attacker creates malicious application that invokes the exposed WifiEvaluationService via IPC/Binder mechanism without required permissions. Service fails to validate caller identity or enforce permission checks before returning sensitive WiFi network details including SSID, signal strength, encryption type, and connected network information.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unexpected IPC calls to WifiEvaluationService from non-system applications via logcat 'Binder' logs; (2) Applications with minimal permissions accessing WiFi evaluation functions; (3) Unusual frequency of WiFi data queries from single package; (4) SELinux denials related to WifiEvaluationService access; (5) Package name mismatches between declared permissions and actual service access attempts
mitigation_strategy: Update TencentWifiSecurity to Jan-2022 SMR Release 1 or later. Implement: (1) Strict Binder permission verification in WifiEvaluationService.onBind() using checkCallingPermission() and package signature validation; (2) Enforce android.permission.ACCESS_WIFI_STATE requirement; (3) SELinux policy restricting service access to authorized system components; (4) Rate limiting on WiFi evaluation queries; (5) Audit logging of all service calls with caller package name and timestamp

threat_vector: Dnsmasq 2.86 heap-based buffer overflow in answer_request function
exploitation_method: Attacker sends specially crafted DNS queries with malformed packet structures to trigger heap buffer overflow in answer_request processing, potentially achieving remote code execution or denial of service without authentication
detection_pattern: Monitor for: dnsmasq process crashes or segmentation faults, unexpected memory allocation patterns, DNS query responses with abnormal sizes or malformed structures, core dumps from dnsmasq process, system logs showing heap corruption errors, unusual dnsmasq CPU/memory spikes, network traffic with DNS packets containing oversized labels or invalid RDATA field lengths
mitigation_strategy: Immediately upgrade dnsmasq to version 2.87 or later; implement network-level DNS query validation and rate limiting; run dnsmasq with minimal privileges in isolated container/namespace; deploy ASLR and stack canaries on system; implement strict input validation for DNS packet sizes; monitor dnsmasq process with watchdog that restarts on crash; consider deploying DNS firewall that validates packet structure before reaching dnsmasq

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1017 - Application Deployment Software
exploitation_method: Attacker compromises or impersonates legitimate application deployment tools (Jenkins, GitLab CI/CD, Ansible, Chef, Puppet) to inject malicious code into build pipelines, deploy backdoors, or modify production configurations without authorization
detection_pattern: Unusual deployment activities: unexpected pipeline executions, modifications to deployment scripts by unauthorized users, deployments outside maintenance windows, changes to credential stores or secrets in CI/CD systems, unexpected binaries in deployment artifacts, failed integrity checks on deployment packages, lateral movement from CI/CD infrastructure to production systems, anomalous git commits or branches in deployment repositories
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict role-based access control (RBAC) for deployment tools; enable multi-factor authentication for all deployment system accounts; digitally sign all deployment artifacts and validate signatures before execution; maintain audit logs of all deployment activities with alerting on suspicious patterns; segment CI/CD infrastructure from production networks; use immutable deployment containers; implement code review requirements for all pipeline changes; regularly scan deployment tools for vulnerabilities and supply chain compromises; conduct integrity monitoring of deployment scripts and configuration files

threat_vector: Uninitialized data in kd_camera_hw driver leading to information disclosure
exploitation_method: Attacker with system privileges interacts with kd_camera_hw driver to trigger code paths that read uninitialized kernel memory or driver buffers, exposing sensitive data (cryptographic keys, credentials, memory addresses) through driver interface or sysfs exposed parameters
detection_pattern: Monitor for: kd_camera_hw ioctl calls with unusual parameter combinations; memory read operations on uninitialized stack/heap via strace/ftrace; kernel warnings about use-of-uninitialized-variable from driver; unexpected data patterns in /sys/kernel/debug entries related to camera driver; valgrind/KASAN reports on driver initialization paths
mitigation_strategy: Explicitly initialize all driver buffers, stack variables, and data structures before use (memset to zero); implement input validation for all ioctl commands; use compiler flags (-Wuninitialized, -fsanitize=memory); audit driver code for data flow from uninitialized regions; restrict driver ioctl access via SELinux/AppArmor policies; update to patched driver version that zeros memory on allocation

threat_vector: Lens Helm Chart Command Injection (CVE-2021-21272)
exploitation_method: Attacker provides malicious arguments in custom Helm chart configuration that get concatenated into shell commands without proper escaping or sanitization. Shell metacharacters in arguments (e.g., backticks, $(), pipes) are interpreted by the shell, allowing arbitrary command execution with user privileges.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unusual shell metacharacters in Helm chart argument values logged by Lens; (2) Unexpected child processes spawned from Lens electron process (e.g., bash, sh executing commands beyond normal helm operations); (3) File system or network activity anomalies following Helm chart deployment; (4) Error messages containing unescaped command substitution syntax in Lens logs
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Lens to version 5.3.4 or later which implements proper argument parameterization. Implement application-level defenses: (1) Use array-based command execution APIs instead of string concatenation for shell commands; (2) Apply strict input validation and sanitization to all Helm arguments; (3) Use shell execution with argument arrays/lists to prevent interpretation of metacharacters; (4) Restrict Helm chart upload/configuration to trusted sources only; (5) Run Lens with minimal user privileges; (6) Monitor and restrict child process execution from Lens application

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1070.002: Indicator Removal - Clear Linux or Mac System Logs
exploitation_method: Attacker executes commands to delete or truncate system log files (e.g., rm -rf /var/log/*, purge_history, log clearing utilities) to remove evidence of malicious activity, lateral movement, or persistence mechanisms from audit trails
detection_pattern: Monitor for: sudden gaps in log timestamps, missing log files that previously existed, truncated log sizes, commands containing 'rm', 'truncate', or 'shred' against /var/log paths, audit log deletions (auditctl disabling), shell history file modifications, sudo log tampering, syslog process restarts without valid reason, file integrity monitoring (FIM) alerts on log directories, 'last' command showing incomplete session records
mitigation_strategy: Deploy centralized logging (syslog, ELK, Splunk) with logs sent to remote immutable storage; enable write-once-read-many (WORM) log archival; configure auditd with immutable rules and remote forwarding; implement file integrity monitoring on /var/log with alert triggers; restrict file deletion permissions via SELinux/AppArmor policies; enable command auditing for root; configure log rotation with tamper detection; monitor for auditd service restarts or configuration changes

threat_vector: ATT&CK: DNS (T1583.002 - Acquire Infrastructure: Domains)
exploitation_method: Attacker registers domains for command and control, phishing, or malware distribution. May use fast flux DNS, domain generation algorithms (DGA), or compromised legitimate domains to evade static blocklists and maintain persistent communication channels.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unusual DNS query patterns (high frequency to single domain, queries for recently registered domains), DNS responses from IPs in threat intelligence feeds, NXDOMAIN responses followed by successful queries, DNS tunneling indicators (excessive subdomain queries, non-standard record types like TXT/NULL for data exfiltration), whois record age anomalies, and mismatches between DNS response IPs and expected infrastructure
mitigation_strategy: Implement DNS filtering and rate-limiting; deploy DNS sinkhole infrastructure; maintain updated threat intelligence feeds for malicious domains; enforce DNSSEC validation; restrict DNS egress to authorized resolvers; monitor and alert on DGA-like patterns; conduct regular domain reputation checks; implement split-DNS to separate internal/external resolution; enable DNS query logging with retention for forensics

threat_vector: Invalid CVE reference or withdrawn vulnerability identifier
exploitation_method: Attacker attempts to reference non-existent, withdrawn, or invalidated CVE identifiers to evade vulnerability tracking systems, confuse patch management processes, or create false positives in security scanning tools
detection_pattern: Monitor for CVE references that: return 'rejected' or 'withdrawn' status from official CVE databases, lack valid CNA assignment, appear in security reports but fail NVD validation, or show inconsistent severity ratings across scanning tools
mitigation_strategy: Implement CVE validation middleware that cross-references all identified vulnerabilities against official NVD and MITRE databases before alerting, maintain allowlists of valid CNAs, configure scanning tools to reject unvalidated CVE identifiers, and establish incident response procedures to investigate anomalous CVE citations

threat_vector: Access control violation through improper permission masking in Snapdragon VMID permission setting
exploitation_method: Attacker exploits insufficient permission validation during VMID (Virtual Machine ID) permission configuration, allowing bypass of intended access controls through malformed permission masks or boundary condition exploitation in the permission validation logic
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unauthorized VMID permission state changes in system logs, permission mask values deviating from expected ranges, VMID configuration requests from unexpected privilege levels, repeated failed permission validation attempts, unexpected transitions between permission states, anomalous hypervisor security policy modifications
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict permission masking validation with explicit whitelist checks before VMID configuration changes; enforce principle of least privilege for permission modification operations; add dual-validation of permission masks using independent verification routines; implement comprehensive audit logging of all VMID permission modifications; conduct regular permission matrix consistency checks; apply security patches addressing permission validation vulnerabilities; restrict VMID configuration to authorized system components only

threat_vector: Distributed data service component with insufficient data access control allowing unauthorized data exposure
exploitation_method: Attacker bypasses or circumvents access control lists (ACLs) in the distributed data service through privilege escalation, token manipulation, or direct API calls to access sensitive data they are not authorized to view
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unauthorized API calls to data endpoints from unexpected service accounts, spike in data read requests from non-standard clients, access log entries showing mismatched user identity vs. requested data ownership, failed access control validation events in application logs, unusual cross-service data access patterns, and abnormal volume of requests to sensitive data endpoints
mitigation_strategy: Implement attribute-based access control (ABAC) with mandatory access checks on every data read operation, enforce principle of least privilege for service identities, validate authorization tokens on each request, implement field-level encryption for sensitive data, audit and rotate service credentials regularly, enable comprehensive access logging with immutable storage, implement rate limiting on data endpoints, and conduct regular security reviews of access control implementations

threat_vector: Missing bounds check in libvcodecdrv leading to out-of-bounds memory read
exploitation_method: Attacker sends specially crafted input to libvcodecdrv codec driver without proper bounds validation, causing the driver to read and disclose adjacent memory regions containing sensitive kernel or process data
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unusual libvcodecdrv system calls with oversized buffer parameters, (2) Kernel logs showing memory access violations or exceptions in codec driver, (3) Processes accessing libvcodecdrv with suspiciously large read operations relative to allocated buffers, (4) Unexpected data leakage patterns in process output following codec operations
mitigation_strategy: Apply security patches to libvcodecdrv that implement proper bounds checking on all buffer operations; use address space layout randomization (ASLR) and stack canaries to reduce information disclosure impact; implement kernel-level memory protection mechanisms; conduct static code analysis to identify all unchecked buffer accesses in codec paths; enforce principle of least privilege for codec driver access

threat_vector: HwNearbyMain module sensitive information exposure to unauthorized actors
exploitation_method: Attacker gains local access to device and exploits insufficient access controls in HwNearbyMain module to read sensitive data (device identifiers, location info, pairing tokens) from process memory or unprotected storage, potentially triggering process crash/restart as side effect
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unexpected HwNearbyMain process restarts with error codes; file access attempts to /proc/[pid]/mem or /dev/mem by unprivileged users; SELinux/AppArmor denials for HwNearbyMain data access; abnormal Bluetooth pairing data reads; multiple rapid process terminations followed by restart cycles; logcat entries showing 'SIGSEGV' or 'Fatal Exception' in HwNearbyMain; unauthorized access to /data/misc/bluetooth or proximity service caches
mitigation_strategy: Apply manufacturer security patches immediately; enforce strict SELinux policies restricting HwNearbyMain data access to authorized processes only; implement memory protection mechanisms (ASLR, DEP); use encrypted storage for sensitive Bluetooth identifiers and pairing tokens; restrict local file permissions on proximity service data to uid 1000+ (system user only); monitor process integrity and restart frequency; disable Nearby/Bluetooth features if unused; audit local privilege escalation vectors; implement runtime application self-protection (RASP) for sensitive data reads

threat_vector: Roundcube XSS via Crafted CSS in HTML Email Messages (CVE-2021-26870)
exploitation_method: Attacker sends HTML email with malicious CSS token sequences that bypass Roundcube's HTML sanitization filters. CSS constructs like `@import`, `behavior`, or property injection with javascript: protocols bypass filtering and execute arbitrary JavaScript in the webmail client's context.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Email headers containing Content-Type: text/html with unusual CSS directives (@import, @keyframes with script-like content); (2) Web logs showing XSS payloads in email message bodies processed by Roundcube; (3) Browser console errors related to CSS parsing followed by script execution; (4) HTTP requests to external resources from within email rendering contexts; (5) Administrative logs showing messages with style tags containing javascript: or vbscript: protocols; (6) Network traffic exfiltrating session cookies or authentication tokens from webmail sessions.
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Roundcube to version 1.4.13 or later (for 1.4.x branch) or 1.5.2 or later (for 1.5.x branch). Implement strict Content Security Policy (CSP) headers restricting style-src to 'none' or 'self' with no external stylesheets. Configure email client to disable HTML rendering by default or render in sandboxed iframe with restricted permissions. Use allowlist-based HTML/CSS sanitization library (e.g., HTML Purifier) with explicit CSS property whitelisting. Disable @import, @keyframes, and behavior CSS at the server-side sanitization layer. Enable security.csp.enable in browser configurations and implement X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff headers.

threat_vector: PeerTube Improper Access Control vulnerability allowing unauthorized access to restricted content
exploitation_method: Attacker manipulates URL parameters, JWT tokens, or API endpoints to bypass permission checks on video content, user accounts, or administrative functions. Bypassing video visibility settings (private/unlisted), accessing other users' draft videos, or escalating privileges to moderator/admin roles through missing authorization validators.
detection_pattern: Log entries showing: requests to private video IDs from non-owner accounts, 200 HTTP responses where 403 Forbidden expected, API calls modifying user roles without privilege verification, unusual access patterns to /api/v1/videos/{id}, JWT token claims mismatch with actual permissions, access logs showing users viewing content outside their visibility scope, database queries revealing permission checks returning true when should return false
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict authorization checks on all endpoints: verify user ownership/permissions before returning video data, enforce role-based access control (RBAC) at API layer, validate JWT token claims against database permissions on each request, implement per-resource ACLs, use middleware to check visibility status before responding, conduct regular security audits of permission logic, enable detailed access logging with audit trails, apply principle of least privilege, keep PeerTube updated to latest version with security patches

threat_vector: Null pointer dereference in GPAC MP4Box gf_isom_get_track_id function (version 0.8.0-rev177-g51a8ef874-master)
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malformed MP4 file with missing or corrupted track data structures that causes gf_isom_get_track_id to dereference a null pointer when attempting to access track metadata, resulting in process crash
detection_pattern: Monitor for: segmentation faults or abnormal termination of MP4Box process when processing specific MP4 files; system logs showing SIGSEGV signal; repeated crash attempts with incrementally modified MP4 files; core dumps indicating null pointer dereference in gf_isom_get_track_id; unusually short process execution times with error codes indicating memory violations
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade GPAC to patched version beyond rev177; implement input validation to verify MP4 file structure integrity before processing; use address space layout randomization (ASLR) and stack canaries at compile time; deploy sandboxing for media processing (containers, seccomp); implement resource limits on MP4Box process; validate track object existence before dereferencing in application code; fuzz testing of MP4 parser with malformed inputs

threat_vector: Dnsmasq 2.86 heap-based buffer overflow in dhcp_reply function
exploitation_method: Attacker sends a specially crafted DHCP packet with oversized or malformed fields that exceeds heap buffer boundaries in the dhcp_reply function, potentially allowing arbitrary code execution when the function processes the malicious input
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) DHCP packets with abnormally large option fields or payload sizes exceeding RFC 2131 specifications; (2) Dnsmasq process crashes or segmentation faults in dhcp_reply context; (3) Unexpected memory access patterns or heap corruption indicators in system logs; (4) Unusual DHCP request sources sending probes with payload sizes incrementally increasing; (5) Error logs containing 'dhcp_reply' stack traces or memory violations
mitigation_strategy: Implement defensive measures: (1) Update Dnsmasq to patched version (post-2.86); (2) Apply input validation to reject oversized DHCP option fields before processing; (3) Deploy Address Space Layout Randomization (ASLR) and stack canaries on DHCP service hosts; (4) Implement strict DHCP packet size limits and option field length enforcement; (5) Isolate DHCP service in a container/sandbox with limited privileges; (6) Use network segmentation to restrict DHCP traffic to trusted subnets; (7) Monitor Dnsmasq process memory consumption and restart on anomalies

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1018 - Remote System Discovery / Internet Connection Discovery
exploitation_method: Attacker executes network reconnaissance commands (ipconfig, ifconfig, route, netstat, nslookup) to enumerate local network configuration, active connections, and routing tables. May use WMI queries or API calls to gather network adapter information and determine internet connectivity paths.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: suspicious execution of ipconfig/ifconfig with unusual parent processes, elevated netstat/ss commands from non-admin users, WMI queries targeting Win32_NetworkAdapterConfiguration, DNS queries to public resolvers from internal systems, route table enumeration via 'route print' or 'ip route show', process execution from temp directories or suspicious locations running discovery tools
mitigation_strategy: Implement application whitelisting to restrict discovery tool execution; disable unnecessary WMI namespaces; use EDR with behavioral analytics to flag rapid network enumeration; segment networks to limit lateral movement; enforce DNS filtering and proxy requirements; monitor and alert on netstat/route commands from suspicious processes; disable command-line tools (cmd.exe, powershell) where not required; implement least privilege to prevent non-admin discovery attempts

threat_vector: Prototype Pollution via unsafe recursive merge in extend2 package versions before 1.0.1
exploitation_method: Attacker passes malicious objects with __proto__, constructor, or prototype properties to the extend() function, causing recursive merge to pollute Object.prototype and inject properties into all JavaScript objects application-wide
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unexpected property additions to Object.prototype in runtime inspection, (2) Log entries showing extend() function calls with objects containing __proto__ or constructor keys, (3) Abnormal behavior in object property lookups, (4) Security scanning tools flagging prototype pollution gadget chains, (5) Network requests with JSON payloads containing {"__proto__":{...}} or {"constructor":{...}} patterns
mitigation_strategy: Immediately upgrade extend2 to version 1.0.1 or later; implement input validation to reject objects with __proto__, constructor, or prototype properties before passing to extend(); use Object.freeze(Object.prototype) in initialization if application permits; employ runtime security policies that prevent modification of Object.prototype; conduct code review for all extend() usage patterns; consider using safer merge alternatives like Object.assign() with sanitization or lodash merge with appropriate guards

threat_vector: Uncontrolled resource consumption in display module leading to integrity compromise
exploitation_method: Attacker sends specially crafted input to display module that triggers excessive memory allocation, CPU usage, or GPU processing without proper bounds checking. This resource exhaustion can cause display corruption, data rendering errors, or denial of service that compromises data integrity during rendering or display operations.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: abnormal memory growth in display process, sustained high CPU/GPU utilization from display module, repeated allocation failures, display buffer overflows in logs, rendering corruption events, process crash dumps from display service, unusual input sizes to rendering functions, memory profiler alerts showing unbounded growth patterns
mitigation_strategy: Implement input validation with strict size limits on display inputs, add resource quotas and memory caps per display operation, use bounded buffers with overflow protection, implement timeout mechanisms for rendering operations, add rate limiting on display refresh cycles, conduct fuzzing and resource consumption testing, deploy resource monitoring with alerts for threshold violations, apply principle of least privilege to display process permissions

threat_vector: wolfSSL wolfMQTT 1.9 heap-based buffer overflow in MqttDecode_Disconnect function
exploitation_method: Attacker sends a malformed MQTT DISCONNECT packet with invalid length field that causes the decoder to write beyond allocated heap buffer boundaries, potentially enabling arbitrary code execution or denial of service
detection_pattern: Monitor for: heap corruption errors, unexpected process termination with segmentation fault, abnormal memory access violations in wolfMQTT process, malformed MQTT DISCONNECT packets with length field mismatches, AddressSanitizer/Valgrind heap-buffer-overflow warnings, unusual process crashes in MqttDecode_Disconnect or MqttClient_DecodePacket stack traces
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade to wolfSSL wolfMQTT version 1.10 or later that includes bounds checking fixes; implement strict input validation on MQTT packet length fields before decoding; deploy memory safety tools (ASan, Valgrind) in development; use Address Space Layout Randomization (ASLR) and DEP/NX bit protections; implement network segmentation to restrict MQTT broker access; monitor process memory allocations for anomalies; apply fuzzing tests to MQTT packet parsing routines

threat_vector: Integer overflow in mdp driver memory allocation leading to buffer overflow and privilege escalation
exploitation_method: Attacker sends crafted input to mdp driver that causes integer overflow in size calculation, resulting in undersized heap allocation. Subsequent write operations overflow the allocated buffer, corrupting adjacent memory structures and overwriting privilege context data.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: kernel memory corruption signatures, unexpected mdp driver ioctl calls with large numeric parameters, kernel page faults in mdp memory regions, failed memory allocation attempts followed by write operations, unexpected privilege escalation of unprivileged processes, kernel sanitizer warnings (KASAN) detecting heap-buffer-overflow in mdp driver, audit logs showing privilege changes without corresponding authorization events
mitigation_strategy: Apply kernel patch validating size parameters before multiplication operations in mdp driver; implement integer overflow checks using safe arithmetic functions; enable CONFIG_UBSAN and CONFIG_KASAN kernel options for runtime detection; restrict mdp driver ioctl access via SELinux/AppArmor policies; use hardware-based CFI/CET to prevent malicious control flow; update to patched mdp driver version; implement memory tagging extension (MTE) on supporting architectures; audit and fuzz mdp driver input validation routines

threat_vector: IBM Security Verify 10.0.0, 10.0.1.0, 10.0.2.0 sensitive version information disclosure in HTTP response headers
exploitation_method: Attacker sends HTTP requests to IBM Security Verify endpoints and examines response headers (e.g., Server, X-Powered-By, X-IBM-Version) to identify exact product version, enabling targeted attack research and vulnerability matching
detection_pattern: Monitor HTTP response headers for: Server header containing 'IBM Security Verify 10.0', X-IBM-Version headers, X-Powered-By headers revealing version strings; log and alert on version disclosure in any outbound HTTP responses; search web server logs for pattern matches to known version disclosure headers
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade to IBM Security Verify 10.0.3.0 or later; configure web server to suppress or obfuscate version-revealing headers (Server, X-Powered-By, X-IBM-Version); implement WAF rules to remove sensitive headers before response transmission; disable unnecessary HTTP headers in application configuration; conduct regular HTTP header audits

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Domain Groups (T1098.003)
exploitation_method: Attacker gains access to Active Directory and adds compromised user accounts to high-privilege domain groups (e.g., Domain Admins, Enterprise Admins) to escalate privileges and maintain persistence across the domain
detection_pattern: Monitor for: memberOf attribute changes in Active Directory, unusual group membership modifications in Security Event ID 4728/4732/4756 (group member additions), changes to sensitive group objects outside change windows, member count anomalies in privileged groups, access token changes showing new group SIDs for previously non-privileged accounts
mitigation_strategy: Implement Group Policy restrictions on sensitive group modifications, enable and monitor Active Directory auditing for group membership changes, use privileged access workstations (PAW) for administrative actions, enforce multi-factor authentication for privileged accounts, conduct regular access reviews of domain group memberships, implement principle of least privilege using role-based access control, and deploy security monitoring tools that alert on unexpected group membership changes

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Exploit Public-Facing Application (SharePoint)
exploitation_method: Attacker identifies unpatched SharePoint instances exposed to the internet and leverages known CVEs (e.g., CVE-2023-29357, CVE-2023-24932) to achieve remote code execution, authentication bypass, or privilege escalation through malicious requests to vulnerable endpoints
detection_pattern: Monitor for: HTTP 500/502 errors from SharePoint endpoints, unusual POST requests to /_vti_bin/ or /sites/ with obfuscated payloads, failed authentication attempts followed by successful admin operations, suspicious ULS log entries with error codes related to deserialization or code execution, and unexpected outbound connections from SharePoint application pool identity
mitigation_strategy: Implement: regular patching cadence for SharePoint and dependent components, network segmentation restricting SharePoint access to authenticated users only, Web Application Firewall (WAF) rules blocking malicious SharePoint patterns, disable unnecessary SharePoint services and features, enforce MFA for all SharePoint administrative accounts, maintain audit logging with alerting on privilege escalation, and conduct regular vulnerability assessments

threat_vector: Null/Invalid Pointer Dereference in GPAC 1.0.1 gf_isom_box_size function
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malformed ISO media file (MP4/MOV) with specially structured box headers that cause gf_isom_box_size to dereference a null or invalid pointer when processing box metadata, triggering a crash or memory fault
detection_pattern: Monitor for: segmentation faults or access violations in libgpac during media file parsing; stack traces containing gf_isom_box_size; abnormal process termination when parsing specific media files; memory access violations at predictable offsets in heap allocations related to box parsing
mitigation_strategy: Update GPAC to patched version >1.0.1; implement input validation before box processing to verify pointer validity; use AddressSanitizer or similar tools in development; deploy sandboxed media parsing with privilege separation; implement watchdog timers for parser operations; validate ISO box structure (size, type fields) before dereferencing; enable NX/DEP and ASLR protections

threat_vector: Stack buffer overflow in libde265 v1.0.8 via fallback-motion.cc put_epel_hv_fallback function in dec265
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious HEVC video file with specially constructed motion compensation parameters that cause out-of-bounds write to stack memory during the epel interpolation process, potentially achieving code execution through stack corruption and return address overwrite
detection_pattern: Monitor for: abnormal dec265 process termination with SIGSEGV/segmentation fault, stack canary corruption detection, unusual memory access patterns near stack frames during video decoding, ASLR bypass attempts, ROP gadget sequencing in crash dumps, or unexpected process behavior following dec265 video file processing of untrusted sources
mitigation_strategy: Update libde265 to patched version beyond 1.0.8, implement strict input validation for HEVC video files before decoding, enable stack protection mechanisms (stack canaries, CFI), run dec265 in restricted sandbox/container with limited privileges, apply address space layout randomization (ASLR), disable execution on stack memory, implement strict bounds checking in motion compensation code, and restrict dec265 usage to trusted video sources only

threat_vector: NocoDB Password Reset Observable Discrepancy (CVE-2021-41329) - Versions 0.9 to 0.83.8
exploitation_method: Attacker submits password reset requests for target email addresses and observes response time differences or error message variations. Valid accounts produce different responses than non-existent accounts, allowing user enumeration and account discovery without authentication.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: multiple sequential password reset requests from single IP address; response time analysis showing statistical differences between requests; unusual patterns of 'user not found' vs 'reset email sent' responses; spike in reset requests to common email formats; bursts of requests to non-existent email addresses followed by specific valid addresses
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade NocoDB to version 0.83.9 or later; implement rate limiting on password reset endpoints (max 5 requests per IP per hour); return identical response messages and timing for both valid and invalid email addresses; add CAPTCHA verification after 3 failed reset attempts; implement account lockout mechanisms; monitor and alert on password reset request anomalies; use generic response: 'If an account exists, you will receive a reset email' regardless of input validity

threat_vector: Insecure deserialization in session cookie
exploitation_method: Attacker modifies serialized object in session cookie to inject malicious payload. Upon deserialization by the application, arbitrary code execution occurs if the application deserializes untrusted data without validation, particularly in Java (ObjectInputStream), Python (pickle), PHP (unserialize), or .NET (BinaryFormatter)
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unexpected exception errors during cookie deserialization, stack traces mentioning ObjectInputStream or pickle operations, unusual process spawning after session validation, cookie payload containing gadget chain signatures (e.g., CommonsCollections patterns), base64-decoded cookie contents showing serialization headers (0xaced0005 for Java, 0x80 for Python pickle), abnormal CPU/memory spikes during authentication phase
mitigation_strategy: 1) Implement cryptographic signing and validation of session cookies using HMAC before deserialization. 2) Use secure serialization formats (JSON) instead of binary serialization. 3) If binary serialization required, use allowlist deserialization filters restricting permitted classes. 4) Enable Java deserialization filters (JEP 290) for Java applications. 5) Avoid pickle.loads() for untrusted data; use safer alternatives like json. 6) Disable dangerous .NET formatters (BinaryFormatter, LosFormatter, ObjectStateFormatter). 7) Implement strict Content Security Policy and HTTPOnly cookie flags. 8) Apply principle of least privilege to application runtime permissions

threat_vector: MassEditRegex CSRF vulnerability in MediaWiki versions <1.35.5, 1.36.0-1.36.2, 1.37.0
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious webpage containing hidden form or script that triggers MassEditRegex API calls without CSRF token validation. When authenticated wiki admin visits the malicious site, their browser automatically submits requests to perform bulk regex edits on wiki content, modifying or deleting pages without their knowledge or consent.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) MassEditRegex API requests lacking valid CSRF tokens in request headers or POST parameters; (2) Bulk edit operations originating from unusual referrer headers or external domains; (3) Sequential regex edit requests with identical patterns from same user session in short time window; (4) Edit logs showing large-scale automated changes by administrative accounts without corresponding user interaction timestamps
mitigation_strategy: Immediately upgrade MediaWiki to version 1.35.5+, 1.36.3+, or 1.37.1+. Implement CSRF token validation on all MassEditRegex operations by requiring valid edittoken parameter. Configure SameSite cookie policy to Strict for session cookies. Disable or restrict MassEditRegex extension for non-essential user roles. Review recent edit logs for suspicious bulk regex patterns and revert unauthorized changes. Implement Content Security Policy headers to prevent cross-origin form submission.

threat_vector: OS Credential Dumping (ATT&CK T1003)
exploitation_method: Attacker uses tools like Mimikatz, LSASS memory dump, /etc/shadow access, or SAM registry extraction to extract plaintext passwords, hashes, or Kerberos tickets from operating system memory or credential storage locations
detection_pattern: Process execution of credential dumping utilities (mimikatz.exe, procdump.exe, gsecdump); LSASS process access events (Event ID 10 with targetObject containing 'lsass'); unusual rundll32/comsvcs calls; /etc/shadow read attempts; ntdump/ntdsutil execution; Kerberos ticket requests from suspicious processes; memory read access to System, Local Security Authority, or SAM
mitigation_strategy: Enable Windows Defender Credential Guard to isolate LSASS in virtualized container; configure Windows Audit Policy to log LSASS access and process creation; restrict local admin privileges; use Protected Process Light (PPL) for LSASS; implement MFA to reduce plaintext password impact; monitor and block execution of known credential dumping tools; use RBAC to limit access to /etc/shadow and SAM registry; deploy EDR solutions with behavioral detection; enforce code integrity policies; patch OS regularly

threat_vector: Unauthenticated SQL Injection via unvalidated qvquery parameter in Plus Addons for Elementor Pro plugin (versions < 5.0.7)
exploitation_method: Attacker sends malicious SQL payload through the qvquery parameter in AJAX request to tp_get_dl_post_info_ajax action without authentication. The parameter is directly processed in database queries without sanitization or parameterized statements, allowing extraction of sensitive data including user credentials, post content, and database structure.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) POST requests to /wp-admin/admin-ajax.php with action=tp_get_dl_post_info_ajax from non-admin sources; (2) qvquery parameters containing SQL keywords (UNION, SELECT, WHERE, CAST, etc.); (3) unusual character sequences like '%27, --, /**/; (4) Multiple rapid AJAX requests with varying payload patterns; (5) Error logs showing database query failures followed by successful requests; (6) Exfiltration attempts to external domains in response data
mitigation_strategy: Immediately update Plus Addons for Elementor to version 5.0.7 or later. Implement nonce verification on all AJAX actions. Use prepared statements with parameterized queries for all database operations. Add input validation and sanitization for qvquery parameter (whitelist allowed characters). Implement rate limiting on tp_get_dl_post_info_ajax endpoint. Require authentication for sensitive AJAX actions. Apply WordPress security headers and disable database query errors in production. Monitor and restrict direct access to admin-ajax.php from non-admin users via WAF rules.

threat_vector: VMware ESXi/Workstation/Fusion CD-ROM device heap overflow (CVE-2021-22026)
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious CD-ROM device I/O requests that write beyond allocated heap buffer boundaries, potentially achieving arbitrary code execution with hypervisor/host privileges through memory corruption
detection_pattern: Monitor for: abnormal CD-ROM device IOCTL sequences, unexpected heap memory access violations, guest VM process crashes with memory corruption indicators, vmkernel panic logs referencing CD device handlers, unusual ATAPI command sequences, memory dump signatures indicating heap corruption patterns
mitigation_strategy: Apply security patches: ESXi 6.5 update ESXi650-202110101-SG or later, ESXi 6.7 update ESXi670-202111101-SG or later, ESXi 7.0 latest patch; Workstation 16.2.1+; Fusion 12.2.1+. Additionally: disable CD-ROM pass-through if unused, restrict VM device access controls, enable ASLR/DEP protections, monitor guest-to-host interaction logs, isolate critical VMs on separate hosts

threat_vector: IBM Security Verify information disclosure via error messages in versions 10.0.0, 10.0.1.0, and 10.0.2.0
exploitation_method: Attacker triggers application errors by sending malformed requests or exploiting edge cases in input validation. Server responds with detailed technical error messages containing sensitive information such as file paths, database queries, internal API endpoints, version numbers, or stack traces visible in browser responses.
detection_pattern: Monitor HTTP responses with status codes 400, 500, 502, 503, etc. containing keywords like 'stack trace', 'SQL', 'database', 'function', 'line', 'file path', '/opt/', or internal domain names. Log all instances where error detail verbosity exceeds security policy thresholds. Track requests designed to trigger errors (malformed XML/JSON, boundary values, path traversal attempts) followed by detailed error responses.
mitigation_strategy: 1) Upgrade to patched versions beyond 10.0.2.0. 2) Configure error handling to return generic messages to end users while logging detailed errors server-side only. 3) Implement Web Application Firewall (WAF) rules to strip sensitive information from error responses. 4) Disable debug mode and verbose logging in production. 5) Set HTTP headers like 'X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff' and 'X-Frame-Options'. 6) Review and test error handling across all endpoints. 7) Implement centralized error logging with access controls separate from user-facing responses.

threat_vector: ATT&CK: IP Addresses
exploitation_method: Attackers use IP address reconnaissance to identify target systems, map network topology, enumerate services on specific ports, and establish command & control communications. This includes using IP addresses to bypass geolocation restrictions, rotate through proxy/VPN infrastructure, or spoof source addresses in attacks.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unusual outbound connections to rare/suspicious IP ranges, multiple failed connection attempts across IP ranges indicating scanning, connections to known malicious IP blocklist entries, geographic anomalies in IP source locations, rapid sequential connections to different IPs from single host, DNS queries resolving to flagged IPs, connections to IPs associated with proxy/VPN services, NetFlow records showing atypical IP communication patterns, BGP hijacking indicators, and IPs with mismatched DNS reverse lookup records.
mitigation_strategy: Implement IP reputation filtering using threat intelligence feeds; deploy network segmentation to restrict IP-based lateral movement; enforce firewall rules based on IP allowlists/denylists; deploy geo-blocking for unauthorized regions; use DNS sinkholing for known malicious IPs; implement egress filtering to prevent C2 communications; monitor and log all IP-based connections; deploy IDS/IPS to detect suspicious IP scanning patterns; implement network access controls requiring authentication regardless of IP; regularly audit and validate IP configurations; use private IP space internally with strict NAT rules; deploy DNS filtering to prevent resolution of suspicious IPs.

threat_vector: Template injection in email system
exploitation_method: Attacker injects malicious template syntax (e.g., Jinja2, Freemarker, Velocity) into user-controlled fields that get processed by email templating engines. Payload executes server-side code during template rendering, leading to RCE, data exfiltration, or privilege escalation.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: template metacharacters in email fields ({{, {%, ${, <#), unusual template function calls in logs, unexpected code execution during email generation, stderr output containing template parsing errors, abnormal system resource usage during mail processing, template syntax errors in application logs with suspicious payloads
mitigation_strategy: Implement input validation and sanitization on all user-supplied email template fields using allowlists; disable dangerous template functions (exec, eval, system calls); use sandboxed template engines with restricted contexts; separate template code from user data; employ Content Security Policy for email templates; conduct regular code review of template implementations; use static analysis tools to detect template injection vulnerabilities; maintain updated templating libraries; implement rate limiting on email generation endpoints

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1053.006 - Scheduled Task/Job: Cron
exploitation_method: Attacker modifies crontab entries or creates new entries in /etc/cron.d/, /etc/cron.daily/, /etc/cron.hourly/, or user crontabs to execute malicious scripts at scheduled intervals for persistence, privilege escalation, or lateral movement
detection_pattern: Monitor crontab modifications via auditd rules on /etc/crontab and /etc/cron.d/; alert on crontab command execution; detect unexpected entries in 'crontab -l' output; watch syslog for CRON session opens with suspicious commands; flag new cron jobs from non-standard users; detect file modifications to cron directories with 'mtime' changes
mitigation_strategy: Restrict crontab access via /etc/cron.allow and /etc/cron.deny; disable cron for unprivileged users; use SELinux/AppArmor policies to restrict cron execution contexts; enable auditd monitoring with '-w /etc/crontab -p wa' and '-w /etc/cron.d/ -p wa'; regularly audit crontab entries with configuration management tools; implement file integrity monitoring on cron directories; run cron jobs with minimal required privileges

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1562.002: Impair Defenses - Disable or Modify Windows Event Logs
exploitation_method: Attacker with elevated privileges uses wevtutil.exe to clear event logs, modifies registry keys (HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\EventLog), disables Event Log service, or uses API calls to suppress log entries. Common commands: wevtutil cl Security, wevtutil sl Security /e:false, or direct deletion of .evtx files from %SystemRoot%\System32\winevt\Logs\
detection_pattern: Monitor for: wevtutil.exe execution with 'cl' or 'sl' parameters; Event ID 1102 (Security log cleared); sudden gaps in sequential Event IDs; Event Log service stopped (Service Control Manager Event ID 7035/7040); access to EventLog registry paths; deletion of .evtx files; PowerShell clearing logs via Clear-EventLog cmdlet; absence of expected logs during incident investigation
mitigation_strategy: Enable centralized log aggregation (SIEM) with immutable forwarding to remote server; configure Windows Event Forwarding (WEF) with TLS encryption; restrict wevtutil.exe and PowerShell event log cmdlets via AppLocker/WDAC; enable audit policy for sensitive registry modifications; implement file integrity monitoring on .evtx files; use Sysmon for parallel logging; enforce principle of least privilege to limit admin accounts; configure Log Failure event (Event ID 1100) monitoring; enable process auditing to detect suspicious tool execution

threat_vector: Use-After-Free/Uninitialized Memory in eID Module
exploitation_method: Attacker triggers eID authentication flow to cause uninitialized memory regions to be read or written, potentially exposing sensitive cryptographic keys, session tokens, or personal identification data stored in adjacent memory regions.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) eID module crashes with segmentation faults during authentication; (2) unpredictable memory access patterns in eID process memory dumps; (3) valgrind/ASAN reports of uninitialized reads; (4) inconsistent cryptographic operation results; (5) eID process spawning with abnormal exit codes; (6) kernel audit logs showing invalid memory access attempts from eID services.
mitigation_strategy: Apply vendor security patches immediately; enable Address Sanitizer (ASAN) in development/staging; implement static analysis (Clang Static Analyzer, Coverity) for memory initialization checks; require memory initialization before use; isolate eID module in restricted SELinux contexts; implement memory tagging extension (MTE) if available; conduct regular fuzzing of eID API inputs; use safe memory libraries; restrict eID process capabilities via seccomp.

threat_vector: WiFi driver missing validation check leading to remote denial of service
exploitation_method: Attacker sends specially crafted WiFi frames or management packets to trigger a null pointer dereference or buffer overflow in the driver's packet processing function, causing kernel panic or system crash without requiring authentication or special privileges
detection_pattern: Monitor for: kernel crash dumps mentioning WiFi driver module, sudden increase in WiFi driver error messages in kernel logs, unexpected system reboots following WiFi traffic anomalies, memory access violations in driver code paths via kdump or kcrash analysis, repeated connection failures from specific MAC addresses followed by system instability
mitigation_strategy: Implement input validation on all WiFi frame fields before processing, add bounds checking on buffer operations in driver, enable kernel address space layout randomization (ASLR) and stack canaries to mitigate exploitation, apply security patches to WiFi driver, restrict WiFi band/channel scanning scope, implement packet sanitization in driver before passing to higher layers, monitor and log anomalous frame patterns, configure watchdog timers for driver timeout recovery

threat_vector: Improper authentication vulnerability in Samsung Internet prior to 16.0.2.19 allows attackers to bypass secret mode password authentication
exploitation_method: Attacker exploits insufficient validation of secret mode password entry by manipulating authentication tokens, bypassing input validation checks, or exploiting race conditions in the authentication logic to gain unauthorized access to secret mode without providing correct credentials
detection_pattern: Monitor for: multiple failed authentication attempts followed by successful secret mode access without valid credentials, authentication bypass attempts in Samsung Internet logs, unusual process behavior accessing secret mode protected data, authentication token manipulation attempts, rapid sequential access requests to secret mode functionality, network traffic showing authentication bypass payloads
mitigation_strategy: Update Samsung Internet to version 16.0.2.19 or later; implement proper input validation and cryptographic verification for secret mode authentication; enforce rate limiting on authentication attempts; use secure token generation and validation mechanisms; implement certificate pinning; enable integrity checks on authentication parameters; conduct security testing of authentication flows; monitor and alert on authentication anomalies

threat_vector: CSRF vulnerability in Vehicle Service Management System 1.0 leading to Stored XSS
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious HTML/JavaScript on external site that triggers unauthorized state-changing requests (e.g., service record modification) to victim's authenticated session. If the application reflects user input into stored fields without sanitization, the attacker can inject malicious scripts that execute in other users' browsers when they view the compromised service records.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) POST/PUT/DELETE requests lacking CSRF tokens or with invalid tokens in logs; (2) requests originating from different referer/origin headers; (3) unusual service record modifications with script tags (<script>, onerror=, onclick=) in fields like service_notes, description, or customer_comments; (4) storage of HTML/JavaScript entities in database fields that should contain plain text; (5) repeated modifications to same records from different user agents/IPs within short timeframe
mitigation_strategy: Implement CSRF tokens (SameSite cookies set to 'Strict' or 'Lax', synchronized token pattern, double-submit cookies); validate Origin and Referer headers on state-changing requests; sanitize and validate all user inputs before storage using allowlist-based HTML filtering; apply Content Security Policy (CSP) headers; encode output contextually when rendering service records; implement input validation requiring only expected data types; conduct parameterized storage and retrieval of service data; perform security code review of service record update functions

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1069.003 Permission Groups Discovery
exploitation_method: Attacker executes commands like 'net group', 'Get-ADGroup', 'id', or 'groups' to enumerate Active Directory groups, local groups, and permission structures to identify privileged accounts and access paths for lateral movement
detection_pattern: Monitor for: repeated execution of group enumeration commands (net group /domain, Get-ADGroup, dsquery group); LDAP queries with GroupMember filters; 4798 events (user enumeration); 4799 events (security group enumeration); Kerberos TGT requests for group-related SPN; command-line arguments containing 'group', 'member', or 'description' in process logs
mitigation_strategy: Implement command-line auditing and block/alert on enumeration tools; restrict LDAP query permissions with Group Policy; disable legacy enumeration commands where possible; use Windows Defender Attack Surface Reduction rules; segment network to limit lateral movement; enforce principle of least privilege so compromised accounts have minimal group memberships; monitor and restrict PowerShell execution with constrained language mode

threat_vector: Missing Authorization in Daybyday CRM v2.0.0-2.2.0 allows low-privilege employee accounts to view all users' appointments
exploitation_method: Attacker with employee-level credentials bypasses authorization checks in appointment retrieval endpoints. The application fails to validate that the requesting user has permission to access appointments belonging to other users, allowing direct API calls or UI manipulation to retrieve appointment data across the organization.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Appointment API requests from single user account accessing unusually high volume of distinct user IDs; (2) Repeated 200-status responses for GET /appointments endpoints with user_id parameters not matching authenticated session owner; (3) Rapid sequential requests to appointment endpoints for different manager/colleague IDs; (4) Access logs showing employee-level accounts querying appointment data for users in different departments/roles; (5) Discrepancy between user's assigned scope and accessed resources in audit logs.
mitigation_strategy: Implement role-based authorization checks: (1) Add server-side authorization middleware validating that authenticated user_id matches requested appointment owner or has explicit manager/admin role; (2) Enforce attribute-based access control (ABAC) checking department/team membership before appointment visibility; (3) Remove user_id as client-supplied parameter—derive from session token only; (4) Add authorization assertions in appointment retrieval queries; (5) Upgrade to patched version >2.2.0; (6) Conduct authorization audit of all data access endpoints; (7) Implement request logging and alerting for cross-user data access attempts.

threat_vector: Dnsmasq 2.86 heap-based buffer overflow in check_bad_address function
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious DNS queries with specially formatted domain names containing bogus wildcard entries that exceed buffer boundaries in check_bad_address function, leading to heap memory corruption and potential code execution
detection_pattern: Monitor for: dnsmasq process crashes or segmentation faults; heap corruption errors in system logs; unusual memory access patterns; DNS queries with excessively long labels or malformed wildcard entries; dnsmasq error messages indicating buffer issues; unexpected dnsmasq service restarts; abnormal CPU spikes during DNS resolution
mitigation_strategy: Immediately upgrade dnsmasq to version 2.87 or later; implement strict input validation on DNS queries at firewall level to reject malformed domain names; configure dnsmasq with address space layout randomization (ASLR) and stack canaries enabled; run dnsmasq in containerized/sandboxed environment with restricted capabilities; monitor dnsmasq process with watchdog to auto-restart on crashes; implement rate limiting on DNS queries from suspicious sources

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1021.004 - Remote Services: SSH
exploitation_method: Attacker uses SSH protocol to gain unauthorized remote access through weak credentials, unpatched vulnerabilities, or compromised keys. Common methods include brute-force attacks against default accounts, exploitation of SSH service misconfigurations, or lateral movement using stolen SSH private keys.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: repeated failed SSH authentication attempts from single source (threshold >5 in 5min), successful logins from unusual geographic locations or IPs, SSH connections outside normal business hours, use of non-standard SSH ports, failed key authentication attempts, privilege escalation commands post-login, or unusual SSH process spawning (sshd spawning shells without normal user interaction). Review /var/log/auth.log, /var/log/secure, failed login attempts, and process execution logs.
mitigation_strategy: Disable password authentication and require SSH key pairs; implement multi-factor authentication; enforce strong SSH key policies (4096-bit RSA minimum); run SSH on non-standard ports; implement rate limiting on connection attempts; restrict SSH access via firewall/ACLs to known admin IPs; disable root login; disable empty passwords; keep SSH daemon patched; implement intrusion detection/prevention for SSH; conduct regular security audits of SSH configurations and access logs.

threat_vector: ATT&CK: LC_LOAD_DYLIB Addition
exploitation_method: Attacker modifies Mach-O binary headers to inject LC_LOAD_DYLIB load commands, forcing the dynamic loader to load malicious dylib files at runtime before legitimate code executes, enabling code execution and persistence on macOS systems.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unusual LC_LOAD_DYLIB commands in binary analysis tools; process execution with unexpected dylib dependencies; fsnotify alerts on modifications to /usr/lib, /opt, or application bundles; code signature validation failures; otool/MachOView binary inspection anomalies; dyld_print_libraries environment variable logs showing unexpected libraries; Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) telemetry showing library load events from non-standard paths; File Integrity Monitoring alerts on executable modification timestamps.
mitigation_strategy: Implement System Integrity Protection (SIP) enforcement on macOS; enable Code Signing requirement verification with codesign validation; deploy Binary Authorization policies preventing execution of unsigned/modified binaries; use Application Allow-listing software; implement dylib path hardening via RPATH restrictions; enable DYLD_INSERT_LIBRARIES blocklist policies; conduct regular code signing audits; deploy runtime integrity monitoring; restrict write permissions on system and application directories; use EDR solutions with dylib loading telemetry.

threat_vector: TIBCO eFTL Server Remote Code Execution via Unsafe Deserialization
exploitation_method: Attacker sends crafted serialized Java objects to the eFTL Server network listener, bypassing input validation. The server deserializes untrusted data without proper checks, allowing instantiation of arbitrary classes that execute code during object construction or method invocation.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unexpected incoming connections to eFTL default ports (8080, 8081); (2) Large or malformed serialized object payloads in network traffic; (3) eFTL server process spawning unexpected child processes or system commands; (4) Exception stack traces in eFTL logs containing 'ClassNotFoundException' or 'NoSuchMethodException' paired with unusual class names; (5) Abnormal memory usage spikes or CPU consumption during deserialization; (6) Failed authentication attempts followed by RCE-like activity patterns.
mitigation_strategy: Apply TIBCO security patches immediately (upgrade to patched versions). Implement network segmentation restricting eFTL Server access to trusted hosts only. Disable remote deserialization if not required. Deploy Web Application Firewall rules to inspect eFTL protocol traffic and block suspicious serialized payloads. Enable eFTL authentication and use TLS/SSL encryption. Monitor process execution and network connections from eFTL service account. Implement runtime application self-protection (RASP) to detect and block deserialization attacks. Maintain audit logging of all eFTL connections and failed operations.

threat_vector: Reflected XSS in Teedy v1.5-v1.9 search functionality - unsanitized search terms reflected in results page
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious URL with JavaScript payload in search parameter (e.g., ?q=<script>alert('XSS')</script>). When victim clicks link or visits URL, unfiltered search term is reflected in HTML response, executing attacker's script in victim's browser with their session credentials.
detection_pattern: Monitor HTTP logs for: (1) search parameters containing script tags, event handlers (onclick, onerror), or HTML entities; (2) response pages containing unescaped user input from search parameters; (3) browser console errors or network requests to external domains from search results pages; (4) application logs showing search queries with script syntax; (5) WAF alerts for script injection attempts in query strings
mitigation_strategy: Implement output encoding: HTML-escape all user-supplied search terms before rendering in results page using framework-provided escaping functions (e.g., OWASP ESAPI). Apply input validation to reject or sanitize search parameters containing script tags and event handlers. Deploy Content Security Policy (CSP) headers to restrict inline script execution. Upgrade to Teedy v1.10+ which patches this vulnerability. Implement WAF rules to block common XSS payloads in search parameters.

threat_vector: Memory corruption in vow driver due to improper input validation leading to local privilege escalation
exploitation_method: Attacker submits crafted input to vow driver without proper bounds checking, causing buffer overflow or heap corruption. This overwrites memory structures to elevate process privileges to SYSTEM level.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: abnormal vow driver ioctl calls with oversized buffers, stack canary violations, unexpected SYSTEM privilege transitions from user-mode processes, vow driver crash dumps with memory corruption signatures, SELinux/AppArmor denials related to vow driver access, suspicious memory allocation patterns preceding privilege escalation
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict input validation with length checks before processing vow driver inputs, use Address Sanitizer (ASan) and Memory Sanitizer in driver testing, enable Control Flow Guard (CFG) and Data Execution Prevention (DEP), apply bounds checking on all buffer operations, restrict vow driver ioctl access via SELinux/AppArmor policies, keep driver firmware updated, disable vow driver if not required, monitor and log all vow driver operations

threat_vector: Rogue backend domains can trigger denial of service attacks against guest VMs through high-frequency event generation in Xen hypervisor environments
exploitation_method: A malicious or compromised backend domain (Dom0 or other privileged domain) floods guest domains with high-frequency events via shared event channels, causing excessive CPU consumption, interrupt handling overhead, and eventual guest unresponsiveness or crash
detection_pattern: Monitor for: excessive event channel activity spike from backend domains, abnormal interrupt counts in guest domain performance counters, CPU usage surges coinciding with event channel traffic, guest VM latency increases, event delivery rates exceeding baseline thresholds by 10x+, kernel logs showing 'event_handle' processing delays
mitigation_strategy: Implement event channel rate limiting per backend domain connection, configure event delivery quotas and backpressure mechanisms, isolate untrusted backend domains with restricted capabilities, enable event coalescing to reduce interrupt overhead, monitor and alert on anomalous event rates, apply Xen security patches addressing event channel handling, use IOMMU isolation to prevent DMA-based event generation, implement watchdog timers to detect unresponsive guests and trigger isolation

threat_vector: Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) Abuse
exploitation_method: Attacker uses WMI classes (Win32_Process, Win32_Service) via wmic.exe or PowerShell to execute arbitrary code, create persistence mechanisms, or perform lateral movement without writing files to disk. Leverages legitimate Windows management interfaces to bypass application whitelisting.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: wmic.exe or powershell.exe spawning child processes with WMI namespaces; Event ID 3 (WMI Event Subscription creation) in WmiTracing logs; Win32_ProcessStartTrace events with suspicious command lines; parent process mismatch (explorer.exe spawning wmic); CommandLine containing 'StdRegProv', 'Win32_Process', 'Create', or 'ExecMethod'; unusual WMI repository access patterns; rapid successive WMI queries from single process.
mitigation_strategy: Disable WMI if not required; restrict WMI namespace access via Group Policy; implement Application Control policies to restrict wmic.exe and PowerShell execution; enable PowerShell Script Block Logging and Transcription; monitor and alert on WMI Event Subscription creation (Event ID 3); use SIEM to correlate WMI activity with suspicious process creation; configure Windows Defender Attack Surface Reduction to block wmic.exe calls from Office/scripts; implement network segmentation to limit lateral movement post-WMI exploitation.

threat_vector: Compromise Software Dependencies and Development Tools (ATT&CK T1195)
exploitation_method: Attacker injects malicious code into legitimate software packages, development libraries, or build tools used by target organizations. Common methods include: typosquatting package names, compromising maintainer accounts, submitting malicious pull requests, or exploiting insecure package repositories. Compromised dependencies are then automatically pulled into downstream applications during builds.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unexpected changes in package repository metadata or file hashes, suspicious package version jumps, new maintainers on previously stable projects, failed cryptographic signature verification, unusual outbound connections from build systems to external IPs, unexpected additions to dependency manifests (package.json, requirements.txt, pom.xml), build process anomalies, and integrity mismatches between expected and deployed binaries. Alert on packages with unusual download spikes or newly registered similar-named packages.
mitigation_strategy: Implement software bill of materials (SBOM) tracking, use dependency pinning with verified hash validation, enforce code signing verification for all packages, establish internal package proxies with content filtering, conduct regular dependency audits using SAST/composition analysis tools (Snyk, Dependabot, WhiteSource), require multi-factor authentication for maintainer accounts, isolate build environments with network segmentation, maintain approved package whitelists, implement runtime application self-protection (RASP), and conduct periodic supply chain security assessments of critical dependencies.

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Socket Filters (T1547.015)
exploitation_method: Attacker loads malicious socket filter kernel extensions (Windows Winsock Kernel-mode Service Provider Interface filters or Linux netfilter modules) to intercept, monitor, or modify network traffic at the kernel level, persisting across reboots
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unexpected kernel module/driver loading via auditctl, dmesg errors; Winsock registry modifications under HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Services\Winsock2\Parameters\Protocol_Catalog9; lsmod output showing suspicious network-related modules; unsigned kernel drivers; unexpected LSP (Layered Service Provider) installations in Windows socket stack
mitigation_strategy: Enforce kernel module signing verification; disable unsigned kernel module loading via secure boot and kernel parameter enforcement; implement driver signature enforcement (DSE) on Windows; audit and whitelist approved socket providers; use file integrity monitoring (FIM) on socket filter configuration files; implement kernel module allowlisting via LSM (Linux) or Device Guard (Windows); restrict driver installation permissions to administrator with MFA

threat_vector: Kentico Xperience 13.0.44 Media Libraries XML-based Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
exploitation_method: Attacker uploads a malicious XML document to the Media Libraries subsystem containing embedded JavaScript payloads in XML attributes or text nodes. When the XML is parsed and rendered in the admin interface or published content without proper sanitization, the script executes in the context of authenticated users' browsers.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: XML files uploaded to Media Libraries containing script tags or event handlers (onload, onerror, onclick); HTTP requests to media library endpoints with XML payloads containing special characters (<, >, &, quotes); admin interface console errors referencing media library components; unusual JavaScript execution originating from media library resources in browser developer tools; WAF/IDS alerts for XML External Entity (XXE) or XSS signatures in upload requests
mitigation_strategy: 1) Upgrade Kentico Xperience to version 13.0.45 or later with XSS sanitization patches; 2) Implement strict XML schema validation for all uploaded documents; 3) Configure Content Security Policy (CSP) headers to restrict script execution sources; 4) Apply output encoding/escaping to all XML-derived content rendered in UI; 5) Disable XML external entity processing; 6) Restrict Media Libraries upload permissions to trusted administrators only; 7) Implement file type whitelisting and validate MIME types; 8) Perform regular security audits of custom media library extensions

threat_vector: Unnecessary privilege/impersonation vulnerability in Trend Micro Apex One and Worry-Free Business Security 10.0 SP1 allowing local privilege escalation
exploitation_method: Local attacker abuses overly-permissive impersonation privileges in Trend Micro security agent processes to execute arbitrary code with elevated system or service account privileges, bypassing intended access controls
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unexpected child processes spawned from Trend Micro agent processes (tmsd.exe, TiWorker.exe, etc.); unusual token impersonation attempts in Windows Security logs (Event ID 4616, 4625); elevation of privilege via security service accounts; suspicious registry modifications under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet; file creation/modification in system directories by non-system accounts; process creation with system-level privileges originating from user sessions
mitigation_strategy: Apply Trend Micro security patches immediately; restrict local administrative access; implement application whitelisting for Trend Micro agent processes; enable Windows Privileged Access Management (PAM); configure Windows Defender for PUA protection; monitor and restrict service account privileges to principle of least privilege; enable Windows Audit Policy for privilege use and process creation; segment administrative access; disable impersonation features if not required

threat_vector: Mortgage Calculator / Loan Calculator WordPress plugin < 1.5.17 - Unauthenticated Stored XSS via mlcalc shortcode attributes
exploitation_method: Attacker with contributor or higher role injects malicious JavaScript into mlcalc shortcode attributes (e.g., button_text, result_text) that are not properly sanitized or escaped before rendering in page output. XSS payload executes in browsers of all site visitors.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Posts/pages containing mlcalc shortcodes with suspicious attribute values containing script tags or event handlers; (2) Web application firewall alerts on mlcalc parameters with encoded/obfuscated JS; (3) Admin audit logs showing users with contributor+ role editing posts with unusual shortcode content; (4) Frontend DOM anomalies in pages with mlcalc shortcodes; (5) Browser console errors from unexpected script execution
mitigation_strategy: Immediately update plugin to version 1.5.17+. Implement input validation/sanitization on all shortcode attributes using WordPress sanitize_text_field() and wp_kses_post(). Use wp_kses_allowed_html() to define whitelist of allowed HTML tags. Apply output escaping with esc_attr() and esc_html() on all shortcode attribute rendering. Restrict contributor role capabilities if not needed. Consider Content Security Policy headers to prevent inline script execution as defense-in-depth.

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1547.014 - Active Setup
exploitation_method: Attacker modifies Active Setup registry keys (HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Active Setup\Installed Components) to execute malicious code during user logon.
