The RunOnce or Run values execute arbitrary commands with user privileges when new users log in or existing users first run applications.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unusual modifications to HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Active Setup\Installed Components registry keys, especially new GUIDs with RunOnce/Run values; (2) Sysmon Event ID 13 (Registry Set Value) targeting Active Setup paths; (3) Unexpected processes spawned during logon with parent process explorer.exe lacking corresponding user action; (4) Commands referencing cmd.exe, powershell.exe, or script interpreters in Active Setup registry values; (5) Timestamp mismatches between registry modification and legitimate software installations.
mitigation_strategy: Implement: (1) Registry monitoring and alerting via Sysmon or Windows Event Forwarding for Active Setup key modifications; (2) Application whitelisting to restrict code execution during logon sequences; (3) Group Policy restrictions on registry editing permissions for Active Setup hive; (4) Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) tuning to detect suspicious child processes from explorer.exe during logon; (5) Regular registry audits and baselining of legitimate Active Setup entries; (6) Disable Active Setup execution via Group Policy if not required in environment; (7) Implement code signing requirements and monitor for unsigned or invalid certificates in Auto-start locations.

threat_vector: Huawei IDAP module Out-of-bounds Read vulnerability leading to Denial of Service
exploitation_method: Attacker sends specially crafted packets or requests to the IDAP module that trigger an out-of-bounds memory read operation. This causes the process to access memory locations beyond allocated buffer boundaries, leading to application crash or system instability.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: sudden IDAP process crashes or restarts, segmentation faults in IDAP logs, unexpected memory access violations, abnormal packet patterns targeting IDAP service ports, core dumps generated by IDAP module, repeated connection attempts with malformed payloads, system log entries showing 'bounds check failure' or memory protection violations
mitigation_strategy: Apply Huawei security patches and firmware updates addressing the IDAP OOB read vulnerability immediately. Implement network segmentation to restrict IDAP service access. Deploy input validation and bounds checking in network request handlers. Enable Address Space Layout Randomization (ASLR) and Data Execution Prevention (DEP). Monitor IDAP process behavior with endpoint detection and response tools. Configure rate limiting on IDAP service ports. Maintain backups for rapid recovery from DoS incidents.

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Linux and Mac Permissions (T1548.001)
exploitation_method: Attacker modifies file permissions using chmod/chown commands to escalate privileges, grant unauthorized access to sensitive files, or modify system binaries. May also abuse sudo capabilities, SUID bits, or capability sets to bypass access controls.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unexpected chmod/chown commands on system binaries or sensitive files, changes to file permissions on /etc/sudoers or /etc/passwd, SUID bit modifications detected via find commands, excessive permission changes in audit logs (ausearch), setcap or getcap command execution, sudo -l enumeration, privilege attribute changes in syslog or auditd records
mitigation_strategy: Implement file integrity monitoring (auditd, osquery, Tripwire) to detect unauthorized permission changes. Use immutable flags (chattr +i) on critical system files. Restrict sudo privileges with principle of least privilege. Employ mandatory access control (AppArmor, SELinux). Monitor and restrict use of setcap/getcap. Regularly audit SUID binaries and remove unnecessary ones. Enable auditd rules for chmod/chown on sensitive paths. Use configuration management to enforce correct permissions.

threat_vector: shelljs Improper Privilege Management vulnerability allows code execution with unintended privilege escalation
exploitation_method: Attacker injects malicious commands into shelljs functions (shell.exec, shell.sed, etc.) that execute with the privilege level of the Node.js process, bypassing intended privilege restrictions when shelljs is used in privileged contexts
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unexpected child process spawning from Node.js with elevated privileges, shelljs API calls with unsanitized user input in process logs, abnormal file system modifications outside expected application directories, uid/gid changes in process execution traces, system call auditing showing privilege escalation attempts from shelljs-spawned processes
mitigation_strategy: Update shelljs to patched version; implement input validation and sanitization for all shelljs function parameters; run Node.js processes with minimal required privileges using least-privilege principle; use security contexts or containers to restrict process capabilities; replace shelljs with safer alternatives like child_process with explicit argument arrays; implement Content Security Policy and code review for all shell command construction; monitor and restrict child process execution via AppArmor/SELinux profiles

threat_vector: TIBCO eFTL Server Remote Code Execution via Unsafe Deserialization
exploitation_method: Attacker sends specially crafted serialized Java objects to the eFTL Server's network interface, bypassing input validation. The server deserializes untrusted data without proper sanitization, allowing instantiation of arbitrary classes that execute code during object construction or method invocation.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unusual incoming network connections to eFTL Server ports with serialized Java magic bytes (0xACED); (2) eFTL server logs showing deserialization errors or ClassNotFoundException for unexpected classes; (3) Process execution spawned by eFTL JVM process; (4) Unexpected outbound connections from eFTL server; (5) Java heap dumps or memory anomalies; (6) Failed authentication attempts followed by serialized object transmission.
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade to patched TIBCO eFTL versions; implement network segmentation restricting eFTL server access to trusted networks; disable remote object deserialization if not required; configure Java deserialization filters (JEP 290) to whitelist known-safe classes only; monitor and log all deserialization activities; implement WAF/IDS rules detecting serialized Java payloads; run eFTL with minimal privileges; regularly audit and update dependency libraries to patch gadget chain vulnerabilities.

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1053: Scheduled Task/Job
exploitation_method: Attacker creates or modifies scheduled tasks (Windows Task Scheduler, cron jobs, or at commands) to execute malicious payloads at predetermined times or intervals, enabling persistence and privilege escalation without user interaction
detection_pattern: Monitor for: schtasks.exe or at.exe process execution with /create or /change parameters; new entries in Windows Task Scheduler XML files (C:\Windows\System32\Tasks\); cron job modifications in /etc/cron.* or crontab files; suspicious task names mimicking legitimate services; tasks with SYSTEM privileges executing unusual binaries; Event ID 106 (Task Registered) and 140 (Task Updated) in Windows Event Viewer; parent process anomalies (cmd.exe, powershell.exe creating tasks)
mitigation_strategy: Restrict scheduled task creation via Group Policy (disable Task Scheduler for non-admin users); monitor and alert on all task creation/modification events; implement application whitelisting for scheduled task executables; enforce MFA and strong credentials to prevent unauthorized administrative access; regularly audit existing scheduled tasks for unauthorized entries; use endpoint detection and response (EDR) tools to flag suspicious task execution patterns; maintain immutable audit logs; disable unnecessary task scheduler services on non-server systems

threat_vector: Stack-based Buffer Overflow in HDF5 1.13.1-1 H5D__create_chunk_file_map_hyper function
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malformed HDF5 file with specially constructed dataset chunk parameters that cause excessive stack allocation in H5D__create_chunk_file_map_hyper, triggering stack overflow and process crash
detection_pattern: Monitor for: HDF5 library segmentation faults or stack overflow exceptions during file parsing; abnormal stack memory usage spikes in HDF5 processes; application crashes immediately after HDF5 file operations; core dumps referencing H5Dchunk.c line regions; ASAN/valgrind stack-buffer-overflow reports in H5D__create_chunk_file_map_hyper
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade HDF5 to patched version ≥1.13.2 or apply vendor security patches; implement file input validation to reject HDF5 files with suspicious chunk dimension parameters; run HDF5 processing in sandboxed environments with stack size limits; disable HDF5 processing for untrusted external files; implement strict RLIMIT_STACK resource limits; use compiler protections (-fstack-protector-all, -D_FORTIFY_SOURCE=2)

threat_vector: Missing Authorization in Daybyday CRM v2.0.0-2.2.0 allows low-privilege employee accounts to view all users' absence records without proper access controls
exploitation_method: Attacker with employee-level credentials directly accesses absence endpoints or API calls that fail to validate authorization checks, allowing enumeration and viewing of absence data for all users regardless of role or department
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Repeated absence API requests from single employee account accessing records outside their department/scope; (2) Absence queries returning user IDs not assigned to requestor's team; (3) Unusual sequential API calls to /api/absences endpoints with different user_id parameters; (4) Access logs showing low-privilege role accessing admin-level absence views; (5) Absence data exports from employee accounts containing organization-wide records
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade to patched version >2.2.0; implement server-side authorization checks on all absence endpoints verifying user role and department scope before returning data; enforce principle of least privilege - ensure employee accounts only access own absence records and those of direct reports if applicable; add RBAC layer validating user_id parameter against requestor's authorized scope; audit existing absence access logs for unauthorized viewing; implement API rate limiting on absence endpoints per user

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Indicator Removal from Tools (T1070.005)
exploitation_method: Attacker modifies or deletes log files, event records, and audit trails from security tools and system utilities to remove evidence of compromise. This includes clearing Windows Event Logs, syslog entries, command history, and disabling logging mechanisms.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: sudden gaps in log timestamps, missing event log sequences, deletion of .history or .bash_history files, execution of 'wevtutil.exe clear-log' or 'rm -rf /var/log/*', disabled audit policies via auditpol commands, truncated or zero-sized log files, file deletion events in audit logs, disabled logging services, and unusual privileged access to log directories.
mitigation_strategy: Implement immutable logging with centralized log aggregation (SIEM) to forward logs to remote server before local deletion. Enable write-once storage for critical logs. Configure log retention policies with legal holds. Monitor log management tools and audit log modifications. Implement file integrity monitoring on log directories. Use OS-level protections like SELinux/AppArmor to restrict log access. Enable redundant audit trails and configure alerts for logging service disruptions.

threat_vector: Unauthenticated arbitrary file download via execute restore src-vis command in FortiOS <7.0.3
exploitation_method: Attacker with local authenticated access executes 'execute restore src-vis' command without proper integrity validation, allowing download of arbitrary files from the filesystem by bypassing file type and path restrictions
detection_pattern: Monitor for: execute restore src-vis commands with unusual file paths in CLI logs; file downloads with mismatched checksums or integrity hashes; access to sensitive paths (/etc, /var) via restore commands; syslog entries showing 'restore' operations with non-standard source URIs; unexpected file write operations following restore commands
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade FortiOS to version 7.0.3 or later; implement CLI access controls restricting 'execute restore' commands to authorized administrators only; enable audit logging for all restore operations; validate and whitelist acceptable restore file sources; implement file integrity monitoring on critical system directories; disable unnecessary restore functionality if not required

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1036.004 - Masquerading: Match Legitimate Name or Location (PubPrn Print Spooler Abuse)
exploitation_method: Attacker exploits the Windows Print Spooler service (spoolsv.exe) to execute arbitrary code or escalate privileges by injecting malicious DLLs into print driver processes, or abusing legitimate printing mechanisms to load unsigned drivers that execute attacker code
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unusual spoolsv.exe child processes, unsigned driver loading, print spooler service crashes/restarts, Registry modifications to HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Print\Monitors, unexpected network connections from spoolsv.exe, DLL injection into print-related processes, Event ID 13 (Registry modification) targeting print driver paths
mitigation_strategy: Disable Print Spooler service on systems that don't require printing; enforce Driver Signature Enforcement; deploy Windows Defender Application Guard; patch CVE-2021-1732 and related spooler vulnerabilities; restrict driver installation via Group Policy (Device installation restrictions); monitor and audit print driver installations; implement privileged access management for spooler-related operations; use AppLocker/WDAC to block unauthorized driver loading

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Exploitation for Defense Impairment
exploitation_method: Attacker identifies and exploits vulnerabilities in security tools, EDR agents, firewall systems, or logging mechanisms to disable, bypass, or degrade their functionality. This may involve exploiting memory corruption flaws in security software, privilege escalation to tamper with security services, or crafting inputs that cause security tools to crash or enter unstable states.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unexpected termination or crashes of security services (EDR, antivirus, firewall processes), sudden gaps in log collection or event forwarding, disabled or stopped security services without authorization, suspicious process injection into security tool processes, elevated privilege access to security configuration files, modification timestamps on security binary files, and alerts from security tools immediately before service interruptions.
mitigation_strategy: Implement defense-in-depth with redundant security layers independent of single tools; enable process protection and early-launch anti-malware (ELAM) on Windows; restrict administrative privileges and monitor privilege elevation attempts; deploy immutable logging to separate systems; use code integrity/memory protection features (DEP, ASLR); keep all security software and underlying OS fully patched; deploy behavior monitoring independent from signature-based detection; configure alerting on security service state changes; maintain air-gapped backup security tooling for critical systems.

threat_vector: Apache Pluto UrlTestPortlet input field XSS vulnerability in versions prior to 3.1.1
exploitation_method: Attacker injects malicious JavaScript code into unvalidated input fields of the UrlTestPortlet component. The payload is reflected in HTTP responses without proper HTML encoding, executing in the victim's browser context with access to session cookies and portlet data.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) HTTP requests to UrlTestPortlet endpoints containing script tags, event handlers (onerror, onload), or encoded JavaScript in query/form parameters; (2) Responses from affected portlet versions containing unescaped user input in HTML context; (3) Browser console errors from injected scripts; (4) Unusual DOM modifications or fetch requests originating from portlet pages; (5) WAF alerts for common XSS payloads matching v3-demo-portlet.war artifact versions <3.1.1
mitigation_strategy: Immediately upgrade Apache Pluto to version 3.1.1 or later containing v3-demo-portlet.war artifact patched build. Implement input validation (whitelist allowed characters for each field), output encoding (HTML entity encoding for all user-controlled data in HTML context), Content Security Policy (CSP) headers restricting inline script execution, and httpOnly/Secure cookie flags. Deploy WAF rules to block known XSS patterns. Conduct security review of custom portlet implementations using Pluto framework to ensure consistent input sanitization.

threat_vector: Safe Mode Boot (T1562.009)
exploitation_method: Attacker modifies boot configuration or uses legitimate Windows tools (msconfig, bcdedit, or physical access) to restart system into Safe Mode with minimal drivers and security services loaded, bypassing endpoint protection software and security monitoring that may not run in Safe Mode
detection_pattern: Monitor for: WMI EventID 12 (ConfigurationManager) boot option changes, Registry modifications to HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\SafeBoot, bcdedit command execution with safeboot parameters, unexpected system restarts with Safe Mode indicators in System Event Log (EventID 6009 with 'Safe Mode' string), process execution anomalies showing security tools not running, absence of normal antivirus/EDR process heartbeats
mitigation_strategy: Implement Secure Boot with UEFI firmware validation, enable BitLocker with TPM requirement to prevent unauthorized boot modifications, configure Group Policy to restrict msconfig and bcdedit access (gp edit, remove Run access), disable unnecessary services in Safe Mode boot registry, enforce BIOS/UEFI password protection, monitor boot configuration changes via Windows Defender Advanced Threat Protection or EDR with pre-boot visibility, implement hardware attestation checks on startup

threat_vector: Unpatched SIPROTEC 5 6MD85/6MD86/6MD89 devices with CPU variant CP300 running firmware versions below V8.83
exploitation_method: Attackers target known vulnerabilities in outdated firmware versions by sending specially crafted network packets or commands to the device management interfaces, potentially gaining unauthorized access, executing arbitrary code, or causing denial of service
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) SIPROTEC devices with firmware versions < V8.83 via SNMP or web interface querying; (2) Unexpected network connections to port 102 (IEC 60870-5-104) or port 80/443 (management interface); (3) Unusual IEC protocol commands or malformed packets; (4) Failed authentication attempts to device management; (5) Privilege escalation attempts; (6) System log entries showing firmware integrity warnings or configuration changes from unauthorized sources
mitigation_strategy: Immediately: (1) Upgrade SIPROTEC 5 6MD85, 6MD86, 6MD89 devices to firmware version V8.83 or later; (2) Isolate affected devices on restricted network segments with firewall rules limiting access to authorized maintenance stations only; (3) Disable unused management interfaces (HTTP/HTTPS if not required); (4) Implement network-based IDS/IPS signatures for known exploit patterns; (5) Require strong authentication and disable default credentials; (6) Conduct regular firmware integrity verification; (7) Enable comprehensive logging and monitoring of device access and configuration changes

threat_vector: HwNearbyMain module Data Processing Errors vulnerability causing process restart
exploitation_method: Attacker sends malformed data packets or triggers unexpected input conditions to the HwNearbyMain module's data processing logic, causing unhandled exceptions or memory corruption that forces process termination and restart
detection_pattern: Monitor for: repeated HwNearbyMain process crashes in system logs, unexpected process restart patterns in logcat, segmentation faults or unhandled exception errors associated with HwNearbyMain, abnormal nearby communication protocol frames in network traffic analysis, repeated system_error or ANR (Application Not Responding) events tied to nearby services
mitigation_strategy: Apply security patches for HwNearbyMain module, implement input validation and sanitization for all data processing functions, add exception handling and bounds checking around data parsing routines, disable or restrict nearby services if not needed, monitor process stability metrics, implement watchdog timeout recovery mechanisms with safe state restoration, and disable network-accessible data processing endpoints if not essential

threat_vector: Booster for WooCommerce plugin < 5.4.9 - Unsanitized wcj_notice parameter XSS in admin dashboard (Pdf Invoicing module)
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious URL with JavaScript payload in wcj_notice parameter. When admin visits the dashboard with Pdf Invoicing module enabled, the unsanitized parameter is reflected in the page output, executing arbitrary JavaScript in admin context with full site privileges.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: wcj_notice parameter in GET/POST requests with HTML/JavaScript tags (<script>, onerror=, onclick=, etc.); Admin audit logs showing unusual dashboard parameter values; WAF alerts on reflected XSS patterns in WordPress admin URLs; Database logs of modified admin notices or settings; Unexpected JavaScript execution in admin dashboard network requests.
mitigation_strategy: 1) Immediately update Booster for WooCommerce to version 5.4.9 or later. 2) Implement input sanitization using sanitize_text_field() on wcj_notice parameter server-side. 3) Apply output escaping with esc_html(), esc_attr(), or esc_js() based on context before displaying. 4) Deploy Web Application Firewall (WAF) rules to block XSS payloads in admin parameters. 5) Restrict admin dashboard access via IP whitelist or VPN. 6) Disable Pdf Invoicing module if not needed until patched. 7) Audit recent admin logs for exploitation attempts.

threat_vector: Clickjacking via iframe embedding
exploitation_method: Attacker embeds a legitimate website in a transparent iframe overlaid on a malicious page, tricking users into clicking on hidden UI elements that trigger unintended actions like account transfers, permission grants, or social engineering attacks.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: X-Frame-Options header absence in responses, Content-Security-Policy frame-ancestors violations, unusual clickthrough patterns from external referrers, iframe nesting depth anomalies, session state changes following cross-origin clicks, CSP violation reports indicating frame embedding attempts
mitigation_strategy: Implement X-Frame-Options: DENY or SAMEORIGIN header on all pages; enforce strict Content-Security-Policy with frame-ancestors 'none' or 'self'; use SameSite cookie attributes (Strict/Lax); implement frame-busting JavaScript; add user interaction verification for sensitive actions; deploy clickjacking detection tools; conduct regular security testing for framing vulnerabilities

threat_vector: Out-of-bounds read in smartphone memory
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious input or triggers specific application state that causes the vulnerable code to read memory addresses beyond allocated buffer boundaries, potentially exposing sensitive data from adjacent memory regions including encryption keys, tokens, or user data
detection_pattern: Monitor for: application crashes with segmentation faults, memory access violations in logs, unexpected data exfiltration patterns, abnormal process memory reads flagged by memory protection tools (AddressSanitizer, Valgrind), suspicious inter-process communication attempts following application errors, and memory dumps containing sensitive plaintext data
mitigation_strategy: Implement bounds checking on all array and buffer accesses; use safe memory APIs (Swift's bounds-checked collections, Kotlin's array safety); enable compile-time protections (stack canaries, ASLR, DEP/NX); conduct static code analysis targeting buffer overflows; perform regular fuzzing and memory sanitizer testing; apply security patches promptly; implement runtime memory protection using OS-level mitigations; restrict sensitive data lifetime in memory with explicit clearing after use

threat_vector: Stored XSS in Vehicle Service Management System 1.0 - Mechanic List Section
exploitation_method: Attacker injects malicious JavaScript payload into mechanic name, email, or other fields in the Mechanic List Section. Payload is stored in the database and executed in the browsers of all users viewing the mechanic list after authentication, potentially stealing session cookies, credentials, or performing unauthorized actions on behalf of victims.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unusual characters or script tags (<script>, javascript:, onerror=, onload=) in mechanic record entries; (2) Log entries showing HTML/JavaScript encoding discrepancies between stored and displayed data; (3) WAF alerts for XSS patterns in POST/PUT requests to mechanic endpoints; (4) Unexpected DOM manipulation or external requests originating from the mechanic list page in browser console logs; (5) Database query logs showing raw HTML/JS in mechanic fields
mitigation_strategy: Implement: (1) Input validation - whitelist allowed characters for mechanic names/emails, reject special characters and script tags; (2) Output encoding - use context-aware encoding (HTML entity encoding) when displaying mechanic data; (3) Content Security Policy (CSP) headers to prevent inline script execution; (4) Use templating engines with auto-escaping enabled; (5) Apply parameterized queries to prevent injection; (6) Implement regular security code reviews and automated SAST scanning for XSS; (7) Conduct penetration testing on login panel components; (8) Educate developers on secure coding practices for user input handling

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Revert Cloud Instance
exploitation_method: Attacker with cloud credentials uses cloud provider APIs (e.g., AWS EC2 CreateImage, revert snapshot, restore from backup) to revert a compromised instance to a previous snapshot or image state, erasing forensic evidence and malicious modifications while maintaining access or persistence mechanisms left in the reverted state.
detection_pattern: Monitor CloudTrail/Cloud Audit Logs for snapshot revert operations (CreateSnapshot, RevertToSnapshot, RestoreDBInstanceFromDBSnapshot), unexpected image restoration, timeline gaps between instance modification and revert actions, mismatches between running process hashes and baseline configurations, sudden loss of recent system logs, and reversions occurring during incident response windows.
mitigation_strategy: Implement immutable snapshots with version control and retention policies; enforce MFA and IP restrictions on snapshot/revert operations; enable cloud provider change logging and alerting; maintain offline backups outside the cloud account; use behavioral EDR tools to detect process anomalies post-revert; establish canary indicators of compromise that survive snapshots; implement cross-account audit aggregation to prevent log deletion; require approval workflows for revert operations.

threat_vector: Spinnaker Gate service allows arbitrary users to create and execute pipelines due to improper permission validation
exploitation_method: Attacker with network access to Spinnaker Gate endpoint bypasses authorization checks by directly invoking pipeline creation APIs (/api/v1/applications/{app}/pipelineConfigs or /api/v1/pipelines) without proper role-based access control (RBAC) validation, then executes arbitrary deployment pipelines across infrastructure
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Pipeline creation API calls from unexpected source IPs or service accounts; (2) POST requests to /api/v1/pipelines or /api/v1/applications/*/pipelineConfigs lacking authorization headers or with invalid credentials; (3) Spike in pipeline execution attempts from single user/service account; (4) Pipeline deployments targeting production environments from non-standard accounts; (5) Gate audit logs showing failed authorization checks followed by successful API calls; (6) Unusual deployment configurations containing suspicious container images or credentials
mitigation_strategy: Implement: (1) Enable and enforce RBAC in Spinnaker Gate via Spring Security or external OIDC/SAML provider; (2) Add explicit permission checks for PIPELINE_CREATE and PIPELINE_EXECUTE operations; (3) Require authentication for all Gate API endpoints; (4) Implement API rate limiting and request validation; (5) Use network segmentation to restrict Gate access; (6) Enable comprehensive audit logging with immutable log storage; (7) Apply principle of least privilege to service accounts; (8) Patch to latest Spinnaker version with permission fixes

threat_vector: containerd SELinux policy bypass on EL8 systems (CVE-2021-21334)
exploitation_method: Attacker creates a container with a custom SELinux label that bypasses default confinement policies. By mounting volumes or executing processes with crafted labels, the container escapes SELinux restrictions and gains unauthorized access to host resources or sibling containers, particularly when containerd versions 1.5.0-beta.0 and later are used without proper policy configuration.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) SELinux denial messages (ausearch output) showing container processes accessing resources with unexpected contexts, (2) containerd logs showing non-standard SELinux label assignments via --security-opt label=..., (3) containers with duplicate or conflicting SELinux contexts, (4) unexpected transitions between container_t and unconfined_t contexts, (5) host file access from container processes with weaker-than-expected SELinux restrictions
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade containerd to version 1.5.11, 1.6.4, or later that includes SELinux policy fixes. Enforce strict SELinux policy (enforcing mode, not permissive). Implement Pod Security Standards or OCI runtime hooks to prevent custom SELinux label assignment. Use SELinux policy templates that define explicit container contexts. Regularly audit SELinux denial logs with tools like audit2why. Restrict container creation permissions to trusted users only.

threat_vector: MediaWiki information disclosure vulnerability allowing unprivileged users to view confidential information including IP addresses and User-Agent headers (CVE-2021-35197)
exploitation_method: Attacker with unprivileged MediaWiki account exploits insufficient access controls in revision history, logs, or API endpoints to retrieve sensitive data (IP addresses, User-Agent strings, request metadata) that should be restricted to administrators. Typically leverages API calls or special page access without proper permission validation.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unprivileged user accounts accessing revision history API endpoints with sensitive parameter queries; repeated requests to Special:Log or api.php?action=query targeting revision data; access logs showing non-admin users retrieving user_text, user_ip, or http_user_agent fields; unusual API calls with format=json containing IP or User-Agent extraction patterns; database query logs showing SELECT statements on revision or logging tables from unprivileged sessions
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade MediaWiki to 1.35.5, 1.36.3, 1.37.1 or later immediately. Implement strict access controls: restrict API query parameters to admins only for sensitive user/IP data; disable public access to revision history containing IP metadata; add database-level permissions preventing unprivileged queries of ip_address and user_agent columns; audit existing logs for unauthorized access; implement WAF rules blocking API calls attempting to enumerate user metadata; enable comprehensive audit logging for all revision/user data access attempts

threat_vector: Insyde InsydeH2O SdLegacySmm SMI handler privilege escalation via untrusted input in kernel versions 5.1-5.4
exploitation_method: Attacker with local access crafts malicious SMI (System Management Interrupt) calls through SdLegacySmm handler without proper input validation, allowing arbitrary code execution in System Management Mode with elevated privileges
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unusual SMI call patterns from non-privileged processes, unexpected SMI handler invocations with suspicious parameters, kernel logs showing SMM entry anomalies, unauthorized firmware/UEFI modifications, privileged instruction execution from user-mode processes, unexpected ring transitions to SMM mode, BIOS/firmware integrity check failures
mitigation_strategy: Immediately update Insyde InsydeH2O firmware to patched versions (5.15.11+, 5.25.11+, 5.34.11+, 5.42.11+), implement SMI input validation hardening, restrict SMI handler access through firmware access controls, enable UEFI Secure Boot with verified certificates, deploy firmware integrity monitoring tools, restrict local administrative access, implement SMM call-gate validation, apply vendor security patches promptly

threat_vector: GraphQL introspection abuse
exploitation_method: Attacker sends introspection queries to GraphQL endpoint to enumerate schema, discover hidden fields, mutations, and internal API structure without authentication, enabling targeted attacks on exposed functionality
detection_pattern: High volume of introspection queries (__schema, __type queries), queries with introspection-related keywords from single IP/user, repeated schema discovery attempts, introspection queries from unexpected sources, anomalous query patterns targeting __schema or __typename fields in short timeframes
mitigation_strategy: Disable introspection in production environments via GraphQL server configuration (introspection: false); implement authentication/authorization before allowing any queries; rate limit introspection queries; monitor and alert on introspection attempt patterns; use query complexity analysis and depth limiting; implement query whitelisting for critical operations; regularly audit exposed schema and remove unnecessary fields from public API

threat_vector: Stored XSS via wcj_delete_role parameter in Booster for WooCommerce plugin (versions < 5.4.9)
exploitation_method: Attacker with admin access crafts malicious request with unsanitized wcj_delete_role parameter containing JavaScript payload. When General module is enabled, the parameter is reflected in admin dashboard without escaping, executing arbitrary JavaScript in admin context.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) HTTP requests to WooCommerce admin with wcj_delete_role parameter containing script tags or encoded JavaScript; (2) Admin dashboard logs showing unusual wcj_delete_role values with special characters or event handlers; (3) Browser console errors indicating injected scripts; (4) Plugin version detection showing Booster < 5.4.9 with General module active
mitigation_strategy: Immediately upgrade Booster for WooCommerce to version 5.4.9 or later. Additionally: (1) Implement input validation whitelist for wcj_delete_role accepting only alphanumeric role identifiers; (2) Apply output escaping using wp_kses_post() or esc_html() on all parameter displays; (3) Restrict admin access via capability checks; (4) Use Content Security Policy headers to block inline script execution; (5) Regularly audit plugin versions and maintain update schedule

threat_vector: Denial of Service vulnerability in Binaryen 104 - assertion abort in wasm::WasmBinaryBuilder::visitRethrow()
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious WebAssembly binary containing specially-formed rethrow instructions that trigger an assertion failure in the visitRethrow() function during WASM parsing/validation, causing the Binaryen compiler to abort and terminate service availability
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unexpected termination of Binaryen processes with SIGABRT signal, (2) Application crash logs containing 'visitRethrow' stack traces, (3) Unusual WASM binary submissions with malformed exception handling opcodes, (4) Process exit codes indicating assertion failures, (5) Repeated failed WASM module validations from same source
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Binaryen to version 105 or later containing the assertion fix; implement pre-validation filtering to reject WASM binaries with malformed exception handling structures; deploy resource limits on WASM processing to prevent cascading failures; add input validation to sanitize WASM binary structures before passing to Binaryen; implement exception handling in wrapper code to gracefully catch Binaryen process terminations rather than propagating service failures

threat_vector: nltk Inefficient Regular Expression Complexity (ReDoS)
exploitation_method: Attacker sends specially crafted input strings to nltk regex operations (tokenization, POS tagging, or pattern matching) that cause catastrophic backtracking, consuming excessive CPU and memory, leading to denial of service
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) CPU usage spikes correlating with nltk processing, (2) thread/process hang events during regex operations, (3) response time degradation (>30s for typically <100ms operations), (4) excessive backtracking logs in regex engine, (5) application logs showing regex timeout exceptions
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade nltk to patched versions, implement input size limits and regex timeout thresholds (e.g., 5-10s max), use compiled regex caching, replace vulnerable patterns with deterministic alternatives, deploy rate limiting on tokenization endpoints, implement separate worker processes with resource quotas for nltk operations, and conduct security testing with regex fuzzing tools

threat_vector: Race condition in HwConnectivityExService API concurrent call handling leading to denial of service
exploitation_method: Attacker rapidly invokes HwConnectivityExService APIs concurrently without proper synchronization, causing state inconsistency, null pointer dereferences, or resource exhaustion that crashes the service or entire system
detection_pattern: Monitor for: repeated HwConnectivityExService method invocations within <100ms timeframe, multiple simultaneous API calls from same/different processes, system_server crashes with stack traces referencing HwConnectivityExService, increasing memory/CPU consumption followed by out-of-memory errors, ANR (Application Not Responding) logs with HwConnectivityExService in blocked threads, logcat entries showing IllegalStateException or ConcurrentModificationException from connectivity service
mitigation_strategy: Implement synchronized access controls or ReentrantLock mechanisms for shared resources in HwConnectivityExService; add request queuing with rate limiting per caller; validate API call sequences and reject concurrent duplicate requests; implement resource pooling with bounded limits; add watchdog timers to detect hung threads; apply vendor security patches; use StrictMode to detect main thread blocking; implement input validation and state machine enforcement

threat_vector: SVG file uploaded with .html extension in Convos chat application to bypass file type restrictions and achieve stored XSS
exploitation_method: Attacker uploads SVG file containing malicious JavaScript code while renaming it with .html extension to bypass MIME type validation. When other users download or view the file in browser context, the SVG's embedded scripts execute with user privileges, potentially stealing session tokens or performing actions on behalf of the victim.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) file uploads where extension doesn't match actual MIME type detected by file analysis, (2) HTML/SVG files uploaded to chat with script tags or event handlers, (3) browser console errors from SVG execution in chat context, (4) unexpected JavaScript execution originating from uploaded file objects, (5) suspicious outbound requests from chat interface following file access
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict file type validation using magic bytes/file signatures rather than extension checking. Serve uploaded files with Content-Disposition: attachment and Content-Type: application/octet-stream to force download instead of rendering. Use Content Security Policy headers to block inline scripts. Implement sandboxed iframe rendering for preview functionality. Validate and sanitize SVG content server-side, stripping script tags and event handlers. Maintain whitelist of allowed file types and reject anything not explicitly permitted.

threat_vector: Taint Shared Content (T1080)
exploitation_method: Attacker modifies files, documents, or code stored in shared repositories (cloud storage, version control, collaborative platforms) to inject malicious payloads. Victims download and execute the compromised content, believing it to be legitimate.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unauthorized file modifications in shared repositories with timestamp anomalies, file hash mismatches against known-good baselines, suspicious commits from compromised accounts, access logs showing unusual modification patterns outside business hours, file version history showing unexpected rollbacks or deletions, integrity check failures on shared content, and user complaints about unexpected file changes.
mitigation_strategy: Implement file integrity monitoring on shared repositories, require code review and approval workflows before publishing, enforce strong access controls and MFA on collaborative platforms, maintain immutable file backups, enable detailed audit logging with retention policies, conduct regular integrity verification against cryptographic hashes, implement endpoint DLP to prevent execution of unverified shared content, and establish user training on verifying file authenticity before use.

threat_vector: Arbitrary file exposure of sensitive information to unauthorized actor
exploitation_method: Attacker gains unauthorized access to files containing sensitive data through misconfigured permissions, path traversal vulnerabilities, unprotected backup files, exposed configuration files, or insecure direct object references (IDOR) that allow reading arbitrary files without proper access controls.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unusual file access patterns outside normal workflow, repeated 404/403 errors followed by successful reads, access to system files or config directories (.env, .git, /etc/passwd equivalents), abnormal read operations on sensitive file types (*.pem, *.key, *.sql, *.backup), file access from unexpected IP ranges or user accounts, directory traversal sequences in logs (../, ..\), access to files outside application root directory, failed authentication attempts preceding file access, bulk file enumeration activities, exfiltration indicators (large data transfers after file access)
mitigation_strategy: Implement principle of least privilege with strict file permissions (remove world-readable flags on sensitive files); use access control lists (ACLs) to restrict file access by user/role; encrypt sensitive data at rest; implement input validation and canonicalization to prevent path traversal; remove sensitive files from web roots; use secure key management systems for credentials instead of file storage; implement Web Application Firewalls (WAF) to detect traversal attempts; conduct regular file permission audits; disable directory listing; implement file integrity monitoring; use secure file serving mechanisms that enforce authentication/authorization; maintain separate secure configurations separate from application code; implement audit logging for all file access events

threat_vector: ReDoS (Regular Expression Denial of Service) via malicious regex input
exploitation_method: Attacker submits crafted input strings designed to cause catastrophic backtracking in regex engines. Patterns like (a+)+b or (a|a)*b with non-matching suffixes force exponential time complexity, consuming CPU and causing application hang or crash.
detection_pattern: Spike in CPU usage correlated with specific input validation functions; regex processing time exceeding normal thresholds (>1 second for single operations); application logs showing regex timeout exceptions; monitoring regex engine performance metrics; WAF logs detecting known malicious regex patterns in user input fields
mitigation_strategy: Implement regex timeout limits using engine-specific mechanisms (e.g., Java Pattern.compile with atomic groups, Python signal alarms, or PCRE2 JIT); use language-native safe regex libraries; conduct regex security audits to identify vulnerable patterns; validate input length before regex processing; employ input sanitization and whitelisting; implement rate limiting on validation endpoints; use ReDoS-resistant alternative matching methods (string matching, finite state machines) where possible

threat_vector: eID module null pointer dereference vulnerability affecting data confidentiality
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malformed eID certificate or authentication request that triggers unvalidated pointer dereference in eID module, causing memory access violations and potential information disclosure through error messages or memory contents
detection_pattern: Monitor for: segmentation faults in eID service logs, NULL pointer exception errors, unexpected eID module crashes, authentication requests with malformed certificate structures, abnormal memory access patterns in eID process, error responses containing memory addresses or uninitialized data, repeated failed eID authentication attempts with specific payload signatures
mitigation_strategy: Implement null pointer checks and bounds validation on all eID certificate parsing and authentication request handlers; add input validation for certificate fields before dereferencing; deploy memory protection mechanisms (ASLR, stack canaries); apply latest security patches to eID module; use secure coding practices with static analysis tools; implement exception handling to gracefully manage null pointer conditions; restrict eID service privileges; monitor and log all eID authentication attempts with detailed request validation failures

threat_vector: Credentials in Registry
exploitation_method: Attacker searches Windows Registry for stored credentials, connection strings, API keys, and passwords in keys like HKLM\SOFTWARE, HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows Credentials, or application-specific registry hives. Tools like reg.exe, PowerShell, or custom scripts extract plaintext or weakly encrypted values.
detection_pattern: Monitor for reg.exe or powershell.exe querying credential-related registry paths (Credentials, CredentialManager, RAS, VPN); audit registry access logs (Event ID 4656, 4663); detect unusual registry enumeration patterns; alert on access to HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Internet Explorer\IntelliForms\Storage2 or similar sensitive hives; flag processes accessing multiple registry credential stores in short timeframes
mitigation_strategy: Restrict registry access via GPO (disable remote registry service); implement LSA protection and credential guard; restrict PowerShell execution policy and logging; disable plaintext credential storage in registry through GPO; use Windows Credential Manager or dedicated secrets management tools; audit and remove hardcoded credentials from registry; enable registry audit logging for sensitive hives; use mandatory integrity control levels to restrict registry access

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Group Policy Discovery (T1615)
exploitation_method: Attacker executes commands like 'gpresult /h report.html', 'Get-GPO', or queries LDAP to enumerate Group Policy Objects (GPOs) and their configurations. This reveals security controls, account policies, software restrictions, and domain architecture without authentication in some cases.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: PowerShell cmdlets Get-GPO, Get-GPOReport execution; gpresult.exe or gpsearch.exe process execution; LDAP queries targeting CN=Policies,CN=System,DC=* paths; WMI queries to Win32_GroupPolicyObject; event log 4662 (Directory Service Object Access); unusual ADSI connections from non-admin user contexts; command-line arguments containing 'gpo', 'policy', or LDAP filter strings.
mitigation_strategy: Restrict gpresult.exe and PowerShell Group Policy cmdlet execution via AppLocker/WDAC for non-administrative users; disable WMI access to Group Policy classes for unprivileged accounts; implement network segmentation to limit LDAP query sources; enable and monitor directory service auditing (SACL on GPO container); enforce MFA for domain admin accounts; use GPO filtering and WMI filters to limit applied policies; regularly audit Group Policy permissions and remove excessive delegations.

threat_vector: CaasKit module path traversal vulnerability leading to MeeTime application denial of service
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious file path requests using directory traversal sequences (../, ..\ , encoded variants) to access files outside intended directories, potentially modifying or deleting critical application files or configuration, causing service unavailability
detection_pattern: Log entries containing: path traversal patterns in request parameters (../, ..\ , %2e%2e, URL-encoded variants); unusual file access attempts outside application root directory; repeated 403/404 errors followed by 500 errors; modifications to critical application configuration files; unexpected process termination or crash dumps; file integrity monitoring alerts on application binaries or config files
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict input validation using allowlist approach for file paths; use canonicalization to resolve all path references and reject any containing parent directory traversals; enforce principle of least privilege for application process; implement file access controls limiting read/write to designated directories only; apply security patches for CaasKit module; deploy WAF rules to block path traversal patterns; monitor and alert on file integrity changes; implement request rate limiting; maintain automated backups of critical configuration files

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1114: Email Collection
exploitation_method: Attacker gains access to email accounts via compromised credentials, phishing, or direct server access. Uses mail client APIs, IMAP/POP3 protocols, or direct mailbox access to exfiltrate emails, contacts, and calendar data for reconnaissance or credential harvesting.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unusual IMAP/POP3 connection patterns from unfamiliar IPs, mass email folder enumeration operations, abnormal mail forwarding rules creation, sudden changes to email account recovery settings, bulk export operations via mail APIs, concurrent login attempts from multiple geographies, and emails forwarded to external accounts. Alert on sensitive terms in exported message metadata.
mitigation_strategy: Implement conditional access policies restricting mail access by location/device; enforce MFA on email accounts; deploy email DLP to block bulk exports; monitor and alert on forwarding rule changes; use mail server logs to detect anomalous protocol activity; enforce modern authentication only (disable legacy protocols); conduct regular access reviews; implement email retention policies; use CASB solutions to monitor cloud email activity.

threat_vector: Null pointer dereference in thread profile trap handler due to missing thread ID validation before dereferencing in Snapdragon platforms
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious input or triggers specific system conditions that cause an invalid or null thread ID to be passed to the trap handler, leading to kernel dereference of null pointer and potential denial of service or code execution
detection_pattern: Monitor kernel logs for: null pointer dereference exceptions, unexpected kernel panics in thread profiling subsystem, segmentation faults with stack traces pointing to trap handler code, repeated crashes in profile_trap_handler or thread context functions, anomalous system calls triggering profiling handlers with invalid thread identifiers
mitigation_strategy: Implement mandatory null checks and thread ID validation before any dereference operation in trap handler; add bounds checking for thread ID ranges; implement defensive coding with early return on validation failure; apply security patches from Qualcomm addressing CVE entries for this specific vulnerability; enable kernel Address Sanitizer (KASAN) for development/testing; implement capability-based access controls restricting who can trigger profiling traps

threat_vector: Stored XSS in Vehicle Service Management System 1.0 Service Requests Section
exploitation_method: Attacker injects malicious JavaScript payload into Service Requests input fields (description, notes, or title fields) that lack proper input validation and output encoding. The payload is persisted in the database and executed in the browser context of any user viewing that service request, allowing session hijacking, credential theft, or malware distribution.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) HTML/JavaScript tags in Service Request database entries (script, iframe, onerror, onclick attributes); (2) Suspicious characters in logs: <, >, quotes, backslashes in request parameters; (3) WAF alerts for XSS patterns; (4) Unusual JavaScript execution in service request pages via browser console; (5) Multiple requests to /service-requests with encoded payloads (%3C, %3E); (6) Admin/user reports of unexpected pop-ups or redirects when accessing service requests
mitigation_strategy: Implement: (1) Input validation - whitelist allowed characters, reject HTML/script tags on submission; (2) Output encoding - use context-aware encoding (HTML entity encoding for displayed content, JavaScript escaping for JS context); (3) Content Security Policy (CSP) headers to restrict inline script execution; (4) Use templating engines with auto-escaping enabled; (5) Sanitize user input with libraries like DOMPurify; (6) Apply security patches immediately; (7) Conduct code review of Service Requests handling logic; (8) Implement HTTP-only, Secure, SameSite cookie flags; (9) Regular security testing with OWASP Top 10 payloads

threat_vector: Apache Avro .NET SDK Resource Exhaustion DoS
exploitation_method: Attacker sends malformed or specially crafted Avro serialized messages with excessive nested structures, large array declarations, or recursive schema definitions that cause the .NET SDK parser to allocate unbounded memory and CPU resources during deserialization, leading to application crash or hang
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Sudden spikes in memory consumption by application processes handling Avro deserialization, (2) High CPU utilization during message processing without corresponding legitimate data volume, (3) Exceptions in Avro deserialization code paths in application logs, (4) Process handles and thread counts increasing abnormally, (5) Avro parser timeouts or unresponsive application instances, (6) Messages with unusually large declared sizes or deeply nested schema structures in network traffic
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Apache Avro .NET SDK to patched version; implement input validation and size limits on Avro message payloads before deserialization; configure resource quotas and timeouts for deserialization operations; use circuit breakers to reject suspicious messages with excessive complexity; monitor and limit application memory/CPU allocation; implement rate limiting on message processing; validate schema schemas against whitelist of known-safe structures; consider running Avro parsing in isolated, resource-constrained processes

threat_vector: Integer overflow in build_model function of xmlparse.c in Expat before 2.4.3
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious XML document with deeply nested elements or excessively large attribute counts that cause integer overflow in buffer size calculations during parsing, leading to heap buffer overflow and potential code execution
detection_pattern: Monitor for: segmentation faults or crashes in applications using Expat; unexpected memory allocation failures; heap corruption signals; XML parsing errors with deeply nested structures (>100 levels); abnormal process termination with core dumps; valgrind/ASAN reports of heap-buffer-overflow in build_model or related parsing functions
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Expat to version 2.4.3 or later; implement input validation to reject XML documents with excessive nesting depth (limit to 256+ levels); use memory-safe XML parsing libraries as alternatives; deploy application sandboxing; enable compiler-level protections (ASLR, DEP/NX, stack canaries); monitor library versions in dependency management systems and apply security patches immediately

threat_vector: Duplicate CVE assignment leading to confusion in vulnerability tracking and patching cycles
exploitation_method: Attackers monitor for CVE duplicate confusion where organizations patch one CVE ID but miss the actual vulnerability due to tracking against wrong identifier, leaving systems exposed
detection_pattern: Monitor for inconsistent vulnerability database references in patch management systems, security scanning tools reporting different CVE IDs for same vulnerability, gaps in remediation timelines for related CVEs, CVSS scoring mismatches for duplicate entries
mitigation_strategy: Implement canonical CVE tracking with automated deduplication checks, require security team to cross-reference CVE aliases in NVD before patches, establish change management requiring verification of actual vulnerability before closure, integrate vulnerability management tools with authoritative CVE feeds that resolve duplicates

threat_vector: Prototype pollution in JavaScript
exploitation_method: Attacker recursively modifies object prototypes by injecting properties through user-controlled input (e.g., '__proto__', 'constructor.prototype') into merge/assign operations, causing unintended property inheritance across all objects in the application
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Suspicious property names in API requests containing '__proto__', 'constructor', 'prototype' keywords; (2) Unexpected modifications to Object.prototype or Function.prototype at runtime; (3) Unusual property access patterns on base objects; (4) Changes to built-in methods or properties in application logs; (5) Error messages indicating undefined behavior in object property lookups
mitigation_strategy: Implement input validation to reject payloads with '__proto__', 'constructor', 'prototype' keywords; use Object.create(null) for untrusted object creation; sanitize object merge operations with explicit property whitelisting; use 'hasOwnProperty()' checks; deploy Object.preventExtensions() and Object.freeze() on critical prototypes; employ static analysis tools (ESLint plugins) to detect unsafe merge patterns; maintain strict Content Security Policy; keep dependencies updated with security patches

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Gather Victim Host Information (T1592)
exploitation_method: Attacker performs passive reconnaissance to collect details about target systems including OS version, software installed, hardware configuration, and network setup through public sources (job postings, GitHub repos, social media), network scanning, website metadata analysis, or DNS/WHOIS lookups before launching targeted attacks
detection_pattern: Monitor for: multiple DNS queries to reconnaissance tools (Shodan, Censys APIs), unusual spike in external port scanning attempts from single source, repeated failed SSH/RDP connection attempts with version probes, abnormal outbound HTTP requests to threat intelligence platforms, OSINT tool usage in proxy/firewall logs, suspicious User-Agent strings indicating scanner tools, enumeration of cloud metadata endpoints, and passive information gathering via web server headers or certificate transparency logs
mitigation_strategy: Implement network segmentation and firewall rules to block unauthorized scanning; restrict DNS query logging and alerting for external reconnaissance services; configure web servers to minimize information leakage (remove version banners, disable directory listing, sanitize headers); monitor and limit cloud metadata endpoint access; enforce rate limiting on failed authentication attempts; use deception (honeypots, false information in public sources); regularly audit public-facing information (GitHub repos, social media, job listings) for sensitive data exposure; implement OSINT monitoring tools to detect when your organization is being actively scanned

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Digital Certificates (T1589.001, T1588.004) - Acquisition and use of fraudulent or stolen certificates for code signing and HTTPS impersonation
exploitation_method: Attacker acquires valid digital certificates through theft, compromise of Certificate Authority (CA), or self-signed spoofing. Certificates are used to sign malware for trusted execution, establish fraudulent HTTPS connections, or perform man-in-the-middle attacks while maintaining SSL/TLS chain of trust.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unusual certificate issuance from internal/trusted CAs; (2) Certificates with mismatched Common Names (CN) or Subject Alternative Names (SANs) from legitimate domains; (3) Code-signing certificates from unknown publishers in process execution logs; (4) Multiple failed SSL/TLS handshakes with certificate validation errors; (5) Reissued certificates for high-value domains within short timeframes; (6) Certificate transparency logs showing unexpected issuances for monitored domains; (7) Self-signed certificates in trusted store; (8) Revoked certificates still being used in network traffic.
mitigation_strategy: Implement: (1) Certificate pinning for critical applications; (2) Monitor and audit all CA-issued certificates via CT logs (Certificate Transparency); (3) Enforce code-signing policies requiring validated publisher certificates; (4) Deploy DNSSEC and CAA DNS records to restrict certificate issuance; (5) Implement certificate-based access controls with short validity windows; (6) Establish automated alerts for unexpected certificate installations; (7) Require mutual TLS (mTLS) with certificate-based client authentication; (8) Regular certificate inventory audits and revocation verification; (9) Use OCSP stapling to validate certificate status in real-time.

threat_vector: Knox Guard unlock bypass via Samsung DeX mode (pre-SMR Jan-2022 Release 1)
exploitation_method: Attacker with physical access connects device to external display/dock via Samsung DeX, exploiting improper authentication checks in Knox Guard that fail to validate security context when DeX mode is activated, allowing temporary bypass of device lock mechanisms
detection_pattern: Monitor for: DeX mode activation during Knox Guard active state, unexpected unlock events correlated with DeX connection, audit logs showing authentication bypass in Knox Guard service, device connection to external display without corresponding unlock attempt, SecurityException suppression in Knox Guard logs
mitigation_strategy: Update to SMR Jan-2022 Release 1 or later; enforce Knox Guard policy requiring re-authentication for DeX mode activation; implement hardware-level authentication verification before DeX mode access; disable DeX mode via MDM/EMM policies on high-security devices; monitor for unauthorized DeX connections via USB/wireless display protocols

threat_vector: SAML Token Abuse (ATT&CK T1187)
exploitation_method: Attacker forges, steals, or modifies SAML assertions to gain unauthorized access to federated applications. Can involve XML signature wrapping attacks, token replay, or compromising identity provider signing keys to create valid tokens for arbitrary users.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: SAML assertion validation failures, unsigned or invalid signature detections, assertions with mismatched issuer/audience claims, unusually old token timestamps, multiple authentication events from single token, failed XML schema validations, tokens with missing KeyInfo elements, assertion modifications detected in audit logs, access from impossible geographical locations following SAML auth, and rapid token reuse across different applications.
mitigation_strategy: Enforce strict SAML assertion validation including signature verification, implement token binding to prevent replay attacks, validate token timing (NotBefore/NotOnOrAfter), use strong cryptographic keys for signing with regular rotation, implement assertion encryption in addition to signing, monitor and log all SAML transactions, deploy anomaly detection for impossible travel scenarios post-SAML auth, maintain short token lifespans, implement conditional access policies, and conduct regular security audits of identity provider configurations.

threat_vector: Pointer Dereference in GPAC 1.0.1 unlink_chunk.isra function leading to Denial of Service
exploitation_method: Attacker provides a crafted MP4 or similar media file with malformed chunk structure that causes the unlink_chunk.isra function to dereference a null or invalid pointer during chunk unlinking operations, resulting in application crash or memory access violation
detection_pattern: Monitor for GPAC process crashes with segmentation faults in unlink_chunk.isra stack traces; observe abnormal memory access violations when processing specific media files; log file processing errors with null pointer exceptions; watch for repeated application termination when handling certain MP4 containers
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade GPAC to version 1.0.2 or later containing the pointer dereference fix; implement input validation for media file chunk structures before processing; add defensive null pointer checks in custom GPAC builds; run GPAC in sandboxed environments with memory protection; use fuzzing tools to test media file inputs; enable address sanitizer (ASAN) during development to catch pointer dereferences early

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Windows Service (T1543.003)
exploitation_method: Attacker creates, modifies, or hijacks Windows services to achieve persistence by placing malicious executable paths in service registry entries or leveraging legitimate service binaries with weak permissions. Service runs with SYSTEM privileges on startup.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: new service creation via sc.exe or registry modifications to HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services; mismatch between service ImagePath and legitimate service executables; service execution from unusual directories; registry events with names like 'Start', 'ImagePath', 'ServiceDll' modified; svchost.exe launching unexpected child processes; access to service control manager APIs.
mitigation_strategy: Apply service isolation and least privilege principles; regularly audit and whitelist legitimate services; monitor service registry hive for changes using Windows Auditing (SACL); restrict permissions on service binary directories; implement application allowlisting for service executables; keep service binaries on write-protected locations; use security monitoring tools to baseline and alert on service modifications; patch privilege escalation vulnerabilities; enforce Code Integrity policies.

threat_vector: GDAL 3.3.0 through 3.4.0 heap-based buffer overflow in PCIDSK file reading (CVE-2021-21898)
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious PCIDSK image file with oversized segment data that exceeds allocated heap buffer. When ReadFromFile processes the PCIDSK segment, the buffer overflow occurs during data copy operations, allowing heap memory corruption and potential code execution.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) GDAL version detection showing 3.3.0-3.4.0 processing PCIDSK files; (2) Heap corruption indicators (segmentation faults, memory access violations); (3) Abnormal PCIDSK file characteristics (segment size fields exceeding reasonable bounds); (4) GDAL error logs containing 'ReadFromFile' or 'PCIDSKBinarySegment' with memory-related failures; (5) Unexpected child process spawning or memory dump files after PCIDSK processing
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade GDAL to version 3.4.1 or later. Implement input validation on PCIDSK files before processing (validate segment size headers against actual file size). Run GDAL operations in sandboxed/containerized environments with memory protection and address space layout randomization (ASLR) enabled. Restrict file processing to trusted sources only. Deploy memory safety tools (AddressSanitizer) in development/testing environments to catch similar issues.

threat_vector: Null pointer dereference in smartphone camera module
exploitation_method: Attacker sends malformed image data or crafted camera API requests that cause the camera service to dereference a null pointer, leading to application crash or denial of service
detection_pattern: Monitor for: repeated camera application crashes with null pointer exceptions in logs, camera service restarts, SIGSEGV signals from camera daemon, abnormal camera API error codes, device unresponsiveness during camera operations, logcat entries showing 'NullPointerException' or 'null reference' in camera framework
mitigation_strategy: Implement null pointer checks before all pointer dereferences in camera module code, use static analysis tools to identify potential null dereferences, enable address sanitizer during development, apply vendor security patches, implement graceful error handling with fallback mechanisms, use memory safety abstractions, and establish input validation for all camera API parameters

threat_vector: SMS Pumping (toll fraud through premium SMS services)
exploitation_method: Attacker compromises application or uses social engineering to trigger unsolicited SMS messages to premium-rate numbers, generating fraudulent charges to victim's phone bill. Often exploits weak verification flows, unpatched APIs, or misconfigured messaging services.
detection_pattern: Sudden spike in outbound SMS volume to unfamiliar numbers; repeated failed authentication attempts followed by SMS sends; SMS sends to known premium-rate number ranges; unusual geographic SMS destinations; multiple SMS sends within seconds to same number; SMS send requests from unusual IP addresses or user agents; authentication logs showing rapid session creation before SMS sends
mitigation_strategy: Implement rate limiting on SMS sends per user/session (e.g., max 1 SMS per minute); require CAPTCHA before SMS delivery; verify user phone number ownership before enabling SMS; monitor and block known premium-rate number prefixes; implement transaction logging with alerting on spike detection; use dedicated SMS provider with fraud detection; require multi-factor approval for SMS-based transactions; segment SMS APIs behind authentication and strict ACLs; conduct regular penetration testing of authentication flows

threat_vector: HwNearbyMain module NULL Pointer Dereference vulnerability leading to process restart
exploitation_method: Attacker sends specially crafted input or triggers specific code path in HwNearbyMain that causes dereferencing of an uninitialized or null pointer, resulting in memory access violation and process crash
detection_pattern: Monitor for: segmentation fault (SIGSEGV) signals from HwNearbyMain process, repeated process restarts within short timeframe, NULL pointer dereference entries in kernel logs (/var/log/kern.log), abnormal termination codes (exit code -11 or similar), crash dumps in /var/crash or system crash logs, sudden spikes in process restart counts in syslog
mitigation_strategy: Apply vendor security patches to HwNearbyMain module; implement input validation and bounds checking before pointer dereference; enable Address Sanitizer (ASan) or similar runtime detection tools during development; deploy application whitelisting to restrict HwNearbyMain execution; monitor process restart frequency with alerting thresholds; implement resource limits to prevent cascading failures; use SELinux or AppArmor to sandbox the process and limit blast radius

threat_vector: Apache James ManagedSieve path traversal in sieve script file storage
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious sieve script filenames containing path traversal sequences (../) to escape the designated sieve script directory, enabling arbitrary file read/write access on the system with James service privileges
detection_pattern: Monitor James logs for: sieve script upload requests with '../' or encoded traversal sequences in filenames; file access attempts outside designated sieve directories; unexpected file modifications in system directories by James process; error messages indicating path normalization failures; audit logs showing file operations with suspicious path patterns in sieve-related transactions
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Apache James to patched version (post-vulnerability fix); implement strict filename validation rejecting path traversal characters (../, .., encoded variants %2e%2e%2f); enforce directory permission restrictions limiting James process to sieve script directory only; deploy application-level path canonicalization and whitelist-based filename validation; monitor file system access with SELinux/AppArmor policies restricting James sieve operations; implement file integrity monitoring on critical system files

threat_vector: PIL.ImageMath.eval arbitrary code execution in Pillow < 9.0.0
exploitation_method: Attacker passes untrusted image processing expressions to PIL.ImageMath.eval() which evaluates them in a Python context, allowing execution of arbitrary code via exec(), lambda functions, or __import__ statements embedded in mathematical expressions
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) PIL.ImageMath.eval() calls with user-supplied arguments in application code review, (2) Pillow version detection showing versions < 9.0.0, (3) Unexpected process spawning or file access following image processing operations, (4) Stack traces containing 'ImageMath.eval' in error logs, (5) AST analysis detecting exec/lambda/import in expression strings passed to image libraries
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Pillow to version 9.0.0 or later which restricts eval scope; implement input validation to reject expressions containing exec, lambda, import, or other dangerous keywords; use AST parsing to validate mathematical expressions only contain safe operations (arithmetic, standard math functions); isolate image processing in sandboxed environments with restricted permissions; never pass user-supplied strings directly to PIL.ImageMath.eval()

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Application Access Token (T1550.001)
exploitation_method: Attacker obtains or forges application access tokens (OAuth, JWT, API tokens) through credential theft, token leakage, or misconfigured endpoints, then uses legitimate token to authenticate and access protected resources without triggering password-based detection controls.
detection_pattern: Unusual geographic IP locations for token usage; token activity outside normal business hours; tokens used from compromised endpoints; excessive API calls from single token; token reuse across unusual service combinations; tokens appearing in logs from unexpected application contexts; abnormal token refresh patterns; token usage after reported device compromise.
mitigation_strategy: Implement token rotation policies with short TTL; enforce token binding to device/IP; use mutual TLS for token transmission; monitor and alert on token anomalies; implement token revocation mechanisms; use hardware security keys for sensitive applications; enforce multi-factor authentication before token issuance; validate token origin and scope; implement rate limiting on token-authenticated requests; maintain comprehensive token usage audit logs; isolate token generation/storage from internet-facing systems.

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Bootkit (T1542.001)
exploitation_method: Attacker gains SYSTEM/kernel access and modifies the Master Boot Record (MBR), UEFI firmware, or bootloader to load malicious code before the operating system kernel initializes. This allows persistence and evasion of OS-level security controls.
detection_pattern: Unexpected MBR/bootloader modifications detected by firmware integrity monitoring; abnormal boot sequence timing; UEFI/BIOS setting changes logged via System event ID 4657; secure boot verification failures; kernel-mode rootkit detection via kernel integrity checks; unauthorized firmware write operations; boot process delays or hangs; memory dumps showing unexpected code execution before OS load
mitigation_strategy: Enable Secure Boot with verified keys and UEFI firmware signing enforcement; implement BIOS/UEFI password protection and firmware integrity monitoring tools; use Trusted Platform Module (TPM) with measured boot and attestation; deploy kernel-mode code integrity (KMCI) and hypervisor-based protection; maintain automated firmware version controls and rollback mechanisms; regularly audit boot configuration integrity; restrict physical access to systems; apply latest firmware patches; implement host-based IDS with kernel-level monitoring; conduct periodic secure boot verification audits

threat_vector: Discourse /message-bus/_diagnostics Denial of Service
exploitation_method: Unauthenticated or low-privileged attacker sends rapid requests to the /message-bus/_diagnostics endpoint, which processes diagnostics data without proper rate limiting. This causes excessive CPU and memory consumption, degrading or completely disabling the Discourse instance for legitimate users.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Spike in HTTP requests to /message-bus/_diagnostics path in web server logs; (2) Unusual 200/500 response codes from that endpoint; (3) Request frequency exceeding normal baseline (e.g., >100 requests/minute from single IP); (4) Corresponding spike in CPU/memory utilization on Discourse server; (5) Response time degradation for legitimate endpoints; (6) Error logs showing resource exhaustion warnings
mitigation_strategy: Implement: (1) Rate limiting on /message-bus/_diagnostics endpoint (e.g., max 5 requests per minute per IP); (2) Restrict endpoint access to authenticated administrators only; (3) Upgrade Discourse to patched version; (4) Deploy WAF rules blocking excessive requests to diagnostic paths; (5) Monitor resource usage and set automated alerts; (6) Implement reverse proxy rate limiting; (7) Consider disabling diagnostics endpoint if not actively used

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Masquerade File Type (T1036.008)
exploitation_method: Attacker modifies file extensions or MIME types to disguise malicious executables as benign documents (e.g., renaming .exe to .pdf, setting fake Content-Type headers). Users or automated systems execute the file based on misleading appearance rather than actual content.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: file extension mismatches between declared type and magic bytes/file headers; PE executable signatures in files with document extensions; anomalous Content-Type headers vs actual file content; process execution of files from unusual locations with double extensions (.pdf.exe); sudden execution of recently created files with suspicious extension patterns; file modification timestamps inconsistent with creation dates
