mitigation_strategy: Implement file validation using magic byte analysis rather than extensions; enforce strict file type whitelisting at email gateways and download handlers; disable file extension hiding in OS; use application whitelisting to restrict executable execution; require code signing for executables; educate users on file type verification; deploy endpoint detection and response (EDR) to monitor suspicious file execution; configure Windows to show full file extensions and disable Run from restricted zones

threat_vector: Null pointer dereference in trap handler due to missing thread ID validation before dereferencing in Snapdragon kernels
exploitation_method: Attacker sends crafted interrupt or exception that triggers trap handler with invalid/NULL thread ID. Handler dereferences thread structure without validation, causing kernel panic or potential privilege escalation if attacker can control memory at dereferenced address.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unexpected kernel panic/oops in trap/exception handlers with NULL pointer dereference stack trace; (2) Anomalous interrupt patterns preceding crashes; (3) Thread ID validation failures in kernel logs; (4) Repeated trap handler invocations with invalid thread context; (5) Memory access violations at consistent kernel addresses during exception processing
mitigation_strategy: Implement mandatory thread ID validation before any dereference: validate thread pointer against active thread table, verify thread state is valid, use bounds checking on thread ID values. Add null checks with early return paths. Implement kernel ASLR and DEP. Deploy runtime monitoring of trap handler behavior. Apply vendor security patches for affected Snapdragon platforms immediately. Use kernel hardening features (stack canaries, CFI) to detect corrupted thread structures.

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1218.011 - Signed Binary Proxy Execution via Rundll32
exploitation_method: Attacker executes arbitrary code by invoking rundll32.exe with malicious DLL or function parameters, leveraging the legitimate Windows utility to bypass execution controls and appear as trusted system process
detection_pattern: Monitor for: rundll32.exe with uncommon command-line arguments (especially containing http://, file:// or unusual .dll paths); rundll32 spawning child processes (cmd.exe, powershell.exe); DLL loading from %temp%, %appdata%, or network shares; rundll32 with network connections (outbound to non-standard ports); parent process abnormalities (launched from script interpreters, office apps, or explorer)
mitigation_strategy: Implement application whitelisting for rundll32.exe execution context; enforce code signing validation; disable rundll32 if not required via Group Policy (gpedit.msc - disable Legacy Component Registry); monitor and restrict DLL loading from user-writable locations; use EDR/HIPS to block rundll32 spawning child processes; implement network segmentation to limit outbound connections; require 64-bit systems where possible (rundll32 is 32-bit legacy)

threat_vector: WP Travel Engine WordPress plugin <5.3.1 - Unescaped Description field in Trip Destination/Activities/Trip Type and Pricing Category pages allowing Stored XSS by low-privileged users
exploitation_method: An attacker with Contributor or Editor role injects malicious JavaScript into the Description field of Trip Destination, Activities, Trip Type, or Pricing Category pages. The unescaped input is stored in the database and reflected in the admin interface and frontend without sanitization, executing arbitrary scripts in browsers of administrators and site visitors.
detection_pattern: Monitor WordPress audit logs for: (1) POST requests to wp-admin pages containing 'trip_destination', 'activities', 'trip_type', or 'pricing_category' with script tags or event handlers in POST data; (2) Database queries writing to postmeta or wp_posts with content containing <script>, javascript:, onerror=, onclick= patterns; (3) User activity logs showing low-privileged users (Contributor/Editor) modifying these custom post types; (4) Frontend rendering of unexpected script execution or suspicious HTML in admin notices/pages.
mitigation_strategy: Immediately update WP Travel Engine plugin to version 5.3.1 or later. Conduct code review ensuring wp_kses_post() or equivalent sanitization functions escape all user input in Description fields before storage. Implement Content Security Policy (CSP) headers to restrict inline script execution. Apply capability restrictions limiting only trusted roles (Administrator/Shop Manager) to modify these fields. Regularly audit user permissions and conduct stored XSS testing on all custom post type input fields.

threat_vector: WOOCS WordPress Plugin Reflected XSS via custom_prices Parameter (versions < 1.3.7.3)
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious URL with JavaScript payload in custom_prices parameter. When victim visits URL, unsanitized parameter is reflected in HTTP response without HTML encoding, causing browser to execute injected script in victim's session context, potentially stealing credentials or session tokens.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) HTTP requests to WOOCS plugin endpoints containing script tags or event handlers in custom_prices parameter (e.g., '<script>', 'onerror=', 'onclick='); (2) 200 responses echoing unencoded special characters from custom_prices parameter; (3) Web Application Firewall alerts for XSS patterns; (4) WordPress error logs showing WOOCS plugin processing suspicious query strings; (5) Unusual JavaScript execution in browser console for sites running vulnerable WOOCS versions
mitigation_strategy: Immediately update WOOCS plugin to version 1.3.7.3 or later. Implement input validation: sanitize custom_prices parameter using wp_sanitize_textarea_field() and output encoding using esc_html() or esc_attr() before rendering. Deploy WAF rules blocking requests with XSS payloads. Use Content Security Policy (CSP) headers to restrict inline script execution. Audit plugin code for similar vulnerabilities in other parameters. Monitor plugin repository for security advisories.

threat_vector: Hardcoded cryptographic key in ControlUp Real-Time Agent (cuAgent.exe) versions before 8.2.5 enabling unauthorized WCF command execution
exploitation_method: Attacker obtains hardcoded key from cuAgent.exe binary via static analysis or reverse engineering. Using this key, attacker crafts WCF (Windows Communication Foundation) messages with valid cryptographic signatures to invoke remote OS command execution endpoints on systems running vulnerable agent versions.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Anomalous WCF service calls to cuAgent listening ports with suspicious command payloads in network traffic; (2) cuAgent.exe process spawning unexpected child processes (cmd.exe, powershell.exe) with unusual arguments; (3) Registry or file system modifications originating from cuAgent service context; (4) Event Viewer logs showing cuAgent accessing credentials or executing system commands; (5) Network connections from cuAgent to unexpected external hosts; (6) Unexpected elevation of privileges associated with cuAgent processes
mitigation_strategy: Immediately upgrade all ControlUp Real-Time Agent installations to version 8.2.5 or later. Implement network segmentation to restrict WCF communication to cuAgent to trusted administrative networks only. Disable WCF remote command execution if not required. Monitor cuAgent process execution and child processes via EDR/SIEM. Implement application whitelisting to prevent unauthorized child process spawning from cuAgent. Audit and restrict service account privileges running cuAgent to minimum necessary permissions. Apply Windows Firewall rules to limit inbound connections to cuAgent ports from authorized management infrastructure only.

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1005: Direct Volume Access
exploitation_method: Attacker gains raw access to physical storage devices or virtual disk volumes, bypassing the file system and OS access controls. Methods include using tools like `dd`, `diskpart`, or custom drivers to read/write directly to disk sectors, often requiring local admin/root privileges or physical device access.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: privileged access to device files (/dev/sda, /dev/disk* on Unix; \Device\HarddiskVolume* on Windows), unusual driver loading, raw disk I/O operations via WMI or kernel APIs, access to volume shadow copies, unexpected NTFS journal reads, forensic tool execution (EnCase, FTK), or raw socket operations to storage controllers. Log abnormal access to partition tables and MBR/GPT regions.
mitigation_strategy: Implement full-disk encryption (BitLocker, LUKS, FileVault) to render raw disk access unreadable; enforce privileged access management (PAM) and restrict local admin rights; use Secure Boot and measured boot to prevent unauthorized driver loading; monitor and restrict access to device files via AppLocker/SELinux; maintain proper physical security controls; audit and log all privileged storage operations; implement EDR solutions that detect direct volume access attempts; use UEFI security features to prevent bootkit attacks.

threat_vector: Segmentation Fault in GPAC 1.0.1 co64_box_new function leading to Denial of Service
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious MP4 file with specially formatted 64-bit chunk offset box (co64) that triggers memory access violation in co64_box_new function when parser attempts to read/initialize the box structure, causing process crash
detection_pattern: Monitor for: GPAC process crashes with SIGSEGV; core dumps referencing co64_box_new in stack trace; sudden termination of media processing threads; repeated failed MP4 parsing attempts from same source; file entries with oversized or malformed co64 box headers (box size field mismatch with actual data)
mitigation_strategy: Update GPAC to patched version >1.0.1; implement input validation for co64 box size and offset fields before processing; run GPAC in sandboxed environment with process restart on crash; enforce file size limits on uploads; add timeout mechanisms for media parsing operations; implement defensive bounds checking in custom GPAC implementations

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1007: System Service Discovery
exploitation_method: Attacker enumerates running services via commands like 'net start', 'systemctl list-units', 'launchctl list', or WMI queries to identify target services for lateral movement, privilege escalation, or persistence mechanisms
detection_pattern: Monitor for: Process execution of service enumeration tools (tasklist, sc query, Get-Service, netstat -ano); WMI queries targeting Win32_Service class; rapid sequential service queries; command-line arguments containing service enumeration patterns; suspicious parent-child process relationships with system tools
mitigation_strategy: Implement endpoint detection and response (EDR) to alert on suspicious service enumeration; restrict WMI access via GPO; disable unnecessary services; use application whitelisting to control execution of enumeration tools; monitor and log all service-related WMI and Win32 API calls; segment network to limit lateral movement post-discovery

threat_vector: Race condition in concurrent requests
exploitation_method: Attacker sends multiple simultaneous requests to bypass state validation, causing application logic to execute out of order. Common in financial transactions, inventory management, or authentication flows where multiple threads access shared resources without proper synchronization, allowing duplicate processing or privilege escalation.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: duplicate transaction IDs within millisecond timeframes, inventory count inconsistencies between requests, authentication tokens validated multiple times simultaneously, database constraint violations on unique fields, sudden spikes in concurrent requests to same endpoint, transaction log entries with identical timestamps from different sessions accessing same resource ID
mitigation_strategy: Implement pessimistic locking (row-level locks), optimistic locking with version numbers, atomic operations and database transactions with appropriate isolation levels (SERIALIZABLE), distributed locks via Redis/Zookeeper, idempotency keys for state-changing operations, request deduplication, and comprehensive testing with concurrent load simulation tools

threat_vector: AppInit DLLs (T1547.010) - Persistence via Registry-based DLL Loading
exploitation_method: Attacker modifies HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Windows registry key (AppInit_DLLs value) to load malicious DLL into processes that call User32.dll at startup, achieving persistence and privilege escalation
detection_pattern: Monitor registry modifications to HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Windows (AppInit_DLLs and LoadAppInit_DLLs); audit unexpected DLL files referenced in AppInit values; log process execution with unusual DLL injection; check Event ID 13 (Registry Object Added or Modified) for these keys; identify DLLs loaded from non-standard paths or with suspicious origins
mitigation_strategy: Disable AppInit_DLLs loading by setting LoadAppInit_DLLs registry value to 0; implement registry modification monitoring and alerting; use endpoint detection and response (EDR) to monitor User32.dll loading chains; enforce code signing requirements for DLLs; restrict write permissions on Windows registry AppInit keys; maintain allowlist of approved AppInit DLLs if legitimate use exists; regularly audit and baseline legitimate AppInit entries

threat_vector: Improper validation of LLM utility timers availability leading to denial of service in Snapdragon platforms
exploitation_method: Attacker sends malformed or specially crafted timer allocation requests that bypass input validation, causing the LLM (Low-Level Microcontroller) utility timer subsystem to exhaust available timer resources or enter an invalid state, preventing legitimate processes from accessing timing functionality and causing system instability or complete service unavailability
detection_pattern: Monitor for: repeated failed timer allocation requests from single process, unusual patterns of timer_create/timer_delete syscalls, timer resource exhaustion warnings in kernel logs, processes hanging on timer operations, elevated context switches without corresponding legitimate timer activity, timer allocation failures with errno ENOMEM or EBUSY when resources appear available
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict input validation for all timer allocation requests including bounds checking on timer parameters; enforce per-process timer quotas and rate limiting; add authentication/authorization checks before timer operations; monitor timer resource pool utilization with automatic cleanup of orphaned timers; implement watchdog mechanisms to detect timer allocation loops; use secure boot to prevent unauthorized firmware modifications to timer drivers; apply vendor security patches for Snapdragon timer subsystem vulnerabilities

threat_vector: CORS misconfiguration allowing arbitrary origin
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious website that makes cross-origin requests to victim application. Misconfigured Access-Control-Allow-Origin header (set to '*' or dynamically reflecting user-supplied origin) permits requests from any domain, enabling credential theft, session hijacking, or unauthorized API calls with victim's browser context.
detection_pattern: Monitor HTTP response headers for overly permissive CORS policies: Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * or Access-Control-Allow-Origin matching suspicious/unknown domains; Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true combined with wildcard origin; requests from unexpected referer/origin headers; unusual cross-origin API calls in access logs; browser console errors followed by successful unauthorized API requests.
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict CORS policy: explicitly whitelist trusted origins in Access-Control-Allow-Origin (never use '*' with credentials); use exact domain matching; implement CORS validation server-side before accepting requests; avoid dynamically reflecting Origin header without validation; set Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true only when necessary; use Sec-Fetch-Site and Sec-Fetch-Mode headers for additional validation; implement SameSite cookie policy; conduct regular security audits of CORS configuration.

threat_vector: Pointer Dereference Vulnerability in GPAC 1.0.1 ShiftMetaOffset.isra causing Denial of Service
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts a malformed MP4 or similar media file with invalid metadata offset values that cause improper pointer arithmetic in the ShiftMetaOffset function. When GPAC processes this file, the function dereferences an invalid or uninitialized pointer, triggering a segmentation fault or crash.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: GPAC process crashes with SIGSEGV signal; abnormal termination of GPAC-based services; segmentation fault logs referencing ShiftMetaOffset; media file processing failures on specifically crafted files; core dumps from libgpac; sudden resource exhaustion followed by process death; repeated crashes when processing specific media files from untrusted sources.
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade GPAC to version 1.1.0 or later with patched pointer validation; implement strict input validation for MP4 metadata offset values before processing; run GPAC in a sandboxed environment with process crash monitoring and automatic restart; disable untrusted media file processing; implement file format validation before GPAC processing; use memory protection tools like AddressSanitizer during testing; apply OS-level protections (DEP/NX, ASLR); monitor system logs for GPAC crashes and alert on repeated failures.

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Malvertising (T1608.004 - Acquire Capabilities: Malvertise)
exploitation_method: Attacker purchases ad space on legitimate ad networks or compromises ad servers to inject malicious content. Users visiting compromised web pages receive malicious advertisements that redirect to exploit kits, malware distribution sites, or phishing pages when clicked or even passively viewed
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unexpected redirects to suspicious domains in HTTP referrer logs; sudden spike in traffic to unknown external domains from legitimate site; ad network responses containing obfuscated JavaScript or suspicious iframe injection; user reports of unexpected pop-ups or redirects from trusted sites; network egress to known malvertising infrastructure; anomalous ad server response sizes or execution of unsigned scripts from ad network CDNs
mitigation_strategy: Implement Content Security Policy (CSP) headers to restrict ad script sources; deploy ad fraud detection and ad verification services; use browser isolation technology for ad content; maintain allowlist of approved ad networks and enforce strict vendor assessment; implement Subresource Integrity (SRI) for ad scripts; monitor and block known malicious ad networks via DNS filtering; educate users on ad-blocking extensions and safe browsing practices; regularly audit ad network partnerships and audit logs for unauthorized script injection

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1648: Serverless Execution
exploitation_method: Attacker deploys malicious functions to serverless platforms (AWS Lambda, Azure Functions, Google Cloud Functions) by exploiting overly permissive IAM roles, uploading backdoored code, or abusing function invocation mechanisms. Functions execute with elevated privileges to exfiltrate data, pivot laterally, or establish persistence.
detection_pattern: Monitor CloudTrail/audit logs for: unusual Lambda/Function CreateFunction calls from unexpected principals, unauthorized function deployments, functions with overbroad execution roles (Admin policies), unexpected environment variable modifications containing credentials, abnormal function invocation patterns or execution duration spikes, function code modifications via UpdateFunctionCode API, concurrent invocations from suspicious sources, network calls to known C2 infrastructure from function execution logs
mitigation_strategy: Implement least-privilege IAM roles per function with minimal required permissions; enable function layer security scanning and code signing verification; enforce VPC restrictions on function execution; deploy function-level WAF rules and rate limiting; use AWS Lambda@Edge security controls; enable CloudTrail logging with log file integrity validation; implement function behavior baselines and anomaly detection; restrict function deployment permissions to authorized CI/CD pipelines only; audit and remove default execution roles; use secrets management services instead of environment variables for credentials

threat_vector: Double free vulnerability in AOD (Always-On Display) module
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious input to AOD module that causes same memory region to be freed twice during execution, corrupting heap metadata and potentially achieving code execution or denial of service
detection_pattern: Monitor for: heap corruption warnings in system logs, AOD process crashes with segmentation faults, unexpected memory allocation failures, abnormal AOD process termination patterns, heap sanitizer warnings (ASAN/UBSAN if enabled), and repeated display service restarts within short time windows
mitigation_strategy: Implement input validation and bounds checking in AOD module, use safe memory management patterns (RAII, smart pointers), enable AddressSanitizer/UBSan during development and testing, apply patch management for AOD module updates, implement memory tagging extensions (MTE) on supported ARM platforms, and enforce strict access controls on AOD module permissions

threat_vector: Go runtime file descriptor mismanagement (CVE-2021-44717) on UNIX systems prior to versions 1.16.12 and 1.17.5
exploitation_method: When Go programs close file descriptor 0 (stdin) incorrectly, subsequent file operations or network connections may inadvertently reuse FD 0 for unintended files or sockets. An attacker can exploit this to redirect writes to arbitrary files or establish unauthorized network connections through predictable file descriptor allocation.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Go application crashes with 'bad file descriptor' errors; (2) unexpected file writes to system files or configuration directories by Go processes; (3) failed network connection attempts followed by file write operations; (4) audit logs showing open() syscalls on FD 0 without corresponding close(); (5) Go processes with multiple stdin file descriptors opened simultaneously; (6) strace/auditd logs showing FD reuse anomalies in Go binaries
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Go runtime to version 1.16.12 or later (1.17.5+ for 1.17.x branch). Implement strict file descriptor management: validate stdin/stdout/stderr FDs remain on 0/1/2, use explicit file descriptor closing patterns, restrict Go process file system permissions via AppArmor/SELinux, monitor and limit file descriptor counts per process, disable unnecessary network capabilities on Go application containers.

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Archive via Custom Method
exploitation_method: Attacker uses custom scripts or non-standard tools (PowerShell, Python, compiled binaries) to compress and archive sensitive files for exfiltration, bypassing native compression detection signatures that focus on WinRAR, 7-Zip, or standard Windows compression utilities.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unusual process spawning with compression/encoding capabilities; high disk I/O on system drives followed by file deletion; custom scripts calling System.IO.Compression APIs; suspicious command-line arguments containing base64, gzip, or custom encoding functions; parent-child process relationships between scripting engines and unfamiliar executables; file system access patterns aggregating data before transmission attempts.
mitigation_strategy: Implement application whitelisting to restrict approved compression tools; deploy behavioral analysis detecting rapid file aggregation and compression patterns; enforce code signing requirements; monitor and restrict PowerShell/Python execution with constrained language mode; conduct periodic file integrity monitoring on sensitive data repositories; segment network access to prevent bulk data staging; require approval workflows for large archive operations in audit logs.

threat_vector: Reflected XSS in Github Read Me Stats renderError function (commit 3c7220e4f7144f6cb068fd433c774f6db47ccb95)
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious URL with unsanitized input parameters that are reflected in the renderError function output. When a victim visits the link, JavaScript payload executes in their browser context with access to session tokens and user data.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) HTTP requests with encoded script tags or event handlers in query parameters (e.g., %3Cscript%3E, onclick=, onerror=); (2) HTML responses containing unescaped user input matching request parameters; (3) CSP violation reports indicating inline script execution; (4) Unusual Unicode/URL encoding in error parameter values; (5) Multiple requests from same source with varying payload mutations
mitigation_strategy: Implement output encoding: HTML-escape all user-supplied input in renderError before rendering (use contextual escaping libraries); Deploy Content Security Policy (CSP) with script-src 'self' to prevent inline execution; Input validation: whitelist allowed characters in error parameters; Use templating engines with auto-escaping enabled; Apply security headers (X-XSS-Protection, X-Content-Type-Options); Conduct code review of renderError function and similar rendering paths; Update to patched version when available; Implement automated SAST scanning in CI/CD pipeline

threat_vector: Accu-Time Systems MAXIMUS 1.0 telnet service remote buffer overflow
exploitation_method: Attacker connects to telnet service on port 23 and sends oversized input string exceeding buffer boundaries in command parsing, causing stack memory corruption and service crash (denial of service). Payload typically targets username or command input fields that lack proper bounds checking.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) telnet connection attempts followed by abnormally large data transfers or malformed command sequences, (2) sudden telnet service crashes with segmentation fault errors in system logs, (3) repeated connection attempts with progressively larger payloads, (4) network traffic containing unusually long input strings (>512 bytes) to telnet port 23, (5) application errors showing stack trace or memory access violations in MAXIMUS logs
mitigation_strategy: Immediately: (1) disable telnet service or restrict access via firewall rules to trusted IPs only, (2) migrate to SSH protocol which supports proper input validation, (3) apply vendor patches when available, (4) implement network-based input size limits and rate limiting on port 23, (5) deploy intrusion detection signatures for oversized telnet payloads, (6) monitor service availability and configure automated alerting for unexpected crashes, (7) apply input validation and bounds checking at application layer if source code access available

threat_vector: Timing Design Defects in Smartphone
exploitation_method: Attackers measure precise timing variations in cryptographic operations, memory access patterns, or authentication processes to extract sensitive information such as encryption keys, PIN codes, or authentication tokens through side-channel analysis and statistical correlation.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unusual power consumption patterns during authentication, statistically anomalous response time variations in cryptographic operations, repeated rapid-fire authentication attempts with microsecond-level timing measurements, abnormal CPU/memory access patterns logged by hardware performance counters, unexpected variance in TLS handshake completion times, or side-channel analysis tool execution on device.
mitigation_strategy: Implement constant-time cryptographic implementations resistant to timing attacks, add random delays and noise to timing-sensitive operations, use cache-oblivious algorithms, isolate cryptographic operations in secure enclaves or TEE (Trusted Execution Environment), apply timing equalization across code paths, monitor and alert on abnormal timing variance, regularly audit cryptographic libraries for timing vulnerabilities, and apply manufacturer security patches for hardware-level timing protections.

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1547.001 - Startup Items (Registry Run Keys / Startup Folder)
exploitation_method: Attacker modifies HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run registry keys or places executable files in Windows startup folders (%APPDATA%\Microsoft\Windows\Start Menu\Programs\Startup) to achieve persistence and execute malicious code upon system reboot
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Registry modifications to Run/RunOnce keys with suspicious values; (2) New .exe, .bat, .vbs, .ps1 files in startup directories with non-standard names; (3) Process execution from startup folders during boot; (4) EventID 13 (Registry Set Value) targeting HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run*; (5) File creation events in startup directories from non-administrative processes; (6) Sysmon EventID 11 detecting files in startup paths with uncommon file sizes or timestamps
mitigation_strategy: (1) Implement Application Whitelisting for startup directories; (2) Enable Windows Defender Application Control (WDAC) to restrict executable runs; (3) Use Group Policy to restrict write permissions to startup folders and Run registry keys for standard users; (4) Deploy endpoint detection and response (EDR) with behavioral monitoring of startup processes; (5) Configure file integrity monitoring (FIM) on critical startup paths; (6) Audit and remove unnecessary startup items regularly; (7) Disable unnecessary startup programs via msconfig; (8) Monitor EventID 13 registry changes in SIEM with alerting on suspicious values

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1546.002: Event Triggered Execution - Screensaver
exploitation_method: Attacker modifies screensaver registry keys (HKCU\Control Panel\Desktop\SCRNSAVE.EXE) or .scr files to point to malicious executable. When screensaver activates after idle timeout, malicious code executes with user privileges without explicit user interaction.
detection_pattern: Registry modifications to HKCU\Control Panel\Desktop or HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Control Panel\Desktop; .scr file creation/modification in System32 or user directories; screensaver process execution from unusual paths; parent process cmd.exe or powershell.exe spawning screensaver binaries; file write events to .scr extensions outside Windows directories
mitigation_strategy: Enforce Group Policy restrictions on screensaver execution; disable screensaver functionality via GPO if not required; implement application whitelisting for .scr file execution; use File Integrity Monitoring on screensaver directories; regularly audit and baseline screensaver registry values; restrict write permissions to System32\*.scr; monitor and alert on registry modifications under Control Panel\Desktop; educate users on idle timeout settings and security implications

threat_vector: Improper input validation in TrustZone memory transfer interface allowing information disclosure in Snapdragon platforms
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malformed memory transfer requests to TrustZone without proper boundary validation, causing out-of-bounds reads that leak sensitive kernel memory, cryptographic keys, or other protected data from secure world into normal world accessible memory regions
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unusual TrustZone SMC calls with oversized or negative memory region parameters, (2) Memory access logs showing reads beyond allocated buffer boundaries, (3) Repeated failed validation checks in TrustZone memory management subsystem, (4) Unexpected data patterns in normal world memory matching protected secure world regions, (5) Kernel audit logs showing SELinux/MAC violations on memory interface access, (6) Hypervisor logs detecting anomalous SMMU (System Memory Management Unit) transactions
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict input validation on all TrustZone memory transfer parameters before processing: (1) Validate memory addresses and sizes against whitelisted ranges, (2) Enforce bounds checking with canary values, (3) Apply principle of least privilege to memory access permissions, (4) Implement SMMU configuration to restrict cross-world memory access, (5) Use compiler-based protections (stack canaries, CFI) in TrustZone code, (6) Regular security audits and fuzzing of memory interface handlers, (7) Deploy runtime memory tagging extensions (MTE) to catch invalid accesses, (8) Keep firmware/bootloader updated with security patches

threat_vector: showdoc is vulnerable to Generation of Error Message Containing Sensitive Information
exploitation_method: Attacker triggers error conditions in showdoc application (invalid file paths, database queries, authentication failures) to generate verbose error messages that expose sensitive information such as file system paths, database connection strings, internal IP addresses, software versions, or configuration details
detection_pattern: Monitor for: HTTP responses with detailed stack traces or error messages containing file paths matching application directory structure, database credentials patterns in response bodies, verbose error codes with system information, unusually large error response payloads (>5KB), error messages disclosing internal IPs or hostnames, application version strings in error output, repeated requests to non-existent endpoints followed by detailed error responses
mitigation_strategy: Implement custom error handling that returns generic error messages to users while logging detailed errors server-side; disable debug mode in production; configure web server and application to suppress stack traces in HTTP responses; implement WAF rules to sanitize error messages before transmission; use centralized logging with proper access controls; apply principle of least privilege for error message verbosity based on user role; conduct security testing to identify and remediate information disclosure endpoints; implement Content Security Policy headers to limit error message exposure

threat_vector: Heap-based buffer overflow in GPAC 1.0.1 MP4Box media.c via crafted MP4 file
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts a malicious MP4 file with specially formatted media chunks that trigger inadequate bounds checking in media.c parser, causing heap memory corruption. This allows overwriting adjacent heap objects to achieve code execution or crash the application.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) MP4Box process crashes with segmentation faults when processing specific files; (2) Heap corruption errors in sanitizer logs; (3) Unexpected memory access patterns detected by AddressSanitizer; (4) Files with abnormally large or malformed mdat/moov box sizes; (5) Process behavior deviations including spawned child processes from MP4Box; (6) Core dumps with corrupted heap metadata
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade GPAC to version 1.1.0 or later with patched media.c bounds checking. Implement: (1) Input validation for MP4 box sizes against file boundaries; (2) Heap protections via compiler flags (-fstack-protector-all, -D_FORTIFY_SOURCE=2); (3) Address Space Layout Randomization (ASLR) and DEP/NX bit enforcement; (4) Run MP4Box in sandboxed environment with restricted file system and process capabilities; (5) Disable automatic MP4 file processing of untrusted sources; (6) Monitor library version in deployment and enforce minimum version policies

threat_vector: Privilege escalation vulnerability in smartphone file system component
exploitation_method: Attacker exploits improper permission validation or race conditions in file system operations to gain elevated privileges, potentially through symlink attacks, capability misuse, or kernel module exploitation
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unauthorized access to /system or /data partitions, unusual SELinux denials, capability changes on processes, repeated EACCES errors followed by successful operations, unexpected UID/GID transitions, modified file permissions on critical system files, and excessive open() calls with O_FOLLOW flags on symbolic links
mitigation_strategy: Apply latest OS security patches immediately, enforce SELinux in enforcing mode, implement strict file system permissions and ACLs, restrict privileged daemon functionality, enable Code Integrity verification, use file system hardening options (immutable flags), monitor and whitelist symlink creation, implement capability-based security model, and conduct regular privilege escalation assessments

threat_vector: Hardcoded JWT Secret Key in QXIP SIPCAPTURE homer-app < 1.4.28
exploitation_method: Attacker obtains the static JWT secret (167f0db2-f83e-4baa-9736-d56064a5b415) from public sources, documentation, or binary analysis. They forge valid JWT tokens to impersonate any user across all affected installations, bypassing authentication and gaining unauthorized access to HOMER 7.x deployments.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) JWT tokens with 'iss' or 'sub' claims that don't match expected user patterns; (2) Sudden spike in authentication tokens from same secret across different customer instances; (3) Tokens with modified 'exp' (expiration) claims set to future dates; (4) Access logs showing authenticated sessions from unfamiliar source IPs with valid JWT but no login events; (5) Configuration management alerts when JWT secret remains unchanged across deployments
mitigation_strategy: Immediately upgrade to QXIP SIPCAPTURE homer-app 1.4.28 or later. For instances on vulnerable versions: (1) Rotate the JWT secret to a cryptographically random value unique per installation; (2) Implement JWT token rotation/invalidation mechanisms; (3) Add token signature validation with pinned per-customer secrets; (4) Deploy API gateway with rate limiting on authentication endpoints; (5) Enable comprehensive JWT claim validation (exp, iat, nbf); (6) Implement mutual TLS for inter-service communication; (7) Audit all existing JWT tokens and invalidate those created before remediation

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Automated Collection (T1123)
exploitation_method: Attacker deploys scripts or tools (web scrapers, API crawlers, automated query tools) to systematically and continuously gather large volumes of data from target systems, applications, or APIs without manual interaction, often to exfiltrate credentials, configurations, or sensitive business data at scale
detection_pattern: High volume of automated requests from single source IP; repeated identical query patterns; requests lacking typical browser user-agent strings; API calls with abnormal frequency or concurrency; bulk data export requests; unusual read operations on sensitive data stores; consistent request timing intervals; failed authentication attempts followed by successful elevated access; network traffic anomalies showing periodic data transfer spikes
mitigation_strategy: Implement rate limiting and request throttling on APIs and web services; deploy Web Application Firewall (WAF) rules to detect and block automated scraping patterns; require authentication with MFA for sensitive data access; monitor and alert on bulk export operations; use CAPTCHA challenges for suspicious activity; implement IP reputation filtering; segment sensitive data with access controls; log all data access with timestamps and user context; conduct regular API security audits; deploy deception (honeypots) to detect reconnaissance

threat_vector: Unrestricted File Upload in Sourcecodester Vehicle Service Management System 1.0 allowing malicious file upload leading to HTML injection
exploitation_method: Attacker uploads a file with HTML/JavaScript content through an unrestricted file upload endpoint. The application stores the file without validation of file type, content, or extension. When the file is accessed or rendered in a web context, the HTML/JavaScript executes in the victim's browser, enabling session hijacking, credential theft, or malware distribution.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) POST requests to upload endpoints with unexpected file extensions or MIME types; (2) Files with HTML tags, script tags, or event handlers in upload directories; (3) Access logs showing retrieval of uploaded files with .html, .htm, .js extensions in normally binary directories; (4) Web server logs indicating 200 responses for user-uploaded files containing <script>, <iframe>, or event handler attributes; (5) File system changes showing executable or web-interpretable files in upload directories with suspicious timestamps
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict file upload controls: (1) Whitelist allowed file types by extension and validate MIME types server-side; (2) Store uploaded files outside web root or in a non-executable directory; (3) Rename uploaded files to remove original extensions and use a mapping system; (4) Set appropriate file permissions (read-only, no execute); (5) Configure web server to not execute scripts in upload directories (.htaccess or nginx config); (6) Scan uploaded files with antivirus/content inspection before storing; (7) Serve uploads with Content-Disposition: attachment headers to force downloads; (8) Implement file size limits; (9) Use security headers like Content-Security-Policy to restrict script execution

threat_vector: Open Redirect via X-Forwarded-Host Header in Rails Action Pack >= 6.0.0
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts HTTP requests with malicious X-Forwarded-Host headers that bypass host validation when allowed_host configurations use overly permissive patterns (e.g., regex or wildcard formats). The header is trusted as authoritative, redirecting users to attacker-controlled domains while appearing legitimate.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) X-Forwarded-Host headers with domains differing from canonical Host header; (2) Redirect response codes (301/302) with Location headers pointing to external domains; (3) GET/POST requests with URL fragments or parameters containing external domain names; (4) Repeated X-Forwarded-Host values not matching configured proxy IP ranges; (5) Web server logs showing mismatched Host vs X-Forwarded-Host headers from untrusted source IPs.
mitigation_strategy: Implement these controls: (1) Use strict allowed_host configurations with exact domain matching rather than regex patterns; (2) Validate X-Forwarded-Host only from trusted proxy IPs using whitelist; (3) Disable X-Forwarded-Host processing if not required; (4) Upgrade Action Pack to patched versions (6.0.4.1+, 6.1.4+, 7.0.1+); (5) Implement Content-Security-Policy headers to restrict redirect targets; (6) Use allowlists for valid redirect destinations in application code.

threat_vector: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) in HUAWEI WS318n network settings configuration interface
exploitation_method: Attacker with local authentication access injects malicious JavaScript payload into network settings fields (SSID, hostname, DNS server, etc.) that lack input validation. When administrator views these settings in web interface, script executes in browser context with admin privileges.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) HTML/JavaScript tags in network configuration database queries (script, iframe, onerror, onload patterns); (2) Unexpected JavaScript in router logs or settings exports; (3) Browser console errors when accessing network settings; (4) Unusual HTTP responses containing unescaped user input; (5) Configuration backups containing encoded or obfuscated script tags
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict input validation: whitelist alphanumeric characters, hyphens, dots for hostnames/SSIDs; HTML-encode all user input before rendering in web interface; apply Content Security Policy (CSP) headers to prevent inline script execution; sanitize all configuration fields server-side before storage; regularly patch firmware; disable unnecessary scripting features; enforce HTTPS for all admin interfaces; implement rate limiting on configuration changes

threat_vector: Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR) in profile endpoint
exploitation_method: Attacker modifies user ID parameter in API calls (e.g., /api/profile/123) to sequentially enumerate and access other users' profiles by incrementing the ID, bypassing authorization checks
detection_pattern: Multiple 200 OK responses for /api/profile/* requests with different user IDs from single source IP; abnormal access pattern to profile endpoints with sequential or randomized user IDs; access to profile data for users outside attacker's authorization scope logged in access/API logs
mitigation_strategy: Implement server-side authorization checks verifying the authenticated user owns the requested resource before returning data; use UUIDs instead of sequential IDs; employ middleware to validate user context against resource ownership; implement rate limiting on profile endpoint; log and alert on suspicious enumeration patterns; conduct access control testing during development

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Application Exhaustion Flood
exploitation_method: Attacker sends high volume of requests to application endpoints, consuming server resources (CPU, memory, network bandwidth) to degrade service availability. Requests may target specific resource-intensive operations or distributed across multiple endpoints.
detection_pattern: Sudden spike in HTTP request rates from single or multiple sources; increased server response times and error rates (503, 504); elevated CPU/memory utilization on application servers; connection pool exhaustion; increased database query latency; patterns of requests to same endpoint(s) within short timeframe; unusual geographic distribution of requests
mitigation_strategy: Implement rate limiting per IP/user account; deploy Web Application Firewall (WAF) with traffic throttling rules; use Content Delivery Network (CDN) with DDoS protection; configure request queuing and timeout policies; implement Circuit Breaker pattern for degraded service; monitor and auto-scale resources; establish baseline traffic profiles for anomaly detection; block or throttle sources exceeding request thresholds

threat_vector: OpenID Connect Provider allows 'none' algorithm for unsigned tokens; pac4j v5.3.0 and prior accepts unsigned tokens by default
exploitation_method: Attacker creates a malicious OIDC provider or intercepts token generation to craft unsigned JWT tokens (algorithm: 'none'). pac4j fails to validate signature requirements and accepts these forged tokens as legitimate, allowing authentication bypass and unauthorized access.
detection_pattern: Log entries showing: JWT tokens with 'alg': 'none' in header; token validation accepting unsigned tokens; authentication succeeding without cryptographic signature verification; sudden authentication from unexpected OIDC providers; tokens lacking 'kid' (key ID) or signature fields in token validation logs
mitigation_strategy: 1) Upgrade pac4j to v5.3.1+ which rejects 'none' algorithm by default; 2) Explicitly configure OidcConfiguration to enforce algorithm whitelist excluding 'none'; 3) Implement server-side token validation requiring non-null algorithm with trusted signing keys; 4) Monitor and reject any JWT with 'alg': 'none' at API gateway/WAF level; 5) Use OAuth2 token introspection endpoint to validate tokens server-side rather than client-side parsing

threat_vector: Code Injection vulnerability in HHEE system
exploitation_method: Attacker injects malicious code through unsanitized input parameters (form fields, API endpoints, configuration files) that are directly executed by the HHEE system interpreter without validation or filtering
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unusual characters or syntax in input logs (quotes, semicolons, brackets, script tags); (2) Failed execution attempts followed by successful injection patterns; (3) Unexpected process spawning from HHEE application; (4) Abnormal increase in error logs with stack traces; (5) WAF/IDS alerts for code injection signatures; (6) Unexpected file modifications in HHEE directories with timestamps correlating to suspicious requests
mitigation_strategy: Implement input validation using whitelist-based filtering and parameterized queries/prepared statements; enforce output encoding for all user-supplied data; deploy Web Application Firewall (WAF) with code injection rulesets; apply principle of least privilege to HHEE service accounts; conduct secure code review and static analysis; implement runtime application self-protection (RASP); maintain updated patching for HHEE and dependencies; isolate HHEE in containerized environment with restricted permissions

threat_vector: Invalid CVE Candidate Submission
exploitation_method: Not applicable - this is an administrative invalid entry rather than an exploitable vulnerability
detection_pattern: Monitor CVE databases and feeds for entries marked as 'withdrawn by CNA' or 'not a security issue'; flag any security tools or scanning systems that reference withdrawn CVE identifiers; audit vulnerability management databases for orphaned or invalid candidate references
mitigation_strategy: Implement CVE feed validation to filter withdrawn candidates before ingestion into security tools; maintain a blocklist of invalidated CVE IDs; regularly reconcile vulnerability databases against official CVE list status; configure alerts when deprecated candidates are referenced in scans; establish procedural controls to verify CVE validity before incident response actions

threat_vector: Incorrect authorization vulnerability in KONICA MINOLTA bizhub series multifunction printers allowing unauthorized access to sensitive functions
exploitation_method: Attacker bypasses authentication checks on administrative functions or user data access by exploiting missing or improperly implemented authorization controls. This typically involves direct API calls, network requests, or firmware manipulation that skip proper permission validation for configuration changes, document access, or credential retrieval.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unauthorized administrative function calls without proper credentials in device logs; unexpected configuration changes without matching authentication events; access to sensitive data endpoints from non-privileged sessions; firmware modification attempts; unusual network requests to device management interfaces from untrusted sources; failed authorization attempts followed by successful access to restricted functions; abnormal document retrieval patterns outside user workflows
mitigation_strategy: Immediately update affected devices to patched firmware versions (bizhub C750i G00-36 or later, C650i/C550i/C450i G00-B7 or later, C360i/C300i/C250i G00-B7 or later). Implement network segmentation isolating printers on dedicated VLAN with restricted access controls. Enforce strong authentication for all administrative access. Disable unnecessary network services. Implement role-based access control (RBAC) at network level. Deploy intrusion detection system monitoring printer traffic. Audit all device configurations and recent administrative changes. Restrict printer firmware updates to authenticated channels only.

threat_vector: DNS Reply ID Mismatch Vulnerability (dnslib ≤0.9.16)
exploitation_method: Attacker sends unsolicited DNS replies with mismatched transaction IDs that dnslib accepts without validation. This enables DNS spoofing attacks where forged responses are accepted as legitimate replies to queries, allowing cache poisoning, man-in-the-middle attacks, and redirection to malicious servers.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) DNS responses received without corresponding queries in query log; (2) Multiple DNS replies with different payloads for same domain within short timeframe; (3) Unexpected DNS A/AAAA record changes in cache; (4) Outbound connections to unexpected IP addresses following DNS queries; (5) Application logs showing dnslib processing replies with ID fields not matching sent query IDs; (6) Network traffic analysis showing unsolicited inbound DNS responses on port 53
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade dnslib to version 0.9.17 or later which validates DNS reply ID matches query ID. Implement network-level protections: (1) DNS query/response correlation checks; (2) DNSSEC validation for critical domains; (3) DNS response rate limiting; (4) Restrict DNS queries to trusted resolvers only; (5) Monitor and alert on any dnslib version <0.9.17 in use; (6) Implement application-level DNS reply validation independent of library; (7) Use DNS over TLS (DoT) or HTTPS (DoH) to encrypt and authenticate DNS traffic

threat_vector: Denial of Service in Binaryen 104 - Assertion abort in wasm::WasmBinaryBuilder::readFunctions
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts a malformed WebAssembly binary with invalid function section data that triggers an assertion failure in the readFunctions parser, causing the Binaryen process to abort and terminate service availability
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unexpected process termination/core dumps of Binaryen-based applications with stack traces containing 'readFunctions', (2) Segmentation faults or assertion failures when processing WASM binaries, (3) Spike in malformed WASM file upload attempts, (4) Exit code indicating assertion failure (typically 134 on Linux), (5) Error logs containing 'wasm::WasmBinaryBuilder' and function parsing failures
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Binaryen to version 105 or later that contains the assertion abort fix. Implement input validation and size limits on WebAssembly binary uploads before processing. Wrap Binaryen calls in try-catch blocks or process isolation with automatic restart. Add rate limiting on WASM file processing endpoints. Use fuzzing-resistant parsing with graceful error handling instead of assertions for untrusted input validation

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1003.002 - OS Credential Dumping: Security Account Manager (SAM)
exploitation_method: Attacker obtains SAM file from %SystemRoot%\System32\config\SAM to extract password hashes offline. Typically requires SYSTEM privileges via volume shadow copy (VSS), registry parsing, or physical access. Tools like Mimikatz, hashcat, or custom scripts parse hashes for credential material.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: VSS deletion commands (vssadmin delete shadows), SAM file copy attempts to unusual locations, suspicious handle access to SAM registry hive, NTDS.dit access, cmd.exe with /c reg commands, HKLM\SAM registry access patterns, abnormal System process behavior, elevated privilege escalation followed by file access in %SystemRoot%\System32\config\
mitigation_strategy: Enable Windows Defender Credential Guard (Hypervisor-protected credentials), enforce multi-factor authentication, restrict local admin accounts, disable unnecessary elevation, enable SMB signing and encryption, protect VSS from deletion (Set-MpPreference -DisableRebootOnCompletion $false), deploy EDR solution monitoring process creation and handle access, restrict Registry Editor access, enable audit policies for SAM access, maintain up-to-date patches, implement application whitelisting for Mimikatz-like tools

threat_vector: IBM PowerVM Hypervisor FW860, FW940, FW950, FW1010 VM isolation bypass via malformed management operations
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts specific sequence of VM management API calls (partition state transitions, memory/resource operations) that exploit boundary condition in hypervisor isolation logic, allowing memory or I/O access between peer virtual machines sharing same physical resources
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unusual VM management operation sequences in hypervisor logs (rapid state transitions without corresponding user requests), (2) Cross-partition memory access violations in firmware audit logs, (3) VMs accessing unexpected device assignments or shared memory regions, (4) Syslog entries showing 'partition isolation violation' or similar boundary errors, (5) Anomalous inter-VM communication patterns detected via network monitoring, (6) Unauthorized resource access between VMs at same security level
mitigation_strategy: Apply IBM firmware patches (update to patched FW860, FW940, FW950, FW1010 versions immediately), implement strict RBAC for VM management operations, disable unnecessary VM inter-partition communication channels, segment VMs by security domain, monitor hypervisor call sequences with behavioral analysis, implement signed firmware verification, configure audit logging for all partition management operations, restrict hypervisor management network access

threat_vector: NocoDB CSV Injection/Formula Injection (CVE-2023-26897) - Versions 0.81.0 through 0.83.8
exploitation_method: Attacker with low privileges creates a new table and injects malicious formulas (starting with =, +, -, @) into table cell values. When another user exports the table to CSV and opens it in spreadsheet applications (Excel, LibreOffice), the formulas execute with the privileges of the user opening the file, potentially leading to arbitrary code execution or data exfiltration.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) table creation followed by rows containing cells with formula indicators (=, +, -, @) at cell start; (2) CSV exports containing formula patterns in logs; (3) Unusual characters in cell data: '=cmd|' '/c powershell', '=1+1*cmd|', '@SUM(1+9)*cmd|'; (4) Users with low privilege levels creating tables with suspicious formula content; (5) Spike in CSV export operations by low-privilege accounts
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade to NocoDB version 0.84.0 or later which sanitizes cell input. Implement input validation to strip or escape formula characters (=, +, -, @, |) at cell start. Configure CSV export settings to prepend single quote (') to all exported values to prevent formula interpretation. Restrict CSV export permissions to trusted users only. Educate users to disable formula auto-execution in spreadsheet applications or open CSVs in text editors first to verify content.

threat_vector: Beaver Themer conditional logic bypass in post archives
exploitation_method: Attacker exploits insufficient enforcement of conditional visibility rules when Beaver Themer layouts are applied to archive pages. By directly accessing archive URLs or manipulating query parameters, they can view content that should be hidden by conditional logic checks, bypassing intended access restrictions on sensitive post data.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unusual archive page requests with parameters that should trigger conditional hiding, (2) Access logs showing archive pages accessed by users lacking required conditions (user role, post status, custom field values), (3) Beaver Themer condition rule logs showing conditional checks that passed unexpectedly, (4) Pattern of requests to archive pages followed by access to hidden content without proper condition fulfillment
mitigation_strategy: Implement server-side validation of all conditional logic rules before rendering content in archive templates, regardless of theme/themer settings. Apply capability checks and content visibility rules at the database query layer, not just template rendering. Regularly audit Beaver Themer conditional logic rules for gaps. Ensure WordPress post meta and user role checks are enforced server-side. Keep Beaver Themer and WordPress core updated. Use security headers to prevent unauthorized archive access.

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1078.003 - Local Account
exploitation_method: Attacker gains access to a compromised system and leverages existing local user accounts (both standard and administrator) to maintain persistence, escalate privileges, or move laterally within the network without creating suspicious new accounts
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unusual login times for dormant local accounts, (2) Failed authentication attempts followed by successful logins with local accounts, (3) Local account creation or modification events (Event ID 4720, 4722, 4738), (4) Privilege Group membership changes (Event ID 4728, 4732), (5) Interactive logons from non-interactive service accounts, (6) Rapid successive logins with different local accounts from same source
mitigation_strategy: (1) Enforce strong password policies and multi-factor authentication on local administrative accounts, (2) Disable unnecessary local accounts and regularly audit active accounts, (3) Implement principle of least privilege - use service accounts with minimal required permissions, (4) Monitor and restrict interactive logons for service/system accounts, (5) Use credential guard and LSA protection on Windows systems, (6) Deploy EDR solutions to detect suspicious local account behavior, (7) Implement conditional access policies restricting logon times and locations

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1222: File System Permissions Weakness
exploitation_method: Attacker modifies NTFS/POSIX permissions (chmod, cacls, icacls) on sensitive files or directories to grant unauthorized read/write/execute access, or removes access controls to escalate privileges or exfiltrate data
detection_pattern: Monitor for: permission change events (Windows Event ID 4670, 4898; auditd rules for chmod/chown), unexpected permission modifications on critical system directories (/etc, /root, C:\Windows), batch permission changes outside maintenance windows, ACL modifications via PowerShell Set-Acl, file access permission elevation for low-privileged accounts
mitigation_strategy: Enforce principle of least privilege through default restrictive permissions; implement file integrity monitoring (Tripwire, AIDE, Windows File Integrity Monitoring); restrict sudo/administrative capabilities for chmod/chown commands; use AppLocker/SELinux to prevent unauthorized permission modifications; regularly audit and enforce permission baselines; restrict access to permission management utilities; enable immutable attributes on critical files where possible

threat_vector: Stored XSS in Vehicle Service Management System 1.0 - Service List Section
exploitation_method: Attacker injects malicious JavaScript payload into Service List input fields during login panel interaction. The payload is stored in the database and executed in the browsers of all users who view the Service List, allowing session hijacking, credential theft, or malware distribution.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unusual script tags or event handlers in Service List database records; (2) HTTP POST requests to Service List endpoints with encoded payload patterns (e.g., <script>, javascript:, onerror=, onload=); (3) Content Security Policy (CSP) violations in browser console logs; (4) Unexpected JavaScript execution in Service List page DOM; (5) Database audit logs showing insertion of HTML/script entities in Service List fields
mitigation_strategy: Implement: (1) Input validation - whitelist allowed characters and reject/sanitize HTML/script tags; (2) Output encoding - HTML-encode all Service List data before rendering in browser using context-appropriate encoding; (3) Content Security Policy (CSP) headers restricting inline script execution; (4) Parameterized queries to prevent injection; (5) Security headers (X-XSS-Protection, X-Content-Type-Options); (6) Regular security patching of the application; (7) Web Application Firewall (WAF) rules detecting XSS payload patterns

threat_vector: CVE-2020-27339 - Duplicate CVE Identifier Confusion
exploitation_method: Attacker leverages CVE database confusion by referencing a reserved/duplicate CVE ID to obfuscate actual vulnerability details, delaying patch analysis and creating tracking gaps in vulnerability management systems
detection_pattern: Monitor for: CVE references pointing to reserved/duplicate IDs in security advisories, mismatched vulnerability metadata in asset management systems, scanning tools reporting inconsistent CVE linkages, delayed or missing patches for canonical CVE versions
mitigation_strategy: Implement CVE deduplication logic in vulnerability tracking systems; cross-reference all CVE IDs against official NVD database; maintain canonical CVE mappings; audit vulnerability management tools for proper CVE alias handling; establish process requiring verification of CVE validity before patch prioritization

threat_vector: Extra Window Memory Injection (T1181)
exploitation_method: Attacker injects malicious code into memory space of legitimate Windows GUI applications by exploiting window message handling. Typically involves WriteProcessMemory or similar APIs to modify heap/stack of target process, then triggering execution via window messages (WM_*) or callback functions.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unusual WriteProcessMemory/VirtualAllocEx calls between unrelated processes; legitimate GUI apps (notepad.exe, explorer.exe) receiving unexpected memory write attempts from suspicious processes; abnormal window message queues with large data payloads; API call sequences involving OpenProcess(PROCESS_VM_WRITE) followed by WriteProcessMemory on low-integrity targets; unexpected exceptions or crashes in GUI application message loops; Sysmon Event ID 8 (CreateRemoteThread) or ID 10 (ProcessAccess) targeting explorer.exe, dwm.exe, or other windowed processes from unexpected sources.
mitigation_strategy: 1) Enable and enforce Code Integrity policies (Device Guard/HVCI) to prevent code injection; 2) Restrict API access via AppLocker/WDAC to block unsigned executables; 3) Run GUI applications with minimal privileges; 4) Implement Control Flow Guard (CFG) and Address Space Layout Randomization (ASLR); 5) Monitor and alert on cross-process memory operations; 6) Apply latest Windows patches for window management vulnerabilities; 7) Disable unnecessary COM/RPC services; 8) Use Exploit Protection settings to harden target processes; 9) Implement endpoint detection and response (EDR) with behavioral analysis of process memory access patterns.

threat_vector: PendingIntent hijacking in Reminder app (versions <12.2.05.0 on Android 11, <12.3.02.1000 on Android 12) allowing privilege escalation
exploitation_method: Attacker intercepts and modifies PendingIntent objects due to insufficient FLAG_IMMUTABLE protection. By crafting malicious intents with matching action/data, attacker can redirect privileged reminder operations to malicious components, executing actions with the Reminder app's permissions.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unexpected intent interception in logcat with action=com.android.reminder.* lacking FLAG_IMMUTABLE, (2) PendingIntent creation without immutability flags in stack traces, (3) Logcat entries showing 'Unable to find app for action' followed by unusual component resolution, (4) SELinux denials for unexpected binder calls to reminder service, (5) Package manager queries for reminder intent handlers from non-system apps
mitigation_strategy: Enforce secure PendingIntent patterns: (1) Require FLAG_IMMUTABLE and FLAG_UPDATE_CURRENT flags on all PendingIntent.get*() calls, (2) Validate explicit intents with component names rather than implicit intents, (3) Implement runtime permission checks before executing reminder actions, (4) Use PendingIntent signature verification via queryIntentActivities() with MATCH_DEFAULT_ONLY, (5) Deploy SELinux policy restricting reminder service binder access, (6) Update Reminder app to patched versions (≥12.2.05.0 for Android 11, ≥12.3.02.1000 for Android 12)

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1547.012: Boot or Logon Autostart Execution - Print Processors
exploitation_method: Attacker registers malicious DLL as print processor in Windows registry (HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Print\Environments\[Platform]\Print Processors). The DLL loads with SYSTEM privileges during printer initialization, executing arbitrary code before user login.
detection_pattern: Monitor registry modifications to Print Processors keys; unexpected DLL entries lacking Microsoft signatures; unusual DLL loading from non-standard print processor paths; Sysmon Event 13 for registry changes to HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Print\Environments; orphaned or unsigned DLLs in %SystemRoot%\System32\spool\prtprocs\; suspicious process creation from spoolsv.exe with unexpected parent or child processes.
mitigation_strategy: Restrict registry write permissions to Print Processors keys (principle of least privilege); implement AppLocker rules to whitelist only approved print processor DLLs; disable Print Spooler service if not required; enable code signing enforcement for print processors; regularly audit and inventory legitimate print processors; deploy endpoint detection and response (EDR) tools with behavioral monitoring; keep Windows and printer drivers patched; implement hardware-backed secure boot to prevent pre-boot modifications.

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Modify Cloud Resource Hierarchy (T1578.003)
exploitation_method: Attacker with compromised cloud credentials or IAM privileges modifies organizational units, resource groups, or folder structures in cloud environments (AWS, Azure, GCP) to move resources between management boundaries, evade monitoring, or gain unauthorized access to isolated workloads
detection_pattern: CloudTrail/Cloud Audit Logs: CreateOrganizationalUnit, MoveAccount, UpdateFolder, ModifyResourceGroup API calls from unexpected principals; Azure: CreateResourceGroup with unusual naming; GCP: SetIamPolicy on folders; alerts on bulk resource relocation; changes to resource tags or hierarchical structure outside change windows; IAM policy modifications enabling cross-organizational access
mitigation_strategy: Implement least-privilege IAM policies restricting organization/folder/resource group modification to specific admin roles; enable CloudTrail/audit logging with immutable storage; use cloud resource locks (AWS Resource Control Policy, Azure Management Locks, GCP Organization Policy Constraints) to prevent hierarchy changes; require multi-factor authentication and approval workflows for structural modifications; monitor and alert on all organization-level API calls; use drift detection tools to identify unapproved resource repositioning; implement RBAC boundaries preventing privilege escalation across organizational units

threat_vector: LiveHelperChat vulnerable to Generation of Error Message Containing Sensitive Information
exploitation_method: Attacker triggers application errors through malformed requests, SQL injection attempts, or invalid input to LiveHelperChat API endpoints. Application responds with verbose error messages containing database paths, SQL queries, internal file structures, or configuration details that reveal system architecture and sensitive information.
detection_pattern: Monitor HTTP response bodies for error messages containing: SQL syntax, database table names, file system paths (/var/www/, C:\), function names, stack traces, API keys, internal IP addresses, database connection strings. Check application logs for repeated error generation from single source IP. Track HTTP status codes 500-599 with verbose error content. Analyze response headers and error pages for disclosure of software versions or internal details.
mitigation_strategy: Implement generic error messages for end-users while logging detailed errors server-side only. Configure application error_reporting to E_ALL but display_errors to OFF in production. Use centralized logging (syslog, ELK) to capture full errors securely. Implement input validation and sanitization on all user inputs. Deploy Web Application Firewall (WAF) rules to strip sensitive information from error responses. Regularly test error handling with fuzzing tools. Conduct security code review of error handling routines. Implement custom error page templates that don't reveal system information.

threat_vector: Symlink following vulnerability in Trend Micro Worry-Free Business Security (on-premises) enabling arbitrary file overwrite with SYSTEM privileges
exploitation_method: Attacker creates symbolic links in predictable temporary directories that Trend Micro services write to without proper validation. When the service writes files following symlinks, attacker-controlled symlinks redirect writes to arbitrary system locations (e.g., system executables, configuration files, service binaries), achieving privilege escalation and code execution.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Failed file operations or permission errors in Trend Micro service logs; (2) Excessive symlink creation in temp directories (%TEMP%, %APPDATA%, C:\Windows\Temp); (3) File writes to system-critical paths (System32, drivers, services) by Trend Micro processes; (4) Unexpected file modifications in protected directories with timestamps correlating to Trend Micro service activity; (5) Event ID 4663 (attempts to access object) on system executables; (6) Rapid file creation followed by deletion patterns indicating race condition exploitation.
mitigation_strategy: Apply vendor security patches immediately. Restrict file permissions on Trend Micro installation and temporary directories to prevent symlink creation (use NTFS ACLs to deny unauthorized Write permissions). Configure AppLocker/Device Guard to restrict Trend Micro service executables. Implement file integrity monitoring (Windows File Integrity Monitoring or third-party tools) on critical system directories. Use least privilege for Trend Micro service accounts. Deploy EDR solution to monitor process-to-file interactions and anomalous SYSTEM-context file operations. Regularly audit and log symlink creation attempts.

threat_vector: Denial of Service in Binaryen 104 - Assertion abort in wasm::WasmBinaryBuilder::visitRethrow()
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts a malicious WebAssembly binary containing an invalid or unexpected rethrow instruction that triggers an assertion failure in the visitRethrow() function during parsing/compilation, causing the Binaryen process to abort.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unexpected process termination of Binaryen compiler with assertion failure logs mentioning 'visitRethrow', (2) WebAssembly binary analysis tools crashing with stack traces referencing wasm::WasmBinaryBuilder::visitRethrow, (3) Resource monitoring showing abrupt termination of wasm compilation jobs, (4) Application logs showing failed wasm module validation with assertion errors
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Binaryen to version 105 or later where this assertion vulnerability is patched. Implement input validation to reject malformed WebAssembly binaries before processing. Deploy timeout mechanisms for wasm compilation tasks. Run Binaryen in sandboxed/containerized environments with resource limits to prevent full system impact. Add try-catch exception handling around wasm binary parsing to gracefully handle crashes rather than aborting.

threat_vector: Guest-initiated kernel memory exhaustion via Linux netback driver
exploitation_method: Unprivileged guest VM sends crafted network packets or triggers excessive descriptor allocation in netback driver, causing unbounded kernel memory consumption in dom0/host, potentially leading to out-of-memory conditions and denial of service
detection_pattern: Monitor for: sudden spike in kernel memory usage correlated with network activity from specific VM; excessive netback ring buffer allocations; dom0 memory pressure events; kernel logs showing netback-related allocation failures; sustained high page fault rates in netback context; /proc/meminfo showing growing Slab or KernelStack usage during guest network activity
mitigation_strategy: Apply kernel patches limiting netback ring descriptor allocations per guest; implement per-VM memory quotas for network driver resources; configure xl/xm domain memory limits with buffer overhead; enable memory ballooning to recover pages; monitor domainU network traffic rates and implement rate limiting; upgrade to patched kernel versions addressing netback memory exhaustion CVEs; use cgroup v2 memory limits to constrain guest network driver memory usage

threat_vector: Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI) in Jinja2
exploitation_method: Attacker injects malicious Jinja2 template syntax (e.g., {{7*7}}, {{config}}, {{self.__init__.__globals__.__builtins__.__import__('os').popen('cmd').read()}}) into user-controllable input fields that are rendered server-side without sanitization, allowing arbitrary code execution through template evaluation.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: suspicious template syntax in logs ({{ }}, {# #}, {% %}); unusual object attribute access patterns (__globals__, __builtins__, __class__); system command execution following template rendering; template engine errors containing user input; abnormal process spawning from application; requests with payload keywords like 'config', 'self', '__import__', 'popen', 'system'.
mitigation_strategy: 1) Use Jinja2 autoescape=True and enable sandbox mode; 2) Never pass raw user input directly to template rendering—use template variables exclusively; 3) Implement strict input validation and sanitization; 4) Use restricted Jinja2 environments with limited globals/filters; 5) Apply principle of least privilege to application process; 6) Use CSP headers; 7) Regular security testing and code review; 8) Keep Jinja2 and dependencies patched.

threat_vector: Spinnaker AppEngine TAR file path traversal vulnerability during deployment extraction
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious TAR archive with specially crafted file paths containing directory traversal sequences (../) that bypass extraction validation. When Spinnaker's AppEngine deployer extracts the TAR without proper path sanitization, files are written outside intended deployment directory, potentially overwriting critical application or system files.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: TAR extraction operations with path traversal indicators (../ or ..\ in archived paths), unexpected file writes outside deployment directories, modification of application configuration files during deployment, TAR archives with suspicious path lengths or encoding, deployment tasks writing to parent directories of intended target, and audit logs showing file creation in unauthorized locations during Spinnaker deployment jobs.
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict TAR extraction validation: (1) Use TAR libraries with built-in path traversal protection; (2) Validate all extracted paths are within intended deployment directory before writing; (3) Sanitize file paths by rejecting entries containing ../, absolute paths, or symbolic links; (4) Run AppEngine deployer with minimal filesystem permissions in containerized/sandboxed environment; (5) Implement file integrity monitoring on deployment targets; (6) Regularly patch Spinnaker to latest version; (7) Use signed/verified deployment artifacts; (8) Implement deployment pipeline scanning to detect malicious TAR structures before extraction.

threat_vector: Memory corruption via race condition in vow driver leading to local privilege escalation
exploitation_method: Attacker exploits timing window between memory allocation and access control enforcement in vow driver syscalls. By triggering concurrent operations on driver resources, attacker forces use-after-free or double-free conditions, allowing arbitrary kernel memory write and code execution at System privilege level.
